**Step 3 — Mean free path:** A molecule sweeps a cylinder of cross-section $\pi\sigma^{2}$. The relative motion of the targets introduces a $\sqrt{2}$ factor:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi \sigma^{2} n}$$

**Step 4 — Number flux across a plane:** $\tfrac{1}{6}$ of molecules move in $+x$ direction:

$$N_{+} = \tfrac{1}{6} n \bar{v}, \quad N_{-} = \tfrac{1}{6} n \bar{v}$$

**Step 5 — Net y-momentum flux from a plane at $x_0$:**

$$\tau_{yx} = \tfrac{1}{6} n \bar{v} \left[ m u_y(x_0 - \lambda) - m u_y(x_0 + \lambda) \right] = -\tfrac{1}{3} n m \bar{v} \lambda \frac{du_y}{dx}$$

**Step 6 — Identify with Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\mu = \tfrac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda = \tfrac{1}{3} \rho \sqrt{\frac{8kT}{\pi m}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi \sigma^{2} n}$$

**Step 7 — Final simplified form** (with $\rho = nm$):

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2}{3\pi^{3/2}\sigma^{2}} \sqrt{mkT} = \frac{5}{16\sigma^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{mkT}{\pi}}}$$

The second form is the Chapman–Enskog first approximation; the numerical prefactor $\tfrac{5}{16}$ replaces the less accurate $\tfrac{2}{3\sqrt{\pi}}$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**Step 1 — Lennard-Jones potential:**

$$U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$

- $\sigma$ = the intermolecular distance at which $U(r) = 0$ (collision diameter).
- $\varepsilon$ = the depth of the potential well at $r = r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$ (well depth).
- The $(\sigma/r)^{12}$ term represents short-range **repulsion** (Pauli exclusion), and the $(\sigma/r)^{6}$ term represents long-range **van der Waals attraction** (London dispersion).

**Step 2 — Chapman–Enskog viscosity:**

$$\mu = \frac{5}{16\,\pi \sigma^{2}\,\Omega_{\mu}(T^{*})} \sqrt{\pi m k T}, \quad T^{*} = \frac{kT}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 3 — Limit to rigid sphere:**

A rigid sphere corresponds to a potential that is zero for $r > \sigma$ and infinite for $r \leq \sigma$. In this limit:
- The attractive term $(\sigma/r)^{6}$ is *removed* $\to$ the molecules experience no attraction.
- The repulsive term $(\sigma/r)^{12}$ is *infinitely steep* $\to$ acts as a hard wall at $r = \sigma$.

In this limit, the scattering cross-section becomes a constant $\pi\sigma^{2}/4$, independent of energy. The collision integral:

$$\Omega_{\mu} = \frac{\text{actual energy-averaged cross-section}}{\text{hard-sphere cross-section}} \to 1$$

Substituting $\Omega_{\mu} = 1$:

$$\mu = \frac{5}{16\,\pi \sigma^{2}} \sqrt{\pi m k T} = \frac{5}{16\sigma^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{mkT}{\pi}}$$

This is **exactly** the rigid-sphere Chapman–Enskog result.

**Step 4 — Role of $\Omega_{\mu}$:** The collision integral $\Omega_{\mu}(T^{*})$ corrects the hard-sphere result for:
- **Energy-dependent scattering cross-sections** in a realistic potential.
- **Orbiting collisions** (low $T^{*}$) where attractive forces curl the trajectories and *increase* the effective cross-section ($\Omega_{\mu} > 1$).
- **Quantum effects** at very low temperatures (negligible for KTU problems).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Application and Estimation Path

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] The viscosity of **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** at **T = 400 K** and **1 atm** is to be predicted using the Chapman–Enskog equation. The Lennard-Jones parameters for CO₂ are $\sigma = 3.941$ Å and $\varepsilon/k = 195.2$ K. Tabulated data: $\Omega_{\mu}(T^{*}) \approx 0.9427$ at $T^{*}=2.05$. Calculate the viscosity in micropoise and in Pa·s.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] Compare the predictions of the rigid-sphere model and the Chapman–Enskog model for the above case. The experimental value at 400 K is $\mu_{\text{exp}} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Comment on the percentage deviation in each case.

---

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**Given:**
- $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$ g/mol
- $T = 400$ K
- $\sigma = 3.941$ Å
- $\varepsilon/k = 195.2$ K
- $\Omega_{\mu} = 0.9427$ at $T^{*} = 400/195.2 = 2.049$ ≈ 2.05 ✓

**Step 1 — Apply the engineering formula:**

$$\mu = \frac{2.6693 \sqrt{MT}}{\sigma^{2}\,\Omega_{\mu}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$\sqrt{MT} = \sqrt{44.01 \times 400} = \sqrt{17604} = 132.68$$

$$2.6693 \times 132.68 = 354.19$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$\sigma^{2} = (3.941)^{2} = 15.53 \, \text{Å}^{2}$$

$$\sigma^{2} \times \Omega_{\mu} = 15.53 \times 0.9427 = 14.64$$

**Step 4 — Final viscosity:**

$$\mu = \frac{354.19}{14.64} = 24.19 \; \mu\text{P}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to SI:**

$$\mu = 24.19 \times 10^{-7} \text{ poise} = 24.19 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.1 \text{ Pa·s} = 2.419 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{CE} \approx 2.42 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**Rigid-sphere prediction (set $\Omega_{\mu} = 1$):**

$$\mu_{RS} = \frac{2.6693 \times 132.68}{15.53 \times 1} = \frac{354.19}{15.53} = 22.80 \; \mu\text{P} = 2.280 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Experimental value:** $\mu_{\text{exp}} = 2.30 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa·s $= 23.0 \; \mu$P

**Percentage deviations:**

$$\%\text{Dev}_{CE} = \frac{2.419 - 2.30}{2.30} \times 100 = +5.17\%$$

$$\%\text{Dev}_{RS} = \frac{2.280 - 2.30}{2.30} \times 100 = -0.87\%$$

**Wait — at this $T^{*}$, the rigid-sphere model *under-predicts* slightly while Chapman–Enskog over-predicts slightly.** The reason is that at $T^{*}=2.05$ the attractive interactions still enhance the cross-section only modestly. The correct tabulated $\Omega_{\mu}$ from a more refined source (e.g., Reid, Prausnitz, Poling) may give $\Omega_{\mu} \approx 1.040$ at this $T^{*}$, which would yield $\mu \approx 21.92 \; \mu$P, deviating from experiment by $-4.7\%$. Students should quote the deviation based on the $\Omega_{\mu}$ value used in the problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing Å and nm, or forgetting that $1\;\text{poise} = 0.1$ Pa·s, costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Omega_{\mu}$ in the denominator:** Using $\mu \propto 1/\sigma^{2}$ alone (rigid sphere) when the question asks for the rigorous model loses up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong $T^{*}$ computation:** Students often divide by $\varepsilon$ directly (in J) instead of $\varepsilon/k$ (in K). Watch out!
> 4. **Not converting MW to g/mol:** The constant 2.6693 requires $M$ in g/mol, not kg/mol.
> 5. **Omitting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor in the mean free path derivation:** Loses 1 mark in derivation parts.
> 6. **Using $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ without unit consistency:** A common source of magnitude errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Topic: Prediction of Gas Viscosity**

### Core Equations (Must Memorize)

$$\mu = \tfrac{5}{16\,\pi \sigma^{2}\,\Omega_{\mu}(T^{*})} \sqrt{\pi m k T} \quad \text{[Chapman–Enskog, full form]}$$

$$\mu = \tfrac{5}{16\,\sigma^{2}}\sqrt{mkT/\pi} \quad \text{[Rigid sphere, $\Omega_{\mu}=1$]}$$

$$\mu\,(\mu\text{P}) = \tfrac{2.6693\sqrt{MT}}{\sigma^{2}\Omega_{\mu}} \quad \text{[Engineering CGS form]}$$

$$\lambda = 1/(\sqrt{2}\,\pi\sigma^{2}n) \quad \text{[Mean free path, rigid sphere]}$$

$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{8kT/(\pi m)} \quad \text{[Mean molecular speed]}$$

### Conceptual Anchors

- **Rigid sphere** = hard, non-attracting billiard balls → $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$ only.
- **Chapman–Enskog** = first-order perturbation solution of Boltzmann equation → realistic potential → $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}/\Omega_{\mu}(T^{*})$.
- **Lennard-Jones (6-12)** = $U(r) = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6}]$ → models both repulsion and attraction.
- **$\Omega_{\mu}(T^{*})$** = dimensionless collision integral, tabulated function of $T^{*}=kT/\varepsilon$.
- **$\Omega_{\mu}=1$** for hard spheres; **$\Omega_{\mu} > 1$** for real gases (especially at low $T^{*}$ where attractions dominate).

### Typical Numerical Traps

- $T^{*} = T/(\varepsilon/k)$ — divide by $\varepsilon/k$ **in K**, not by $\varepsilon$ in J.
- $1$ Å $= 10^{-10}$ m; $1$ μP $= 10^{-7}$ poise $= 10^{-8}$ Pa·s.
- The engineering constant 2.6693 uses $M$ in g/mol, $T$ in K, $\sigma$ in Å.
- At moderate $T$ (around room $T$), $\Omega_{\mu} \approx 1$ for noble gases (He, Ar) but $\Omega_{\mu} \approx 1.5$–$2$ for polar or strongly interacting molecules.

### One-Line Exam Answer Templates

- **"Define viscosity from kinetic theory"** → "Viscosity is the proportionality constant in Newton's law $\tau_{yx} = -\mu\, du_y/dx$, arising from the net y-momentum flux carried by molecules crossing a plane due to thermal motion."
- **"Why is $\Omega_{\mu}$ needed?"** → "To correct the rigid-sphere result for the energy-dependent scattering cross-section of real molecules interacting via a soft, attractive–repulsive potential."
- **"State the LJ potential and parameters"** → "$U(r) = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12}-(\sigma/r)^{6}]$; $\sigma$ is the collision diameter (zero-crossing), $\varepsilon$ is the well-depth energy."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy transport: thermal conductivity and the mechanism of energy transport - prediction of thermal conductivity of gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy transport: thermal conductivity and the mechanism of energy transport - prediction of thermal conductivity of gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Thermal Conductivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Conductivity (k)** is the proportionality constant in **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**, which relates the heat flux (energy transferred per unit area per unit time) to the temperature gradient within a medium.

In one dimension, Fourier's law is mathematically expressed as:

$$q_y = -k \frac{dT}{dy}$$

where $q_y$ is the **heat flux** in the $y$-direction (W/m²), $k$ is the **thermal conductivity** (W/m·K), and $\frac{dT}{dy}$ is the **temperature gradient** (K/m). The negative sign indicates that heat flows spontaneously from regions of higher temperature to regions of lower temperature (i.e., down the temperature gradient).

The thermal conductivity is a **transport property** of the medium — a macroscopic manifestation of microscopic energy transport events occurring between molecules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Hot Potato Relay"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Commuter Train at Rush Hour**
> Imagine a crowded commuter train (representing a gas) where each passenger carries a "hot potato" of thermal energy. When a fast-moving molecule at the hot end collides with a slower molecule, kinetic energy is transferred — much like passengers bumping into each other and exchanging energy. The thermal conductivity describes **how efficiently** this energy "relay" occurs across the train (the gas layer). In a dense crowd (high pressure gas), the bumps are frequent → fast energy transfer → higher k. In a sparse crowd (low pressure), the bumps are rare → slower transfer.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are routinely used in transport phenomena calculations involving gases:

- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/(molecule·K)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mol
- **Standard pressure:** $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- **Standard temperature:** $T = 273.15$ K

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The thermal conductivity of gases lies typically in the range **0.01 – 0.1 W/m·K** (e.g., air ≈ 0.026 W/m·K at 25 °C), which is *two orders of magnitude lower* than that of liquids and *three orders of magnitude lower* than that of metals. This is a **favourite conceptual question** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Energy Transport in Gases

In gases, thermal energy is transported via **molecular motion and intermolecular collisions**. The mechanism can be broken down into discrete steps:

- **Step 1: Molecular Motion.** Molecules in a gas are in continuous, random thermal motion. Their translational kinetic energy is given by $\frac{1}{2} m \bar{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$.

- **Step 2: Energy Carriage.** Each molecule acts as a microscopic "carrier" of thermal energy. Faster (hotter) molecules move with greater velocities, while slower (colder) molecules move with lesser velocities.

- **Step 3: Intermolecular Collisions.** When molecules collide, they exchange momentum and energy. Hot, fast-moving molecules transfer kinetic energy to cold, slow-moving molecules.

- **Step 4: Net Energy Flux.** In the presence of a temperature gradient, there is a **net transport of energy** from hot regions to cold regions, even though individual molecules travel in random directions.

- **Step 5: Quantification via Mean Free Path.** The average distance a molecule travels between successive collisions is the **mean free path ($\lambda$)**, which sets the characteristic length scale for energy transfer.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Equations

- **Why is thermal conductivity of gases low?** Because gases are sparse media; molecules are far apart, so energy transfer is hindered by long mean free paths.
- **Why does k depend weakly on pressure?** Both $\rho$ and $\lambda$ depend on pressure, but in opposite and cancelling ways ($\rho \propto P$, $\lambda \propto 1/P$).
- **Why is k different for monatomic vs polyatomic gases?** Monatomic gases store energy only in translational modes; polyatomic gases also store energy in rotational and vibrational modes, which participate in energy transport through the Eucken correction.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are exam-critical. Memorize the formulas, their limiting cases, and the units of each variable.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Applies to** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_y = -k \dfrac{dT}{dy}$ | Fourier's Law of heat conduction | $q_y$: W/m², $k$: W/m·K | All media |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{1}{3} C_v \, \rho \, \bar{v} \, \lambda$ | Simple kinetic theory result | W/m·K | Dilute monatomic gas |
| 3 | $\bar{v} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$ | Mean molecular speed | m/s | Ideal gas |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 n} = \dfrac{RT}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 N_A P}$ | Mean free path (between collisions) | m | Hard-sphere gas |
| 5 | $C_v^{\text{mono}} = \dfrac{3}{2}\dfrac{R}{M}$ | Molar heat capacity, monatomic gas | J/kg·K | Monatomic gas |
| 6 | $C_v^{\text{di}} = \dfrac{5}{2}\dfrac{R}{M}$ | Molar heat capacity, diatomic gas | J/kg·K | Diatomic gas (moderate T) |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{\mu}{M}\!\left(C_p + \dfrac{5}{4}R\right)$ | Eucken's formula for polyatomic gas | W/m·K | Polyatomic gas |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{15}{4} \dfrac{R}{M} \mu$ | Monatomic Chapman-Enskog result | W/m·K | Monatomic gas |
| 9 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda$ | Simple kinetic theory of viscosity | Pa·s | Dilute gas |
| 10 | $Pr = \dfrac{C_p \mu}{k M}$ | Prandtl number (dimensionless) | — | All fluids |
| 11 | $Pr_{\text{mono}} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$ | Prandtl number, monatomic gas | — | Monatomic gas |
| 12 | $Pr_{\text{poly}} \approx 0.7 - 1.0$ | Prandtl number, polyatomic gas | — | Polyatomic gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** In the formulas above, the expression $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) must always be written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX when used inside markdown tables. The bare vertical bar `\|` breaks KTU's preferred markdown rendering pipeline used in the official 2024 scheme e-textbooks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal conductivity of gases is critical in designing:

- **Heat exchangers** in cryogenic air-separation plants (LNG, N₂, O₂ production)
- **Insulation systems** in buildings and aerospace (vacuum + low-k gas layers in double-glazed windows)
- **Reactor cooling** in nuclear and chemical engineering
- **Atmospheric modelling** for heat-transfer predictions in climate engineering
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Conductivity from Simple Kinetic Theory

We begin with a one-dimensional gas layer between two parallel plates at temperatures $T_1$ (hot, top) and $T_2$ (cold, bottom), separated by a small distance $L$. A temperature gradient exists along the $y$-axis. We use the following assumptions:

- The gas is dilute and ideal.
- Molecules behave as hard spheres of diameter $d$.
- Molecules are distributed uniformly on average.
- No net mass flux (purely conductive transport).

**Step 1: Net flux from a thin reference plane at y = 0**

Consider a thin reference plane at $y = 0$. Molecules crossing this plane from above have, on average, their last collision at $y = -\frac{2}{3}\lambda$ (the minus sign for "above" — closer to the hot plate). Molecules crossing from below have, on average, their last collision at $y = +\frac{2}{3}\lambda$ (closer to the cold plate).

**Step 2: Number flux per unit area (one-sixth rule)**

For an isotropic gas, only $\frac{1}{6}$ of the molecules per unit volume move in any one of the six Cartesian directions ($\pm x, \pm y, \pm z$). The number of molecules crossing unit area per unit time in either the $+y$ or $-y$ direction is:

$$N = \frac{1}{6} n \bar{v}$$

where $n$ is the number density (molecules per unit volume) and $\bar{v}$ is the mean molecular speed.

**Step 3: Energy carried per molecule**

If $\varepsilon$ is the average molecular energy (a function of $T$), then the molecules arriving from the hot side carry energy $\varepsilon(y = -\frac{2}{3}\lambda)$ and those from the cold side carry $\varepsilon(y = +\frac{2}{3}\lambda)$.

**Step 4: Net energy flux**

The net upward energy flux is:

$$q_y = \frac{1}{6} n \bar{v} \left[ \varepsilon\!\left(-\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) - \varepsilon\!\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) \right]$$

**Step 5: Taylor expansion and simplification**

Expanding $\varepsilon$ to first order in $y$ around $y = 0$:

$$\varepsilon\!\left(-\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) - \varepsilon\!\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) = -\frac{4}{3}\lambda \frac{d\varepsilon}{dy}$$

Substituting:

$$q_y = \frac{1}{6} n \bar{v} \left( -\frac{4}{3}\lambda \frac{d\varepsilon}{dy} \right) = -\frac{2}{9} n \bar{v} \lambda \frac{d\varepsilon}{dy}$$

**Step 6: Convert molecular energy to temperature**

Since $n \frac{d\varepsilon}{dT} = \rho C_v$ (where $\rho$ is mass density and $C_v$ is specific heat capacity at constant volume, per unit mass), we have $n \frac{d\varepsilon}{dy} = \rho C_v \frac{dT}{dy}$. Substituting:

$$q_y = -\frac{2}{9} n \bar{v} \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{n} \rho C_v \frac{dT}{dy} = -\frac{2}{9} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda \frac{dT}{dy}$$

**Step 7: Compare with Fourier's Law**

Comparing with $q_y = -k \frac{dT}{dy}$:

$$k = \frac{2}{9} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda$$

The standard textbook form (BSL) is the simpler $\frac{1}{3}$ coefficient; the $\frac{2}{9}$ arises from the more rigorous collision-frequency weighting. Both forms are accepted in KTU evaluations; the $\frac{1}{3}$ form is the **conventional** one to write.

$$\boxed{k = \frac{1}{3} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Eucken's Correction for Polyatomic Gases

For polyatomic gases, simple kinetic theory underpredicts k by ~30% because it neglects **internal energy modes** (rotation, vibration) that also transport energy during collisions. **Eucken (1913)** introduced an empirical correction:

**Step 1: Identify the two energy-transport mechanisms**

- **Translational energy transport** (kinetic theory form): $k_{\text{trans}} = \mu C_v^{\text{trans}} / M$ where $C_v^{\text{trans}} = \frac{3}{2} R/M$
- **Internal energy transport** (diffusion of internal modes): $k_{\text{int}} = \mu C_v^{\text{int}} / M$ where $C_v^{\text{int}} = C_v - C_v^{\text{trans}}$

**Step 2: Apply the modified expression**

The total thermal conductivity is weighted sum:

$$k = \mu \left( C_v^{\text{trans}} f_{\text{trans}} + C_v^{\text{int}} f_{\text{int}} \right) / M$$

where $f_{\text{trans}} = \frac{5}{2}$ (Eucken factor for translation) and $f_{\text{int}} = 1$ (diffusion-like for internal modes).

**Step 3: Simplify**

$$k = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left( \frac{5}{2} C_v^{\text{trans}} + C_v^{\text{int}} \right) = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left( \frac{5}{2} \cdot \frac{3R}{2M} + C_v - \frac{3R}{2M} \right)$$

After algebra (and using $C_p = C_v + R$):

$$\boxed{k = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left( C_p + \frac{5}{4}R \right)}$$

**Step 4: Special case — monatomic gas**

For monatomic gas: $C_p = \frac{5}{2}R$, so:

$$k = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left( \frac{5R}{2} + \frac{5R}{4} \right) = \frac{15R}{4M} \mu$$

This matches the **Chapman–Enskog exact result** for monatomic gases.

### 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Independence of k for Dilute Gases

We start from the kinetic-theory result $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda$ and substitute the ideal-gas relations:

$$\rho = \frac{MP}{RT}, \quad \bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}, \quad \lambda = \frac{RT}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 N_A P}$$

**Step-by-step combination:**

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{MP}{RT} \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{RT}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 N_A P}$$

**Step 1: Cancel P (numerator and denominator)**

$$\frac{MP \cdot RT}{RT \cdot P} = M$$

Wait, careful — let's group term by term:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{MP}{RT} \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{RT}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 N_A P}$$

**Step 2: Combine the P-terms**

The first factor contributes $P$ in numerator, last contributes $P$ in denominator. So **P cancels entirely**.

**Step 3: Combine the T-terms**

The factor $\frac{P}{RT}$ has $1/T$; $\bar{v}$ has $\sqrt{T}$; $\lambda$ has $T$. So overall $T \cdot \sqrt{T} / T = \sqrt{T}$.

**Step 4: Combine the M-terms**

$\frac{MP}{RT}$ contributes $M$; $\bar{v}$ contributes $1/\sqrt{M}$. So overall $M \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} = \sqrt{M}$.

**Final form (independent of P, dependent on $\sqrt{T/M}$):**

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{M} \cdot \sqrt{T} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi M}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 N_A}$$

$$k = \frac{1}{3} C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8MT}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 N_A} = \frac{2 C_v}{3\pi d^2 N_A} \sqrt{\frac{MT}{\pi}}$$

$$\boxed{k \propto \sqrt{T} \quad \text{(and independent of P)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The result $k \propto \sqrt{T}$ holds only for monatomic gases.** For polyatomic gases, the T-dependence is stronger (typically $k \propto T^{0.7}$ to $T^{0.9}$) because vibrational modes are progressively activated at higher T. The pressure-independence, however, is universal for **dilute gases**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: k vs. μ

| **Property** | **Viscosity ($\mu$)** | **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Governing law | Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Fourier's law of conduction: $q = -k \frac{dT}{dy}$ |
| Kinetic-theory form | $\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda$ | $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda$ |
| Pressure dependence | Independent (dilute gas) | Independent (dilute gas) |
| Temperature dependence | $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$ | $k \propto \sqrt{T}$ (mono) or $T^{0.7-0.9}$ (poly) |
| Ratio $k / (\mu C_v)$ | — | $1$ (simple KT), $1 + \frac{9R}{5 C_v M}$ (Eucken) |
| Typical magnitude (air, 300 K) | $\mu \approx 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s | $k \approx 0.026$ W/m·K |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mechanism of Energy Transport in a Gas

The following Mermaid block illustrates the *flow* of energy transport in a dilute gas, viewed as a sequence of microscopic-to-macroscopic events.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot plate at T1] -->|Molecular emission| B[Fast hot molecules]
    B -->|Random walk with mean free path lambda| C[Mid-plane y=0]
    A2[Cold plate at T2] -->|Molecular emission| D[Slow cold molecules]
    D -->|Random walk with mean free path lambda| C
    C -->|Net energy flux qy| E[Temperature gradient dT dy]
    E -->|Fourier law| F[Thermal conductivity k]
    F -->|Combined with mu and Cp| G[Eucken correction]
    G -->|Final design equation| H[Heat exchanger design]
    C -->|Microscopic event| I[Collision transfers momentum and energy]
    I -->|Conservation laws| J[Net transfer of thermal energy]
    J -->|Macroscopic limit| F
    style A fill:#ffd166
    style A2 fill:#118ab2
    style F fill:#06d6a0
    style G fill:#ef476f
    style H fill:#073b4c
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The k–μ–Pr Connection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Mean molecular speed v_bar] --> A2[Kinetic theory of gases]
    A3[Mean free path lambda] --> A2
    A4[Density rho] --> A2
    A5[Specific heat Cv] --> A2
    A2 --> B1[Thermal conductivity k]
    A2 --> B2[Viscosity mu]
    B1 --> C1[Prandtl number Pr = Cp mu divided by k M]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Heat transfer correlations]
    C1 --> D2[Reactor design]
    C1 --> D3[Compressible flow analysis]
    style A2 fill:#ef476f
    style C1 fill:#06d6a0
    style D1 fill:#118ab2
    style D2 fill:#118ab2
    style D3 fill:#118ab2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Regimes of Gas Thermal Conductivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Total pressure P] --> R1{P is low vacuum}
    P --> R2{P is moderate}
    P --> R3{P is high vacuum}
    R1 --> S1[lambda large, k decreases with P]
    R2 --> S2[Pressure independent, k proportional to sqrt T]
    R3 --> S3[Mean free path greater than apparatus, k decreases with P]
    T[Temperature T] --> R2
    T --> S4[For polyatomic gas, vibrational modes activated at high T]
    R2 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Stronger T dependence, exponent 0.7 to 0.9]
    style R1 fill:#ffd166
    style R2 fill:#06d6a0
    style R3 fill:#ef476f
    style S5 fill:#118ab2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guidance:** Each node label uses raw uppercase alphanumeric text only. No bold, italic, or HTML markup is embedded inside the `"..."` strings — this is enforced by the Mermaid compilation safeguard rules of the KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define thermal conductivity and state Fourier's law of heat conduction. What are the SI units of thermal conductivity?

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal Conductivity** is the property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat. It is the proportionality constant in Fourier's law.

**Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction:**

$$q_y = -k \frac{dT}{dy}$$

where $q_y$ is the heat flux (W/m²) in the $y$-direction, $k$ is the thermal conductivity, and $dT/dy$ is the temperature gradient (K/m). The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the direction of decreasing temperature.

**SI Units of $k$:** W/(m·K) or equivalently J/(m·s·K).

**(3 marks — Definition: 1 mark, Fourier's law: 1 mark, Units: 1 mark.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **three** mechanisms of energy transport in matter and identify which one is dominant in gases.

**Model Answer:**

The three mechanisms of energy transport are:

1. **Conduction** — Energy transfer through molecular interactions and collisions (dominant in solids, liquids, and gases).
2. **Convection** — Energy transfer by bulk motion of a fluid (dominant in flowing fluids).
3. **Radiation** — Energy transfer by electromagnetic waves (significant at high temperatures; occurs in vacuum too).

**In gases, conduction is the dominant mechanism** because gas molecules are in continuous thermal motion and energy is carried across the fluid by molecular collisions.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for each mechanism, no partial credit for naming only two.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Starting from the kinetic theory of gases, derive an expression for the thermal conductivity of a monatomic ideal gas. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the Eucken correlation, calculate the thermal conductivity of air at 320 K and 1 atm, given:
> - Viscosity of air at 320 K: $\mu = 1.92 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
> - Molar mass of air: $M = 28.97 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - For air, $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol·K)
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**

- The gas is dilute and behaves as an ideal gas.
- Molecules are rigid, non-attracting hard spheres of diameter $d$.
- Only translational energy is transported (monatomic gas).
- No net mass flux; the system is in steady state.
- Molecular chaos and isotropic velocity distribution.

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.1):**

The number of molecules per unit volume is $n = N/V$. The number of molecules crossing unit area per unit time in any one direction is $\frac{1}{6} n \bar{v}$. Consider a reference plane at $y = 0$. Molecules arriving from above had their last collision at $y = -\frac{2}{3}\lambda$ on average; those from below at $y = +\frac{2}{3}\lambda$.

The net upward energy flux is:

$$q_y = \frac{1}{6} n \bar{v} \left[ \varepsilon\!\left(-\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) - \varepsilon\!\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) \right]$$

Using Taylor expansion:

$$\varepsilon\!\left(-\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) - \varepsilon\!\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda\right) \approx -\frac{4}{3}\lambda \frac{d\varepsilon}{dy}$$

Substituting and using $n \frac{d\varepsilon}{dT} = \rho C_v$:

$$q_y = -\frac{2}{9} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda \frac{dT}{dy}$$

Comparing with Fourier's law:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{2}{9} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda \quad \text{(rigorous form)}}$$

The conventional form, using the standard $\frac{1}{3}$ coefficient (acceptable in KTU exams):

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda$$

**Mark allocation:** Assumptions: 2 marks, Setup of number flux: 2 marks, Taylor expansion: 1 mark, Final expression: 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**

- $\mu = 1.92 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $M = 28.97 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol·K)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Eucken's Formula:**

$$k = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left(C_p + \frac{5}{4}R\right)$$

**Step 1: Compute $C_p + \frac{5}{4}R$**

$$C_p + \frac{5}{4}R = 29.1 + \frac{5}{4}(8.314) = 29.1 + 10.3925 = 39.4925 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\mu/M$**

$$\frac{\mu}{M} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^{-5}}{28.97 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.627 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s·mol/kg}$$

Converting units carefully: $\frac{\text{Pa·s}}{\text{kg/mol}} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s}^2\text{)·s}}{\text{kg/mol}} = \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{m·s}}$

So $\frac{\mu}{M} = 6.627 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m·s).

**Step 3: Multiply**

$$k = (6.627 \times 10^{-4}) \times (39.4925) = 0.02617 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 0.0262 \text{ W/(m·K)}}$$

**Comparison with literature:** Experimental value of k for air at 320 K is ≈ 0.0274 W/(m·K). Eucken's prediction is within ~5% — excellent agreement.

**Mark allocation:** Formula statement: 2 marks, Numerical substitution: 3 marks, Final answer with units: 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the physical mechanism of energy transport in gases. How does the **Eucken correction** modify the simple kinetic theory result for polyatomic gases? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal gas layer of thickness 5 cm is held between a hot plate at 400 K and a cold plate at 300 K. The thermal conductivity of the gas is 0.030 W/(m·K) and is assumed constant. Calculate:
> - (i) The heat flux through the gas layer. **(3 marks)**
> - (ii) The temperature at the midplane of the gas layer. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Physical Mechanism of Energy Transport in Gases:**

In a gas, thermal energy is transported by the **random thermal motion of molecules and the intermolecular collisions** that occur between them. The mechanism proceeds as follows:

1. **Thermal agitation:** All gas molecules possess kinetic energy proportional to $T$ ($\frac{1}{2} m \bar{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$). Molecules in hotter regions are, on average, faster.
2. **Molecular migration:** Hot molecules migrate from hot regions toward cold regions; cold molecules migrate oppositely.
3. **Energy exchange at collisions:** When molecules collide, they exchange kinetic energy. Hot molecules lose energy; cold molecules gain energy.
4. **Net flux:** Despite the random, symmetric nature of molecular motion, the **temperature gradient breaks the symmetry** in the energy distribution of molecules crossing any reference plane. There is a net flux of energy from hot to cold.

**Eucken Correction:**

The simple kinetic theory result $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda$ accounts only for the transport of **translational** energy. In **polyatomic gases**, energy is also stored in **rotational and vibrational** modes, and these modes are transported by a different mechanism — akin to the diffusion of internal energy.

Eucken (1913) proposed a **weighted sum**:

$$k = k_{\text{trans}} + k_{\text{int}} = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left( \frac{5}{2} C_v^{\text{trans}} + C_v^{\text{int}} \right)$$

After algebraic simplification (using $C_v^{\text{int}} = C_v - C_v^{\text{trans}}$ and $C_p = C_v + R$):

$$k = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left( C_p + \frac{5}{4}R \right)$$

This reduces to $k = \frac{15R}{4M}\mu$ for monatomic gases (matches Chapman–Enskog) and gives ~30% higher $k$ for polyatomic gases.

**Mark allocation:** Mechanism: 4 marks (1 mark per step), Eucken derivation: 3 marks.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**

- Plate separation: $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
- Hot plate: $T_1 = 400$ K
- Cold plate: $T_2 = 300$ K
- Thermal conductivity: $k = 0.030$ W/(m·K) (constant)

**Part (i): Heat Flux**

Fourier's law (one-dimensional, steady state, constant $k$):

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dy} = -k \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L} = k \cdot \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}$$

$$q = 0.030 \times \frac{400 - 300}{0.05} = 0.030 \times 2000 = 60 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q = 60 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**Part (ii): Midplane Temperature**

For constant $k$ and steady state, the temperature profile is **linear** in $y$:

$$T(y) = T_1 + \left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}\right) y$$

At midplane, $y = L/2 = 0.025$ m:

$$T_{\text{mid}} = 400 + \left(\frac{300 - 400}{0.05}\right) (0.025) = 400 + (-2000)(0.025) = 400 - 50 = 350 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{mid}} = 350 \text{ K}}$$

**Mark allocation:** Part (i): 3 marks, Part (ii): 4 marks (linear profile: 2 marks, substitution: 1 mark, final answer: 1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Fourier's law:** Students often write $q = +k \frac{dT}{dy}$ (missing the negative sign). This loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit confusion in $\mu/M$:** Forgetting that the denominator must be in kg/mol (not g/mol). 1 g/mol = $10^{-3}$ kg/mol — a common slip.
> 3. **Wrong Eucken formula:** Some students write $k = \mu C_v / M$ (the simple KT form) even for polyatomic gases. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Assuming $k$ varies with T:** The problem states $k$ is constant. Students who use a $T^{1/2}$ dependence in part (b) of Question B lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Eucken and Chapman–Enskog:** Eucken gives $\frac{15R}{4M}\mu$ for monatomic gases *as a special case*; do not present it as the general formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points Only**

- **Fourier's law** is to heat conduction what Newton's law is to momentum transfer. Always include the **negative sign**.
- **Thermal conductivity** in gases arises from **translational + internal energy transport** by molecular collisions.
- **Simple kinetic theory:** $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho C_v \bar{v} \lambda$ (monatomic, dilute).
- **Eucken's formula (general):** $k = \frac{\mu}{M}\!\left(C_p + \frac{5}{4}R\right)$ (use for polyatomic gases).
- **Monatomic special case:** $k = \frac{15R}{4M}\mu$.
- **Pressure independence of $k$** for dilute gases: $\rho \uparrow$ and $\lambda \downarrow$ cancel as $P$ varies. **Fails at very low pressure** (Knudsen regime) and at very high pressure (real-gas effects).
- **Temperature dependence:** $k \propto \sqrt{T}$ for monatomic; $k \propto T^{0.7\text{–}0.9}$ for polyatomic (stronger due to vibrational mode activation).
- **Prandtl number** for monatomic gases is $2/3 \approx 0.667$; for polyatomic gases $0.7$ – $1.0$. Air: $Pr \approx 0.71$.
- **Order of magnitude:** $k_{\text{gas}} \sim 0.01$–$0.1$ W/m·K (much lower than liquids and solids).
- **Mean free path** scales as $\lambda = \frac{RT}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 N_A P}$ — the inverse dependence on $P$ is the *key* to the pressure-independence argument.
- **Numerical constants to remember:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol, $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- **KTU favourite comparison questions:** Always expect a "compare viscosity and thermal conductivity in gases" question — common features (pressure independence, $\sqrt{T}$ scaling) and differences ($k$ has internal-mode correction, $k$ involves $C_v$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of temperature and pressure on thermal conductivity of gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of temperature and pressure on thermal conductivity of gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Thermal conductivity of a gas (k)** is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient under steady-state, one-dimensional conductive conditions. It is the transport property that quantifies a gas's intrinsic ability to conduct thermal energy through molecular motion and intermolecular energy exchange.

$$q = -k \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where:
- $q$ = heat flux (**W/m²**)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (**W/m·K**)
- $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (**K/m**)

From the rigorous **kinetic theory of monatomic gases**, thermal conductivity is given by:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \, \rho \, \bar{v} \, \lambda \, C_{v}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = gas density (**kg/m³**)
- $\bar{v}$ = mean molecular speed (**m/s**)
- $\lambda$ = mean free path (**m**)
- $C_{v}$ = specific heat capacity at constant volume (**J/kg·K**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike liquids and solids, gases exhibit a unique behavior — their thermal conductivity is **largely independent of pressure** over a wide range. This counter-intuitive result emerges directly from kinetic theory and is a frequently tested concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded market lane where people (gas molecules) are constantly colliding and exchanging shopping bags (energy). The thermal conductivity depends on:
- **How fast** they move (temperature effect)
- **How heavy** they are (molecular weight)
- **How often** they bump into each other (which depends on both density and their size)

Now, if you **squeeze the lane narrower (increase pressure)** — there are more people, BUT they also travel a **shorter distance** between bumps. These two effects perfectly cancel out! Hence, pressure has **little effect** on thermal conductivity of gases at moderate conditions.

However, if you **heat the lane (increase temperature)** — molecules move **much faster**, and even though collisions are more frequent, the energy carried per collision is far greater. So thermal conductivity **increases with temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** *For ideal gases → k is a function of T only, NOT pressure.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Universal Gas Constant:** R = 8.314 J/mol·K
- **Typical k for air at 300 K:** ≈ 0.0262 W/m·K
- **Typical k for hydrogen at 300 K:** ≈ 0.182 W/m·K (lightest molecule → highest k)
- **Typical k for monatomic gases:** Lower than polyatomic gases due to lower $C_v$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic from Kinetic Theory

Starting from the kinetic theory expression:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \, \rho \, \bar{v} \, \lambda \, C_{v}$$

We can express each variable in terms of fundamental gas properties:

### Step 1: Mean Molecular Speed
$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

### Step 2: Mean Free Path
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, \sigma^{2} \, n} = \frac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, \sigma^{2} \, P}$$

### Step 3: Number Density
$$n = \frac{N}{V} = \frac{P}{k_{B} T}$$

### Step 4: Density Relation
$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$$

### Step 5: Substituting All Together
$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{PM}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, \sigma^{2} \, P} \cdot C_{v}$$

The **pressure P cancels out** from the numerator and denominator, which is the key insight that explains why k is independent of pressure at moderate conditions.

## 2.2 Effect of Temperature on Thermal Conductivity

After the P-cancellation, the remaining temperature dependence yields:

$$k \propto T^{1/2}$$

For real gases, experimental data shows:

$$k = k_{0} \left(\frac{T}{T_{0}}\right)^{n}$$

where $n$ typically lies between **0.5 and 1.0** for most gases:
- Monatomic gases: $n \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$
- Polyatomic gases: $n \approx 0.8$ to $1.0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Always write $k \propto T^{n}$ (not exactly $T^{1/2}$) for real gases because the simple kinetic theory uses a rigid-sphere model which is an approximation.

## 2.3 Effect of Pressure on Thermal Conductivity

### Region 1: Low to Moderate Pressure
$$k = \text{constant (independent of P)}$$

The increase in number density is exactly compensated by the decrease in mean free path.

### Region 2: High Pressure (Near Critical Point)
$$k \text{ increases with P}$$

Gas behavior deviates from ideality; molecules cluster and energy transfer is enhanced.

### Region 3: Very Low Pressure (Knudsen Regime)
$$k \text{ decreases with P}$$

When $\lambda$ becomes comparable to system dimensions, molecules travel from hot wall to cold wall without collisions (ballistic transport). Here:

$$k \propto P$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v}$ | Fundamental kinetic theory expression |
| 2 | $k = \frac{5}{2} \mu C_{v}$ | For **monatomic** ideal gases (Eucken) |
| 3 | $k = \mu \left( C_{v} + \frac{9R}{4M} \right)$ | **Eucken's correlation** for polyatomic gases |
| 4 | $\frac{k}{\mu C_{v}} = 1 + \frac{9}{4} \left( \frac{\gamma - 1}{\text{constant}} \right)$ | Eucken form using $\gamma$ |
| 5 | $\frac{k M}{\mu C_{v}} = \frac{9\gamma - 5}{4}$ | Compact Eucken form (molar basis) |
| 6 | $k = k_{0} \left( T/T_{0} \right)^{n}$ | Temperature dependence (n ≈ 0.5–1.0) |
| 7 | $k = \text{const. (P-independent)}$ | Moderate pressure region |
| 8 | $k \propto P$ | Low pressure / Knudsen regime |
| 9 | $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ | Maxwell mean speed |
| 10 | $\lambda = k_{B} T / (\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} P)$ | Mean free path |
| 11 | $Pr = \frac{\mu C_{p}}{k}$ | Prandtl number linkage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Trap:** Always carry units — $k$ in W/m·K, $\mu$ in Pa·s, $C_v$ in J/kg·K, $M$ in kg/mol.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat exchanger design** for cryogenic gas processing
- **Re-entry vehicle thermal protection** (high-T, low-P boundary layers)
- **Insulation of vacuum-jacketed pipelines** (Knudsen regime exploitation)
- **Atmospheric heat transfer** modeling
- **Combustion chamber cooling** calculations in jet engines
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure-Independence of k for Ideal Gases

Starting from the rigorous kinetic theory result:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v}$$

Substituting $\rho = PM / (RT)$:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{PM}{RT} \cdot \bar{v} \cdot \lambda \cdot C_{v}$$

Substituting $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8RT / (\pi M)}$:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{PM}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \lambda \cdot C_{v}$$

Simplifying the mass term $M$ under the square root:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{P}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT \cdot M}{\pi}} \cdot \lambda \cdot C_{v}$$

Now substituting $\lambda = k_{B} T / (\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} P)$:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{P}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT \cdot M}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} P} \cdot C_{v}$$

The $P$ in the numerator and denominator cancel:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT \cdot M}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2}} \cdot C_{v}$$

The $T$ under the square root combines with the explicit $T$ in the numerator:

$$k = \frac{k_{B} \, C_{v}}{3 \sqrt{2} \, \pi^{3/2} \, R \, \sigma^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 M}{\pi}} \cdot T^{1/2}$$

Therefore, the final expression is:

$$\boxed{\,k = \underbrace{\left( \frac{k_{B} \, C_{v} \sqrt{8 M}}{3 \sqrt{2} \, \pi^{2} \, R \, \sigma^{2}} \right)}_{\text{constant for a given gas}} \cdot T^{1/2}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** $k$ depends only on $T^{1/2}$ and **not on P**, validating the kinetic theory prediction.

## 3.2 Derivation: Eucken's Correlation for Polyatomic Gases

The simple monatomic formula $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v}$ underpredicts $k$ for polyatomic gases because it ignores the **translational-rotational energy transfer** separately from translational.

For a **monatomic gas**, $C_{v} = \frac{3}{2} R/M$, and the thermal conductivity reduces to:

$$k = \frac{5}{2} \mu C_{v} = \frac{15}{4} \cdot \frac{\mu R}{M}$$

For **polyatomic gases**, Eucken proposed that translational and internal energy modes transport heat with different effective mean free paths:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda_{tr} \left( C_{v,tr} + \frac{\rho D_{11} C_{v,int}}{\lambda_{int}/\lambda_{tr}} \right)$$

Using the Chapman-Enskog theory, the final result (Eucken's empirical formula) is:

$$k = \mu \left( C_{v} + \frac{9R}{4M} \right)$$

Using the relation $C_{v} = R/[M(\gamma - 1)]$:

$$\frac{R}{M C_{v}} = \gamma - 1$$

Substituting this back:

$$k = \mu C_{v} \left[ 1 + \frac{9}{4} (\gamma - 1) \right] = \frac{\mu C_{v} (9\gamma - 5)}{4}$$

Or in molar form:

$$\boxed{\,k = \frac{\mu C_{v,m}}{4} (9\gamma - 5)\,}$$

where $C_{v,m}$ is the **molar** heat capacity.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem:** The thermal conductivity of nitrogen at 300 K is **0.026 W/m·K**. Estimate the conductivity at 600 K, assuming $n = 0.8$.

**Solution:**

$$k_{2} = k_{1} \left( \frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} \right)^{n} = 0.026 \times \left( \frac{600}{300} \right)^{0.8}$$

$$k_{2} = 0.026 \times (2)^{0.8}$$

$$k_{2} = 0.026 \times 1.741 = 0.0453 \text{ W/m·K}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Regime | Pressure Behavior | Physical Reason | Engineering Application |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| **Low P (Vacuum)** | $k \propto P$ | Ballistic flow, few collisions | Vacuum insulation |
| **Moderate P** | $k = $ const. | Density ↑, mean free path ↓ (cancel) | Heat exchangers, pipelines |
| **High P (Near $P_c$)** | $k \uparrow$ with P | Non-ideal clustering | Supercritical CO₂ systems |
| **Temperature ↑** | $k \uparrow$ always | Higher molecular speed | Combustion, furnaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** "Does thermal conductivity of an ideal gas depend on pressure?" → Answer: **NO**, it depends only on temperature (within kinetic theory limits).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Factors Affecting Thermal Conductivity of Gases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Conductivity of Gas] --> B[State Variables]
    A --> C[Molecular Properties]
    A --> D[Pressure Regime]
    B --> B1[Temperature T]
    B --> B2[Pressure P]
    C --> C1[Molecular Weight M]
    C --> C2[Collision Diameter sigma]
    C --> C3[Heat Capacity Cv]
    D --> D1[Low P Knudsen]
    D --> D2[Moderate P Ideal]
    D --> D3[High P Real Gas]
    B1 --> E1[k proportional to T power n]
    B2 --> E2[k independent of P in ideal range]
    C1 --> E3[Lighter gas higher k]
    C2 --> E4[Smaller sigma higher k]
    C3 --> E5[Polyatomic higher k]
    D1 --> F1[k proportional to P ballistic]
    D2 --> F2[k approximately constant]
    D3 --> F3[k increases with P non ideal]
    E1 --> OUT[Final k value]
    E2 --> OUT
    E3 --> OUT
    E4 --> OUT
    E5 --> OUT
    F1 --> OUT
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Eucken Correlation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Gas Type] --> MONO[Monatomic]
    START --> POLY[Polyatomic]
    MONO --> M1[Cv equals 3R over 2M]
    M1 --> M2[k equals 5 by 2 mu Cv]
    POLY --> P1[Internal energy modes exist]
    P1 --> P2[Apply Eucken Correction]
    P2 --> P3[k equals mu times Cv plus 9R over 4M]
    P3 --> P4[Equivalent to mu Cv times 1 plus 9 by 4 gamma minus 1]
    M2 --> FINAL[Thermal Conductivity]
    P4 --> FINAL
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Three Pressure Regimes of Gaseous Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REG[Pressure Regimes for Gas Thermal Conductivity] --> R1[Region 1: Low Pressure]
    REG --> R2[Region 2: Moderate Pressure]
    REG --> R3[Region 3: High Pressure]
    R1 --> R1A[Mean free path much greater than system size]
    R1 --> R1B[Ballistic molecular transport]
    R1 --> R1C[k proportional to P]
    R2 --> R2A[Mean free path much less than system size]
    R2 --> R2B[Continuum regime with collisions]
    R2 --> R2C[k independent of P]
    R3 --> R3A[Near critical point non ideal behavior]
    R3 --> R3B[Molecular clustering effects]
    R3 --> R3C[k increases with P]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Mechanism of Energy Transfer in Gases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HW[Hot Wall] --> M1[Molecule A high KE]
    M1 --> M2[Molecule B intermediate KE]
    M2 --> M3[Molecule C low KE]
    M3 --> CW[Cold Wall]
    M1 --> E1[Energy transferred via collision]
    M2 --> E1
    M3 --> E1
    E1 --> R[Net Result: Heat flows from hot to cold]
    HW --> R
    CW --> R
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State whether the thermal conductivity of an ideal gas depends on pressure. Justify using kinetic theory."**

**Model Answer:**
The thermal conductivity of an ideal gas is **independent of pressure** at moderate conditions. According to kinetic theory:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v}$$

When expressed in terms of $P$ and $T$, the pressure terms cancel out, leaving $k$ as a function of $T$ only. This is because as $P$ increases, the number density $n$ increases (which tends to increase $k$), but the mean free path $\lambda$ decreases proportionally (which tends to decrease $k$). These two effects exactly cancel each other.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Write Eucken's correlation for the thermal conductivity of polyatomic gases."**

**Model Answer:**
Eucken's correlation is:

$$k = \mu \left( C_{v} + \frac{9R}{4M} \right)$$

In terms of the specific heat ratio $\gamma$:

$$\frac{k}{\mu C_{v}} = 1 + \frac{9}{4}(\gamma - 1)$$

This accounts for the additional energy transfer through internal (rotational and vibrational) modes of polyatomic molecules, which is not captured by the simple monatomic kinetic theory formula.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Derive an expression for the thermal conductivity of an ideal gas using the kinetic theory of gases. Show clearly how pressure independence arises. Discuss the effect of temperature on k.**

#### (a) Derivation of k (7 Marks)

Starting from the fundamental kinetic theory result for a monatomic gas:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v}$$

Substituting the Maxwell mean speed:

$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

And the mean free path:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} n} = \frac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} P}$$

And the gas density:

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$$

Plugging in:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{PM}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} P} \cdot C_{v}$$

The $P$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$k = \frac{k_{B} C_{v}}{3 \sqrt{2} \pi^{2} \sigma^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8M}{\pi R}} \cdot T^{1/2}$$

#### (b) Effect of Temperature (7 Marks)

The derived expression shows that:

$$k \propto T^{1/2}$$

For real gases, the experimentally observed temperature dependence is:

$$k = k_{0} \left( \frac{T}{T_{0}} \right)^{n}$$

where $n$ typically lies between **0.5 and 1.0**.

The reason k increases with temperature is:
1. **Higher mean molecular speed** $\bar{v}$ → faster energy transport
2. **Greater internal energy per molecule** at higher T → more energy carried per collision
3. **Increased frequency of molecular collisions** with higher kinetic energy

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse $k \propto T^{1/2}$ (kinetic theory prediction for rigid spheres) with the more general empirical form $k \propto T^{n}$ where $n = 0.5$–$1.0$ for real gases. Always state the empirical form for full marks in KTU exams. Also, **never write** "k increases with T because mean free path increases" — $\lambda$ actually **decreases** slightly with T for a fixed density scenario, but the velocity effect dominates.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Discuss the Eucken's correction for polyatomic gases. Compare the thermal conductivities of monatomic and polyatomic gases. Comment on the pressure dependence of k in different regimes.**

#### (a) Eucken's Correction and Comparison (7 Marks)

For **monatomic gases**, the kinetic theory gives:

$$k_{mono} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v,tr} = \frac{5}{2} \mu C_{v} = \frac{15}{4} \cdot \frac{\mu R}{M}$$

with $C_{v} = \frac{3R}{2M}$ (no internal modes).

For **polyatomic gases**, Eucken recognized that translational and internal (rotational + vibrational) energies are transported by molecules differently. The modified expression is:

$$k_{poly} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda \left[ C_{v,tr} + \left( \frac{\rho D_{11}}{\mu} \right) C_{v,int} \right]$$

For real polyatomic gases, this reduces to the empirical Eucken formula:

$$k = \mu \left( C_{v} + \frac{9R}{4M} \right) = \mu C_{v} \left[ 1 + \frac{9}{4}(\gamma - 1) \right]$$

Polyatomic gases have **higher k** than monatomic gases of comparable molecular weight because of additional energy transport via internal degrees of freedom.

#### (b) Pressure Regimes (7 Marks)

| Regime | Behavior | Reason |
|--------|----------|--------|
| **Low P** (Knudsen) | $k \propto P$ | Molecules travel ballistically; few collisions |
| **Moderate P** (Ideal) | $k = $ const. | Density × mean free path = constant |
| **High P** (Real) | $k \uparrow$ with P | Non-ideal clustering, enhanced energy transfer |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often incorrectly state that "k is independent of pressure for ALL gases at ALL pressures." The correct statement is: **k is independent of pressure only in the ideal gas regime at moderate pressures.** At very low pressures (Knudsen regime) and at very high pressures (near critical point), pressure dependence becomes significant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Core Equation:** $k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{v} \lambda C_{v}$ — the foundational kinetic theory expression
- [x] **Pressure Independence:** Proven by cancellation of $P$ in numerator and denominator
- [x] **Temperature Dependence:** $k \propto T^{n}$ where $n = 0.5$–$1.0$ (use $0.5$ for monatomic in rigid-sphere model)
- [x] **Eucken's Formula:** $k = \mu (C_{v} + 9R/4M)$ for polyatomic gases
- [x] **Monatomic Formula:** $k = \frac{5}{2} \mu C_{v} = \frac{15}{4} (\mu R/M)$
- [x] **Lighter gases** (H₂, He) have **higher k** due to higher mean speed $\bar{v}$
- [x] **Three pressure regimes:** Low (k∝P), Moderate (k=const.), High (k↑)
- [x] **Mean free path expression:** $\lambda = k_{B} T / (\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} P)$
- [x] **Mean molecular speed:** $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$
- [x] **Linkage to viscosity:** Both $k$ and $\mu$ arise from the same molecular transport mechanism (momentum and energy transfer by molecular motion)
- [x] **Knudsen number** $Kn = \lambda / L$ determines the regime (low P when $Kn > 0.1$)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship between thermal conductivity and viscosity of gases. Thermal conductivity of solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationship between thermal conductivity and viscosity of gases. Thermal conductivity of solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermal Conductivity of Gases — Formal Definition

**Thermal conductivity (k)** of a gas is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient, governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**:

$$q_y = -k \frac{dT}{dy}$$

For gases, the mechanism of thermal conduction is the **transport of kinetic energy of molecules** from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature through random molecular motion and collisions. The fundamental driving force is the **temperature gradient** that creates a non-uniform distribution of molecular speeds across the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The relationship between thermal conductivity (k) and viscosity (μ) is one of the **highest-weightage derivations** in Transport Phenomena. Examiners frequently test the *Eucken-type correlation* and the *Prandtl number* for gases.

The intrinsic microscopic transport of momentum, energy, and mass in a gas is governed by the **kinetic theory of gases**, which assumes:
1. Molecules are rigid, non-attracting spheres of diameter *d*.
2. The gas is sufficiently dilute that only binary collisions occur.
3. Between collisions, molecules travel in straight lines with mean free path *λ*.

## 1.2 Thermal Conductivity of Solids — Formal Definition

Thermal conductivity of solids describes the **transport of thermal energy through a solid medium** via two principal carriers:
- **Lattice vibrations (phonons)** — dominant in **dielectric (non-metallic) solids** such as ceramics, polymers, and insulators.
- **Free electrons** — dominant in **metallic solids** (Wiedemann–Franz mechanism).

The mathematical definition remains identical to Fourier's Law:

$$q = -k_{solid} \nabla T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Boltzmann constant: **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/(mol·K)**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Avogadro's number: **N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol**

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Transport Phenomena as a "City Traffic System"

Imagine a busy two-lane highway:
- **Viscosity (μ)** is like **friction between cars moving in adjacent lanes** — fast cars dragging along slower ones (momentum transfer perpendicular to flow).
- **Thermal conductivity (k)** is like **passengers carrying hot coffee** — energy hopping between cars (energy transfer perpendicular to temperature gradient).
- **Diffusivity (D_AB)** is like **cars changing lanes** — mass moving from a crowded lane to an empty lane.

The **same molecules** are responsible for all three transports. Therefore, **k, μ, and D_AB are not independent** — they are linked by the molecular motion that drives them all. This is the heart of Module 1 of Transport Phenomena.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Takeaway:** A gas that "carries momentum well" (high μ) also "carries energy well" (high k), because both quantities are transported by the *same* molecules undergoing the *same* random collisions. This empirical observation is what we will quantify mathematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microscopic Origins of Transport in Gases

From the kinetic theory of dilute monatomic gases, every transport property has the form:

$$\text{Transport Property} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \cdot f(\text{carrier quantity})$$

where:
- **ρ** = density of the gas (kg/m³)
- **ū** = mean molecular speed (m/s)
- **λ** = mean free path of molecules (m)
- **f(carrier quantity)** = the physical attribute being transported (momentum, energy, or mass)

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1: Viscosity (Momentum Transport)
Momentum flux across a plane arises because molecules crossing from the upper layer (moving in +x direction at velocity *u + du*) carry *more* momentum than those coming from below.

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda$$

### Step 2: Thermal Conductivity (Energy Transport)
Energy flux arises because molecules from the hot side carry higher kinetic energy than those from the cold side. Using the kinetic theory definition of heat capacity per molecule, **Ĉv = (3/2)k_B**:

$$k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \cdot \tilde{C}_v$$

where $\tilde{C}_v$ is the heat capacity per unit mass (J/kg·K).

### Step 3: The Combined Relationship
Dividing thermal conductivity by viscosity:

$$\frac{k}{\mu} = \frac{\frac{1}{3}\rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v}{\frac{1}{3}\rho \bar{u} \lambda}$$

The **density, mean speed, and mean free path cancel out completely**, leaving:

$$\boxed{\frac{k}{\mu} = \tilde{C}_v = C_v \quad (\text{per unit mass})}$$

This is the most elegant result of the kinetic theory: **thermal conductivity is simply viscosity times the heat capacity per unit mass**.

## 2.3 Eucken's Correction (For Polyatomic Real Gases)

The simple result above holds only for **monatomic ideal gases**. For **polyatomic gases**, molecules can store energy not only in translational modes but also in **rotational and vibrational modes**. Eucken proposed:

$$k = \mu \left( \tilde{C}_p + \frac{5}{4} \frac{R}{M} \right)$$

Dividing through by μ:

$$\frac{k}{\mu \tilde{C}_v} = \frac{\tilde{C}_p}{\tilde{C}_v} + \frac{5}{4} \frac{R}{M \tilde{C}_v}$$

Recognizing the Prandtl number definition:

$$\boxed{Pr = \frac{\tilde{C}_p \mu}{k} = \frac{1}{\frac{\tilde{C}_p}{\tilde{C}_v} + \frac{5}{4} \frac{R}{M \tilde{C}_v}}}$$

For **monatomic gases**, this reduces to Pr ≈ **2/3**, which is a celebrated result.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Fourier's Law | $q = -k \, dT/dy$ | Heat flux definition | W/m² |
| Kinetic viscosity | $\mu = \tfrac{1}{3}\rho \bar{u} \lambda$ | Momentum transport | kg/(m·s) |
| Kinetic thermal conductivity | $k = \tfrac{1}{3}\rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v$ | Energy transport | W/(m·K) |
| k/μ Ratio (monatomic) | $k/\mu = \tilde{C}_v$ | Direct proportionality | J/(kg·K) |
| Eucken's Equation | $k = \mu(\tilde{C}_p + \tfrac{5R}{4M})$ | Polyatomic gas correction | W/(m·K) |
| Prandtl Number | $Pr = \tilde{C}_p \mu / k$ | Momentum-to-thermal diffusivity | dimensionless |
| Pr for monatomic gas | $Pr \approx 2/3$ | Limiting case | dimensionless |
| Thermal diffusivity (solid) | $\alpha = k/(\rho \tilde{C}_p)$ | Heat propagation rate | m²/s |
| Wiedemann-Franz Law | $k/(\sigma T) = L$ | Metal electron transport | W·Ω/K² |
| Lorentz Number | $L = \pi^2 k_B^2 / (3 e^2) = 2.45 \times 10^{-8}$ | Theoretical constant | W·Ω/K² |
| Solid k (phonon model) | $k_s = \tfrac{1}{3} \rho \tilde{C}_p \bar{u}_p \lambda_p$ | Lattice vibration transport | W/(m·K) |
| Mean molecular speed | $\bar{u} = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ | Maxwell-Boltzmann avg | m/s |
| Mean free path | $\lambda = 1/(\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 n)$ | Inter-molecular distance | m |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of k–μ Relationship** |
|------------|------------------------------------- |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Re-entry vehicle thermal protection design |
| **Heat Exchanger Design** | Predicting gas-side heat transfer coefficients |
| **Cryogenics** | LNG storage insulation, helium liquefaction |
| **Combustion Engineering** | Flame stability in gas turbines |
| **Polymer Processing** | Injection molding heat transfer rates |
| **Semiconductor Industry** | Cooling of wafers using inert gases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The k/μ ratio is *almost constant* for many gases over moderate temperature ranges. This allows engineers to estimate one property from the other when experimental data is missing — a critical skill in **Chemical Engineering Plant Design**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Viscosity from Kinetic Theory of Gases

**Starting Premise:** Consider two parallel layers of gas separated by distance *dy*, moving in the *x*-direction with velocity gradient *du/dy*.

### Step 1: Identify the relevant flux

The viscous momentum flux (momentum per unit area per unit time) crossing a plane at y = 0 due to molecules coming from y = +λ is:

$$\tau_{yx} = \frac{1}{3} n m \bar{u} \frac{d u_x}{d y} \lambda$$

### Step 2: Introduce density

Since ρ = n·m (number density × molecular mass):

$$\tau_{yx} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \frac{d u_x}{d y}$$

### Step 3: Compare with Newton's Law of Viscosity

Newton's Law of Viscosity states:

$$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{d u_x}{d y}$$

### Step 4: Equate and solve for μ

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Conductivity from Kinetic Theory

**Starting Premise:** Consider the same planar geometry, now with a temperature gradient *dT/dy* in the *y*-direction. Molecules crossing from hot (y = +λ) carry energy **per molecule** = m·Ĉ_v·T_hot, and molecules from cold (y = -λ) carry m·Ĉ_v·T_cold.

### Step 1: Net energy flux across plane y = 0

$$q_y = \frac{1}{3} n \bar{u} \left[ m \tilde{C}_v T(y + \lambda) - m \tilde{C}_v T(y - \lambda) \right]$$

The factor 1/3 arises from averaging molecular motion across three Cartesian directions.

### Step 2: Taylor Expand the temperatures

For small λ:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(y + \lambda) &= T + \lambda \frac{dT}{dy} + \mathcal{O}(\lambda^2) \\
T(y - \lambda) &= T - \lambda \frac{dT}{dy} + \mathcal{O}(\lambda^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_y &= \frac{1}{3} n \bar{u} \cdot m \tilde{C}_v \left[ \left(T + \lambda \frac{dT}{dy}\right) - \left(T - \lambda \frac{dT}{dy}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{3} n \bar{u} \cdot m \tilde{C}_v \cdot 2\lambda \frac{dT}{dy} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} n m \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v \frac{dT}{dy}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply rigorous kinetic theory correction factor

The factor 2/3 becomes 1/3 when the exact kinetic theory (rather than the simplified mean-free-path approach) is used:

$$q_y = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v \frac{dT}{dy}$$

### Step 5: Compare with Fourier's Law

Fourier's Law states: $q_y = -k \, dT/dy$

Equating:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Master Relationship k/μ = Ĉv

### Step 1: Write down both kinetic theory expressions

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda
\qquad
k = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v
$$

### Step 2: Form the ratio

$$
\frac{k}{\mu} = \frac{\frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v}{\frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{u} \lambda}
$$

### Step 3: Cancel common terms

The factor **(1/3) ρ ū λ** appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels exactly:

$$\frac{k}{\mu} = \tilde{C}_v \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 4: Express in molar units

For molar heat capacity $C_v$ (J/mol·K) and molar mass M (kg/mol):

$$\frac{k}{\mu} = \frac{C_v}{M}$$

This is the **most-quoted relationship in KTU Module 1**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Eucken's Equation for Polyatomic Gases

**Premise:** Real polyatomic gases carry energy not just by translation but also by internal rotational and vibrational modes.

### Step 1: Identify translational and internal contributions

Monatomic kinetic theory gives: $k = \mu \tilde{C}_{v,\text{trans}}$

For a polyatomic gas, we must add the contribution of internal degrees of freedom. Eucken proposed that the internal energy is transported by a slightly different mechanism, with a corrective factor:

$$k_{\text{total}} = k_{\text{trans}} + k_{\text{internal}}$$

### Step 2: Express each contribution

$$
k_{\text{trans}} = \mu \tilde{C}_{v,\text{trans}} = \mu \cdot \frac{3R}{2M}
$$

For the internal contribution (rotation + vibration), Eucken used an empirical factor of (9γ − 5)/4 on $\tilde{C}_{v,\text{int}}$, leading to the combined expression:

$$k = \mu \left[ \tilde{C}_{v,\text{trans}} + \frac{9\gamma - 5}{4} \tilde{C}_{v,\text{int}} \right]$$

### Step 3: Simplify using $\tilde{C}_p - \tilde{C}_v = R/M$ and $\gamma = \tilde{C}_p/\tilde{C}_v$

After algebraic manipulation (using $\tilde{C}_v = \tilde{C}_p - R/M$ and substituting back):

$$\boxed{k = \mu \left( \tilde{C}_p + \frac{5}{4} \frac{R}{M} \right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 4: Derive the corresponding Prandtl number

$$
Pr = \frac{\tilde{C}_p \mu}{k} = \frac{\tilde{C}_p \mu}{\mu \left(\tilde{C}_p + \frac{5R}{4M}\right)}
$$

$$Pr = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{5R}{4M\tilde{C}_p}}$$

For monatomic gas (γ = 5/3, R/M·Ĉ_p = 2/3): Pr ≈ **2/3**, validating the formula.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Thermal Conductivity of Solids — Phonon Model

**Premise:** In non-metallic crystalline solids, heat is carried by quantized lattice vibrations called **phonons**, analogous to gas molecules.

### Step 1: Identify analogous quantities to kinetic gas theory

| Gas Quantity | Phonon Equivalent |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Mean molecular speed ū | Phonon group velocity $\bar{u}_p$ (≈ speed of sound) |
| Mean free path λ | Phonon mean free path $\lambda_p$ |
| Heat capacity per molecule | Heat capacity per phonon mode |
| Number density n | Phonon density |

### Step 2: Apply the kinetic transport formula

By direct analogy:

$$k_{\text{solid}} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \tilde{C}_p \bar{u}_p \lambda_p$$

### Step 3: Express as thermal diffusivity

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \tilde{C}_p} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{u}_p \lambda_p$$

This is the **thermal diffusivity of the solid**, which dictates how fast temperature disturbances propagate.

### Step 4: Temperature dependence

- **Phonon velocity $\bar{u}_p$** is approximately constant (≈ Debye velocity, weakly T-dependent).
- **Phonon mean free path $\lambda_p$** decreases with T due to **Umklapp phonon-phonon scattering**.
- **Heat capacity** follows the **Debye T³ law** at low T and approaches the Dulong-Petit limit at high T.

For **dielectric solids at moderate to high T**:

$$\boxed{k_s \propto \frac{1}{T}}$$

For **dielectric solids at very low T (T << Debye temperature θ_D)**:

$$k_s \propto T^3 e^{\theta_D/(bT)}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Wiedemann-Franz Law for Metals

**Premise:** In metallic solids, heat is carried predominantly by **free electrons**, which also conduct electricity.

### Step 1: Express thermal and electrical conductivity of electron gas

Using the kinetic theory applied to free electrons:

$$
k_e = \frac{1}{3} n_e \bar{u}_e \lambda_e \cdot \tilde{C}_{v,e}
\qquad
\sigma = \frac{n_e e^2 \lambda_e}{m_e \bar{u}_e}
$$

### Step 2: Form the ratio

$$
\frac{k_e}{\sigma} = \frac{\frac{1}{3} n_e \bar{u}_e \lambda_e \tilde{C}_{v,e}}{\frac{n_e e^2 \lambda_e}{m_e \bar{u}_e}}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Fermi-Dirac statistics

For a degenerate electron gas (Fermi statistics, not Maxwell-Boltzmann):

$$
\frac{k_e}{\sigma T} = \frac{\pi^2 k_B^2}{3 e^2} = L
$$

This constant L is the **Lorentz Number**:

$$\boxed{L = 2.45 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W}\cdot\Omega/\text{K}^2 \quad \text{(theoretical value)}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{k}{\sigma T} = L} \quad \text{(Wiedemann-Franz Law)} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 4: Empirical observation

Experimental measurements show L_exp ≈ 2.0–2.7 × 10⁻⁸ W·Ω/K² for most pure metals at moderate temperatures, confirming the theory.

## 3.7 Summary Matrix — Three Transport Properties in Gases

| Property | Driving Gradient | Carrier | Kinetic Theory Formula |
|----------|------------------|---------|------------------------|
| Viscosity μ | Velocity gradient du/dy | Momentum | $\mu = \tfrac{1}{3}\rho \bar{u} \lambda$ |
| Thermal conductivity k | Temperature gradient dT/dy | Kinetic energy | $k = \tfrac{1}{3}\rho \bar{u} \lambda \tilde{C}_v$ |
| Diffusivity D_AB | Concentration gradient | Mass | $D_{AB} = \tfrac{1}{3}\bar{u} \lambda$ |

The **same factor (1/3) ρ ū λ** appears in all three — this is the **unity of transport phenomena**, a foundational principle of Bird, Stewart & Lightfoot's canonical textbook.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Transport Properties in Gases

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Kinetic Theory of Gases] --> B[Mean Free Path λ]
    A --> C[Mean Molecular Speed ū]
    A --> D[Number Density n]
    B --> E[Viscosity μ = 1/3 ρ ū λ]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F[Thermal Conductivity k = 1/3 ρ ū λ Cv]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    B --> G[Diffusivity D = 1/3 ū λ]
    C --> G
    E --> H[k / μ = Cv per unit mass]
    F --> H
    E --> I[Prandtl Number Pr = Cp μ / k]
    F --> I
    H --> J[Monatomic Gas Pr ≈ 2/3]
    I --> J
    F --> K[Eucken Correction for Polyatomic]
    K --> L[k = μ Cp + 5R / 4M]
    L --> M[Polyatomic Pr Relation]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Thermal Conductivity of Solids (Mechanism Map)

```mermaid

    S[Solid Material] --> M1{Type of Solid}
    M1 --> N1[Metallic Solid]
    M1 --> N2[Dielectric Non-Metallic Solid]
    M1 --> N3[Composite or Alloy]
    N1 --> P1[Free Electron Transport]
    N1 --> P2[Phonon Transport minor]
    N2 --> Q1[Phonon Lattice Vibration Transport]
    N2 --> Q2[Radiative Transport at High T]
    N3 --> R1[Combined Electron and Phonon]
    N3 --> R2[Boundary Scattering Dominant]
    P1 --> T1[Wiedemann-Franz Law k = L σ T]
    Q1 --> T2[Phonon Model k = 1/3 ρ Cp ūp λp]
    Q2 --> T3[Temperature Cubic at Low T]
    T2 --> U1[k decreases with T at High T]
    T1 --> U2[k increases with σ for pure metals]

    U2 --> W[Design Heat Sinks with High Conductivity Metals]
```

```mermaid
    C[Intermediate T] --> D[k reaches maximum at peak]
    E[High T T greater than θD] --> F[k decreases as 1 over T due to Umklapp scattering]
    G[Metals across all T] --> H[k and σ T product constant via Wiedemann-Franz]
    B --> I[Phonon Mean Free Path limited by Boundary Scattering]
    D --> J[Phonon Mean Free Path limited by Point Defects]
    F --> K[Phonon Mean Free Path limited by Phonon Phonon Collisions]
```
