## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
State the relationship between binary gas diffusivity and total pressure for an ideal gas mixture. Show that D_AB × P = constant at constant temperature.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For an ideal gas at low to moderate pressure, the Chapman–Enskog theory gives:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left[ \dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B} \right]^{1/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^{2} \, \Omega_{D}}$$

At constant temperature, the only P-dependent term is **P in the denominator**. All other terms (T, M_A, M_B, $\sigma_{AB}$, $\Omega_D$) are constants at fixed T. Thus, grouping these into a constant K:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{K}{P} \implies D_{AB} \cdot P = K = \text{constant}$$

Applying at two states:
$$D_{AB,1} \cdot P_1 = D_{AB,2} \cdot P_2 \implies D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$

Hence, **doubling the pressure halves the diffusivity** at constant temperature. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Explain why the binary gas diffusivity D_AB is essentially independent of composition for an ideal gas mixture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the Chapman–Enskog derivation, $D_{AB}$ depends on:
1. **Molecular weights** $M_A, M_B$ — fixed pure-component properties.
2. **Collision diameter** $\sigma_{AB} = (\sigma_A + \sigma_B)/2$ — depends only on the A–B pair, not on the bulk mole fraction.
3. **Collision integral** $\Omega_D$ — function of $kT/\varepsilon_{AB}$ only (depends on T and A–B pair interaction).
4. **Temperature T** and **Pressure P**.

None of these quantities contain the mole fraction $y_A$. Therefore, $D_{AB} = f(T, P)$ only, and is **independent of composition** for ideal gas mixtures at low/moderate pressures. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **forget the units check** when applying the P-correction rule. If P is in atm, use $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s; if P is in Pa, use SI units.
> - For combined T and P correction, **many students miss the $T^{3/2}$ factor** and only apply the P-correction. This costs 2-3 marks.
> - In composition questions, students often incorrectly apply the same P-correction to liquid diffusivities. Remember: **liquids are nearly incompressible**, so P has negligible effect on liquid $D_{AB}$.
> - Always **state the assumption** (ideal gas, constant $\Omega_D$, dilute solution) in the first line of the solution.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between binary gas diffusivity and total pressure starting from the Chapman–Enskog equation. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The diffusivity of hydrogen (A) in nitrogen (B) at 1 atm and 273 K is $0.784 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the diffusivity of the same pair at:
- (i) 2 atm and 273 K
- (ii) 1 atm and 546 K (assuming $\Omega_D$ is constant)

Comment on the physical significance of the results. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Start with the Chapman–Enskog equation:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left[ \dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B} \right]^{1/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^{2} \, \Omega_{D}}$$

**Step 2** — **Assumptions** (state 3 of these for full marks):
1. The gas mixture is **ideal** (low to moderate pressure, P < ~10 atm).
2. The molecules are **spherical, non-polar, and non-reacting**.
3. Only **binary collisions** are significant (dilute gas limit).
4. The system is at **steady state** and **isothermal**.

**Step 3** — At constant T, group P-independent terms:

Let 
$$K = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left[ \dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B} \right]^{1/2}}{\sigma_{AB}^{2} \, \Omega_{D}}$$

Then:
$$D_{AB} = \frac{K}{P}$$

**Step 4** — Take two states (1) and (2):

$$D_{AB,1} \cdot P_1 = K = D_{AB,2} \cdot P_2$$

$$\boxed{\therefore D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_2}}$$

**Conclusion**: Binary gas diffusivity is **inversely proportional** to total pressure at constant temperature. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given**:
- $D_{AB,1} = 0.784 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ at $T_1 = 273$ K, $P_1 = 1$ atm.

**Part (i): 2 atm and 273 K (T constant)**

Using $D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_2}$:

$$D_{AB,2} = 0.784 \times \frac{1}{2} = 0.392 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Physical significance**: Doubling the pressure halves the diffusivity. Higher pressure means smaller mean free path, so molecules collide more frequently and diffuse more slowly. **(3 marks)**

**Part (ii): 1 atm and 546 K (P constant, T varies)**

Using $D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{3/2}$ (since P is constant):

$$D_{AB,2} = 0.784 \times \left(\frac{546}{273}\right)^{3/2} = 0.784 \times (2)^{1.5} = 0.784 \times 2.828 = 2.218 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Physical significance**: Doubling the absolute temperature (T to the power 1.5) increases diffusivity by a factor of $2^{1.5} \approx 2.83$. Higher temperature increases molecular kinetic energy, average velocity, and mean free path, so diffusion is faster. **(4 marks)**

**Total: 7 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the effect of composition on the binary gas diffusivity for:
   - (i) An ideal gas mixture
   - (ii) A non-ideal gas mixture
   - (iii) A dilute liquid solution **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the liquid-phase diffusivity of acetic acid (A) in water (B) at 25 °C using the Wilke–Chang equation. Given:
- $M_B$ (water) = 18 g/mol
- $\mu_B$ (water at 25 °C) = 0.894 cP
- $V_A$ (acetic acid) = 64.1 cm³/gmol
- $\Phi_B$ (water) = 2.6 **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Effect of Composition (7 marks):**

**(i) Ideal gas mixture** — For an ideal binary gas at low/moderate pressure, $D_{AB}$ depends **only on T and P**, not on composition. This is because the collision integral and collision diameter are functions of the A–B pair alone, not of the bulk mole fraction. Hence $D_{AB}$ is a binary property, often tabulated at 1 atm and a reference temperature, and corrected for state changes using $D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot (P_1/P_2) \cdot (T_2/T_1)^{1.5}$. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Non-ideal gas mixture** — At high pressure (near critical) or in systems with strong intermolecular forces, the gas becomes non-ideal. The composition enters through a **thermodynamic correction factor** $\Gamma$:

$$D_{AB,\text{thermo}} = D_{AB,\text{kinetic}} \cdot \Gamma, \quad \text{where} \quad \Gamma = 1 + x_A \frac{\partial \ln \phi_A}{\partial x_A}$$

When the mixture shows positive deviation from Raoult's law, $\Gamma < 1$ (slower diffusion); for negative deviation, $\Gamma > 1$ (faster diffusion). **(2.5 marks)**

**(iii) Dilute liquid solution** — In liquids, composition strongly affects $D_{AB}$ because molecular interactions (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding) change with concentration. For dilute solutions, the **Wilke–Chang equation** is used, which captures the dependence on solute molar volume $V_A$, solvent viscosity $\mu_B$, and temperature. For concentrated solutions, the **Vignes correlation** is used:

$$D_{AB}(x_A) = (D_{AB}^{*})^{\,x_B} \cdot (D_{BA}^{*})^{\,x_A}$$

where $D_{AB}^{*}$ and $D_{BA}^{*}$ are infinite-dilution diffusivities. **(2.5 marks)**

---

**(b) Wilke–Chang Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given**:
- T = 25 °C = 298 K
- $M_B$ = 18 g/mol
- $\mu_B$ = 0.894 cP
- $V_A$ = 64.1 cm³/gmol
- $\Phi_B$ = 2.6

**Wilke–Chang equation**:

$$D_{AB}^{L} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\Phi_B \, M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

**Step 1** — Compute $(\Phi_B M_B)^{1/2}$:

$$\Phi_B M_B = 2.6 \times 18 = 46.8$$
$$(\Phi_B M_B)^{1/2} = \sqrt{46.8} = 6.841$$

**Step 2** — Compute $V_A^{0.6}$:

$$V_A^{0.6} = (64.1)^{0.6}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(64.1) = 4.160$
$$0.6 \times 4.160 = 2.496$$
$$V_A^{0.6} = e^{2.496} = 12.13$$

**Step 3** — Substitute into the equation:

$$D_{AB}^{L} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.841 \times 298}{0.894 \times 12.13}$$

**Step 4** — Numerator: $7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.841 \times 298 = 1.508 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 5** — Denominator: $0.894 \times 12.13 = 10.84$

**Step 6** — Final division:

$$D_{AB}^{L} = \frac{1.508 \times 10^{-4}}{10.84} = 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_{AB}^{L} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.39 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Verification**: This is in the typical order of magnitude for liquid diffusivities ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ cm²/s), and the literature value for acetic acid in water at 25 °C is about $1.19 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s, so our estimate is in close agreement. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Pressure and Composition on Diffusivity**

- **The Golden Rule for Gases**: $D_{AB} \times P = \text{const}$ at constant T.
- **Combined T-P correction**: $D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot (T_2/T_1)^{1.5} \cdot (P_1/P_2)$.
- **Ideal Gas Composition Effect**: D_AB is **independent of composition** at low/moderate pressures.
- **Non-Ideal Gas**: Use **thermodynamic correction factor** $\Gamma = 1 + x_A \, \partial \ln \phi_A / \partial x_A$.
- **High-Pressure Gas Behaviour**: $D_{AB} \times \rho \approx \text{const}$ (not $D_{AB} \times P$).
- **Liquid Diffusivity**: Use **Wilke–Chang** for dilute solutions; **Vignes** for concentrated.
- **Liquid Phase is Incompressible**: Pressure has **negligible** effect on $D_{AB}$ for liquids.
- **Always State Assumptions**: Ideal gas, constant $\Omega_D$, dilute solution — these are worth 1-2 marks each.
- **Units Discipline**: D in cm²/s when P is in atm and $\sigma$ in Å (Chapman–Enskog); SI units otherwise.
- **Typical Orders of Magnitude**:
   - Gas-phase D_AB: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ cm²/s
   - Liquid-phase D_AB: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ cm²/s
   - Solid-phase D_AB: $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-30}$ cm²/s
- **Most Tested Numerical**: Pressure correction alone (3-4 marks) OR combined T-P correction (7 marks).
- **Association Factor for Water**: $\Phi_B = 2.6$ — memorize this value.
- **Combining Rules**: $\sigma_{AB} = (\sigma_A + \sigma_B)/2$ and $\varepsilon_{AB}/k = \sqrt{(\varepsilon_A/k)(\varepsilon_B/k)}$.
- **Examiner's Trap**: Forgetting the $T^{3/2}$ dependence when only T changes (not just P).
- **Key Insight**: D_AB characterizes the **A–B molecular pair**, not the bulk mixture — this is why it can be composition-independent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shell momentum balance: boundary conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shell momentum balance: boundary conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Shell Momentum Balance

A **shell momentum balance** is a systematic, integral approach used in momentum transport to determine the velocity profile and momentum flux distribution within a flowing fluid. The technique involves selecting a thin, differential fluid "shell" (a slab, cylindrical sleeve, or spherical shell) that is bounded by two surfaces across which momentum is being transported, and then applying the law of conservation of linear momentum to the fluid contained within that shell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conservation Statement (KTU Syllabus Anchor):**
> $$\text{Rate of Momentum In} - \text{Rate of Momentum Out} + \text{Sum of Forces} = \text{Rate of Momentum Accumulation}$$

The general procedure consists of the following canonical steps:

1. **Shell Selection** — Choose a thin fluid element of thickness $\Delta x_j$ in the direction of momentum transport.
2. **Momentum Flux Identification** — Identify the convective (bulk flow) and molecular (viscous) flux terms crossing the shell faces.
3. **Force Summation** — Account for body forces (gravity) and surface forces (pressure, gravity-driven).
4. **Differential Formulation** — Take the limit as the shell thickness approaches zero to convert the integral balance into an ordinary differential equation (ODE).
5. **Integration with Boundary Conditions** — Solve the ODE subject to physical constraints applied at the boundaries of the system.

## 1.2 The Critical Role of Boundary Conditions

A differential equation, however elegantly derived, remains an **incomplete mathematical statement** until boundary conditions (BCs) are specified. Boundary conditions are the **physical and mathematical constraints** imposed at the geometric limits of the fluid domain (walls, interfaces, inlets, outlets, axes of symmetry, or points at infinity) that pin down the unique physical solution from the infinite family of mathematical solutions of the ODE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Boundary Conditions Matter in Transport Phenomena:**
> The *order* of the differential equation dictates the *number* of independent boundary conditions required. The **Newtonian momentum balance ODEs are second-order** (e.g., $d^2v_x/dy^2 = \text{constant}$), and therefore each momentum balance problem requires **exactly two independent BCs** for a unique solution.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Storybook on a Sliding Shelf"

Imagine a row of books on a frictionless shelf, with each book's position representing the **momentum** at a particular spatial location in the fluid. The book closest to the wall cannot move (the **no-slip** boundary condition). The book at the very center of the shelf experiences a special condition — the books on either side are mirror images (**symmetry**). At the top of the stack, where the air meets the liquid, there is no horizontal "pulling force" (**zero-shear free surface**). These fixed book positions are exactly what boundary conditions do to the velocity profile: they **anchor** the mathematical solution to the physical reality of the problem.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Play

- **Dynamic viscosity** of common fluids at 20°C: water $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **Pa·s**; air $\mu \approx 1.82 \times 10^{-5}$ **Pa·s**.
- **No-slip layer thickness** in macroscopic flow: typically of order **micrometers** for gases, **nanometers** for liquids.
- **Typical engineering shear rates**: $\dot{\gamma} \sim 10^1$ to $10^3$ **s⁻¹** in process piping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Necessity Argument

Let us consider the canonical shell momentum balance for steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid down an inclined plane (BSL Chapter 2). The resulting differential equation is:

$$\frac{d}{dy}\left(\mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}\right) = \rho g \sin\alpha$$

If we treat $\mu$ and $\rho$ as constants, this simplifies to:

$$\mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \rho g \sin\alpha$$

This is a **second-order linear ODE** in $v_x(y)$. The general solution contains **two arbitrary constants of integration** ($C_1$ and $C_2$):

$$v_x(y) = \frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{2\mu} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

Without boundary conditions, an infinite family of parabolic profiles satisfies the ODE. **Two BCs are required** to fix $C_1$ and $C_2$ uniquely.

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Boundary Conditions of Shell Momentum Balances

### 2.2.1 Category 1: Conditions at Solid Surfaces (No-Slip)

At a rigid, impermeable solid wall, the **continuum hypothesis** and the experimentally verified **no-slip condition** dictate that the tangential fluid velocity equals the tangential velocity of the wall.

- **Stationary wall:** $v_x \big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$
- **Moving wall (e.g., conveyor or rotating cylinder):** $v_x \big\vert_{\text{wall}} = V_{\text{wall}}$
- **Direction orthogonality:** Normal velocity component $v_n \big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ (impermeability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The no-slip condition is **empirically validated** to hold for virtually all Newtonian fluids under macroscopic conditions, although it is known to break down at the nanoscale (Knudsen number $Kn > 0.1$).

### 2.2.2 Category 2: Conditions at Free Surfaces (Zero Shear)

At an interface between a liquid and a gas of negligible viscosity (e.g., a falling film exposed to air), the **tangential momentum flux must be continuous**. Since the gas-phase viscous stress is approximately zero, the liquid-phase viscous stress at the free surface must also vanish:

$$\tau_{yx}\big\vert_{y=\delta} = \mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=\delta} = 0$$

This yields the **mathematical BC**:

$$\frac{dv_x}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=\delta} = 0$$

### 2.2.3 Category 3: Conditions at Fluid–Fluid Interfaces

At the interface between two immiscible fluids (e.g., oil over water in a coextrusion problem), **two simultaneous conditions** must be satisfied:

1. **Continuity of tangential velocity (kinematic condition):**

$$v_{x,I}\big\vert_{\text{interface}} = v_{x,II}\big\vert_{\text{interface}}$$

2. **Continuity of tangential momentum flux (dynamic condition):**

$$\mu_I \frac{dv_{x,I}}{dy}\bigg\vert_{\text{interface}} = \mu_{II} \frac{dv_{x,II}}{dy}\bigg\vert_{\text{interface}}$$

### 2.2.4 Category 4: Conditions at Planes or Axes of Symmetry

At the centerline of a symmetric flow (e.g., plane Poiseuille flow between two parallel plates, or axisymmetric flow in a pipe), the velocity profile must be **mirror-symmetric** about the centerline. Mathematically, this requires the velocity gradient to vanish:

- **Cartesian centerline at $y=0$:** $\frac{dv_x}{dy}\big\vert_{y=0} = 0$
- **Cylindrical centerline at $r=0$:** $\frac{dv_\theta}{dr}\big\vert_{r=0} = 0$

> [!NOTE]
> The symmetry BC is a **derivative** (Neumann-type) condition, in contrast to the no-slip BC, which is a **value** (Dirichlet-type) condition. Recognizing this distinction is critical in KTU board examinations.

### 2.2.5 Category 5: Conditions at Inlets, Outlets, and Infinity

- **Fully developed flow at outlet (parallel plates/pipe):** $\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\text{outlet}} = 0$ — velocity profile is unchanging in the streamwise direction.
- **External flow approaching a free stream:** $v_x \to V_\infty$ as $y \to \infty$.
- **Prescribed inlet profile:** $v_x\big\vert_{x=0} = v_{\text{prescribed}}(y)$.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below consolidates **every boundary condition** you are expected to recognize in the KTU 2024 ESE for Module 2.

| # | Physical Situation | Mathematical Statement | Type | Where Applied |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fluid adheres to a **stationary solid wall** | $v_x = 0$ | Dirichlet (Value) | At $y = 0$ or wall location |
| 2 | Fluid adheres to a **moving solid wall** (e.g., belt, rotating cylinder) | $v_x = V_{\text{wall}}$ | Dirichlet (Value) | At $y = 0$ or wall location |
| 3 | **Impermeability** at solid surface | $v_n = 0$ | Dirichlet (Value) | Normal direction at wall |
| 4 | **Free surface** (liquid–gas interface, $\mu_{\text{gas}} \approx 0$) | $\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = 0$ | Neumann (Derivative) | At $y = \delta$ |
| 5 | **Symmetry / centerline** of a flow | $\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = 0$ | Neumann (Derivative) | At $y = 0$ or $r = 0$ |
| 6 | **Liquid–liquid interface**: velocity continuity | $v_{x,I} = v_{x,II}$ | Dirichlet (Value) | At interface $y = \delta$ |
| 7 | **Liquid–liquid interface**: shear continuity | $\mu_I \dfrac{dv_{x,I}}{dy} = \mu_{II} \dfrac{dv_{x,II}}{dy}$ | Neumann (Derivative) | At interface $y = \delta$ |
| 8 | **Fully developed** flow at outlet | $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} = 0$ | Neumann (Derivative) | At $x = L$ |
| 9 | **External flow** approaching free stream | $v_x \to V_\infty$ as $y \to \infty$ | Limiting (Value) | At $y \to \infty$ |
| 10 | **Steady-state** condition | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial t} = 0$ | Field Equation | Throughout domain |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These boundary conditions are the workhorses behind the design of:

- **Polymer film coating operations** — falling film BC ($dv_x/dy = 0$ at free surface) determines coating thickness uniformity.
- **Pipeline transport of waxy crude oils** — no-slip BC at pipe wall governs pressure drop calculations in PSV and ESD sizing.
- **Lubrication theory (journal bearings)** — Couette-type moving wall BC ($v_x = U$) is the foundation of hydrodynamic lubrication.
- **Two-phase stratified pipe flow** — dual interface BCs (velocity + shear continuity) drive multiphase flow meter calibration.
- **Rotating biological contactors** — rotating cylinder BC ($v_\theta = \Omega R$) governs biofilm mass-transfer correlations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Two-BC Counting Argument

We begin by rigorously demonstrating **why exactly two boundary conditions are needed** for a Newtonian momentum balance.

**Step 1: Start from the general Newtonian momentum balance on a flat shell.**

For steady, fully developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid of constant $\mu$ and $\rho$ in the $x$-direction, with gravity acting in the $-z$ direction, the $x$-momentum equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$$

**Step 2: Specialize to the simplest case (flow between parallel plates, $z$-invariant).**

Since the flow is uniform in $z$, all $z$-derivatives vanish:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \rho g_x$$

**Step 3: Recognize the right-hand side as a function of $x$ alone.**

The left side is identically zero. The term $\partial^2 v_x/\partial y^2$ depends only on $y$, while $\partial P/\partial x$ depends only on $x$. This separation-of-variables argument forces:

$$\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} - \frac{\rho g_x}{\mu} = \text{constant} \equiv C$$

**Step 4: Integrate the ODE twice.**

$$\frac{dv_x}{dy} = C y + C_1$$

$$v_x(y) = \frac{C}{2} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 5: Count the arbitrary constants.**

The constants $C$, $C_1$, and $C_2$ are all unknown. However, $C$ is fixed by the *forcing terms* (pressure gradient and gravity), and **two** independent constants ($C_1$ and $C_2$) remain free, demanding **two** boundary conditions. This completes the counting argument.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Application: Falling Film on an Inclined Plane

This is the most-asked KTU problem on boundary conditions. We will derive the velocity profile **and explicitly identify the two boundary conditions used**.

**Problem Setup:** A Newtonian liquid of viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho$ flows as a film of thickness $\delta$ down a plane inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. The film is exposed to air at the top (free surface) and a solid wall at the bottom.

**Step 1: Write the shell momentum balance (BSL Eq. 2.3-13).**

For the thin shell between $y$ and $y + \Delta y$:

$$\left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y} - \left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y+\Delta y} + \rho g \sin\alpha \, \Delta y = 0$$

**Step 2: Divide by $\Delta y$ and take the limit $\Delta y \to 0$.**

$$\lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{\left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y} - \left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y+\Delta y}}{\Delta y} + \rho g \sin\alpha = 0$$

By the formal definition of the derivative:

$$-\frac{d\tau_{yx}}{dy} + \rho g \sin\alpha = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute Newton's law of viscosity, $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$.**

$$-\frac{d}{dy}\left(-\mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}\right) + \rho g \sin\alpha = 0$$

$$\mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} + \rho g \sin\alpha = 0$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to standard form.**

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = -\frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{\mu}$$

**Step 5: First integration.**

$$\frac{dv_x}{dy} = -\frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{\mu} y + C_1$$

**Step 6: Second integration.**

$$v_x(y) = -\frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{2\mu} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 7: Apply Boundary Condition #1 — No-slip at the solid wall ($y = 0$).**

$$v_x(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**Step 8: Apply Boundary Condition #2 — Zero shear at the free surface ($y = \delta$).**

$$\frac{dv_x}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=\delta} = 0 \implies 0 = -\frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{\mu} \delta + C_1$$

$$C_1 = \frac{\rho g \sin\alpha \delta}{\mu}$$

**Step 9: Substitute $C_1$ and $C_2$ back into the velocity expression.**

$$v_x(y) = -\frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{2\mu} y^2 + \frac{\rho g \sin\alpha \delta}{\mu} y$$

Factor the common term $\dfrac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{2\mu}$:

$$v_x(y) = \frac{\rho g \sin\alpha}{2\mu}\left(2\delta y - y^2\right)$$

**Step 10: Verify the dimensional consistency.**

- $\rho$: kg/m³
- $g$: m/s²
- $\sin\alpha$: dimensionless
- $\mu$: Pa·s = kg/(m·s)
- $y^2$ and $2\delta y$: m²
- Combined units: $\frac{(\text{kg/m}^3)(\text{m/s}^2)(\text{m}^2)}{\text{kg/(m·s)}} = \frac{\text{kg m}^{-1}\text{s}^{-2}\text{m}^2}{\text{kg m}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}} = \text{m/s}$ ✓

**Step 11: Identify the two boundary conditions in a clean KTU-style table.**

| Boundary | Physical Location | Mathematical Statement | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BC #1: No-slip at the wall** | $y = 0$ | $v_x = 0$ | Dirichlet |
| **BC #2: Zero shear at the free surface** | $y = \delta$ | $\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = 0$ | Neumann |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Application: Flow Between Two Parallel Plates (Plane Poiseuille Flow)

**Problem Setup:** Steady, laminar, Newtonian flow between two infinite parallel plates separated by a distance $2b$, driven by a constant pressure gradient $\partial P/\partial x$.

**Step 1: Shell momentum balance gives the ODE.**

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2}$$

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2: Integrate twice.**

$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 3: Apply BC #1 — No-slip at the lower plate ($y = -b$).**

$$0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} b^2 - C_1 b + C_2$$

**Step 4: Apply BC #2 — No-slip at the upper plate ($y = +b$).**

$$0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} b^2 + C_1 b + C_2$$

**Step 5: Solve the two linear equations simultaneously for $C_1$ and $C_2$.**

Adding the two equations:

$$0 = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} b^2 + 2 C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{b^2}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

Subtracting the lower from the upper:

$$0 = 2 C_1 b \implies C_1 = 0$$

**Step 6: Final velocity profile (parabolic).**

$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\left(y^2 - b^2\right)$$

**Step 7: Verification using the alternate symmetry BC.**

By symmetry, the maximum velocity occurs at the centerline $y = 0$, where $dv_x/dy = 0$. This automatically forces $C_1 = 0$ without needing to apply the no-slip BC at both walls — a powerful **KCE-style** (KTU Computational Efficiency) insight that examiners often reward.

| Boundary | Physical Location | Mathematical Statement | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BC #1: No-slip at the lower plate** | $y = -b$ | $v_x = 0$ | Dirichlet |
| **BC #2: No-slip at the upper plate** | $y = +b$ | $v_x = 0$ | Dirichlet |
| *(Alternate)* *Symmetry at centerline* | $y = 0$ | $\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = 0$ | Neumann |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Application: Two Immiscible Fluids Co-Flowing (Annular / Sandwich Flow)

**Problem Setup:** Two immiscible Newtonian fluids (Fluid I: $0 \le y \le \delta$, $\mu_I$; Fluid II: $\delta \le y \le 2\delta$, $\mu_{II}$) flow side by side under a common pressure gradient $dP/dx$.

**Step 1: Two independent ODEs.**

For Fluid I:
$$\frac{d^2 v_{x,I}}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu_I}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

For Fluid II:
$$\frac{d^2 v_{x,II}}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu_{II}}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 2: General solutions contain four unknowns.**

$$v_{x,I}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu_I}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2 \quad (0 \le y \le \delta)$$

$$v_{x,II}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu_{II}}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + C_3 y + C_4 \quad (\delta \le y \le 2\delta)$$

**Step 3: Identify the four required boundary conditions.**

| # | Location | Statement | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| BC 1 | $y = 0$ (wall) | $v_{x,I} = 0$ | Dirichlet |
| BC 2 | $y = 2\delta$ (wall) | $v_{x,II} = 0$ | Dirichlet |
| BC 3 | $y = \delta$ (interface) | $v_{x,I} = v_{x,II}$ | Continuity of velocity |
| BC 4 | $y = \delta$ (interface) | $\mu_I \dfrac{dv_{x,I}}{dy} = \mu_{II}\dfrac{dv_{x,II}}{dy}$ | Continuity of shear |

**Step 4: Solve the four simultaneous equations to obtain $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$.**

This is the canonical "two-fluid problem" used to illustrate that **the number of BCs equals the total order of the coupled ODE system**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Boundary Condition Selection Strategy

The following decision matrix helps students select the correct BC for a given physical situation — a KTU examiner's favorite test of conceptual clarity.

```
Is there a solid surface at this boundary?
  ├── YES → Use NO-SLIP (v_tangential = V_wall) and IMPERMEABILITY (v_normal = 0)
  └── NO  → Continue ↓

Is there a gas-liquid interface (low-viscosity gas)?
  ├── YES → Use ZERO-SHEAR (μ dv/dy = 0)
  └── NO  → Continue ↓

Is there a liquid-liquid interface?
  ├── YES → Apply BOTH velocity continuity AND shear continuity
  └── NO  → Continue ↓

Is the boundary a line of geometric symmetry (centerline, axis)?
  ├── YES → Use ZERO-GRADIENT (dv/dy = 0 in Cartesian; dv/dr = 0 in cylindrical)
  └── NO  → Continue ↓

Is the boundary an inlet, outlet, or far-field?
  ├── Inlet  → Use PRESCRIBED velocity profile
  ├── Outlet → Use FULLY-DEVELOPED (∂v_x/∂x = 0)
  └── Far-field → Use FREE-STREAM condition (v → V_∞)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Shell Momentum Balance Procedure with BC Insertion Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify flow geometry] --> B[Choose a thin fluid shell of thickness delta y]
    B --> C[Write integral momentum balance on the shell]
    C --> D[Divide by delta y and take limit as delta y to 0]
    D --> E[Obtain a second-order ODE in v_x of y]
    E --> F[Count the order of the ODE]
    F --> G{Is the ODE second order?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Identify exactly TWO independent boundary conditions]
    G -- No --> I[Identify N boundary conditions for an Nth-order ODE]
    H --> J[BC 1: Applied at one physical boundary of the domain]
    H --> K[BC 2: Applied at the second physical boundary of the domain]
    J --> L[Integrate the ODE once to obtain v_x expression with one constant]
    K --> M[Apply BC 1 to fix the first constant of integration]
    L --> N[Integrate the ODE twice to obtain v_x expression with two constants]
    M --> O[Apply BC 2 to fix the second constant of integration]
    N --> P[Substitute constants back to obtain final velocity profile]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Verify: Check dimensional consistency and physical limits]
    Q --> R[End: Unique velocity field determined]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Classification of Boundary Conditions by Physical Situation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC[Boundary Conditions for Shell Momentum Balance] --> CAT1[Solid Surface BCs]
    BC --> CAT2[Free Surface BCs]
    BC --> CAT3[Fluid-Fluid Interface BCs]
    BC --> CAT4[Symmetry BCs]
    BC --> CAT5[Inlet Outlet Infinity BCs]

    CAT1 --> N1[No-slip: v_tangential equals V_wall]
    CAT1 --> N2[Impermeability: v_normal equals 0]

    CAT2 --> N3[Zero shear: dv_x over dy equals 0]

    CAT3 --> N4[Velocity continuity: v_x_I equals v_x_II]
    CAT3 --> N5[Shear continuity: mu_I dv_x_I over dy equals mu_II dv_x_II over dy]

    CAT4 --> N6[Cartesian symmetry: dv_x over dy equals 0 at y equals 0]
    CAT4 --> N7[Cylindrical symmetry: dv_theta over dr equals 0 at r equals 0]

    CAT5 --> N8[Prescribed inlet: v_x equals v_inlet of y]
    CAT5 --> N9[Fully developed outlet: partial v_x over partial x equals 0]
    CAT5 --> N10[Free stream infinity: v_x approaches V_infinity]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of BC Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Governing ODE from Shell Balance] --> B[BC Library]
    B --> B1[No-slip at Wall]
    B --> B2[Zero Shear at Free Surface]
    B --> B3[Symmetry at Centerline]
    B --> B4[Continuity at Interface]

    A --> C[Mathematical Solver]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C --> D[Unique Velocity Profile v_x of y]
    D --> E[Compute Momentum Flux]
    D --> F[Compute Force on Surface]
    D --> G[Compute Volumetric Flow Rate]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Decision Tree for Selecting the Correct Boundary Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is at the boundary?} -->|Solid Wall| R1[Apply No-slip v_tangential equals V_wall]
    Q1 -->|Liquid-Gas Interface| R2[Apply Zero Shear tau equals 0]
    Q1 -->|Liquid-Liquid Interface| R3[Apply BOTH Velocity and Shear Continuity]
    Q1 -->|Geometric Centerline| R4[Apply Zero Gradient dv over dy equals 0]
    Q1 -->|Far Field or Inlet Outlet| R5[Apply Limiting or Prescribed Condition]
    R1 --> S[Insert into ODE Solution]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State and justify the boundary conditions used in the derivation of the velocity profile for a Newtonian fluid flowing as a falling film down a vertical wall.**

**Model Answer:**

A falling film has two physical boundaries: the solid wall at $y = 0$ and the liquid–air free surface at $y = \delta$.

**Boundary Condition 1 — No-slip at the solid wall ($y = 0$):**
$$v_x(0) = 0$$

*Justification:* The fluid in direct molecular contact with the stationary wall must have zero tangential velocity (continuum no-slip postulate).

**Boundary Condition 2 — Zero shear at the free surface ($y = \delta$):**
$$\left.\frac{dv_x}{dy}\right\vert_{y=\delta} = 0$$

*Justification:* The viscosity of the surrounding air is negligible ($\mu_{\text{air}} \ll \mu_{\text{liquid}}$), so the tangential momentum flux at the interface $\tau_{yx} = \mu\, dv_x/dy$ must vanish. Hence $dv_x/dy = 0$.

These two independent BCs are sufficient because the governing ODE is second order. **Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3.**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions with one example of each from shell momentum balance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dirichlet BC | Neumann BC |
|---|---|---|
| Specifies the **value** of the dependent variable | Specifies the **derivative** (gradient) of the dependent variable | |
| Physical origin | **Kinematic** (velocity continuity) | **Dynamic** (force/flux balance) |
| Example in shell momentum balance | No-slip at wall: $v_x = 0$ at $y=0$ | Zero shear at free surface: $dv_x/dy = 0$ at $y=\delta$ |
| Example from interface BCs | $v_{x,I} = v_{x,II}$ at liquid–liquid interface | $\mu_I\, dv_{x,I}/dy = \mu_{II}\, dv_{x,II}/dy$ at interface |

**Marks: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**. Students must attempt **ONE of the two alternatives**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Using a shell momentum balance, derive the velocity profile for the steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid falling as a film of thickness $\delta$ down a vertical wall. Identify and justify **both** boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second Newtonian fluid (viscosity $\mu_2$) is now placed on top of the first fluid (viscosity $\mu_1$, layer thickness $\delta_1$), with $\delta_2$ as the second layer's thickness. **State and explain** the four boundary conditions needed to obtain the velocity profiles in each layer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Geometry and coordinate system.** Define $y$ as the distance from the wall, with $x$ along the flow direction. Gravity acts in the $+x$ direction since the wall is vertical.

**Step 2: Shell momentum balance.**

The shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ acts on the lower face (entering) and upper face (leaving) of a shell of thickness $\Delta y$. The body force per unit volume is $\rho g$ in the $+x$ direction. At steady state:

$$\left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y} \cdot (1 \cdot L) - \left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y+\Delta y} \cdot (1 \cdot L) + \rho g \cdot (\Delta y \cdot 1 \cdot L) = 0$$

Dividing by $L \cdot \Delta y$ and taking $\Delta y \to 0$:

$$-\frac{d\tau_{yx}}{dy} + \rho g = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute Newton's law of viscosity** ($\tau_{yx} = -\mu\, dv_x/dy$):

$$\mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = -\rho g$$

**Step 4: Integrate twice.**

$$v_x(y) = -\frac{\rho g}{2\mu} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 5: Apply BC #1 (no-slip at wall, $y=0$):**

$$v_x(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$$

**Step 6: Apply BC #2 (zero shear at free surface, $y=\delta$):**

$$\frac{dv_x}{dy}\bigg\vert_{\delta} = 0 \implies -\frac{\rho g}{\mu} \delta + C_1 = 0 \implies C_1 = \frac{\rho g \delta}{\mu}$$

**Step 7: Final velocity profile.**

$$\boxed{v_x(y) = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu}\left(2\delta y - y^2\right)}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Shell balance (1.5) → ODE (1) → Integration (1) → BCs stated and applied (2.5) → Final profile (1).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

For a two-layer falling film, define Fluid I ($0 \le y \le \delta_1$, viscosity $\mu_1$) and Fluid II ($\delta_1 \le y \le \delta_1 + \delta_2$, viscosity $\mu_2$). The two coupled second-order ODEs have **four** constants of integration, requiring **four** BCs.

| BC # | Location | Statement | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BC 1** | $y = 0$ (lower wall) | $v_{x,I} = 0$ | No-slip at rigid, impermeable wall |
| **BC 2** | $y = \delta_1 + \delta_2$ (top free surface) | $\dfrac{dv_{x,II}}{dy} = 0$ | Zero shear (negligible air viscosity) |
| **BC 3** | $y = \delta_1$ (interface) | $v_{x,I} = v_{x,II}$ | Velocity continuity (no slip between two liquids) |
| **BC 4** | $y = \delta_1$ (interface) | $\mu_1 \dfrac{dv_{x,I}}{dy} = \mu_2 \dfrac{dv_{x,II}}{dy}$ | Shear stress continuity (momentum flux balance) |

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Two fluid ODE identification (1.5) + BC 1 statement + justification (1) + BC 2 (1) + BC 3 (1.5) + BC 4 (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the velocity profile for steady, laminar, Newtonian flow between two infinite parallel plates separated by a distance $2b$ under a constant pressure gradient $dP/dx$. State **all** boundary conditions used and verify the symmetry argument. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **how and why** the boundary conditions change when (i) the upper plate moves with a constant velocity $V$ in the $x$-direction (Couette flow), and (ii) a thin immiscible oil film of viscosity $\mu_o$ is introduced at the wall. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1: Shell balance on a differential slab.**

For fully developed flow, the $x$-momentum balance on a shell of thickness $\Delta y$ gives:

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx}\Delta y + \left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y} - \left.\tau_{yx}\right\vert_{y+\Delta y}$$

Dividing by $\Delta y$ and taking the limit:

$$\frac{d\tau_{yx}}{dy} = \frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Newton's law.**

$$\mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 3: Integrate twice.**

$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 4: Apply two BCs.**

- **BC #1 (no-slip at lower plate, $y = -b$):** $v_x(-b) = 0$
- **BC #2 (no-slip at upper plate, $y = +b$):** $v_x(+b) = 0$

**Step 5: Solve.**

Subtracting the two BC equations: $C_1 = 0$. Adding: $C_2 = -\dfrac{b^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$.

**Step 6: Final profile.**

$$\boxed{v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(y^2 - b^2\right)}$$

**Symmetry verification:** The profile is an even function of $y$ (depends on $y^2$), so $dv_x/dy\big\vert_{y=0} = 0$. This recovers the symmetry BC automatically.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Shell balance (1) + ODE (1) + Integration (1) + BC statement and application (2.5) + Symmetry check (1.5).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Couette Flow — upper plate moving with velocity $V$:**

The shell balance and ODE are identical. However, the BCs change:

| Boundary | Statement | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| At lower plate ($y = 0$) | $v_x = 0$ | Stationary wall, no-slip |
| At upper plate ($y = b$) | $v_x = V$ | Moving wall, fluid adheres |

The solution becomes the superposition of a linear Couette profile and a parabolic Poiseuille profile:

$$v_x(y) = \frac{V y}{b} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(y^2 - by\right)$$

**(ii) Thin oil film at the wall:**

The presence of an immiscible oil film introduces an **interface** at the wall, replacing the no-slip BC with **two new BCs**:

| BC | Location | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity continuity | Oil–fluid interface | $v_{x,\text{oil}} = v_{x,\text{fluid}}$ |
| Shear continuity | Oil–fluid interface | $\mu_o \dfrac{dv_{x,\text{oil}}}{dy} = \mu \dfrac{dv_{x,\text{fluid}}}{dy}$ |

This **slip condition** is the physical basis of **lubricant drag reduction** in pipelines — a high-payoff concept in petrochemical engineering.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):** (i) Couette BC change and superposition (3) + (ii) Interface BCs and engineering significance (4).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Dirichlet and Neumann BCs:** Students often write $v_x = 0$ at a free surface — this is **wrong**. Free surfaces require a derivative BC, not a value BC.
> 2. **Forgetting the impermeability condition:** When using cylindrical coordinates (pipe flow), many students neglect $v_r = 0$ at the wall in addition to the tangential no-slip.
> 3. **Sign errors in shear continuity:** At a liquid–liquid interface, the stress continuity is $\mu_I\, dv_{x,I}/dy = \mu_{II}\, dv_{x,II}/dy$ — students often write an erroneous minus sign.
> 4. **Wrong count of BCs:** For a two-fluid problem, students sometimes apply only two BCs to a fourth-order system. The correct count is **four**.
> 5. **Forgetting the symmetry BC for fully developed outlet:** When the problem statement says "fully developed," students must explicitly note that $\partial v_x/\partial x = 0$ at the outlet.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **shell momentum balance** is the integral method of solving momentum transport by applying Newton's second law to a thin fluid element.
- The number of required boundary conditions **equals the order of the governing ODE** (typically 2 for Newtonian shell balances).
- The **five canonical BC categories** are: solid surface, free surface, fluid–fluid interface, symmetry, and inlet/outlet/infinity.
- **No-slip BC** ($v_tangential = V_{\text{wall}}$) is a **Dirichlet (value)** condition rooted in the continuum hypothesis.
- **Zero-shear BC** at a free surface ($\mu\, dv/dy = 0$) is a **Neumann (derivative)** condition arising from negligible gas-phase viscosity.
- **Symmetry BC** ($dv_x/dy = 0$ at centerline) is the most elegant and computationally efficient BC for plane Poiseuille, Hagen–Poiseuille, and concentric-cylinder flows.
- At a **liquid–liquid interface**, **two BCs apply simultaneously**: velocity continuity (kinematic) and shear continuity (dynamic).
- At the **inlet**, use a prescribed velocity profile; at the **outlet** of a fully developed flow, use $\partial v_x/\partial x = 0$.
- For **external flow** over a flat plate (Blasius-type), use the free-stream condition $v_x \to V_\infty$ as $y \to \infty$.
- The shell balance method gives a **second-order ODE** that can be solved only after the correct number of independent BCs is applied — examiners test this rigorous counting habit heavily.
- **Dimensional verification** of the final velocity profile is a hallmark of a high-scoring KTU answer — always include a unit check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of shell balance to simple flow systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application of shell balance to simple flow systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **shell balance** in momentum transport is a differential control-volume approach in which a thin fluid shell (a slab, cylindrical sleeve, or annular ring) is imagined within a flowing fluid, and Newton's second law is applied to the net rate of momentum accumulation, influx, efflux, and the sum of forces (pressure, gravity, viscous, and surface) acting on the shell. By letting the shell thickness shrink to the differential limit ($\Delta x, \Delta r, \Delta y \to 0$), the integral momentum balance is converted into an **ordinary differential equation (ODE)** for the velocity profile. The boundary conditions (no-slip at walls, symmetry at the centerline, zero shear at free surfaces) are then applied to obtain the closed-form velocity distribution for simple geometries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Application of shell balance to the derivation of velocity profiles for:
> 1. Flow of a falling film
> 2. Flow through a circular tube (Hagen–Poiseuille flow)
> 3. Flow through an annular space
> 4. Flow between parallel plates
> 5. Adjacent flow of two immiscible fluids

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pouring honey down the inside of a wide glass tube. The honey touching the glass wall sticks to it (no-slip), while the honey in the middle flows faster. Now imagine slicing the tube horizontally into ten ultra-thin rings. Each ring has honey entering from above and leaving below, and the walls of each ring "drag" against the neighbouring rings. The **shell balance** is simply Newton's bookkeeping ledger applied to a single one of these rings: count the momentum entering, count the momentum leaving, account for gravity and pressure pushing it, and balance the books. When you make the ring infinitesimally thin, the bookkeeping turns into calculus, and out pops the famous parabolic (or half-parabolic) velocity profile.

- **Key physical constants and standards used in this module:**
  - Fluid density: $\rho$ in **kg/m³**
  - Dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ in **Pa·s** or **kg/(m·s)**
  - Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = \mu / \rho$ in **m²/s**
  - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
  - Shear stress: $\tau_{yx}$ in **Pa** or **N/m²**
  - Pressure: $P$ in **Pa** or **N/m²**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Shell"?** A *shell* is a hollow, bounded region of fluid (a slab, a tube-within-a-tube, or a ring). The word emphasises that we are not balancing the whole fluid, just a thin envelope of it, then taking the limit as its thickness goes to zero.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generalised Shell Balance Strategy
The five-step recipe that examiners expect every KTU student to write down at the start of any solution:

1. **Draw the geometry** and identify the direction of flow (axis of motion, $z$ or $x$).
2. **Choose a thin shell** of thickness $\Delta x$ (Cartesian), $\Delta r$ (cylindrical), or $\Delta y$ (between parallel plates).
3. **Write the integral momentum balance** for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow:
   $$\sum F_z = \int_{\text{shell}} \rho v_z (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA$$
4. **Identify all forces** — pressure forces on the two end faces, gravity (body force), and viscous shear on the side faces.
5. **Let the shell thickness approach zero** to obtain the ODE; integrate; apply **boundary conditions**.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Boundary Conditions You Must Memorise
| Boundary Type | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Solid wall (no-slip) | $v_z = 0$ at the wall | Fluid velocity equals wall velocity |
| Free surface / gas interface | $\tau = -\mu \, \dfrac{dv_z}{dy}\bigg\vert_{\text{surface}} = 0$ | No shear stress from the gas side |
| Centreline symmetry | $\dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0} = 0$ | Velocity is maximum; profile is mirror-symmetric |
| Continuity at fluid–fluid interface | $v_z^{(1)} = v_z^{(2)}$ and $\tau^{(1)} = \tau^{(2)}$ | No slip and stress equilibrium between the two fluids |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the single most important compilation you will need for Module 2 numerical problems. All symbols are KTU-board standard; use $\vert$ for "evaluated at" and never the ASCII pipe $\vert$ inside the table column.

| # | System | Velocity Profile $v_z(x, r, y)$ | Max Velocity $v_{\max}$ | Mean Velocity $\langle v_z \rangle$ | Volumetric Flow Rate $Q$ | Wall Shear Stress $\tau_w$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Falling film (thickness $\delta$, $x \downarrow$) | $v_z(x) = \dfrac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta x - \dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right)$ | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}$ at $x = \delta$ | $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu}$ | $Q = \dfrac{\rho g W \delta^{3}}{3\mu}$ | $\tau_w = \rho g \delta$ |
| 2 | Hagen–Poiseuille (circular tube, radius $R$) | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$ | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4\mu L}$ at $r = 0$ | $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{8\mu L}$ | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8\mu L}$ | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P R}{2 L}$ |
| 3 | Plane Poiseuille (parallel plates, gap $2b$) | $v_z(y) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)$ | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P b^{2}}{2\mu L}$ at $y = 0$ | $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{\Delta P b^{2}}{3\mu L}$ | $Q = \dfrac{2 \Delta P b^{3} W}{3\mu L}$ | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P b}{L}$ |
| 4 | Couette flow (one moving plate, gap $b$, upper plate at $V$) | $v_x(y) = \dfrac{V y}{b}$ | $v_{\max} = V$ at $y = b$ | $\langle v_x \rangle = \dfrac{V}{2}$ | $Q = \dfrac{V b W}{2}$ | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\mu V}{b}$ |
| 5 | Annular Poiseuille (inner $R_i$, outer $R_o$) | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\!\left[R_o^{2} - r^{2} - \dfrac{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \ln(R_o/r)\right]$ | — | — | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{8\mu L}\!\left[R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} - \dfrac{(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}\right]$ | $\tau_{w,o} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2L}\!\left[R_o - \dfrac{R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}}{2R_o \ln(R_o/R_i)}\right]$ |
| 6 | Adjacent immiscible fluids in a tube (radii $R_1$ for fluid 1, $R_2$ for fluid 2, $R_2 \le R_1$) | see derivation in §3.5 | — | — | — | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Falling film** → condensers in thermal power plants, falling-film evaporators in the dairy and pharmaceutical industry, wetted-wall absorbers for CO₂ capture.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille** → design of intravenous cannulae, capillary rheometers, microfluidic lab-on-chip devices, water-supply pipelines (laminar regime).
- **Plane Poiseuille / Couette** → slider bearings, air-lubricated tracks, viscometer calibration, polymer extrusion dies.
- **Annular flow** → concentric-rod heat exchangers, coaxial oil pipelines, piston-cylinder lubrication films.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner pattern:** KTU Part B questions almost always combine *derivation of velocity profile* (7 marks) with *computation of mean velocity, flow rate, or shear stress* (7 marks). Practise substituting the boundary conditions *before* integrating the differential equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flow of a Falling Film (Steady, Laminar, Incompressible)
**System:** A liquid film of constant thickness $\delta$ and width $W$ flows down a vertical wall under gravity. Flow is steady, fully-developed, and laminar. The free surface is at $x = 0$ and the wall is at $x = \delta$. Velocity is in the $z$-direction (downward) and depends only on $x$.

**Step 1 — Choose the shell:** A thin slab between $x$ and $x + \Delta x$, with cross-section $W \cdot \Delta x$ and length $L$ along $z$.

**Step 2 — Forces on the shell:**

- *Pressure forces:* Both ends of the shell are open to atmosphere; the pressure is constant along the film, so $P_1 = P_2 = P_{\text{atm}}$ and the net pressure force is **zero**.
- *Gravity (body force):* The weight of the fluid in the shell is
  $$F_{\text{grav}} = \rho \, g \, (\Delta x \, W \, L)$$
  acting in the $+z$ direction.
- *Viscous shear forces:* On the inner face (at $x$), the fluid above drags the shell downward; on the outer face (at $x + \Delta x$), the fluid below resists. Using the Newtonian definition,
  $$\tau_{zx}\bigg\vert_{x} = \mu \frac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x}, \qquad \tau_{zx}\bigg\vert_{x+\Delta x} = \mu \frac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x+\Delta x}$$
  Multiplying by the area $W L$ gives the net viscous force.

**Step 3 — Integral momentum balance (steady state, $z$-component):**
$$0 = (\rho g)(\Delta x \, W L) + (W L)\!\left[\tau_{zx}\big\vert_{x} - \tau_{zx}\big\vert_{x+\Delta x}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $W L$ and rearrange:**
$$\frac{\tau_{zx}\big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - \tau_{zx}\big\vert_{x}}{\Delta x} = -\rho g$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$** (this is the heart of the shell balance — turning finite difference into a derivative):
$$\frac{d\tau_{zx}}{dx} = -\rho g$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Newton's law of viscosity** $\tau_{zx} = \mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dx}$:
$$\mu \frac{d^{2}v_z}{dx^{2}} = -\rho g \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2}v_z}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu}$$

**Step 7 — Integrate once:**
$$\frac{dv_z}{dx} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu} x + C_1$$

**Step 8 — Boundary condition (BC-1):** At the free surface $x = 0$, the shear stress vanishes (gas cannot sustain a tangential stress):
$$\tau_{zx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = \mu \frac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_1 = 0$$

**Step 9 — Integrate again:**
$$v_z(x) = -\frac{\rho g}{2\mu} x^{2} + C_2$$

**Step 10 — Boundary condition (BC-2):** At the wall $x = \delta$, the no-slip condition gives $v_z = 0$:
$$0 = -\frac{\rho g}{2\mu} \delta^{2} + C_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}$$

**Step 11 — Final velocity profile:**
$$\boxed{\,v_z(x) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta x - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)\,}$$

**Step 12 — Derived quantities:**
- Maximum velocity (at $x = \delta$): $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}$
- Mean velocity (integration over the cross-section, divide by $\delta$):
$$\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{1}{\delta}\int_{0}^{\delta} v_z(x)\,dx = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu} = \tfrac{2}{3}\,v_{\max}$$
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = W \delta \langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{\rho g W \delta^{3}}{3\mu}$
- Wall shear stress (at $x = \delta$): $\tau_w = \mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=\delta} = \rho g \delta$

> [!NOTE]
> The mean velocity is exactly **two-thirds** of the maximum. This $\tfrac{2}{3}$ ratio is a favourite KTU *Part A* quiz question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hagen–Poiseuille Flow (Flow Through a Circular Tube)
**System:** Steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal tube of radius $R$ and length $L$, driven by a pressure drop $\Delta P = P_0 - P_L$.

**Step 1 — Choose the shell:** A cylindrical sleeve of radius $r$, thickness $\Delta r$, and length $L$.

**Step 2 — Forces on the shell:**
- Pressure force on the upstream face (at $z = 0$): $P_0 \cdot (2\pi r \, \Delta r)$
- Pressure force on the downstream face (at $z = L$): $P_L \cdot (2\pi r \, \Delta r)$
- Viscous force on the inner curved surface (at $r$): the fluid at smaller $r$ shears the shell; the force is
$$F_{\text{visc,in}} = \tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r} \cdot (2\pi r L)$$
- Viscous force on the outer curved surface (at $r + \Delta r$):
$$F_{\text{visc,out}} = \tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r+\Delta r} \cdot \big[2\pi (r+\Delta r) L\big]$$
- Gravity acts horizontally; for a horizontal tube its $z$-component is zero.

**Step 3 — Momentum balance (steady, $z$-direction):**
$$0 = (2\pi r \, \Delta r)(P_0 - P_L) + (2\pi r L)\,\tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r} - (2\pi (r+\Delta r) L)\,\tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r+\Delta r}$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $2\pi L \, \Delta r$ and let $\Delta r \to 0$:**
$$\lim_{\Delta r \to 0} \left[ \frac{r \, (P_0 - P_L)}{L} - \frac{(r+\Delta r)\,\tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r+\Delta r} - r\,\tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r}}{\Delta r} \right] = 0$$

Expanding the difference quotient (product rule):
$$\frac{d(r \tau_{rz})}{dr} = \frac{r (P_0 - P_L)}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate once with respect to $r$:**
$$r \, \tau_{rz} = \frac{(P_0 - P_L)}{2L} r^{2} + C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau_{rz} = \frac{(P_0 - P_L)}{2L} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 6 — Boundary condition (centreline):** At $r = 0$ the velocity must remain finite; the term $C_1/r$ would blow up unless $C_1 = 0$.
$$\tau_{rz} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2L} r \quad \text{where } \Delta P = P_L - P_0 \text{ is the pressure drop}$$

**Step 7 — Newton's law of viscosity** in cylindrical coordinates (for $v_z = v_z(r)$):
$$\tau_{rz} = \mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$$
$$\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2L} r \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} r$$

**Step 8 — Integrate:**
$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} r^{2} + C_2$$

**Step 9 — No-slip at the wall** $r = R$, $v_z = 0$:
$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} R^{2} + C_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 10 — Final velocity profile (Hagen–Poiseuille parabola):**
$$\boxed{\,v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\,}$$

**Step 11 — Derived quantities:**
- $v_{\max}$ at $r = 0$: $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4\mu L}$
- Mean velocity: $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\pi R^{2}}\int_{0}^{R} v_z(r) (2\pi r) dr = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{8\mu L} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{\max}$
- Volumetric flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille equation):
$$Q = \pi R^{2} \langle v_z \rangle = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8\mu L}$$
- Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = \mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R} = -\dfrac{\Delta P R}{2L}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fourth-power dependence $Q \propto R^{4}$ is the most famous result in micro-fluidics. Doubling the tube radius increases the flow rate by **sixteen** times. This is why a single clogged artery branch can drastically reduce organ perfusion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flow Between Parallel Plates (Plane Poiseuille Flow)
**System:** Two horizontal infinite parallel plates separated by a gap $2b$. The pressure drops by $\Delta P$ over a length $L$. Coordinate $y$ is measured from the centre plane, with walls at $y = \pm b$.

**Step 1 — Shell:** A thin rectangular slab from $y$ to $y + \Delta y$, depth $W$ (perpendicular to flow), length $L$.

**Step 2 — Forces on the shell:**
- Pressure force: $(P_0 - P_L)(W \Delta y) = \Delta P (W \Delta y)$ (opposes the flow)
- Viscous force on the upper face: $\tau_{yz}\big\vert_{y+\Delta y} (W L)$
- Viscous force on the lower face: $-\tau_{yz}\big\vert_{y} (W L)$

**Step 3 — Momentum balance (steady):**
$$0 = \Delta P (W \Delta y) + (W L)\!\left[\tau_{yz}\big\vert_{y} - \tau_{yz}\big\vert_{y+\Delta y}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $W \Delta y$ and let $\Delta y \to 0$:**
$$0 = \Delta P + L \frac{d\tau_{yz}}{dy} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d\tau_{yz}}{dy} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate:** $\tau_{yz} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} y + C_1$

**Step 6 — Boundary condition (symmetry at centreline):** $\dfrac{dv_z}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=0} = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$

$$\tau_{yz} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L} y$$

**Step 7 — Newton's law of viscosity:** $\mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dy} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} y$

$$v_z(y) = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} y^{2} + C_2$$

**Step 8 — No-slip at $y = b$:** $0 = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} b^{2} + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P b^{2}}{2\mu L}$

**Step 9 — Velocity profile:**
$$\boxed{\,v_z(y) = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)\,}$$

- $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P b^{2}}{2\mu L}$ at $y = 0$
- $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{2}{3}\,v_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P b^{2}}{3\mu L}$
- $Q = \langle v_z \rangle (2b W) = \dfrac{2 \Delta P b^{3} W}{3\mu L}$
- Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = -\mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=b} = \dfrac{\Delta P b}{L}$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Couette Flow (Drag-Induced, No Pressure Gradient)
**System:** Fluid confined between two infinite parallel plates; lower plate stationary ($v_x = 0$ at $y = 0$), upper plate moving at constant velocity $V$ ($v_x = V$ at $y = b$). No pressure gradient.

**Step 1 — Shell balance:** Identical setup to §3.3 but with $\Delta P = 0$.

**Step 2 — Resulting ODE:** $\dfrac{d\tau_{yx}}{dy} = 0 \Rightarrow \tau_{yx} = \text{constant} = \tau$

**Step 3 — Newton's law of viscosity:** $\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = \tau \Rightarrow v_x(y) = \dfrac{\tau}{\mu} y + C_2$

**Step 4 — Boundary conditions:** $v_x(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$; $v_x(b) = V \Rightarrow \tau = \mu V / b$.

**Step 5 — Velocity profile (linear):**
$$\boxed{\,v_x(y) = V \frac{y}{b}\,}$$

- $v_{\max} = V$ at $y = b$
- $\langle v_x \rangle = V / 2$
- Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = \mu V / b$ (constant throughout)

> [!TIP]
> The Couette profile is **linear**; the Poiseuille profile is **parabolic**. KTU examiners love asking the student to *contrast* the two and to identify the missing body force / pressure gradient that produces each shape.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Adjacent Flow of Two Immiscible Fluids in a Circular Tube
**System:** A circular tube of radius $R$ carries two Newtonian fluids stacked concentrically: Fluid 1 (inner core, radius $\kappa R$, viscosity $\mu_1$) and Fluid 2 (outer annulus, $\kappa R \le r \le R$, viscosity $\mu_2$). A common pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$ drives both.

**Step 1 — Shell balance in Fluid 1 ($0 \le r \le \kappa R$):** The derivation is identical to Hagen–Poiseuille (§3.2) with boundary condition of finite velocity at $r = 0$:
$$v_{z,1}(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu_1 L}\!\left[(\kappa R)^{2} - r^{2}\right] + v_{z,i}$$
where $v_{z,i}$ is the velocity at the interface (to be determined).

**Step 2 — Shell balance in Fluid 2 ($\kappa R \le r \le R$):** Same ODE, but with a different viscosity. The general solution satisfying $\tau_{rz}$ finite at $r = \kappa R$ is:
$$v_{z,2}(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu_2 L}\!\left[R^{2} - r^{2}\right] + A \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{R}\right) + v_{z,w}$$
where $A$ is determined from interface matching and $v_{z,w}$ is the wall velocity (zero for fixed wall).

**Step 3 — Matching conditions at the interface** $r = \kappa R$:
- *No-slip:* $v_{z,1}(\kappa R) = v_{z,2}(\kappa R)$
- *Continuity of shear stress:* $\mu_1 \dfrac{dv_{z,1}}{dr}\bigg\vert_{\kappa R} = \mu_2 \dfrac{dv_{z,2}}{dr}\bigg\vert_{\kappa R}$

**Step 4 — Apply no-slip at the wall** $r = R$: $v_{z,2}(R) = 0$.

**Step 5 — Closed-form velocity profile (final, exam-ready form):**
$$\boxed{\,v_{z,2}(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu_2 L}\!\left[R^{2} - r^{2} - \frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}\!\left(\frac{R^{2} - (\kappa R)^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right) \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)\right]\,}$$

The interface velocity $v_{z,i}$ can be obtained by evaluating either expression at $r = \kappa R$. The volumetric flow rate is the integral $Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$, split at $r = \kappa R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The less-viscous fluid will form the *core* (lowest shear) and "lubricate" the more-viscous annular fluid. This is exploited in oil-recovery pipelines carrying heavy crude around a kerosene core to reduce pumping power.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What
| Driving Force | Geometry | Key Result | Memorise? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravity | Vertical film | Half-parabola, $v_{\max} = \rho g \delta^{2}/(2\mu)$ | ✅ |
| Pressure + gravity | Inclined film | Same ODE, replace $g$ with $g \cos\theta$ | ✅ |
| Pressure | Circular tube | Full parabola, $Q \propto R^{4}$ | ✅✅ |
| Pressure | Parallel plates | Full parabola, $Q \propto b^{3}$ | ✅✅ |
| Plate motion | Parallel plates | Linear Couette profile | ✅ |
| Pressure + interface | Two-fluid tube | Piecewise, log-term correction | ⚠️ if time permits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow-Chart of the Generic Shell Balance Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Identify the geometry and the direction of flow"] --> B["Step 2: Choose a thin shell of finite thickness delta_x or delta_r or delta_y"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Sketch all forces acting on the shell - Pressure, Gravity, Viscous shear, Surface"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Write the steady integral momentum balance in the flow direction"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Divide by the shell volume and take the limit delta to zero to obtain the ODE"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Substitute Newton law of viscosity to convert tau into dv/dx"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Integrate the ODE once and apply symmetry or free-surface BC"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Integrate again and apply the no-slip boundary condition at the wall"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Final velocity profile v_z of x or r or y"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Derive v_max, v_mean, Q, and tau_w for the engineering application"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Shell-Balance Toolkit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEOM["Geometry Library"]
        G1["Falling Film"]
        G2["Circular Tube"]
        G3["Parallel Plates"]
        G4["Annulus"]
        G5["Two-Fluid Tube"]
    end
    subgraph BC["Boundary Condition Engine"]
        B1["No-slip at solid wall"]
        B2["Zero shear at free surface"]
        B3["Symmetry at centreline"]
        B4["Continuity at fluid interface"]
    end
    subgraph SOLVER["Differential Solver"]
        S1["Form integral momentum balance"]
        S2["Take limit delta to zero"]
        S3["Integrate ODE twice"]
        S4["Apply BC1 and BC2"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Engineering Outputs"]
        O1["Velocity profile v_z"]
        O2["Maximum velocity v_max"]
        O3["Mean velocity v_mean"]
        O4["Volumetric flow rate Q"]
        O5["Wall shear stress tau_w"]
    end
    GEOM --> SOLVER
    BC --> SOLVER
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
    O1 --> O5
```

### 4.3 Visual Geometry–Formula Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["Falling Film: half-parabola"] --> R1["v_max at the wall, v_zero at the wall, dv/dx zero at the free surface"]
    F2["Hagen-Poiseuille: full parabola"] --> R2["v_max at the centre, v_zero at the wall, dv/dr zero at the centre"]
    F3["Plane Poiseuille: full parabola"] --> R3["v_max at the midplane, v_zero at both walls"]
    F4["Couette: straight line"] --> R4["v_zero at the stationary wall, v_V at the moving wall"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-to-equation quick reference:**
> - Half-parabola → one non-zero boundary condition + one zero-stress condition.
> - Full parabola → two no-slip conditions (or one no-slip + one symmetry).
> - Straight line → one no-slip at fixed wall, one no-slip at moving wall, no pressure gradient.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. State and justify the two boundary conditions used to obtain the velocity profile of a falling liquid film on a vertical wall.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The two boundary conditions are:
1. **Zero shear stress at the free surface** ($x = 0$): The adjacent gas phase cannot sustain any tangential stress, hence
$$\tau_{zx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = \mu \frac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$$
2. **No-slip at the solid wall** ($x = \delta$): The fluid in immediate contact with the stationary wall must have the same velocity as the wall,
$$v_z\big\vert_{x=\delta} = 0$$
These two conditions allow evaluation of the two integration constants arising from the second-order ODE $\mu \dfrac{d^{2}v_z}{dx^{2}} = -\rho g$.

---

**Q2. The Hagen–Poiseuille equation shows that $Q \propto R^{4}$. Explain the engineering significance of this fourth-power dependence.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The fourth-power dependence means the volumetric flow rate is extraordinarily sensitive to the tube radius. A small change in $R$ produces a disproportionately large change in $Q$. Engineering consequences:
- In **microfluidic devices** (lab-on-chip), even slight clogging of a channel drastically reduces throughput, which is why channels are sized carefully and filters are used upstream.
- In **medical devices**, a 19 % narrowing of an artery (a 19 % reduction in radius) reduces blood flow by ~55 %, illustrating why stenoses are clinically serious.
- In **pipeline design**, doubling the diameter reduces pumping power by a factor of sixteen for the same mass throughput, a key factor in long-distance crude-oil and natural-gas pipelines.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Falling Film + Capillary Flow Combination

**(a)** Starting from a momentum shell balance, derive the velocity profile for the steady, laminar, gravity-driven flow of a Newtonian liquid film of thickness $\delta$ down a vertical wall. Hence obtain the maximum velocity, mean velocity, volumetric flow rate per unit width, and the wall shear stress.
*(7 marks)*

**(b)** A capillary tube of radius $0.5$ mm and length $0.20$ m carries water ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) under a pressure difference of $20$ kPa. Compute (i) the maximum velocity, (ii) the mean velocity, (iii) the volumetric flow rate, and (iv) the wall shear stress.
*(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to (a)
1. Choose a shell between $x$ and $x + \Delta x$; the flow is in the $+z$ direction, downward.
2. Forces: gravity = $\rho g W L \Delta x$; pressure forces = 0 (atmospheric on both ends); viscous forces from shear on the two faces.
3. Steady momentum balance yields $\dfrac{d\tau_{zx}}{dx} = -\rho g$.
4. Substitute $\tau_{zx} = \mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dx}$ → $\mu \dfrac{d^{2}v_z}{dx^{2}} = -\rho g$.
5. Integrate once: $\dfrac{dv_z}{dx} = -\dfrac{\rho g}{\mu} x + C_1$; BC-1 at $x = 0$ (free surface): $\tau_{zx} = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.
6. Integrate again: $v_z = -\dfrac{\rho g}{2\mu} x^{2} + C_2$; BC-2 at $x = \delta$ (wall): $v_z = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}$.
7. **Velocity profile:** $v_z(x) = \dfrac{\rho g}{\mu}\!\left(\delta x - \dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right)$.
8. **Maximum velocity** at $x = \delta$: $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}$.
9. **Mean velocity:** $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\delta}\int_{0}^{\delta} v_z \, dx = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu} = \tfrac{2}{3} v_{\max}$.
10. **Volumetric flow rate per unit width:** $q = \delta \langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{\rho g \delta^{3}}{3\mu}$.
11. **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=\delta} = \rho g \delta$.

##### Model Solution to (b)
Given: $R = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m; $L = 0.20$ m; $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; $\Delta P = 20 \text{ kPa} = 2.0 \times 10^{4}$ Pa.

(i) **Maximum velocity:**
$$v_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4\mu L} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{4})(5.0 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{4(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(0.20)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{4} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.25 \text{ m/s}$$

(ii) **Mean velocity:** $\langle v_z \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{\max} = 3.125$ m/s.

(iii) **Volumetric flow rate:**
$$Q = \pi R^{2} \langle v_z \rangle = \pi (5.0 \times 10^{-4})^{2}(3.125) = \pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.125 = 2.45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

(iv) **Wall shear stress:**
$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{4})(5.0 \times 10^{-4})}{2(0.20)} = \frac{10}{0.4} = 25 \text{ Pa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Falling-Film & Hagen–Poiseuille Problems:**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Applying the no-slip condition at the *free surface* instead of the wall. Remember: free surface = zero **shear**; wall = zero **velocity**.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the pressure difference $\Delta P$ in Hagen–Poiseuille is $P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}$; sign errors propagate and make $v_z$ negative.
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Writing $v_{\max} = \rho g \delta^{2}/\mu$ instead of the correct $\rho g \delta^{2}/(2\mu)$. Always re-derive at $x = \delta$.
> - **Common mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Unit mismatch — converting $\mu$ from cP to Pa·s ($\mu_{\text{cP}} \times 10^{-3} = \mu_{\text{Pa·s}}$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Couette + Plane Poiseuille Alternative

**(a)** Two large parallel plates are separated by a gap of $5$ mm. The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate moves at $1.0$ m/s in the $x$-direction. The space between the plates is filled with oil of viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s. There is no applied pressure gradient. Using a momentum shell balance, derive the velocity profile and compute the shear stress on the lower plate and the force required to drag a $0.5$ m × $0.5$ m section of the upper plate.
*(7 marks)*

**(b)** A horizontal slit of half-gap $b = 2.0$ mm, width $W = 0.10$ m, and length $L = 1.0$ m is used to deliver a polymer melt ($\mu = 50$ Pa·s) under a pressure drop of $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. Using a shell balance, derive the velocity profile and compute the volumetric flow rate.
*(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to (a)
1. Choose a shell between $y$ and $y + \Delta y$ in the gap; flow is in the $x$-direction.
2. Pressure forces = 0 (no $\Delta P$). Only viscous forces act: $\tau_{yx}\big\vert_{y+\Delta y} - \tau_{yx}\big\vert_{y}$ (force per unit area).
3. Momentum balance: $\dfrac{d\tau_{yx}}{dy} = 0 \Rightarrow \tau_{yx} = \text{constant} = \tau$.
4. Newton's law: $\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = \tau \Rightarrow v_x(y) = \dfrac{\tau}{\mu} y + C_2$.
5. Boundary conditions: $v_x(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$; $v_x(b) = V \Rightarrow \tau = \mu V / b$.
6. **Velocity profile:** $v_x(y) = V y / b = \dfrac{(1.0) y}{0.005} = 200\,y$ m/s (with $y$ in m).
7. **Shear stress on lower plate:** $\tau = \mu V / b = (0.1)(1.0)/(0.005) = 20$ Pa.
8. **Force on upper plate section:** $F = \tau \cdot A = 20 \times (0.5)(0.5) = 5.0$ N.

##### Model Solution to (b)
1. Shell between $y$ and $y + \Delta y$; flow in $z$-direction.
2. Pressure forces: $\Delta P \cdot (W \Delta y)$; viscous forces on the two horizontal faces.
3. Momentum balance: $\dfrac{d\tau_{yz}}{dy} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$.
4. Integrate: $\tau_{yz} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} y + C_1$. Symmetry at $y = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$.
5. Newton's law: $\mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dy} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} y \Rightarrow v_z(y) = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} y^{2} + C_2$.
6. No-slip at $y = b$: $C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P b^{2}}{2\mu L}$.
7. **Velocity profile:** $v_z(y) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)$.
8. **Volumetric flow rate:**
$$Q = \int_{-b}^{b} v_z(y) W\,dy = W \int_{-b}^{b} \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\!\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right) dy = \frac{2 \Delta P b^{3} W}{3\mu L}$$
9. Substitute numbers:
$$Q = \frac{2(5 \times 10^{5})(2.0 \times 10^{-3})^{3}(0.10)}{3(50)(1.0)} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{5} \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.10}{150} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}{150} = 5.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Couette & Plane Poiseuille:**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Mixing up *drag-induced Couette* with *pressure-driven plane Poiseuille*. In Couette, $\Delta P = 0$ and the profile is **linear**; in plane Poiseuille the profile is **parabolic**.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting that plane Poiseuille uses $2b$ as the *total* gap; many students mistakenly substitute $b$ in the area calculation. The area is $2bW$.
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Writing the wall shear stress as $\tau_w = \mu V / b$ for Couette is correct, but for plane Poiseuille it is $\tau_w = \Delta P b / L$, not $\mu V / b$.
> - **Common mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Mixing the two boundary conditions — for Couette they are *both* no-slip (one stationary, one moving); for plane Poiseuille they are *two* no-slip walls or one no-slip + one symmetry.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for "Application of Shell Balance to Simple Flow Systems":**
>
> ✅ **Shell balance = finite control volume + limit Δ → 0** that converts integral momentum balance into an ODE.
> ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram** of the shell showing pressure, gravity, and shear forces *before* writing any equation.
> ✅ **Newton's law of viscosity** ($\tau = \mu \, dv/dy$) is the constitutive equation that closes the momentum balance.
> ✅ **Falling film → half-parabola** with $v_{\max}$ at the wall, zero shear at the free surface.
> ✅ **Hagen–Poiseuille → full parabola** with $v_{\max}$ at the centreline; $Q \propto R^{4}$.
> ✅ **Plane Poiseuille → full parabola** between two parallel plates; $Q \propto b^{3}$.
> ✅ **Couette → linear profile** when one plate drags the fluid; no pressure gradient.
> ✅ **Two-fluid annular flow** has a piecewise profile with a logarithmic correction term to match shear stress at the interface.
> ✅ **Memorise the universal ratios**: $\langle v \rangle / v_{\max} = \tfrac{2}{3}$ for parabolic profiles, $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for Hagen–Poiseuille, $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for Couette.
> ✅ **Wall shear stress formulas** (most-tested in Part A):
>     - Falling film: $\tau_w = \rho g \delta$
>     - Circular tube: $\tau_w = \Delta P R / (2L)$
>     - Parallel plates: $\tau_w = \Delta P b / L$
>     - Couette: $\tau_w = \mu V / b$
> ✅ **Sanity check**: in steady state, momentum in = momentum out + net force, so when the body force (gravity) drives the flow, the velocity grows parabolically from the free surface to the wall.
> ✅ **Engineering rule of thumb**: $Q \propto R^{4}$ (tube) and $Q \propto b^{3}$ (slit) — increasing the dimension of a channel is the most effective way to enhance throughput.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
