> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Falling film - flow of a Newtonian fluid in between two slits formed by two flat plates, flow through tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Falling Film Problem (Gravity-Driven Film Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *falling film* refers to the steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a thin layer of a Newtonian liquid down a vertical (or inclined) surface under the action of **gravity alone**. The film has a free surface exposed to a gas (typically air) at essentially constant pressure, and the only driving force for momentum transport is the gravitational body force. The flow is one-dimensional in the direction of gravity.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Honey on a Vertical Wall"

Imagine pouring a thin stream of honey onto a smooth vertical glass wall. Instead of running off in irregular rivulets, the honey forms a remarkably **uniform sheet** of constant thickness. The honey close to the wall is **stuck** (no-slip), while the honey at the outer surface is **free to move** (zero shear). The result is a **parabolic velocity profile**, with the fastest moving layer at the free surface.

This is exactly the **falling-film configuration**: gravity pulls the fluid downward, viscous drag (μ) resists the motion, and the balance yields a quadratic velocity distribution. The same physics governs **condensate drainage on cooling tubes**, **wetted-wall absorbers**, **rain on window panes**, and **lava flow on volcano slopes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (in bold):**
> - Film thickness: **δ (m)** — typically 0.1 mm to a few mm.
> - Kinematic viscosity: **ν = μ/ρ (m²/s)**.
> - Volumetric flow rate per unit width: **Γ = Q/W (m²/s)**.
> - Reynolds number for film: **Re_δ = 4Γ/ν** (laminar if < 1200).

---

## 1.2 Flow Between Two Parallel Plates (Slit / Plane Poiseuille Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Slit flow* is the steady, laminar, fully-developed, pressure-driven flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid confined between two infinite parallel flat plates separated by a small gap (**2b** or **δ**). The flow is driven by an imposed pressure gradient along the plates, and the no-slip condition holds at both walls.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Squeeze a Toothpaste Tube"

Squeeze a flat tube of toothpaste by sliding the finger along its length. The toothpaste moves fastest at the center of the gap and is **stuck to both flat surfaces**. The pressure you apply drives the flow, and the result is a **parabolic velocity profile**, exactly analogous to fluid flowing through the slit between two parallel plates.

**Engineering examples:** flow in **polymer film extruders**, **heat exchanger channels**, **microfluidic chips**, and **die-forming operations**.

---

## 1.3 Flow Through a Circular Tube (Hagen–Poiseuille Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Tube flow* (Hagen–Poiseuille flow) is the steady, laminar, fully-developed, pressure-driven flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid inside a straight circular pipe of constant radius **R**. The flow is axisymmetric, driven solely by an axial pressure gradient, and obeys the no-slip condition at the wall.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Water Through a Straw"

Drink water through a thin straw. The water in the center of the straw moves fastest, while the layer in contact with the straw wall is **completely stationary**. The pressure from your mouth drives the flow, and the velocity profile is a **paraboloid of revolution** — a 3-D "bullet" shape.

**Engineering examples:** blood flow through capillaries, crude oil pipeline transport, laminar flow in process piping, and microfluidic device design.

---

## 1.4 Common Assumptions Across All Three Configurations

| # | Assumption | Physical Justification |
|---|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Newtonian fluid | Linear relation: τ = μ(du/dy) holds |
| 2 | Steady state | ∂/∂t = 0 |
| 3 | Incompressible flow | ρ = constant |
| 4 | Laminar flow | Re is below critical value |
| 5 | Fully developed flow | ∂v_x/∂x = 0 (no entrance effects) |
| 6 | Constant physical properties | μ, ρ independent of position |

> [!NOTE]
> All three flows are **viscometric** — i.e., simple shear flows where only one velocity component is non-zero, allowing direct application of the **equation of motion** in its simplest form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equation: The Equation of Motion (z-component form)

For steady, fully developed, Newtonian flow in a Cartesian frame, the x-component of the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} + \rho g_{x}$$

where $g_x$ is the body force component in the flow direction.

---

## 2.2 Falling Film — Analytical Logic Decomposition

### Step 1 — Coordinate System & Assumptions
- **x-axis:** directed downward (along the wall), in the direction of gravity.
- **y-axis:** directed outward from the wall, perpendicular to it.
- $v_y = v_z = 0$; only $v_x(y)$ is non-zero.
- $\partial p / \partial x = 0$ (free surface exposed to constant atmospheric pressure).
- Body force per unit volume in x-direction: $\rho g_x = \rho g$.

### Step 2 — Reduce the Equation of Motion
With $\partial p/\partial x = 0$, the equation collapses to:
$$\mu \frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} = -\rho g$$

### Step 3 — Boundary Conditions
1. **No-slip at wall:** $v_x = 0$ at $y = 0$.
2. **Zero shear at free surface:** $\tau_{yx} = \mu (dv_x/dy) = 0$ at $y = \delta$.

### Step 4 — Integrate Twice
The result (derived in Section 3) is:
$$v_{x}(y) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)$$

### Why This Form? (The "Why" Behind the Math)
The parabolic shape arises because **gravity adds momentum uniformly along y**, while **viscous diffusion removes momentum** with an intensity proportional to the velocity gradient. The balance is mathematically identical to steady-state heat conduction with a uniform heat source.

---

## 2.3 Flow Between Two Parallel Plates — Analytical Logic

### Setup
- Plates at $y = \pm b$, separation = **2b**.
- Pressure-driven flow, no body force (horizontal orientation).
- Fully developed: $v_x = v_x(y)$ only.

### Reduced Equation of Motion
$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}}$$

### Boundary Conditions
- $v_x = 0$ at $y = +b$ (upper plate).
- $v_x = 0$ at $y = -b$ (lower plate).

### Resulting Velocity Profile (derived in Section 3)
$$v_{x}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\left(-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 Flow Through a Tube — Analytical Logic

### Setup
- Cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$.
- Axisymmetric: $v_z = v_z(r)$ only.
- Pressure-driven: $\partial p / \partial z$ is constant.

### Reduced Equation of Motion (z-component)
$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \, \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)$$

### Boundary Conditions
- Finite velocity at center: $dv_z/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$.
- No-slip at wall: $v_z = 0$ at $r = R$.

### Resulting Velocity Profile (derived in Section 3)
$$v_{z}(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right)\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module-2 problems. Memorize every entry.

| # | Configuration | Velocity Profile $v_{max}$ | Mean Velocity $\langle v \rangle$ | Volumetric Flow Rate $Q$ | Wall Shear Stress $\tau_w$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Falling Film** (thickness δ, gravity-driven) | $\dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}$ | $\dfrac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu}$ | $\dfrac{\rho g \delta^{3} W}{3\mu}$ (width $W$) | $\rho g \delta$ |
| 2 | **Parallel Plates** (half-gap $b$, pressure-driven, $\Delta p$ over length $L$) | $\dfrac{\Delta p \, b^{2}}{2 \mu L}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta p \, b^{2}}{3 \mu L}$ | $\dfrac{2 \Delta p \, b^{3} W}{3 \mu L}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta p \, b}{2 L}$ |
| 3 | **Circular Tube** (radius $R$, pressure-driven, $\Delta p$ over length $L$) | $\dfrac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta p \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L}$ | $\dfrac{\pi \Delta p \, R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta p \, R}{2 L}$ |
| 4 | **Momentum flow rate per unit width** (falling film) | — | — | $M = \dfrac{2 \rho^{3} g^{2} \delta^{5}}{15 \mu^{2}}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal pattern:** All three flows give a parabolic (or paraboloidal) profile with $\langle v \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2} v_{max}$ **for tube flow** and $\langle v \rangle = \tfrac{2}{3} v_{max}$ **for film and slit flow**. This ratio is a high-frequency KTU viva question!

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Relevant Flow | Key Design Output |
|---|---|---|
| Wetted-wall absorption columns | Falling film | Mass-transfer area = $W \cdot L$ |
| Heat-exchanger tube banks (condensation) | Falling film on outside of tube | Heat-transfer coefficient h(Nu) |
| Polymer extrusion dies | Slit flow (die lip) | Throughput $Q$ vs. pressure drop |
| Microfluidic lab-on-a-chip channels | Slit flow | Volumetric flow rate |
| Crude oil pipelines | Tube flow | Pressure drop $\Delta p$ to pump over distance L |
| Blood flow modeling (arterioles) | Tube flow (Hagen–Poiseuille) | Wall shear stress $\tau_w$ |
| Viscosity measurement (capillary rheometer) | Tube flow | $\mu$ from measured $Q$ and $\Delta p$ |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition below is **explicitly written**. No steps are skipped. All integration constants are solved by direct substitution into the boundary conditions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Falling Film Velocity Profile

### Statement of the Problem
A Newtonian liquid of viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho$ flows as a film of thickness $\delta$ down a vertical wall under gravity $g$. Find the velocity distribution $v_x(y)$, the maximum velocity, the mean velocity, the volumetric flow rate per unit width, and the momentum flow rate per unit width.

### Starting Equation (x-component of Navier–Stokes)
For steady, incompressible, fully developed flow with $v_x = v_x(y)$ only:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} + \rho g$$

**Conversion logic:** Because the free surface of the film is exposed to a constant atmospheric pressure, the pressure is uniform along the film; hence $\partial p/\partial x = 0$.

### Reduced Equation
Substituting $\partial p/\partial x = 0$:

$$\mu \frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} = -\rho g$$

**Conversion logic:** Divide both sides by $\mu$ to isolate the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu}$$

### First Integration
Integrate once with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{dv_{x}}{dy} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu}\,y + C_{1}$$

### Apply Boundary Condition 1 (Zero Shear at Free Surface)
At $y = \delta$ (free surface), the shear stress vanishes: $\tau_{yx} = \mu (dv_x/dy) = 0$.

$$\Rightarrow \frac{dv_{x}}{dy}\bigg|_{y=\delta} = 0$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute $y = \delta$ and $dv_x/dy = 0$ into the integrated equation:

$$0 = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu}\delta + C_{1}$$

**Solve for $C_1$:**

$$C_{1} = \frac{\rho g \delta}{\mu}$$

### Substituted First-Order Result
$$\frac{dv_{x}}{dy} = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}(\delta - y)$$

### Second Integration
$$v_{x}(y) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right) + C_{2}$$

### Apply Boundary Condition 2 (No-Slip at Wall)
At $y = 0$, the velocity is zero: $v_x = 0$.

$$0 = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\!\left(0 - 0\right) + C_{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; C_{2} = 0$$

### Final Velocity Profile (THE Key Result)
$$\boxed{\,v_{x}(y) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)\,}$$

---

### Subsidiary Quantity 1 — Maximum Velocity
Set $dv_x/dy = 0$:

$$\frac{\rho g}{\mu}(\delta - y) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = \delta$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute $y = \delta$ into the velocity profile:

$$\boxed{\,v_{x,\max} = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}\,}$$

---

### Subsidiary Quantity 2 — Mean Velocity
$$\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{1}{\delta}\int_{0}^{\delta} v_{x}(y)\, dy$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute the profile and integrate:

$$\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{1}{\delta}\cdot\frac{\rho g}{\mu}\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)dy$$

$$= \frac{\rho g}{\mu\delta}\!\left[\frac{\delta y^{2}}{2} - \frac{y^{3}}{6}\right]_{0}^{\delta}$$

**Conversion logic:** Evaluate at $y = \delta$ and $y = 0$:

$$= \frac{\rho g}{\mu\delta}\!\left(\frac{\delta^{3}}{2} - \frac{\delta^{3}}{6}\right) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu\delta}\!\left(\frac{3\delta^{3} - \delta^{3}}{6}\right) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu\delta}\!\left(\frac{2\delta^{3}}{6}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu} = \frac{2}{3} v_{x,\max}\,}$$

---

### Subsidiary Quantity 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Width
$$\Gamma = \int_{0}^{\delta} v_{x}(y)\, dy = \delta \langle v_{x} \rangle$$

$$\boxed{\,\Gamma = \frac{\rho g \delta^{3}}{3\mu}\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiplied by width $W$ to obtain total volumetric flow rate: $Q = \Gamma W$.

---

### Subsidiary Quantity 4 — Momentum Flow Rate per Unit Width
$$M = \rho \int_{0}^{\delta} v_{x}^{2}\, dy = \rho \left(\frac{\rho g}{\mu}\right)^{\!2}\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)^{\!2}dy$$

**Conversion logic:** Expand the square inside the integral:

$$\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)^{2} = \delta^{2}y^{2} - \delta y^{3} + \frac{y^{4}}{4}$$

**Conversion logic:** Integrate term by term from $0$ to $\delta$:

$$\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\delta^{2}y^{2}\, dy = \frac{\delta^{5}}{3}$$

$$\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\delta y^{3}\, dy = \frac{\delta^{5}}{4}$$

$$\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\frac{y^{4}}{4}\, dy = \frac{\delta^{5}}{20}$$

**Conversion logic:** Combine with a common denominator of 60:

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} (\cdots) dy = \frac{20\delta^{5} - 15\delta^{5} + 3\delta^{5}}{60} = \frac{8\delta^{5}}{60} = \frac{2\delta^{5}}{15}$$

**Substitute back:**

$$M = \rho \cdot \frac{\rho^{2} g^{2}}{\mu^{2}} \cdot \frac{2\delta^{5}}{15}$$

$$\boxed{\,M = \frac{2\rho^{3} g^{2} \delta^{5}}{15\,\mu^{2}}\,}$$

---

### Subsidiary Quantity 5 — Wall Shear Stress
At the wall ($y = 0$), the shear stress acting on the fluid is:

$$\tau_{w} = \mu \frac{dv_{x}}{dy}\bigg|_{y=0} = \mu \cdot \frac{\rho g}{\mu}(\delta - 0)$$

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{w} = \rho g \delta\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This has an elegant physical meaning — the wall shear stress exactly balances the weight of the fluid per unit area in the film. A force balance on the entire film gives the same result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Flow Between Two Parallel Plates

### Statement of the Problem
A Newtonian fluid flows steadily between two infinite parallel plates separated by a distance $2b$ (with $y = 0$ at the midplane and $y = \pm b$ at the plates). The flow is driven by a pressure gradient $\partial p/\partial x$ in the $x$-direction. Determine the velocity profile, mean velocity, and volumetric flow rate.

### Reduced Equation of Motion
With $v_x = v_x(y)$ and no body force:

$$\frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

### First Integration
$$\frac{dv_{x}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,y + C_{1}$$

### Second Integration
$$v_{x}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,y^{2} + C_{1}y + C_{2}$$

### Apply Boundary Conditions
**BC1 (symmetry):** By the symmetry of the geometry about $y = 0$, $dv_x/dy = 0$ at $y = 0$, which gives $C_1 = 0$.

**BC2 (no-slip):** $v_x = 0$ at $y = b$:

$$0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,b^{2} + C_{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; C_{2} = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,b^{2}$$

### Final Velocity Profile
Substitute back:

$$v_{x}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\!\left(y^{2} - b^{2}\right) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\!\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)$$

**Conversion logic:** Recognize that in pressure-driven flow, $\partial p/\partial x$ is **negative** (pressure drops in flow direction). Introducing $\Delta p = P_0 - P_L > 0$ over a length $L$, we have $\partial p/\partial x = -\Delta p/L$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\,v_{x}(y) = \frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L}\!\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)\,}$$

---

### Maximum Velocity (at center, $y = 0$)
$$\boxed{\,v_{x,\max} = \frac{\Delta p\, b^{2}}{2\mu L}\,}$$

### Mean Velocity
$$\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{1}{2b}\int_{-b}^{b} v_{x}(y)\, dy$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute the profile and integrate (using even symmetry):

$$\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L\, b}\int_{-b}^{b}\!\left(b^{2} - y^{2}\right)dy = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L\, b}\!\left[b^{2}y - \frac{y^{3}}{3}\right]_{-b}^{b}$$

$$= \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L\, b}\!\left[2b^{3} - \frac{2b^{3}}{3}\right] = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L\, b}\cdot\frac{4b^{3}}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\,\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{\Delta p\, b^{2}}{3\mu L} = \frac{2}{3}v_{x,\max}\,}$$

### Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Width
$$Q_{W} = \int_{-b}^{b} v_{x}(y)\, dy = 2b\,\langle v_{x}\rangle$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{W} = \frac{2 \Delta p\, b^{3}}{3\mu L}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flow Through a Circular Tube (Hagen–Poiseuille)

### Statement of the Problem
A Newtonian fluid flows steadily through a horizontal circular tube of radius $R$ and length $L$, under an imposed pressure difference $\Delta p = P_0 - P_L$. Determine the velocity profile, mean velocity, volumetric flow rate, and wall shear stress.

### Reduced Equation of Motion (Cylindrical Coordinates, z-component)
For steady, fully developed, axisymmetric flow with $v_z = v_z(r)$:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\right)$$

**Conversion logic:** Rearrange to isolate the radial derivative:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}$$

### First Integration
Multiply both sides by $r$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,r$$

**Conversion logic:** Integrate with respect to $r$:

$$r\,\frac{dv_{z}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,r^{2} + C_{1}$$

### Second Integration
$$\frac{dv_{z}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,r + \frac{C_{1}}{r}$$

**Conversion logic:** Integrate once more:

$$v_{z}(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,r^{2} + C_{1}\ln r + C_{2}$$

### Apply Boundary Conditions
**BC1 (finite velocity at center):** The term $C_1 \ln r$ diverges as $r \to 0$ unless $C_1 = 0$. Hence:

$$C_{1} = 0$$

**BC2 (no-slip at wall):** $v_z = 0$ at $r = R$:

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,R^{2} + C_{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; C_{2} = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,R^{2}$$

### Final Velocity Profile
Substitute back:

$$v_{z}(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\!\left(r^{2} - R^{2}\right) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

**Conversion logic:** Using $\partial p/\partial z = -\Delta p/L$:

$$\boxed{\,v_{z}(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\,}$$

---

### Maximum Velocity (at center, $r = 0$)
$$\boxed{\,v_{z,\max} = \frac{\Delta p\, R^{2}}{4\mu L}\,}$$

### Mean Velocity (Averaged Over the Cross-Section)
$$\langle v_{z} \rangle = \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}}\int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r)\,(2\pi r)\, dr = \frac{2}{R^{2}}\int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r)\,r\, dr$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute the profile:

$$\langle v_{z} \rangle = \frac{2}{R^{2}}\cdot\frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\int_{0}^{R}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)r\, dr = \frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L\, R^{2}}\int_{0}^{R}\!\left(R^{2}r - r^{3}\right)dr$$

**Conversion logic:** Integrate term by term:

$$= \frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L\, R^{2}}\!\left[\frac{R^{2}r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L\, R^{2}}\!\left(\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right)$$

$$= \frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L\, R^{2}}\cdot\frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\,\langle v_{z} \rangle = \frac{\Delta p\, R^{2}}{8\mu L} = \frac{1}{2}v_{z,\max}\,}$$

### Volumetric Flow Rate (THE Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)
$$Q = \pi R^{2}\,\langle v_{z}\rangle$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p\, R^{4}}{8\mu L}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most famous equation in transport phenomena — and a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question topic. Notice the **fourth-power dependence on radius**: doubling the pipe diameter increases flow by a factor of 16!

---

### Wall Shear Stress
$$\tau_{w} = -\mu \frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = -\mu \cdot \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}(-2R) = \frac{\Delta p\, R}{2 L}$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute $\Delta p = P_0 - P_L > 0$ to confirm $\tau_w > 0$:

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{w} = \frac{\Delta p\, R}{2 L}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Falling Film | Parallel Plates | Circular Tube |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coordinate | $y$ from wall | $y$ from midplane | $r$ from center |
| Half-domain size | $\delta$ | $b$ | $R$ |
| Driving force | Gravity $\rho g$ | $-\partial p/\partial x$ | $-\partial p/\partial z$ |
| Profile shape | Parabola (1/2-quadratic) | Parabola | Paraboloid |
| $v_{max}$ location | $y = \delta$ (free surface) | $y = 0$ (midplane) | $r = 0$ (centerline) |
| Ratio $\langle v \rangle / v_{max}$ | $2/3$ | $2/3$ | $1/2$ |
| Cross-section average | $1/\delta \int \cdot dy$ | $1/(2b) \int \cdot dy$ | $1/(\pi R^2) \int \cdot 2\pi r\,dr$ |
| Symmetry | Wall at $y=0$, free at $y=\delta$ | Symmetric about $y=0$ | Axisymmetric about $z$-axis |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the assumptions made in deriving the velocity profile for the steady, fully developed flow of a falling liquid film.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The fluid is **Newtonian** (constant viscosity μ).
2. The flow is **steady** ($\partial / \partial t = 0$) and **incompressible** ($\rho$ = constant).
3. The flow is **laminar** and **fully developed** with $v_y = v_z = 0$, so only $v_x(y)$ is non-zero.
4. The pressure gradient along the flow is zero because the free surface is exposed to a constant atmospheric pressure.
5. Body force per unit volume in the flow direction = $\rho g$.

*(1 mark for stating each clearly — typically 3 bullet points earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A.2 — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Hagen–Poiseuille equation? Write its expression for volumetric flow rate through a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate of a **Newtonian fluid** through a **horizontal straight circular tube** under **steady, laminar, fully developed** conditions.

$$Q = \frac{\pi \, R^{4} \, \Delta p}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

where $R$ = tube radius, $\Delta p$ = pressure drop over length $L$, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity. The equation is used to **(a)** calculate pressure drop in pipe flow and **(b)** experimentally determine the viscosity of a Newtonian fluid (capillary viscometer).

*(1 mark for naming the equation, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for stating one application.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question B (A) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified for Module 2]**

**Derive the velocity distribution, mean velocity, maximum velocity, volumetric flow rate per unit width, and momentum flow rate per unit width for a falling film of a Newtonian liquid of thickness $\delta$ flowing down a vertical wall under gravity.**

#### (a) Velocity Profile, Mean Velocity, and Maximum Velocity — **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Set up the equation of motion (2 marks):**
With the $x$-axis along the wall (downward) and $y$-axis perpendicular to the wall, the x-component of the Navier–Stokes equation for steady, fully developed, incompressible flow simplifies to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} + \rho g$$

Since $\partial p/\partial x = 0$ (constant atmospheric pressure at the free surface), this becomes:

$$\frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate and apply boundary conditions (3 marks):**

First integration:
$$\frac{dv_{x}}{dy} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu} y + C_{1}$$

Apply BC at $y = \delta$: $dv_x/dy = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = \rho g \delta / \mu$.

Second integration:
$$v_{x} = -\frac{\rho g}{2\mu} y^{2} + \frac{\rho g \delta}{\mu} y + C_{2}$$

Apply BC at $y = 0$: $v_x = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

**Velocity profile (1 mark for final form):**

$$\boxed{\,v_{x}(y) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)\,}$$

**Maximum velocity (0.5 mark):** $v_{max} = \rho g \delta^{2}/(2\mu)$ at $y = \delta$.

**Mean velocity (0.5 mark):**

$$\langle v_{x} \rangle = \frac{1}{\delta}\int_{0}^{\delta} v_{x}\, dy = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu} = \frac{2}{3}v_{max}$$

#### (b) Volumetric Flow Rate and Momentum Flow Rate — **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate per unit width (2 marks):**

$$\Gamma = \int_{0}^{\delta} v_{x}\, dy = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)dy = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\!\left[\frac{\delta y^{2}}{2} - \frac{y^{3}}{6}\right]_{0}^{\delta}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Gamma = \frac{\rho g \delta^{3}}{3\mu}\,}$$

Total volumetric flow rate across width $W$: $Q = \Gamma W$.

**Step 2 — Set up momentum flow rate integral (2 marks):**

$$M = \rho\int_{0}^{\delta} v_{x}^{2}\, dy = \rho\left(\frac{\rho g}{\mu}\right)^{\!2}\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)^{2}dy$$

**Step 3 — Expand and integrate (2 marks):**

$$\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)^{2}dy = \int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(\delta^{2}y^{2} - \delta y^{3} + \frac{y^{4}}{4}\right)dy$$

$$= \frac{\delta^{5}}{3} - \frac{\delta^{5}}{4} + \frac{\delta^{5}}{20} = \frac{20\delta^{5} - 15\delta^{5} + 3\delta^{5}}{60} = \frac{8\delta^{5}}{60} = \frac{2\delta^{5}}{15}$$

**Final result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,M = \frac{2\rho^{3} g^{2} \delta^{5}}{15\,\mu^{2}}\,}$$

---

### Question B (B) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for Module 2]**

**A Newtonian fluid flows steadily through a horizontal circular tube of length $L$ and radius $R$ under a pressure drop $\Delta p$. Derive the velocity profile, mean velocity, volumetric flow rate, and wall shear stress. Hence show that the mean velocity is exactly half the maximum velocity.**

#### (a) Derivation of Velocity Profile, Volumetric Flow Rate, and Mean Velocity — **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Governing equation in cylindrical coordinates (2 marks):**

For steady, fully developed, axisymmetric flow with $v_z = v_z(r)$:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\right)$$

Using $\partial p/\partial z = -\Delta p/L$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta p}{\mu L}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice and apply boundary conditions (3 marks):**

After integration and using $C_1 = 0$ (finiteness at $r = 0$) and $v_z = 0$ at $r = R$:

$$v_{z}(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate (2 marks):**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r)\,(2\pi r)\, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L}\int_{0}^{R}\!\left(R^{2}r - r^{3}\right)dr = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L}\!\left(\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p R^{4}}{8\mu L}\,} \quad \textbf{(Hagen–Poiseuille equation)}$$

**Step 4 — Mean velocity (1 mark):**

$$\langle v_{z} \rangle = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{\Delta p R^{2}}{8\mu L}$$

#### (b) Maximum Velocity, Wall Shear Stress, and the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ Relation — **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Maximum velocity at centerline (2 marks):**
At $r = 0$:

$$v_{z,\max} = \frac{\Delta p R^{2}}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 2 — Wall shear stress (3 marks):**

$$\tau_{r z}\big|_{r=R} = -\mu \frac{dv_{z}}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = -\mu\cdot\frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}(-2R) = \frac{\Delta p R}{2L}$$

**Step 3 — Show the half-velocity relation (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\langle v_{z} \rangle}{v_{z,\max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\Delta p R^{2}}{8\mu L}}{\dfrac{\Delta p R^{2}}{4\mu L}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\therefore \langle v_{z} \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,v_{z,\max} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error on $\partial p/\partial x$ or $\partial p/\partial z$:** Most students drop a sign and end up with a *negative* velocity. Always remember $\partial p/\partial x = -\Delta p / L$ and that $\Delta p > 0$ for flow in the positive x-direction.
> 2. **Forgetting the integration constant $\ln r$ in cylindrical coordinates:** Failing to set $C_1 = 0$ using the "finite velocity at $r = 0$" boundary condition leads to a logarithmic term and full marks deduction.
> 3. **Incorrect dimension analysis on $\tau_w$:** $\tau_w$ in Hagen–Poiseuille has units of Pa, not N. Always check units.
> 4. **Confusing $R$ vs $D$ in the Hagen–Poiseuille formula:** A very common mistake is writing $D^4$ instead of $R^4$. The $R^4$ dependence is the **single most testable fact** in this topic.
> 5. **Skipping the substitution step:** Even when the final answer is correct, KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** if you do not explicitly write the substitution of $y = \delta$ (for film) or $r = R$ (for tube) into the boundary conditions. Show *every* step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Falling film velocity profile:** $v_x = (\rho g/\mu)(\delta y - y^2/2)$.
- [x] **Falling film maximum velocity** at $y = \delta$: $v_{max} = \rho g \delta^{2}/(2\mu)$.
- [x] **Falling film mean velocity:** $\langle v \rangle = \rho g \delta^{2}/(3\mu) = (2/3)v_{max}$.
- [x] **Falling film volumetric flow rate per unit width:** $\Gamma = \rho g \delta^{3}/(3\mu)$.
- [x] **Falling film momentum flow rate per unit width:** $M = 2 \rho^{3} g^{2} \delta^{5}/(15 \mu^{2})$.
- [x] **Falling film wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \rho g \delta$ (balances fluid weight per unit area).
- [x] **Slit flow velocity profile:** $v_x = (\Delta p / 2\mu L)(b^{2} - y^{2})$.
- [x] **Slit flow mean velocity:** $\langle v \rangle = \Delta p b^{2}/(3\mu L) = (2/3)v_{max}$.
- [x] **Slit flow Q per unit width:** $Q_W = 2 \Delta p b^{3}/(3\mu L)$.
- [x] **Tube velocity profile (Hagen–Poiseuille):** $v_z = (\Delta p / 4\mu L)(R^{2} - r^{2})$.
- [x] **Tube mean velocity:** $\langle v \rangle = \Delta p R^{2}/(8\mu L) = (1/2)v_{max}$.
- [x] **Tube volumetric flow rate:** $Q = \pi \Delta p R^{4}/(8\mu L)$ — **fourth-power dependence on radius**.
- [x] **Tube wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \Delta p R/(2L)$.
- [x] **Universal identity:** For slit/film flow, $\langle v \rangle = (2/3) v_{max}$; for tube flow, $\langle v \rangle = (1/2) v_{max}$.
- [x] **Symmetry principle:** The mean-to-max velocity ratio depends on the **symmetry of the cross-section**, not on the driving force (gravity or pressure).
- [x] **Units to remember:** $[\mu] = \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $[\rho] = \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $[Q] = \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$, $[\tau] = \text{Pa}$, $[g] = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.
- [x] **Assumption checklist (5 items):** Newtonian, steady, incompressible, laminar, fully developed.
- [x] **Boundary condition at free surface:** $\tau = 0$ (zero shear).
- [x] **Boundary condition at solid wall:** $v = 0$ (no-slip).
- [x] **Boundary condition at centerline ($r = 0$):** finite $v$ implies $C_1 = 0$ in cylindrical ODE.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** wetted-wall absorbers (falling film); polymer extrusion dies (slit); pipeline transport and capillary viscometry (tube).
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** Students often confuse $\Delta p = P_0 - P_L$ with $P_L - P_0$. Remember, **pressure drops in the direction of flow**, so $\Delta p > 0$ for forward flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow through annulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow through annulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flow through an annulus** refers to the fully-developed, steady, laminar, isothermal flow of a Newtonian fluid in the axial (z) direction through the *crescent-shaped* cross-sectional region bounded by two fixed, smooth, coaxial (concentric) cylindrical surfaces — an inner cylinder of radius $R_i = \kappa R$ and an outer cylinder of radius $R_o = R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing physical picture.** A fluid is forced through the gap between two concentric cylinders (or between a cylinder and a coaxial rod). The flow is treated using the **equation of motion in cylindrical coordinates** with the simplifications: $\rho =$ constant, steady state, fully developed, axisymmetric ($v_\theta = 0$, no $\theta$-dependence), and unidirectional ($v_r = 0$, $v_z = v_z(r)$ only).

The geometry is described by the **radius ratio**, a dimensionless KTU-highlighted parameter:

$$\kappa \;=\; \frac{R_i}{R_o}, \qquad 0 \;<\; \kappa \;<\; 1$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine drinking a thick milkshake through a straw that has a solid rod placed exactly down its centre.** The liquid cannot flow in the central blocked zone, so it is squeezed through the *ring-shaped gap* around the rod. Because the gap is narrowest near the rod and widest at the outer wall, the **velocity profile is no longer a simple parabola** (as in an empty pipe) — it is *skewed*. The maximum velocity is **NOT at the geometric centreline** but is pushed outward, towards $r = R_o$, when the inner rod is present.

This single intuition — *the maximum velocity shifts toward the outer wall* — is what differentiates annular flow from classical Hagen–Poiseuille pipe flow, and it is the most testable KTU insight.

**Everyday / industrial examples** that match this concept:
- **Heat-exchanger tubes** with central thermowells or fins.
- **Lubricating-oil films** between a shaft and its journal bearing.
- **Polymer melt flow** in a concentric-screw extruder.
- **Blood flow** in the region surrounding a catheter inside a large artery.
- **Coaxial cable manufacturing**, where insulating polymer is extruded through an annular die.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units (SI) |
|:------:|:--------|:------------------:|
| $\mu$ | Newtonian viscosity | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | $\text{kg·m}^{-3}$ |
| $R_o = R$ | Outer cylinder radius | $\text{m}$ |
| $R_i = \kappa R$ | Inner cylinder radius | $\text{m}$ |
| $V_i$ | Axial velocity of inner wall | $\text{m·s}^{-1}$ |
| $V_o$ | Axial velocity of outer wall | $\text{m·s}^{-1}$ |
| $-\dfrac{dP}{dz}$ | Pressure-gradient driving force | $\text{Pa·m}^{-1}$ |
| $\kappa$ | Radius ratio (dimensionless) | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Starting Point — Equation of Continuity in Cylindrical Coordinates

For an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$, the *equation of continuity* reads:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\bigl(r\,v_r\bigr) \;+\; \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} \;+\; \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} \;=\; 0$$

## 2.2 Physical Assumptions (List Justification)

| # | Assumption | Mathematical Form | Why It Is Justified |
|:-:|:-----------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Steady flow | $\partial(\cdot)/\partial t = 0$ | Process operating at constant throughput. |
| 2 | Fully developed | $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$ | Velocity shape no longer changes along z. |
| 3 | Axisymmetric | $\partial(\cdot)/\partial \theta = 0$ | Geometry and BCs are independent of $\theta$. |
| 4 | Unidirectional axial flow | $v_r = 0,\; v_\theta = 0$ | Continuity forces $v_z = v_z(r)$ only. |
| 5 | Constant $\rho$, $\mu$ | — | Newtonian, isothermal, incompressible. |
| 6 | Gravity either absent or perpendicular to z | $\rho g_z = 0$ | Horizontal annulus; gravity does not drive axial flow. |

Substituting (1)–(6) into the *z-component of the equation of motion*:

$$0 \;=\; -\frac{dP}{dz} \;+\; \mu\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)$$

This is a 2nd-order linear ODE in $r$, requiring **two boundary conditions** at the cylinder walls.

## 2.2.1 Boundary Conditions (KTU Board-Exam Essentials)

$$v_z\bigl(r = \kappa R\bigr) \;=\; V_i \qquad \text{(no-slip at inner wall)}$$

$$v_z\bigl(r = R\bigr) \;=\; V_o \qquad \text{(no-slip at outer wall)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the boxed results.** In KTU 14-mark derivations, the velocity-profile equation, mean velocity, and maximum-velocity condition are awarded nearly full marks. Most mark loss happens at the integration constants and the log-term manipulations.

### 2.3.1 General velocity profile (Couette + Poiseuille superposed)

$$
\boxed{\;v_z(r) \;=\; V_i \;+\; (V_o - V_i)\,\frac{\ln(r / R_i)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} \;+\; \frac{R_o^{\,2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)^{\!2} - \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R_o}{r}\right]\;}
$$

### 2.3.2 Special-case velocity profiles

| Case | Conditions | Resulting Profile |
|:----:|:----------:|:------------------|
| **Pure Poiseuille** (both walls stationary) | $V_i = V_o = 0$ | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{R_o^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R_o}\right)^{\!2} - \dfrac{1-\kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\dfrac{R_o}{r}\right]$ |
| **Pure Couette** (no pressure gradient) | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = 0$ | $v_z(r) = V_i + (V_o - V_i)\,\dfrac{\ln(r / R_i)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)}$ |
| **Couette + Poiseuille** (inner moving, outer fixed) | $V_o = 0,\;V_i \neq 0$ | $v_z(r) = V_i\!\left[\,1 - \dfrac{\ln(R_o / r)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)}\right] + \dfrac{R_o^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R_o}\right)^{\!2} - \dfrac{1-\kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\dfrac{R_o}{r}\right]$ |
| **Plug-flow limit** | $V_i = V_o = V$, $\dfrac{dP}{dz}=0$ | $v_z(r) = V$ (uniform — solid-body translation) |

### 2.3.3 Location of maximum velocity (pure Poiseuille case)

Set $\dfrac{dv_z}{dr} = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{r_{\max}}{R_o} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{2\,\ln(1/\kappa)}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For $\kappa \to 0$ (empty pipe), $r_{\max}/R_o = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$, recovering the classical Hagen–Poiseuille result. As $\kappa \to 1$ (very thin gap), $r_{\max}/R_o \to 1$, i.e. the maximum velocity is pushed against the outer wall.

### 2.3.4 Mean (average) velocity — pure Poiseuille case

$$
\boxed{\;\langle v_z\rangle \;=\; \frac{Q}{\pi(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})} \;=\; \frac{R_o^{2}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 + \kappa^{2} \;-\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]\;}
$$

### 2.3.5 Volumetric flow rate — pure Poiseuille case

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{\pi R_o^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,(1 - \kappa^{4}) \;-\; \frac{(1 - \kappa^{2})^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]\;}
$$

### 2.3.6 Shear-stress distribution

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{rz}(r) \;=\; -\mu\,\frac{dv_z}{dr} \;=\; -\frac{r}{2}\,\frac{dP}{dz} \;+\; \frac{C_1\,\mu}{r}\;}
$$

where $C_1$ is the integration constant determined by the wall velocities.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Equation

- **Design of concentric-tube heat exchangers** — sizing pumps for given coolant flow.
- **Pressure-drop estimation** in annular catalyst-packed reactors.
- **Lubrication theory** (Reynolds equation) — journal-bearing load capacity.
- **Wire-coating extrusion dies** — uniformity of insulating jacket.
- **Pipeline transport of waxy crude oils** with central heating rods.
- **Rheometry** — concentric-cylinder (Couette) viscometers measure $\mu$ by matching torque with these profiles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the General Velocity Profile

### Step A — Write the starting equation of motion

For a Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho,\mu$, in cylindrical coordinates, the z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation is:

$$
\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \;+\; \mu\!\left[\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial \theta^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial z^{2}}\,\right] + \rho\,g_z
$$

### Step B — Apply the assumptions (Section 2.2)

With $\partial v_z/\partial t = 0$, $v_r = v_\theta = 0$, $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$, $\partial(\cdot)/\partial\theta = 0$, and $g_z = 0$, **every** term on the LHS and the last two viscous terms vanish:

$$
0 \;=\; -\frac{dP}{dz} \;+\; \mu\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)
$$

### Step C — Rearrange into integrable form

Multiply by $r/\mu$:

$$
\frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,r \;=\; \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)
$$

### Step D — First integration

Integrate both sides with respect to $r$:

$$
\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,r^{2} \;=\; r\,\frac{dv_z}{dr} \;-\; C_1
$$

Solve for $dv_z/dr$:

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,r \;+\; \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

### Step E — Second integration

Integrate again with respect to $r$:

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,r^{2} \;+\; C_1\,\ln r \;+\; C_2
$$

### Step F — Apply the two no-slip boundary conditions

BC1: at $r = \kappa R$, $\;v_z = V_i$:

$$
V_i \;=\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,(\kappa R)^{2} \;+\; C_1\,\ln(\kappa R) \;+\; C_2
$$

BC2: at $r = R$, $\;v_z = V_o$:

$$
V_o \;=\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,R^{2} \;+\; C_1\,\ln R \;+\; C_2
$$

### Step G — Subtract BC2 − BC1 to eliminate $C_2$

$$
V_o - V_i \;=\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,R^{2}\bigl(1 - \kappa^{2}\bigr) \;+\; C_1\,\ln\!\frac{R}{\kappa R}
$$

$$
V_o - V_i \;=\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,R^{2}\bigl(1 - \kappa^{2}\bigr) \;+\; C_1\,\ln\!\frac{1}{\kappa}
$$

### Step H — Solve for $C_1$

$$
\boxed{\;C_1 \;=\; \frac{V_o - V_i \;-\; \dfrac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\bigl(1 - \kappa^{2}\bigr)}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\;}
$$

### Step I — Solve for $C_2$ from BC2

$$
C_2 \;=\; V_o \;-\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,R^{2} \;-\; C_1\,\ln R
$$

### Step J — Substitute $C_1$, $C_2$ back into the integrated form

Write $\ln r = \ln(R) + \ln(r/R)$, so:

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,r^{2} \;+\; C_1\,[\ln R + \ln(r/R)] \;+\; C_2
$$

Using $C_1 \ln R + C_2 = V_o - \dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}R^{2}$ from Step I:

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; V_o \;-\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) \;+\; C_1\,\ln\!\frac{r}{R}
$$

Insert $C_1$ from Step H:

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; V_o \;-\; \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) \;+\; \frac{V_o - V_i - \dfrac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}\bigl(1 - \kappa^{2}\bigr)}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{r}{R}
$$

### Step K — Rearrange to the canonical Board-exam form

Multiply numerator and denominator of the log term by $-1$ and group the pressure-gradient contributions:

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; V_o \;+\; (V_i - V_o)\,\frac{\ln(R_o / r)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)} \;+\; \frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{\!2} \;-\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R}{r}\,\right]
$$

This is the **general result** quoted in the KTU Formula Sheet (§2.3.1). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum-Velocity Location (Pure Poiseuille Case)

Set $V_i = V_o = 0$ in the general solution. The velocity profile reduces to:

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{\!2} \;-\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R}{r}\,\right]
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $r$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,-\frac{2r}{R^{2}} \;-\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\!\left(-\frac{1}{r}\right)\,\right] \;=\; 0
$$

Simplify (drop the non-zero prefactor):

$$
-\frac{2r}{R^{2}} \;+\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{r\,\ln(1/\kappa)} \;=\; 0
$$

Solve for $r$:

$$
\frac{2r^{2}}{R^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{r_{\max}}{R} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{2\,\ln(1/\kappa)}} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Volumetric Flow Rate (Pure Poiseuille Case)

The volumetric flow rate is obtained by integrating $v_z$ over the annular cross-section:

$$
Q \;=\; \int_{R_i}^{R_o} v_z(r)\,2\pi r\,dr \;=\; \frac{\pi R^{2}}{2\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\int_{R_i}^{R_o}\!\left[\,r - \frac{r^{3}}{R^{2}} \;-\; \frac{(1 - \kappa^{2})\,r}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R}{r}\,\right]dr
$$

Evaluating the three integrals separately:

$$
\int_{R_i}^{R_o} r\,dr \;=\; \frac{R^{2} - R_i^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{2}(1 - \kappa^{2})
$$

$$
\int_{R_i}^{R_o} \frac{r^{3}}{R^{2}}\,dr \;=\; \frac{R^{4} - R_i^{4}}{4R^{2}} \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4}(1 - \kappa^{4})
$$

For the log integral, let $u = \ln(R/r)$, so $r = Re^{-u}$, $dr = -Re^{-u} du$. Limits: at $r = R_i$, $u = \ln(1/\kappa)$; at $r = R$, $u = 0$:

$$
\int_{R_i}^{R_o} r\ln\!\frac{R}{r}\,dr \;=\; \int_{\ln(1/\kappa)}^{0} R\,e^{-u}\,u\,(-R\,e^{-u})\,du \;=\; R^{2}\!\int_{0}^{\ln(1/\kappa)} u\,e^{-2u}\,du
$$

The integral $\int_{0}^{a} u e^{-2u} du = \dfrac{1}{4}\!\left[1 - e^{-2a}(1 + 2a)\right]$ with $a = \ln(1/\kappa)$ gives $e^{-2a} = \kappa^{2}$. Thus:

$$
\int_{R_i}^{R_o} r\ln\!\frac{R}{r}\,dr \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4}\!\left[\,1 - \kappa^{2} + 2\kappa^{2}\ln(1/\kappa)\,\right]
$$

Substituting all three pieces back:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 + \kappa^{2} - \frac{(1 - \kappa^{2})^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)} \,\right]\!\cdot 2 \;\;\text{(after algebra, equivalent form)}
$$

After tidy algebraic rearrangement (multiply numerator and denominator terms by 2 to put into canonical Board form):

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,(1 - \kappa^{4}) \;-\; \frac{(1 - \kappa^{2})^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\right]\;} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

Dividing by the cross-sectional area $\pi R^{2}(1-\kappa^{2})$ gives the mean velocity of §2.3.4.

---

## 3.4 Limiting-Case Verification Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Limit | Substitution | Reduced formula | Physical meaning |
|:-----:|:-------------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| $\kappa \to 0$ | $1-\kappa^{2} \to 1$, $\ln(1/\kappa) \to \infty$ | $v_z(r) \to \dfrac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[1 - (r/R)^{2}\right]$, $Q \to \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ | Recovers **Hagen–Poiseuille** flow in a circular pipe. |
| $\kappa \to 1$ | Expand $1-\kappa^{2} \approx 2(1-\kappa)$, $\ln(1/\kappa) \approx (1-\kappa)$ | $v_z \to$ linear in $r$ between two close walls | Approaches **plane Couette–Poiseuille** between parallel plates. |
| $-dP/dz \to 0$, $V_i = 0,\; V_o = V$ | $\ln$ term only | $v_z(r) = V\,\dfrac{\ln(r/R_i)}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}$ | **Pure Couette flow** (logarithmic profile). |
| $V_i = V_o = V,\; -dP/dz = 0$ | — | $v_z(r) = V$ | **Solid-body (plug) translation** of the fluid. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU examiners:** Because physical annulus geometry is difficult to render precisely in Mermaid, the schematic below is a *block-level functional architecture diagram* that captures the entire analytical workflow of the annular-flow problem — from problem statement to design outputs.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Analytical Workflow for Annular Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem statement: fluid, geometry, wall velocities, dP/dz"] --> B["Write z-momentum eqn in cylindrical coordinates"]
    B --> C["Apply 6 simplifying assumptions (steady, fully developed, axisymmetric, unidirectional, constant rho/mu, no body force)"]
    C --> D["Reduced ODE: 0 = -dP/dz + mu * (1/r) * d/dr (r dv_z/dr)"]
    D --> E["Integrate twice: v_z(r) = (r^2/4mu) * dP/dz + C1 ln(r) + C2"]
    E --> F["Apply no-slip BCs at r = kappa R and r = R"]
    F --> G["Solve algebraic system for C1 and C2"]
    G --> H["General velocity profile v_z(r)"]
    H --> I1["Special Case A: V_i = V_o = 0"]
    H --> I2["Special Case B: dP/dz = 0"]
    H --> I3["Special Case C: V_o = 0, V_i nonzero"]
    H --> I4["Special Case D: V_i = V_o = V"]
    I1 --> J1["Pure Poiseuille: locate r_max, compute Q and mean velocity"]
    I2 --> J2["Pure Couette: logarithmic profile, compute wall shear"]
    I3 --> J3["Couette-Poiseuille superposed profile"]
    I4 --> J4["Plug flow: v_z = V everywhere"]
    J1 --> K["Engineering outputs: pump sizing, heat-exchanger delta P, bearing load, rheometer torque"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
```


**Reading the diagram:** the fluid "velocity maximum" is shown displaced toward the outer wall, in agreement with the analytical result $r_{\max}/R = \sqrt{(1-\kappa^{2})/(2\ln(1/\kappa))}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the term "radius ratio" $\kappa$ for an annular flow system and explain why the velocity profile in an annulus is non-symmetric even when both cylinders are stationary.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **radius ratio** is defined as $\kappa = R_i / R_o$, the ratio of inner to outer cylinder radii. When both cylinders are stationary, the velocity profile is determined purely by the pressure gradient and is given by the pure-Poiseuille annular expression. Unlike Hagen–Poiseuille flow in a single pipe, the no-slip condition is enforced at **two different radii** ($r = \kappa R$ and $r = R$) rather than at a single centreline. This breaks the geometric symmetry of the profile: the term $(1-\kappa^{2})/\ln(1/\kappa)\,\ln(R/r)$ is *not symmetric* about the centreline, so the maximum velocity is shifted outward. As $\kappa \to 0$ the asymmetry vanishes and the parabolic profile of pipe flow is recovered.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *State the assumptions made while deriving the velocity profile for fully developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal annulus.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Steady-state flow: $\partial/\partial t = 0$.
2. Fully developed: $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$.
3. Axisymmetric: $\partial(\cdot)/\partial\theta = 0$.
4. Unidirectional axial flow: $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$.
5. Constant fluid properties: $\rho =$ const, $\mu =$ const.
6. Laminar, Newtonian behaviour, and horizontal orientation so that $g_z = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

### OR

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** *Starting from the equation of motion in cylindrical coordinates, derive the velocity profile for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal annulus with both cylinders stationary.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Obtain an expression for the maximum velocity and its location. Show that your result correctly reduces to the Hagen–Poiseuille expression as $\kappa \to 0$.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Write the starting equation** — z-component of Navier–Stokes in cylindrical coordinates (1 mark):

$$
0 \;=\; -\frac{dP}{dz} \;+\; \mu\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) \quad\text{(after applying the 6 assumptions)}
$$

2. **Integrate once** (1 mark):

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,r \;+\; \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

3. **Integrate again** (1 mark):

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; \frac{r^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} \;+\; C_1\,\ln r \;+\; C_2
$$

4. **Apply BCs** $v_z = 0$ at $r = \kappa R$ and $r = R$ (1.5 marks):

$$
C_1 \;=\; -\frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\,\frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)},\qquad C_2 \;=\; -\frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} - C_1 \ln R
$$

5. **Final assembled form** (1.5 marks):

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{\!2} \;-\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R}{r}\,\right]
$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

1. **Differentiate** $v_z(r)$ w.r.t. $r$ and set to zero (2 marks):

$$
0 \;=\; -\frac{2r}{R^{2}} \;+\; \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{r\,\ln(1/\kappa)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{r_{\max}}{R} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}}
$$

2. **Substitute** $r = r_{\max}$ back into $v_z$ to get $v_{z,\max}$ (2 marks):

$$
v_{z,\max} \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{2\ln(1/\kappa)} - \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R}{r_{\max}}\,\right]
$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$
v_{z,\max} \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 + \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\bigl(\,1 - \ln 2\,\bigr)\,\right]
$$

3. **Limit check** as $\kappa \to 0$ (2 marks): $1 - \kappa^{2} \to 1$, $\ln(1/\kappa) \to \infty$, so $r_{\max}/R \to 1/\sqrt{2}$, and the profile collapses to $v_z = (R^{2}/4\mu)(-dP/dz)\,[1-(r/R)^{2}]$, with $v_{z,\max} = (R^{2}/4\mu)(-dP/dz)/2$ — exactly the Hagen–Poiseuille maximum. $\blacksquare$

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *For an annulus with inner cylinder moving axially at velocity $V_i$ and the outer cylinder stationary, derive the combined Couette–Poiseuille velocity profile assuming a constant pressure gradient $-dP/dz$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For purely pressure-driven annular flow, derive an expression for the volumetric flow rate $Q$. Hence obtain the mean velocity $\langle v_z\rangle$.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Same governing ODE** as in Q3(a), but BCs change to $v_z(\kappa R) = V_i$, $v_z(R) = 0$ (1 mark).
2. **General solution** $v_z = (r^{2}/4\mu)(dP/dz) + C_1\ln r + C_2$ (1 mark).
3. **Apply BCs** and subtract to find $C_1$ (2 marks):

$$
C_1 \;=\; \frac{-V_i \;-\; \dfrac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}(1 - \kappa^{2})}{\ln(1/\kappa)}
$$

4. **Find** $C_2$ from $v_z(R) = 0$ (1 mark).
5. **Final combined profile** (2 marks):

$$
v_z(r) \;=\; V_i\!\left[\,1 - \frac{\ln(R_o / r)}{\ln(R_o / R_i)}\right] \;+\; \frac{R_o^{2}}{4\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)^{\!2} - \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\frac{R_o}{r}\,\right]
$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

1. **Set up** $Q = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} v_z(r)\,(2\pi r)\,dr$ with $V_i = V_o = 0$ (1 mark).
2. **Break** the integrand into three parts and integrate each (3 marks):

$$
\int_{R_i}^{R_o} r\,dr = \tfrac{R^{2}}{2}(1-\kappa^{2}),\quad \int r^{3}\,dr/R^{2} = \tfrac{R^{2}}{4}(1-\kappa^{4})
$$

$$
\int_{R_i}^{R_o} r\ln(R/r)\,dr = \tfrac{R^{2}}{4}\bigl[\,1 - \kappa^{2} + 2\kappa^{2}\ln(1/\kappa)\,\bigr]
$$

3. **Combine** to obtain $Q$ (2 marks):

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,(1 - \kappa^{4}) - \frac{(1 - \kappa^{2})^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]
$$

4. **Divide by annular area** $\pi R^{2}(1-\kappa^{2})$ to get mean velocity (1 mark):

$$
\langle v_z\rangle \;=\; \frac{R^{2}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[\,1 + \kappa^{2} - \frac{1 - \kappa^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right] \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in annular-flow problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the no-slip condition at BOTH walls** — only the centreline condition is set in ~30% of answer sheets. Both BCs at $r = \kappa R$ and $r = R$ are mandatory for the two constants $C_1$, $C_2$.
> 2. **Sign confusion in the pressure-gradient term** — KTU examiners strictly penalise incorrect sign of $(-dP/dz)$. *The pressure gradient that DRIVES the flow is positive in magnitude; the mathematical $dP/dz$ is negative.* Use $-dP/dz > 0$ in the final boxed formula.
> 3. **Wrong limit recovery** — failing to show that the result collapses to Hagen–Poiseuille for $\kappa \to 0$ costs at least 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — using radius in cm and viscosity in poise simultaneously loses 0.5 mark per occurrence. Convert everything to SI at the start.
> 5. **Mean-velocity vs. maximum-velocity mix-up** — they are NOT equal; the ratio $v_{z,\max}/\langle v_z\rangle$ varies with $\kappa$ and is approximately 1.5 only in the limit $\kappa \to 0$.
> 6. **Logarithm argument error** — $\ln(R/r)$ vs. $\ln(r/R)$ introduces a global sign flip; double-check by substituting $r = R$ (must give $V_o$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Annular flow** = axial laminar Newtonian flow between two concentric cylinders; parameter $\kappa = R_i / R_o$.
- [x] **Governing ODE** after simplifications: $0 = -dP/dz + \mu\,\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,dv_z/dr\right)$.
- [x] **BCs**: no-slip at both walls — $v_z(\kappa R) = V_i$ and $v_z(R) = V_o$.
- [x] **General solution** is a **superposition** of a parabolic (Poiseuille) part and a logarithmic (Couette) part.
- [x] **Maximum velocity** for the pure-Poiseuille case lies at $r_{\max}/R = \sqrt{(1-\kappa^{2})/(2\ln(1/\kappa))}$, **outside** the geometric centreline — pushed toward the outer wall.
- [x] **Volumetric flow rate** for pure Poiseuille: $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\!\left[(1-\kappa^{4}) - \dfrac{(1-\kappa^{2})^{2}}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$.
- [x] **Mean velocity** $\langle v_z\rangle = Q / [\pi R^{2}(1-\kappa^{2})]$ — always less than $v_{z,\max}$.
- [x] **Pure Couette limit** ($dP/dz = 0$) is logarithmic in $r$, **not** linear (linearity only emerges as $\kappa \to 1$, i.e. the parallel-plate limit).
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille recovery** as $\kappa \to 0$ is the most-trusted sanity check.
- [x] **Shear stress** $\tau_{rz} = -\mu\,dv_z/dr$ is **linear in $r$** plus a $1/r$ Couette contribution.
- [x] **Engineering uses**: heat-exchanger tubes, journal bearings, polymer extrusion dies, coaxial-cable insulation, catheterised blood flow.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow of two adjacent immiscible fluids. General transport equations for momentum: derivation of continuity equation and equation of motion in rectangular coordinates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2: MOMENTUM TRANSPORT — IMMISCIBLE FLUIDS & GOVERNING EQUATIONS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions

### 🔹 Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids

Two adjacent immiscible fluids are two distinct liquid phases (such as oil and water) flowing in contact with each other without mixing at the molecular level. They are separated by a well-defined **interface** (a moving boundary of zero thickness in idealized analysis) across which there is no mass transfer but there exists continuity of velocity and continuity of tangential stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In immiscible fluid flow, the key assumption is that the interface between the two fluids is a *sharp, well-defined boundary* where velocity, temperature, and tangential force are continuous, but density and viscosity undergo a step change.

### 🔹 General Transport Equations for Momentum

The general transport equation for momentum is the differential equation that describes the conservation of linear momentum in a flowing fluid element. It is derived by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** (Rate of momentum accumulation = Rate of momentum in − Rate of momentum out + Sum of forces acting) to a fluid element.

### 🔹 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

The continuity equation is the mathematical statement of the principle of conservation of mass applied to a fluid element. For a fluid of constant density (incompressible), it reduces to the divergence-free condition of the velocity field.

### 🔹 Equation of Motion

The equation of motion describes the rate of change of momentum per unit volume of a fluid element. For a Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$, it reduces to the **Navier-Stokes equation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### 🪣 Analogy 1: Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids
Imagine a jar half-filled with **kerosene** and half with **water**. When you tilt the jar, the two liquids flow past each other in a *stratified* manner — they never blend. The kerosene (lighter) flows over the water (heavier), and the boundary between them is called the **interface**. In engineering, this is precisely how **multiphase flow in pipes**, **coating operations**, and **liquid-liquid extraction columns** work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** Crude oil and water in petroleum pipelines flow as adjacent immiscible fluids. The interface location depends on the volumetric flow rates, viscosities, and densities of each phase (governed by the Lockhart-Martinelli correlation in advanced analysis).

### 🌊 Analogy 2: Transport Equation for Momentum
Consider water flowing through a garden hose. If you push harder at the inlet (force), the water exits faster (momentum changes). The equation of motion mathematically expresses this *push* (force = pressure + gravity + viscous + surface forces) and its effect on the fluid's motion. Similarly, the continuity equation simply says: *"what flows in must either flow out or accumulate"* — like a budget balance for fluid mass.

### 🧊 Analogy 3: Continuity Equation
Think of a **leaky bucket** of water. If 5 litres/sec flow into the bucket and 3 litres/sec leak out, then 2 litres/sec must be accumulating inside (the water level rises at that rate). The continuity equation is the mathematical form of this balance.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:---------------:|
| $\rho$ | Density of fluid | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = \text{kg/(m·s)}$ |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity ($\mu/\rho$) | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\sigma$ | Interfacial tension | $\text{N/m}$ |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| $P$ | Pressure | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| $v_x, v_y, v_z$ | Velocity components | $\text{m/s}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Flow of Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids — Theoretical Framework

When two immiscible fluids flow in a channel or pipe, the analysis involves:

1. **Identifying the interface location** using the concept of equivalent length or pressure drop balance.
2. **Applying the Navier-Stokes equation** in each fluid region separately.
3. **Matching boundary conditions at the interface:**
   - Continuity of velocity: $v_{1,t} = v_{2,t}$ (tangential) and $v_{1,n} = v_{2,n}$ (normal)
   - Continuity of tangential stress: $\tau_{1} = \tau_{2}$
   - Jump in normal stress balanced by interfacial tension: $(P_1 - P_2) = \sigma \left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For the typical KTU problem of two immiscible fluids flowing between parallel plates (plane Poiseuille flow with two layers), the velocity profile is **parabolic in each fluid**, but the **constants of integration differ** for each layer. The no-slip condition is applied at the solid walls, and velocity continuity is applied at the interface.

### Stepwise Logic for Two-Fluid Flow Analysis:

- **Step 1:** Define the geometry. For parallel plates, let fluid 1 occupy $0 \le y \le h_1$ and fluid 2 occupy $h_1 \le y \le H$ (with $H$ = total gap).
- **Step 2:** Write the simplified Navier-Stokes equation for steady, fully-developed, unidirectional flow in each layer.
- **Step 3:** Integrate twice in each layer to obtain velocity profiles $v_z(y)$.
- **Step 4:** Apply **four boundary conditions:**
  1. No-slip at lower wall: $v_z(0) = 0$
  2. No-slip at upper wall: $v_z(H) = 0$
  3. Velocity continuity at interface: $v_z(h_1^-) = v_z(h_1^+)$
  4. Stress continuity at interface: $\mu_1 \left.\dfrac{dv_z}{dy}\right\vert_{h_1^-} = \mu_2 \left.\dfrac{dv_z}{dy}\right\vert_{h_1^+}$
- **Step 5:** Solve for the four integration constants.
- **Step 6:** Compute volumetric flow rate by integrating $v_z$ across the cross-section.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Transport Equations

The derivation of the continuity equation and equation of motion rests on three fundamental principles:

1. **Conservation of Mass** → Continuity Equation
2. **Conservation of Momentum (Newton's Second Law)** → Equation of Motion
3. **Constitutive Equation for Newtonian Fluid** → $\tau = -\mu \nabla v$

These are applied to a **differential fluid element** of fixed identity (Lagrangian) or to a control volume (Eulerian), with the result being the same in differential form.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical equations** required for KTU 2024 Module 2 problems. Master these to score full marks.

### Table A: Transport Equations — General Vector & Cartesian Forms

| # | Equation Name | General Vector Form | Rectangular (x, y, z) Coordinate Form |
|:-:|:--------------|:--------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Continuity Equation** (constant $\rho$) | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ | $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ |
| 2 | **Continuity Equation** (variable $\rho$) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ |
| 3 | **Equation of Motion** (Navier-Stokes, constant $\rho$, $\mu$) | $\rho \dfrac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ | See expanded x-component below |
| 4 | **x-Component of Equation of Motion** | — | $\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$ |
| 5 | **y-Component of Equation of Motion** | — | $\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial t} + v_x\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} + v_y\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial y} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_y$ |
| 6 | **z-Component of Equation of Motion** | — | $\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_x\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} + v_y\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_z$ |

### Table B: Velocity Profile for Two Immiscible Fluids Between Parallel Plates

| Quantity | Expression |
|:---------|:-----------|
| Velocity in fluid 1 ($0 \le y \le h_1$) | $v_{z1}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_1}\left(\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)(y^2 - h_1 y) + A$ |
| Velocity in fluid 2 ($h_1 \le y \le H$) | $v_{z2}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_2}\left(\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)(y^2 - H y) + B$ |
| Interface velocity | $v_{z,\text{int}}$ from continuity of velocity |
| Volumetric flow rate per unit width | $Q/w = \int_0^{h_1} v_{z1}\, dy + \int_{h_1}^{H} v_{z2}\, dy$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The equation of motion is the cornerstone of designing chemical process equipment — heat exchangers, mixers, pumps, distillation columns, reactors, and pipelines. Every fluid flow simulation software (COMSOL, ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM) solves the Navier-Stokes equation in some form.
