# 📘 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation in Rectangular Coordinates

### 🟢 Setup and Assumptions

We consider a **differential fluid element** fixed in space through which the fluid flows (Eulerian approach). The element is a rectangular box with sides $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, $\Delta z$ located at point $(x, y, z)$ in a Cartesian coordinate system. The fluid has density $\rho$ which may be a function of position and time.

> **Key Principle:** *Rate of mass accumulation inside the element = Net rate of mass flowing in − Net rate of mass flowing out*

### 🟢 Mass Flow Through Each Face

The mass flow rate through any face = (density) × (velocity component normal to face) × (area of face).

**Mass flow rate in the x-direction through the left face (at $x$):**
$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = \left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x} \cdot \Delta y \Delta z$$

**Mass flow rate out the x-direction through the right face (at $x + \Delta x$):**
$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} = \left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x} \cdot \Delta y \Delta z$$

**Net outflow in x-direction:**
$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{net,out}} = \left[\left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - \left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x}\right] \Delta y \Delta z$$

Similarly for y and z directions:

**Net outflow in y-direction:**
$$\dot{m}_{y,\text{net,out}} = \left[\left(\rho v_y\right)\Big\vert_{y+\Delta y} - \left(\rho v_y\right)\Big\vert_{y}\right] \Delta x \Delta z$$

**Net outflow in z-direction:**
$$\dot{m}_{z,\text{net,out}} = \left[\left(\rho v_z\right)\Big\vert_{z+\Delta z} - \left(\rho v_z\right)\Big\vert_{z}\right] \Delta x \Delta y$$

### 🟢 Mass Accumulation Inside the Element

The rate of mass accumulation inside the element is:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{accum}} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### 🟢 Applying Conservation of Mass

The mass balance states:
$$\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Rate of mass in} - \text{Rate of mass out}$$

Equivalently:
$$\text{Rate of accumulation} + \text{Net rate of mass out} = 0$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z + \left[\left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - \left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x}\right] \Delta y \Delta z + \left[\left(\rho v_y\right)\Big\vert_{y+\Delta y} - \left(\rho v_y\right)\Big\vert_{y}\right] \Delta x \Delta z + \left[\left(\rho v_z\right)\Big\vert_{z+\Delta z} - \left(\rho v_z\right)\Big\vert_{z}\right] \Delta x \Delta y = 0$$

### 🟢 Dividing Through by Element Volume

Divide the entire equation by $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - \left(\rho v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x}}{\Delta x} + \dfrac{\left(\rho v_y\right)\Big\vert_{y+\Delta y} - \left(\rho v_y\right)\Big\vert_{y}}{\Delta y} + \dfrac{\left(\rho v_z\right)\Big\vert_{z+\Delta z} - \left(\rho v_z\right)\Big\vert_{z}}{\Delta z} = 0$$

### 🟢 Taking the Limit as Δx, Δy, Δz → 0

In the limit, each difference quotient becomes a partial derivative:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial \left(\rho v_x\right)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \left(\rho v_y\right)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \left(\rho v_z\right)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### 🟢 Final Form of the Continuity Equation

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial \left(\rho v_x\right)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \left(\rho v_y\right)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \left(\rho v_z\right)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

**Incompressible (constant $\rho$) form:**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0}$$

**In compact vector notation:**
$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equation of Motion in Rectangular Coordinates (x-component)

### 🟢 Setup and Assumptions

We apply **Newton's Second Law** to a differential fluid element of size $\Delta x \times \Delta y \times \Delta z$:

$$\text{Rate of change of momentum} = \text{Sum of forces acting on the element}$$

The forces acting on the element are classified into:
1. **Body forces** (gravity, electromagnetic) — act throughout the volume.
2. **Surface forces** (pressure + viscous stresses) — act on each face.

### 🟢 Rate of Change of Momentum (x-component)

By analogy with the continuity derivation, the net rate of x-momentum outflow in the x-direction is:
$$\left[\left(\rho v_x v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - \left(\rho v_x v_x\right)\Big\vert_{x}\right] \Delta y \Delta z$$

Net rate of x-momentum outflow in the y-direction (due to flow of y-momentum carrying x-velocity):
$$\left[\left(\rho v_y v_x\right)\Big\vert_{y+\Delta y} - \left(\rho v_y v_x\right)\Big\vert_{y}\right] \Delta x \Delta z$$

Net rate of x-momentum outflow in the z-direction:
$$\left[\left(\rho v_z v_x\right)\Big\vert_{z+\Delta z} - \left(\rho v_z v_x\right)\Big\vert_{z}\right] \Delta x \Delta y$$

**Rate of x-momentum accumulation:**
$$\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial t} \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

The momentum balance (analogous to mass balance):
$$\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y v_x)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z v_x)}{\partial z} = \sum F_x$$

### 🟢 Body Force in x-direction (Gravity)

If gravity acts in the z-direction (so $g_x = 0$), the body force per unit volume is $\rho g_x$. The total x-body force on the element:
$$F_{x,\text{body}} = \rho g_x \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### 🟢 Surface Forces — Pressure and Viscous Stresses

**Pressure forces (normal stresses):** Pressure acts inward on each face. The net pressure force in the x-direction:
$$F_{x,\text{pressure}} = \left[P\Big\vert_{x} - P\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x}\right] \Delta y \Delta z = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

**Viscous forces:** There are three normal viscous stresses ($\tau_{xx}, \tau_{yy}, \tau_{zz}$) and six shear stresses ($\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$). The net x-viscous force on the element:

For the **xx**-component (normal stress on x-face):
$$F_{x,\tau_{xx}} = \left[\tau_{xx}\Big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - \tau_{xx}\Big\vert_{x}\right] \Delta y \Delta z = \dfrac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

For the **yx**-component (shear stress on y-face acting in x-direction):
$$F_{x,\tau_{yx}} = \left[\tau_{yx}\Big\vert_{y+\Delta y} - \tau_{yx}\Big\vert_{y}\right] \Delta x \Delta z = \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

For the **zx**-component (shear stress on z-face acting in x-direction):
$$F_{x,\tau_{zx}} = \left[\tau_{zx}\Big\vert_{z+\Delta z} - \tau_{zx}\Big\vert_{z}\right] \Delta x \Delta y = \dfrac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### 🟢 Constitutive Equations for Newtonian Fluid

For a Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$, the stress components are:

$$\tau_{xx} = -2\mu \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v})$$
$$\tau_{yy} = -2\mu \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v})$$
$$\tau_{zz} = -2\mu \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} + \dfrac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v})$$
$$\tau_{yx} = \tau_{xy} = -\mu \left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}\right)$$
$$\tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz} = -\mu \left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial x}\right)$$
$$\tau_{zy} = \tau_{yz} = -\mu \left(\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial y}\right)$$

### 🟢 Combining All Terms

Substituting into the momentum balance and dividing by $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:

$$\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y v_x)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z v_x)}{\partial z} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$

### 🟢 Substitution of Newtonian Stresses

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$), we obtain:
$$\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} = -2\mu \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2}$$
$$\dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} = -\mu \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}\right) = -\mu \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} - \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial y \partial x}$$
$$\dfrac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} = -\mu \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial x}\right) = -\mu \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} - \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z \partial x}$$

Adding these three:
$$\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} = -\mu \left(2\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial y \partial x} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z \partial x}\right)$$

Using continuity $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$, differentiate with respect to x:
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial y \partial x} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z \partial x} = 0$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial y \partial x} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z \partial x} = -\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2}$$

Substituting back:
$$\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} = -\mu \left(2\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} - \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2}\right) = -\mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) = -\mu \nabla^2 v_x$$

### 🟢 Final x-Component of Equation of Motion (Navier-Stokes)

$$\boxed{\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x}$$

**Similarly, the y and z components:**

$$\boxed{\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial y} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_y}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_y}$$

$$\boxed{\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_z}$$

### 🟢 Compact Vector Form (Substantial Derivative)

The left-hand side can be compactly written as $\rho \dfrac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt}$ where:
$$\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

Thus in vector form:
$$\boxed{\rho \dfrac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}}$$

This is the famous **Navier-Stokes equation** for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity.

# 📘 STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Derivation Flow of Transport Equations

The following diagram maps the logical flow of deriving the transport equations from first principles:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Define Differential Fluid Element] --> B[Element: dx, dy, dz at point x,y,z]
    B --> C[STEP 2: Identify Mass or Momentum Fluxes Through Each Face]
    C --> D[STEP 3: Apply Conservation Principle]
    D --> E{Mass or Momentum?}
    E -->|Mass| F[Continuity Equation]
    E -->|Momentum| G[Newton Second Law]
    F --> F1[Net mass out + accumulation = 0]
    F1 --> F2[Divide by volume and take limit]
    F2 --> F3[Continuity: drho/dt + div rho v = 0]
    G --> G1[Identify body forces: gravity]
    G1 --> G2[Identify surface forces: pressure + viscous stresses]
    G2 --> G3[Use Newtonian constitutive relations]
    G3 --> G4[Apply continuity to simplify viscous terms]
    G4 --> G5[Final Navier Stokes Equation]
```

## 4.2 Architecture — Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids Flow System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Immiscible Fluids Setup] --> B[Fluid 1: mu1, rho1 in region 0 to h1]
    A --> C[Fluid 2: mu2, rho2 in region h1 to H]
    B --> D[Apply Navier Stokes in each layer]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Integrate twice to get velocity profiles]
    E --> F[Apply Four Boundary Conditions]
    F --> F1[BC1: No slip at lower wall v_z 0 equals 0]
    F --> F2[BC2: No slip at upper wall v_z H equals 0]
    F --> F3[BC3: Velocity continuity at interface]
    F --> F4[BC4: Shear stress continuity at interface]
    F1 --> G[Solve for four integration constants]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Final velocity profiles v_z1 y and v_z2 y]
    H --> I[Compute volumetric flow rate Q]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow — From Shell Balance to Differential Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shell Balance Approach] --> B[Force Balance on Element]
    B --> C[Rate of Momentum In minus Out]
    C --> D[Gravity Force]
    D --> E[Pressure Force]
    E --> F[Viscous Force]
    F --> G[Take limit as thickness goes to zero]
    G --> H[Differential Equation of Motion]
    H --> I[Boundary Conditions]
    I --> J[Integrate to get velocity profile]
    J --> K[Volumetric flow rate and average velocity]
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Forces Acting on the Differential Element

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Element[DIFFERENTIAL ELEMENT dx dy dz] --> FaceXRight[Right Face: x plus dx]
    Element --> FaceXLeft[Left Face: x]
    Element --> FaceYTop[Top Face: y plus dy]
    Element --> FaceYBot[Bottom Face: y]
    Element --> FaceZBack[Back Face: z plus dz]
    Element --> FaceZFront[Front Face: z]
    FaceXRight --> PxR[Pressure Px and Stress tauxx plus d tauxx]
    FaceXLeft --> PxL[Pressure Px and Stress tauxx]
    FaceYTop --> PyT[Pressure Py and Stress tauyy plus d tauyy]
    FaceYBot --> PyB[Pressure Py and Stress tauyy]
    FaceZBack --> PzB[Pressure Pz and Stress tauzz plus d tauzz]
    FaceZFront --> PzF[Pressure Pz and Stress tauzz]
    Element --> Body[Body Force: rho times g per unit volume]
```

# 📘 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the physical significance of the continuity equation for an incompressible fluid.**

**Model Answer:**

The continuity equation for an incompressible fluid in rectangular coordinates is:
$$\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

This equation states that the **net volumetric flow rate out of any infinitesimal fluid element is zero**. Physically, it represents the **principle of conservation of mass** for a fluid whose density remains constant. It is a **kinematic constraint** that must be satisfied by any physically admissible velocity field for an incompressible fluid. Mathematically, it expresses the **divergence-free** nature of the velocity field, meaning the velocity streamlines neither originate nor terminate within the fluid.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the boundary conditions to be satisfied at the interface of two adjacent immiscible fluids in relative motion.**

**Model Answer:**

The boundary conditions at the interface of two adjacent immiscible fluids are:

1. **Continuity of velocity (kinematic condition):** The tangential components of velocity are continuous across the interface: $v_{1,t} = v_{2,t}$. The normal components are also continuous: $v_{1,n} = v_{2,n}$ (no mass transfer across the interface).

2. **Continuity of tangential stress:** The tangential viscous stress is continuous: $\tau_{1,t} = \tau_{2,t}$.

3. **Jump in normal stress:** The difference in normal stresses is balanced by the interfacial tension: $P_1 - P_2 = \sigma \left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$, where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the principal radii of curvature of the interface and $\sigma$ is the interfacial tension.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Derive the continuity equation in rectangular coordinates for a fluid of variable density. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two immiscible fluids of viscosities $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ flow in laminar fashion between two parallel horizontal plates separated by a distance $H$. The denser fluid (viscosity $\mu_1$) occupies the region $0 \le y \le h_1$ and the lighter fluid (viscosity $\mu_2$) occupies $h_1 \le y \le H$. The pressure gradient in the flow direction (z) is $dP/dz$. Derive the velocity profile in each fluid. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Continuity Equation (7 Marks)

**Statement of Principle:** The principle of conservation of mass is applied to a differential fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ fixed in space.

**Step 1 — Mass fluxes through each face:**

- In at $x$: $\rho v_x \, dy \, dz$
- Out at $x + dx$: $(\rho v_x + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} dx)\, dy \, dz$
- In at $y$: $\rho v_y \, dx \, dz$
- Out at $y + dy$: $(\rho v_y + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} dy)\, dx \, dz$
- In at $z$: $\rho v_z \, dx \, dy$
- Out at $z + dz$: $(\rho v_z + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} dz)\, dx \, dy$

**Step 2 — Mass balance:**

$$\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz = -\left[\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Final equation:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

**For incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant):**
$$\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

---

#### Part (b) — Velocity Profiles for Two Immiscible Fluids (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
- Steady flow
- Laminar, fully-developed, unidirectional flow ($v_z = v_z(y)$, $v_x = v_y = 0$)
- Constant pressure gradient $dP/dz$
- Gravity acts in z-direction only (no body force component in y)

**Step 1 — Equation of motion in z-direction simplifies to:**

$$0 = -\dfrac{dP}{dz} + \mu \dfrac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2}$$

So:
$$\dfrac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice in each region:**

For fluid 1 ($0 \le y \le h_1$, viscosity $\mu_1$):
$$v_{z1}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_1}\dfrac{dP}{dz} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

For fluid 2 ($h_1 \le y \le H$, viscosity $\mu_2$):
$$v_{z2}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_2}\dfrac{dP}{dz} y^2 + C_3 y + C_4$$

**Step 3 — Apply four boundary conditions:**

1. **No-slip at lower wall:** $v_{z1}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
2. **No-slip at upper wall:** $v_{z2}(H) = 0 \Rightarrow C_4 = -C_3 H - \dfrac{1}{2\mu_2}\dfrac{dP}{dz} H^2$
3. **Velocity continuity at interface:** $v_{z1}(h_1) = v_{z2}(h_1)$
4. **Stress continuity at interface:** $\mu_1 \dfrac{dv_{z1}}{dy}\Big\vert_{h_1} = \mu_2 \dfrac{dv_{z2}}{dy}\Big\vert_{h_1}$

**Step 4 — Final velocity profiles (after solving):**

$$v_{z1}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_1}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left[h_1 y - y^2\right] + \dfrac{A}{\mu_1}\left[h_1 - y\right]$$

$$v_{z2}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu_2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left[H y - y^2 - h_1 H + h_1^2\right] + \dfrac{A}{\mu_2}\left[H - y\right]$$

where $A$ is determined by velocity continuity at the interface.

---

### 📝 Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)
**(a)** Starting from Newton's second law of motion, derive the equation of motion in rectangular coordinates (x-component) for a Newtonian fluid of constant density and viscosity. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Simplify the Navier-Stokes equation for the case of steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of an incompressible fluid between two parallel plates separated by a distance $2h$, where the upper plate moves with velocity $U$ and the lower plate is stationary. Obtain the velocity profile. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Equation of Motion (x-component) (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
- Newtonian fluid
- Constant density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$
- Isothermal flow
- Body force is gravity (with components $g_x$, $g_y$, $g_z$)

**Step 1 — Newton's second law on fluid element:**

$$\text{Rate of change of x-momentum} = \text{Sum of x-forces}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_x^2)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_y v_x)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v_z v_x)}{\partial z} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$

**Step 2 — Substituting Newtonian stresses (constant $\rho$, $\mu$):**

After using continuity equation to eliminate cross-derivative terms:

$$\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$$

**Step 3 — Vector compact form:**

$$\rho \dfrac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Couette Flow Velocity Profile (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state: $\partial / \partial t = 0$
- Fully-developed: $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$
- Unidirectional: $v_x = v_y = 0$, $v_z = v_z(y)$
- Constant pressure gradient: $dP/dz = 0$ (no imposed pressure gradient)
- Plates separated by distance $2h$, lower plate at $y = -h$ stationary, upper plate at $y = +h$ moves with velocity $U$

**Step 1 — Simplified z-component of Navier-Stokes:**

$$0 = \mu \dfrac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate twice:**

$$v_z(y) = A y + B$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- $v_z(-h) = 0 \Rightarrow -Ah + B = 0 \Rightarrow B = Ah$
- $v_z(+h) = U \Rightarrow Ah + B = U \Rightarrow 2Ah = U \Rightarrow A = U/(2h)$

**Step 4 — Final velocity profile:**

$$\boxed{v_z(y) = \dfrac{U}{2h}(y + h)}$$

This is a **linear velocity profile** characteristic of plane Couette flow.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign error in pressure gradient:** Many students write $\dfrac{dP}{dz}$ as positive when deriving velocity profiles. KTU conventions follow the sign where $-\dfrac{dP}{dz}$ drives the flow forward. **Lose 1 mark** for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the substantial derivative terms:** In deriving the equation of motion, students often omit the convective acceleration terms $(v_x \partial v_x/\partial x + ...)$. The LHS is $\rho D\mathbf{v}/Dt$, NOT just $\rho \partial v_x/\partial t$. **Lose 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Improper use of continuity to simplify viscous terms:** A common mistake is to forget to use the incompressible continuity equation to eliminate cross-derivative terms like $\partial^2 v_y/(\partial y \partial x)$. **Lose 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Missing boundary conditions in two-fluid flow:** Students frequently apply only 2 boundary conditions (walls) but forget velocity AND stress continuity at the interface. You need **4 conditions** for 4 constants. **Lose 2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Confusion between fluid element and control volume:** Be precise: the continuity equation derivation uses a **fixed Eulerian control volume**, not a moving fluid element. State this clearly.
>
> 6. **Not stating incompressibility assumption explicitly:** When simplifying the continuity equation, the step from $\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ to $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ requires the assumption $\rho$ = constant. **Lose 1 mark** if not stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Continuity equation** is the conservation of mass in differential form: $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$
- [x] For **incompressible fluids**, continuity reduces to: $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$
- [x] **Equation of motion** is Newton's second law applied to a fluid element: $\rho D\mathbf{v}/Dt = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$
- [x] The substantial derivative $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla)$ converts the Eulerian to Lagrangian description
- [x] The LHS of Navier-Stokes has two parts: **local** (transient) + **convective** acceleration
- [x] The RHS of Navier-Stokes has three parts: **pressure** + **viscous** + **gravity** forces per unit volume
- [x] **Newtonian constitutive relation** for shear stress: $\tau_{yx} = -\mu (\partial v_x/\partial y + \partial v_y/\partial x)$
- [x] For two adjacent **immiscible fluids**, apply 4 boundary conditions: 2 wall no-slip + 1 velocity continuity + 1 stress continuity
- [x] **Interface condition:** $P_1 - P_2 = \sigma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$ — the Young-Laplace equation
- [x] In two-fluid flow, the **velocity profile is parabolic in each layer** but the parabola coefficients differ due to different viscosities
- [x] **Continuity is a kinematic constraint**; **momentum equation is dynamic**
- [x] For **plane Couette flow** (moving plate, no pressure gradient): linear velocity profile
- [x] For **plane Poiseuille flow** (stationary plates, pressure-driven): parabolic velocity profile
- [x] The **Navier-Stokes equation** is the workhorse of CFD codes (ANSYS, COMSOL, OpenFOAM)
- [x] **Physical interpretation** of viscous term $\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v}$: it represents the diffusion of momentum by molecular motion
- [x] **Body force per unit volume** = $\rho \mathbf{g}$ (for gravity only)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navier-Stokes equation and Euler equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Navier-Stokes equation and Euler equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Navier-Stokes Equation (NSE):** The fundamental equation of change for momentum in a *Newtonian fluid* with **constant density (ρ)** and **constant viscosity (μ)**, expressing the conservation of linear momentum in vector form: $$ \rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \;=\; -\nabla p \;+\; \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{v} \;+\; \rho \vec{g} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Euler Equation of Motion:** The simplified, *inviscid* form of the Navier-Stokes equation obtained by setting the viscosity $\mu = 0$. It expresses momentum balance for a **frictionless (non-viscous)** fluid: $$ \rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \;=\; -\nabla p \;+\; \rho \vec{g} $$ where the **substantial (material) derivative** is: $$ \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} \;=\; \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} \;+\; (\vec{v}\cdot \nabla)\vec{v} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are pushing a heavy shopping cart through a crowded supermarket aisle: - **Euler Equation** is like predicting the cart's motion by considering *only* the slope of the floor (gravity) and the slope of the ramp at the entrance (pressure). You **ignore** how the cart scrapes against the floor or how the wheels rub on tiles. → *Frictionless flow.* - **Navier-Stokes Equation** is the realistic model: it accounts for the **sliding friction** of the wheels on the floor (viscous diffusion term $\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}$), **in addition to** the slope and ramp. → *Viscous flow.* > [!NOTE] > The Euler equation is essentially the **Navier-Stokes equation stripped of its "viscous friction" term**. The Euler equation is mathematically simpler and is used for ideal-fluid analyses, whereas the NSE is the workhorse equation for real engineering flows (boundary layers, pipe flow, lubrication, etc.). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit | |---|---|---| | **ρ** | Fluid density | kg/m³ | | **μ** | Dynamic viscosity | Pa·s (or kg/m·s) | | **ν = μ/ρ** | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s | | **p** | Pressure | Pa (N/m²) | | **g** | Gravitational acceleration | m/s² | | **v** | Velocity vector | m/s | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Hook:** The NSE is the *momentum* analogue of Fourier's Law (heat) and Fick's Law (mass) — it is the **constitutive + conservation** equation merged into one. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Derivation Logic Flow (Step-Wise Bullets) The Navier-Stokes equation emerges from a clean, four-step logical chain. Each step is essential and frequently appears in KTU derivations. **Step 1 — Begin with the general Equation of Motion** (valid for *any* continuum): $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}\vec{v}) = -\nabla p \;-\; [\nabla \cdot \vec{\tau}] + \rho \vec{g} $$ > This is just Newton's 2nd law applied to a fluid element: rate of momentum accumulation + net efflux of momentum = pressure force + viscous force + gravity. **Step 2 — Restrict to incompressible Newtonian fluid** ($\rho = $ const, $\mu = $ const): - Density may be taken *inside* the time derivative. - The momentum flux for a Newtonian fluid is: $$ \vec{\tau} \;=\; -\mu \left[ \nabla \vec{v} + (\nabla \vec{v})^{\dagger} \right] + \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\mu - \kappa\right)(\nabla \cdot \vec{v})\vec{\delta} $$ where $\kappa$ is the **dilatational viscosity** (often dropped for monatomic incompressible fluids). **Step 3 — Evaluate $\nabla \cdot \vec{\tau}$:** - For incompressible flow: $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$. - Therefore $\nabla \cdot \vec{\tau} = \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{v}$ (this is a famous result). **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$ \rho\left[\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}\right] = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g} $$ This is the **Navier-Stokes equation**. **Step 5 — Set μ = 0:** The viscous diffusion term vanishes, leaving: $$ \rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g} $$ This is the **Euler equation**. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Term - $\rho \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t}$ — *Local acceleration* (unsteadiness at a fixed point). - $\rho(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$ — *Convective acceleration* (flow carrying momentum from upstream). - $-\nabla p$ — Pressure-gradient driving force. - $\mu \nabla^{2}\vec{v}$ — *Viscous diffusion* of momentum (molecular momentum transport). - $\rho \vec{g}$ — Body force per unit volume (gravity). > [!NOTE] > The Euler equation is **not** the same as Bernoulli's equation, although they are related. Bernoulli's equation is the **Euler equation integrated along a streamline** for steady, inviscid, irrotational flow. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **minimum** set of equations a KTU student must memorize for Module 2 viva/ESE. | # | Equation Name | Vector Form | Key Assumption | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | General Equation of Motion | $\partial_t(\rho\vec{v})+\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}\vec{v})=-\nabla p-(\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau})+\rho\vec{g}$ | Any continuum | | 2 | Newtonian Fluid Stress | $\vec{\tau}=-\mu[\nabla\vec{v}+(\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}]+\frac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla\cdot\vec{v})\vec{\delta}$ | Newtonian, compressible | | 3 | Incompressible Newtonian Stress | $\vec{\tau}=-\mu[\nabla\vec{v}+(\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}]$ | $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$ | | 4 | **Navier-Stokes Equation** | $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}=-\nabla p+\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}+\rho\vec{g}$ | $\rho,\mu=$ const | | 5 | **Euler Equation** | $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}=-\nabla p+\rho\vec{g}$ | $\mu=0$ (inviscid) | | 6 | Navier-Stokes (x-component) | $\rho\left(\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t}+v_x\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}+v_y\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}+v_z\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right)=-\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}+\mu\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial x^{2}}+\dfrac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial y^{2}}+\dfrac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial z^{2}}\right)+\rho g_x$ | Cartesian, constant μ | | 7 | Substantial Derivative | $\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}=\dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t}+(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$ | Definition | | 8 | Kinematic Form of NSE | $\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}=-\dfrac{1}{\rho}\nabla p+\nu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}+\vec{g}$ | Divide by ρ, with $\nu=\mu/\rho$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Euler equation models inviscid flow over airfoils (pressure distribution, lift). - **Chemical Engineering:** NSE used in reactor mixing, pipe flow design, and non-Newtonian-flow analogues. - **Biomedical:** Blood flow in large arteries (NSE with non-Newtonian rheology). - **Oceanography:** Euler equation + Coriolis term for geophysical fluid dynamics. - **CFD Software:** All commercial CFD solvers (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM) discretize the NSE as their core. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Navier-Stokes Equation We start from the general equation of motion, valid for any Newtonian or non-Newtonian fluid. **Step A — Start with the Equation of Continuity + Newton's 2nd Law:** For a fluid element of volume $dV$ and surface $dS$, the integral form is: $$ \int_{V}\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho\vec{v})\,dV + \int_{S}\rho\vec{v}(\vec{v}\cdot d\vec{S}) = -\int_{S}p\,d\vec{S} - \int_{S}\vec{\tau}\cdot d\vec{S} + \int_{V}\rho\vec{g}\,dV $$ **Step B — Convert to differential form using Gauss's divergence theorem:** Apply the divergence theorem $\int_{S}\vec{A}\cdot d\vec{S}=\int_{V}(\nabla\cdot\vec{A})\,dV$ to the flux terms: $$ \int_{V}\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho\vec{v})\,dV + \int_{V}\nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}\vec{v})\,dV = -\int_{V}\nabla p\,dV - \int_{V}\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}\,dV + \int_{V}\rho\vec{g}\,dV $$ **Step C — Combine under a single volume integral (the integrand form):** $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho\vec{v}) + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}\vec{v}) = -\nabla p - (\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}) + \rho\vec{g} $$ This is the **general equation of motion** (vector form). **Step D — Apply incompressibility ($\rho = $ constant):** Since $\rho$ is constant, the continuity equation reduces to $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$, and we can write: $$ \rho\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + \rho\nabla\cdot(\vec{v}\vec{v}) = -\nabla p - (\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}) + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step E — Substantive derivative identity:** Using the vector identity $\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v} = \nabla\cdot(\vec{v}\vec{v}) - \vec{v}(\nabla\cdot\vec{v})$ and $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$: $$ \nabla\cdot(\vec{v}\vec{v}) = (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v} $$ Therefore: $$ \rho\left[\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}\right] = -\nabla p - (\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}) + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step F — Substitute the Newtonian stress tensor:** For a Newtonian fluid: $$ \vec{\tau} = -\mu\left[\nabla\vec{v} + (\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}\right] + \frac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla\cdot\vec{v})\vec{\delta} $$ Since $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$, the dilatational term vanishes: $$ \vec{\tau} = -\mu\left[\nabla\vec{v} + (\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}\right] $$ **Step G — Compute $\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}$:** Taking the divergence term-by-term, and using $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$: $$ \nabla\cdot\vec{\tau} = -\mu\nabla\cdot\left[\nabla\vec{v} + (\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}\right] $$ For a constant-μ, incompressible Newtonian fluid, this reduces to the beautiful result: $$ \nabla\cdot\vec{\tau} = -\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} $$ **Step H — Substitute back into the equation of motion:** $$ \rho\left[\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}\right] = -\nabla p - (-\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}) + \rho\vec{g} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\rho\left[\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}\right] = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}\;} $$ This is the **Navier-Stokes equation** for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant $\rho$ and $\mu$. ## 3.2 Full Derivation of the Euler Equation The Euler equation is obtained in one step: **set the viscosity to zero**. **Step 1 — Start from NSE:** $$ \rho\left[\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}\right] = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step 2 — Set μ = 0** (frictionless fluid, e.g. ideal gas at high Re or superfluid helium): $$ \rho\left[\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}\right] = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g}\;} $$ This is the **Euler equation of motion** for an inviscid fluid. ## 3.3 Component (Cartesian) Form of the Navier-Stokes Equation The NSE in Cartesian coordinates is the most heavily tested form. For the x-component: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_x $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU students MUST memorize all three components (x, y, z).** Examiners commonly write down the x-component and ask the student to write the y- and z-components. For the y-component: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial t} + v_x\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} + v_y\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + v_z\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2}v_y}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_y}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_y}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_y $$ For the z-component: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_x\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} + v_y\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2}v_z}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_z}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_z}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_z $$ ## 3.4 Cylindrical and Spherical Forms (Reference Only) > [!NOTE] > For cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z), the r-component of NSE contains a centrifugal term $\rho v_{\theta}^{2}/r$ — a common KTU trick question. The r-component in cylindrical coordinates: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} + v_z\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} - \frac{v_\theta^{2}}{r}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} + \mu\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_r}{\partial \theta^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_r}{\partial z^{2}}\right] + \rho g_r $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Equation | Flow Scenario | Recommended Equation | Why | |---|---|---| | Pipe flow of water | NSE (steady, unidirectional) | Viscous effects dominate | | Flow over a flat plate at low Re | NSE (full) | Boundary layer is viscous | | Air flow over an aircraft wing (far field) | Euler equation | Viscosity negligible outside BL | | Shock wave analysis | Euler equation (1-D) | Inviscid assumption valid | | Lubrication flow | Simplified NSE (Stokes/creeping flow) | Inertial terms negligible | | Microscopic flow in porous media | NSE (creeping flow limit) | Very low Re | | Ideal gas in supersonic nozzle | Euler equation (compressible form) | Inviscid, adiabatic | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Derivation Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Newton's 2nd Law applied to fluid element] --> B[General Equation of Motion: ∂ρv/∂t + ∇·ρvv = -∇p - ∇·τ + ρg] B --> C{Apply incompressibility: ∇·v = 0, ρ = const} C --> D[Substantial derivative emerges: ρ Dv/Dt = -∇p - ∇·τ + ρg] D --> E{Substitute Newtonian stress: τ = -μ[∇v + ∇v†]} E --> F[Evaluate ∇·τ = -μ ∇²v for constant μ] F --> G[NAVIER-STOKES EQUATION: ρ Dv/Dt = -∇p + μ∇²v + ρg] G --> H{Set viscosity μ = 0 for inviscid limit} H --> I[EULER EQUATION: ρ Dv/Dt = -∇p + ρg] G --> J{Integrate along streamline for steady, irrotational flow} J --> K[BERNOULLI EQUATION: p/ρ + v²/2 + gz = const] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Physical Interpretation of Each Term ```mermaid flowchart LR NSE["Navier-Stokes Equation
ρ Dv/Dt = -∇p + μ∇²v + ρg"] NSE --> P1[Inertial term ρ Dv/Dt] NSE --> P2[Pressure force -∇p] NSE --> P3[Viscous diffusion μ∇²v] NSE --> P4[Body force ρg] P1 --> P1a[Local acceleration: ∂v/∂t] P1 --> P1b[Convective acceleration: v·∇v] P3 --> P3a[Origin: molecular momentum transport] P3 --> P3b[Role: smooths velocity gradients] P4 --> P4a[Usually gravity, gx=0, gy=0, gz=-g] P2 --> P2a[Drives fluid from high p to low p] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Term-by-Term Comparison: NSE vs. Euler ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NSE["Navier-Stokes Equation (Viscous)"] A1[ρ Dv/Dt] --> A2[= -∇p] A2 --> A3[+ μ∇²v] A3 --> A4[+ ρg] end subgraph EUL["Euler Equation (Inviscid)"] B1[ρ Dv/Dt] --> B2[= -∇p] B2 --> B3[+ ρg] end NSE ---|Set μ = 0| EUL A3 -. REMOVED .-> B3 ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Hint for Exams:** Draw the NSE → set μ=0 box with a strike-through on the viscous term when deriving the Euler equation. Examiners award full method marks if the "set μ=0" step is clearly shown. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]** **State the Navier-Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity. Explain each term in the equation.** **Model Answer:** The Navier-Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant density and viscosity is: $$ \rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g} $$ Term-wise explanation: - **$\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}$** — Net rate of change of momentum per unit volume (inertial term, includes local + convective acceleration). - **$-\nabla p$** — Pressure-gradient force per unit volume; drives fluid from high-pressure to low-pressure regions. - **$\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}$** — Viscous force per unit volume; represents diffusion of momentum due to molecular motion. - **$\rho\vec{g}$** — Body force per unit volume (gravity). > *(This is a direct 3-mark KTU definition question; expect full marks for stating the equation and naming all four terms.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Differentiate between the Euler equation and the Navier-Stokes equation.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Navier-Stokes Equation | Euler Equation | |---|---|---| | Viscosity | Includes viscous term $\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}$ | Viscous term absent (μ = 0) | | Applicability | Real (viscous) fluids | Ideal (inviscid) fluids | | Mathematical form | $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}=-\nabla p+\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}+\rho\vec{g}$ | $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}=-\nabla p+\rho\vec{g}$ | | Examples | Pipe flow, boundary layer | Flow over airfoil (far field), shock waves | | Difficulty | 2nd-order PDE | 1st-order PDE | The Euler equation is a special case of the NSE for inviscid flow. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 3]** **(a) Starting from the general equation of motion for a fluid, derive the Navier-Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity. State clearly all the assumptions. (7 marks)** **(b) Hence obtain the Euler equation of motion. Using the Euler equation, derive Bernoulli's equation for steady, incompressible, irrotational flow along a streamline. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. Incompressible fluid: $\rho = $ constant → $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$. 2. Newtonian fluid with constant $\mu$. 3. Constant gravitational field $\vec{g}$. 4. Laminar, single-phase, single-component flow. **Step 1:** General equation of motion: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho\vec{v}) + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{v}\vec{v}) = -\nabla p - (\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}) + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step 2:** Apply incompressibility: $$ \rho\frac{\partial\vec{v}}{\partial t} + \rho(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v} = -\nabla p - (\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau}) + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step 3:** For Newtonian fluid: $$ \vec{\tau} = -\mu\left[\nabla\vec{v}+(\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}\right] $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the divergence: $$ \nabla\cdot\vec{\tau} = -\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute: $$ \boxed{\;\rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}\;} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Set μ = 0:** $$ \rho\frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g} \quad \text{(Euler equation)} $$ **Step 2 — Expand the substantial derivative for steady flow:** For steady flow, $\partial \vec{v}/\partial t = 0$. Using the vector identity $(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v} = \nabla(v^{2}/2) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$: $$ \rho[\nabla(v^{2}/2) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})] = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step 3 — Apply irrotationality:** For irrotational flow, $\nabla\times\vec{v} = 0$, so: $$ \rho\nabla(v^{2}/2) = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate:** Let $\vec{g} = -g\hat{k}$ (gravity in -z direction): $$ \nabla\left(\frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} + p + \rho g z\right) = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Bernoulli's equation:** $$ \boxed{\;\frac{v^{2}}{2} + \frac{p}{\rho} + gz = \text{constant along a streamline}\;} $$ --- ### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a) Write the Navier-Stokes equation in vector form and convert it to the x, y, z components in Cartesian coordinates. (7 marks)** **(b) For a steady, incompressible, fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe of radius R, the only non-zero velocity is $v_z = v_z(r)$. Starting from the Navier-Stokes equation, derive the velocity profile $v_z(r)$ and the maximum velocity. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Vector form:** $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g} $$ **Cartesian components:** x-component: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_x}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_x $$ y-component: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial t} + v_x\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} + v_y\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + v_z\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2}v_y}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_y}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_y}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_y $$ z-component: $$ \rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_x\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} + v_y\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2}v_z}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_z}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}v_z}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_z $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Assumptions:** Steady $\left(\partial/\partial t = 0\right)$, incompressible, fully-developed $\left(\partial v_z/\partial z = 0\right)$, axisymmetric $\left(\partial/\partial\theta = 0\right)$, horizontal pipe $\left(g_z = 0\right)$, and $v_r = v_\theta = 0$. **Step 1:** z-component of NSE in cylindrical coordinates: $$ 0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) $$ (All convective and acceleration terms vanish.) **Step 2:** Rearrange: $$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} $$ **Step 3:** Note that for fully-developed flow in a horizontal pipe, $dp/dz = -\Delta p/L = $ constant (pressure-driven flow). **Step 4:** Integrate once: $$ r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}r^{2} + C_1 $$ **Step 5:** Apply boundary condition: $dv_z/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$ (finite, symmetric velocity) → $C_1 = 0$. $$ \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}r $$ **Step 6:** Integrate again: $$ v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}r^{2} + C_2 $$ **Step 7:** Apply no-slip BC: $v_z = 0$ at $r = R$: $$ C_2 = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}R^{2} $$ **Step 8:** Final velocity profile (Hagen-Poiseuille flow): $$ \boxed{\;v_z(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\;} $$ **Step 9:** Maximum velocity at $r = 0$: $$ \boxed{\;v_{z,\max} = -\frac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\;} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. Students often **drop the substantial derivative** $D\vec{v}/Dt$ when first writing NSE — you will lose 2 marks immediately. > 2. Forgetting that $\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$ is required to collapse the dilatational term in the Newtonian stress — loses 1 mark. > 3. In the pipe-flow derivation, **failing to justify why convective terms vanish** (steady + fully-developed + unidirectional) costs a method mark. Always state: "$\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$ and $v_r = v_\theta = 0$". > 4. Sign error: $\dfrac{dp}{dz}$ is **negative** for flow in the +z direction. Always write the final answer with the negative sign made explicit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Starting point** is the *general equation of motion*, valid for any continuum. - [x] **Newtonian fluid** assumption allows substituting $\vec{\tau} = -\mu[\nabla\vec{v} + (\nabla\vec{v})^{\dagger}]$. - [x] **Incompressibility** ($\nabla\cdot\vec{v}=0$) collapses the dilatational term and the continuity equation. - [x] **Constant μ** gives the clean result $\nabla\cdot\vec{\tau} = -\mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v}$. - [x] The **Navier-Stokes equation** is: $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^{2}\vec{v} + \rho\vec{g}$. - [x] The **Euler equation** is the NSE with $\mu = 0$: $\rho\dfrac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g}$. - [x] **Bernoulli's equation** comes from the Euler equation + steady + irrotational assumptions. - [x] The NSE is a **second-order, non-linear PDE** — non-linearity comes from $(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$. - [x] The Euler equation is a **first-order PDE** (pressure appears only as a gradient). - [x] **Physical interpretation of each term:** inertia, pressure force, viscous diffusion, body force. - [x] **KTU favourite questions:** Derive NSE → Euler → Bernoulli (in sequence); or apply NSE to a simple geometry like pipe flow, falling film, or flow between parallel plates. - [x] **Cartesian component forms** must be memorized for all three directions. - [x] **Limiting cases** to remember: μ → 0 (Euler), ρ → 0 (Stokes/creeping flow, inertial terms dropped), steady state (∂/∂t = 0). - [x] **Units check:** Every term in NSE has units of $\text{N/m}^{3}$ (force per unit volume).

topics.title: Transport equations in curvilinear coordinates (no derivation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transport equations in curvilinear coordinates (no derivation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Transport Phenomena**, the three fundamental governing equations — **Continuity (mass)**, **Navier–Stokes (momentum)**, and **Energy (heat)** — are universally expressed in terms of vector operators (`∇`, `∇·`, `∇²`) that are **coordinate-system independent**. However, to obtain physically solvable scalar PDEs, these operators must be **expanded** in the chosen coordinate geometry.

**Curvilinear coordinates** are non-Cartesian orthogonal systems in which the coordinate lines are curves rather than straight lines. The two systems of greatest engineering relevance are:

| Coordinate System | Variables | Typical Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical** | $r,\ \theta,\ z$ | Pipe flow, cylindrical reactors, agitator shafts, heat exchangers |
| **Spherical** | $r,\ \theta,\ \phi$ | Droplets, bubbles, packed beds, catalysis pellets, ionospheric dispersion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2):** Students are expected to **write the final form** of the three transport equations in cylindrical and spherical coordinates. **Derivation from Cartesian form is OUT of scope** — these expanded forms are to be memorized/applied directly.

## 1.2 The Three Scalar/Vector Transport Equations (Vector Form)

Before curvilinear expansion, recall the universal differential forms:

**Continuity (Incompressible Newtonian Fluid):**
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$

**Navier–Stokes (Equation of Motion, constant $\mu$, $\rho$):**
$$\rho \frac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

**Energy Equation (constant $\rho$, $C_p$, $k$):**
$$\rho C_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot{q}_{\text{gen}}$$

where $\dfrac{D}{Dt}=\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}+v_x\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}+v_y\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}+v_z\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$ is the **substantial (material) derivative**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of the vector equations as a *universal business contract* written in English. The contract terms never change (mass, momentum, and energy are always conserved). But to actually execute the contract in **Japan** or **France**, the same words must be **translated** into Japanese or French — the meaning is identical, but the alphabet/grammar is different. Cylindrical and spherical coordinates are simply the "Japanese" and "French" versions of the same conservation contract. The fluid doesn't "know" about coordinates — *we* pick the most convenient language (geometry) to describe it.

## 1.4 Orthogonal Curvilinear Coordinate Geometry

For any orthogonal system $(q_1, q_2, q_3)$ with scale (metric) factors $(h_1, h_2, h_3)$:

- **Cylindrical** $(r, \theta, z)$: $\quad h_1=1,\ h_2=r,\ h_3=1$
- **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$: $\quad h_1=1,\ h_2=r,\ h_3=r\sin\theta$

The **del operator** `∇` in such a system is:
$$\nabla = \hat{e}_1 \frac{1}{h_1}\frac{\partial}{\partial q_1} + \hat{e}_2 \frac{1}{h_2}\frac{\partial}{\partial q_2} + \hat{e}_3 \frac{1}{h_3}\frac{\partial}{\partial q_3}$$

The **Laplacian** of a scalar $\psi$ is:
$$\nabla^2 \psi = \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3}\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial q_1}\!\left(\frac{h_2 h_3}{h_1}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial q_1}\right)+\frac{\partial}{\partial q_2}\!\left(\frac{h_1 h_3}{h_2}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial q_2}\right)+\frac{\partial}{\partial q_3}\!\left(\frac{h_1 h_2}{h_3}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial q_3}\right)\right]$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cylindrical Coordinates $(r,\theta,z)$

Let the velocity components be $v_r,\ v_\theta,\ v_z$ and the unit vectors $\hat{e}_r,\ \hat{e}_\theta,\ \hat{e}_z$.

### (A) Continuity Equation

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\,v_r\right) + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0\;}$$

### (B) Navier–Stokes Equations (r, θ, z components)

**r-component:**
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}-\frac{v_\theta^{2}}{r}+v_z\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z}\right)=-\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}+\rho g_r+\mu\!\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_r}{\partial \theta^{2}}-\frac{2}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta}+\frac{\partial^{2} v_r}{\partial z^{2}}\right]$$

**θ-component:**
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta}+\frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}+v_z\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z}\right)=-\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta}+\rho g_\theta+\mu\!\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_\theta}{\partial \theta^{2}}+\frac{2}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}+\frac{\partial^{2} v_\theta}{\partial z^{2}}\right]$$

**z-component:**
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta}+v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)=-\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}+\rho g_z+\mu\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial \theta^{2}}+\frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial z^{2}}\right]$$

### (C) Energy Equation

$$\rho C_p\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta}+v_z\frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right)=k\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial \theta^{2}}+\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial z^{2}}\right]+\Phi+\dot{q}_{\text{gen}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The viscous dissipation function $\Phi$ in cylindrical coordinates:
> $$\Phi = 2\mu\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r}\right)^{2} + \frac{1}{r}\!\left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}\right)\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{v_r}{r}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}+\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r}-\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right)^{2}\right]$$

---

## 2.2 Spherical Coordinates $(r,\theta,\phi)$

Let the velocity components be $v_r,\ v_\theta,\ v_\phi$ (radial, polar, azimuthal) and the unit vectors $\hat{e}_r,\ \hat{e}_\theta,\ \hat{e}_\phi$.

### (A) Continuity Equation

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2} v_r\right) + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(v_\theta \sin\theta\right) + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi} = 0\;}$$

### (B) Navier–Stokes Equations (r, θ, φ components)

**r-component:**
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}+\frac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \phi}-\frac{v_\theta^{2}+v_\phi^{2}}{r}\right)=-\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}+\rho g_r+\mu\!\left[\nabla^{2}v_r-\frac{2}{r^{2}}v_r-\frac{2}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta}-\frac{2v_\theta\cot\theta}{r^{2}}-\frac{2}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi}\right]$$

**θ-component:**
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta}+\frac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \phi}+\frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}-\frac{v_\phi^{2}\cot\theta}{r}\right)=-\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta}+\rho g_\theta+\mu\!\left[\nabla^{2}v_\theta+\frac{2}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}-\frac{v_\theta}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}-\frac{2\cos\theta}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi}\right]$$

**φ-component:**
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \theta}+\frac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi}+\frac{v_\phi v_r}{r}+\frac{v_\theta v_\phi\cot\theta}{r}\right)=-\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial \phi}+\rho g_\phi+\mu\!\left[\nabla^{2}v_\phi-\frac{v_\phi}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}+\frac{2}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \phi}+\frac{2\cos\theta}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \phi}\right]$$

where
$$\nabla^{2}\psi=\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial \phi^{2}}$$

### (C) Energy Equation

$$\rho C_p\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta}+\frac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \phi}\right)=k\!\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial \phi^{2}}\right]+\Phi+\dot{q}_{\text{gen}}$$

---

## 2.3 🔥 KTU 2024 High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Exam-Revision Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For cylindrical coordinates** — the **$\theta$ and $z$ components are mirror-equivalent** to the Cartesian $x$ and $z$ components **plus centrifugal terms** $\left(\pm\frac{v_\theta^2}{r},\ \pm\frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}\right)$ **and extra Laplacian curvature terms** $\left(\pm\frac{2}{r^2}\frac{\partial(\cdot)}{\partial \theta}\right)$. For **spherical**, additional $\cot\theta$ terms appear because latitude circles shrink to zero at the poles.

| # | Operator | Cylindrical $(r,\theta,z)$ | Spherical $(r,\theta,\phi)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gradient $\nabla\psi$ | $\hat e_r \partial_r\psi + \hat e_\theta \frac{1}{r}\partial_\theta\psi + \hat e_z \partial_z\psi$ | $\hat e_r \partial_r\psi + \hat e_\theta \frac{1}{r}\partial_\theta\psi + \hat e_\phi \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi\psi$ |
| 2 | Divergence $\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v$ | $\frac{1}{r}\partial_r(r v_r)+\frac{1}{r}\partial_\theta v_\theta+\partial_z v_z$ | $\frac{1}{r^{2}}\partial_r(r^{2}v_r)+\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\theta(v_\theta\sin\theta)+\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi v_\phi$ |
| 3 | Laplacian $\nabla^{2}\psi$ | $\frac{1}{r}\partial_r\!\left(r\partial_r\psi\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\partial_{\theta}^{2}\psi+\partial_{z}^{2}\psi$ | $\frac{1}{r^{2}}\partial_r(r^{2}\partial_r\psi)+\frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\partial_\theta(\sin\theta\,\partial_\theta\psi)+\frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\partial_\phi^{2}\psi$ |
| 4 | Continuity | $\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v=0$ | $\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v=0$ |
| 5 | Viscous term in NS | $\mu\nabla^{2}\mathbf v$ | $\mu\nabla^{2}\mathbf v$ (component-wise with curvature corrections) |
| 6 | Conduction term in energy | $k\nabla^{2}T$ | $k\nabla^{2}T$ |
| 7 | Material derivative $D\psi/Dt$ | $\partial_t\psi+v_r\partial_r\psi+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\partial_\theta\psi+v_z\partial_z\psi$ | $\partial_t\psi+v_r\partial_r\psi+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\partial_\theta\psi+\frac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi\psi$ |
| 8 | Gravitational components | $g_r,\ g_\theta,\ g_z$ | $g_r,\ g_\theta,\ g_\phi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - **Cylindrical NS** → solves for fully-developed pipe flow (Hagen–Poiseuille), Couette flow between rotating cylinders (Taylor–Couette), boundary layers on cylinders.
> - **Spherical NS** → solves for flow around raindrops, catalysis on pellets, sedimentation of particles, Stoke's law derivation.
> - **Spherical energy** → critical for temperature distribution in catalyst pellets (Thiele modulus analysis).
> - **Cylindrical energy** → essential for finned-tube heat exchangers, reactor jackets, geothermal boreholes.

---

## 2.4 Common Simplifications (Exam Shortcuts)

| Simplification | Resulting Form |
|---|---|
| **Steady state** | $\partial/\partial t = 0$ |
| **Axisymmetric flow** (cylindrical) | $\partial/\partial \theta = 0,\ v_\theta = 0$ |
| **Fully developed pipe flow** | $v_z = v_z(r)$ only, $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$ |
| **Swirl-free radial flow** | $v_\phi = 0$ |
| **Incompressible + constant $\mu,\rho,C_p,k$** | As given above |
| **Spherical symmetry** | $\partial/\partial\theta = \partial/\partial\phi = 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Students often forget the **$-\dfrac{v_\theta^{2}}{r}$ centrifugal term** in the $r$-component of cylindrical NS — this is the **#1 mark-loser** in KTU valuation of this topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Directive:** *"(no derivation)"* — the curriculum asks for the **final curvilinear forms**, not the chain-rule expansion from Cartesian. The analytical matrix below, however, walks through the **methodology and logic** (without showing tedious vector algebra) so that the student understands *where each term originates* — satisfying outcome-based learning while respecting the syllabus boundary.

## 3.1 Methodology: How a Vector Operator is "Translated" into Curvilinear Form

A vector operator changes when we change coordinates because:

- The **unit vectors themselves** depend on position: $\dfrac{\partial \hat e_r}{\partial \theta}=\hat e_\theta$, $\dfrac{\partial \hat e_\theta}{\partial \theta}=-\hat e_r$, etc.
- **Differential arc-lengths** along each coordinate are $ds_i = h_i\, dq_i$.
- A **chain rule** is applied to each Cartesian partial derivative: $\partial_x = \cos\theta\,\partial_r - \frac{\sin\theta}{r}\partial_\theta$, $\partial_y = \sin\theta\,\partial_r + \frac{\cos\theta}{r}\partial_\theta$.

### Step 1 — Start with the General Orthogonal Form

Using the scale factors $(h_1,h_2,h_3)$ introduced in §1.4, the **gradient**, **divergence**, and **Laplacian** take the standard compact form. The metric terms $h_2 h_3 / h_1$ etc. encode the curvature of the coordinate lines.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Scale Factors

| System | $h_1$ | $h_2$ | $h_3$ | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cylindrical $(r,\theta,z)$ | $1$ | $r$ | $1$ | $h_2 h_3 / h_1 = r$ → generates the $\frac{1}{r}\partial_r(r\,\partial_r\psi)$ term |
| Spherical $(r,\theta,\phi)$ | $1$ | $r$ | $r\sin\theta$ | $h_2 h_3 / h_1 = r^{2}\sin\theta$ → generates the $\frac{1}{r^{2}}\partial_r(r^{2}\,\partial_r\psi)$ term |

### Step 3 — Convective (Inertial) Term: Substantial Derivative Expansion

The substantial derivative $\rho\,\mathbf v\!\cdot\!\nabla\mathbf v$ in vector form contains **products of velocity components and unit-vector derivatives**, which generate the **centrifugal and Coriolis-like terms**.

**Cylindrical (key extra terms):**
$$(\mathbf v\!\cdot\!\nabla)\mathbf v = \left(v_r\partial_r+\tfrac{v_\theta}{r}\partial_\theta+v_z\partial_z\right)\mathbf v - \underbrace{\tfrac{v_\theta^{2}}{r}\,\hat e_r + \tfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}\,\hat e_\theta}_{\text{centrifugal pair}}$$

**Spherical (key extra terms):**
$$(\mathbf v\!\cdot\!\nabla)\mathbf v = \left(v_r\partial_r+\tfrac{v_\theta}{r}\partial_\theta+\tfrac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi\right)\mathbf v - \underbrace{\tfrac{v_\theta^{2}+v_\phi^{2}}{r}\,\hat e_r - \tfrac{v_\phi^{2}\cot\theta}{r}\,\hat e_\theta + \tfrac{v_\theta v_\phi\cot\theta}{r}\,\hat e_\phi}_{\text{centrifugal + Coriolis triplet}}$$

### Step 4 — Viscous Term: Vector Laplacian with Curvature Corrections

The viscous force per unit volume $\mu\nabla^{2}\mathbf v$ in a curvilinear system is **not** simply the component-wise scalar Laplacian — extra terms arise from differentiating the unit vectors. The general result is:

$$\mu\nabla^{2}\mathbf v = \mu\left[\nabla^{2}v_1\,\hat e_1 + \nabla^{2}v_2\,\hat e_2 + \nabla^{2}v_3\,\hat e_3\right] + \text{curvature corrections}$$

**Cylindrical correction terms:**
- In $r$-component: $+\dfrac{2\mu}{r^{2}}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta}-\dfrac{2\mu v_r}{r^{2}}$ (note: the latter is often absorbed into the rearranged form)
- In $\theta$-component: $-\dfrac{2\mu}{r^{2}}\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}-\dfrac{\mu v_\theta}{r^{2}}$ (similarly rearranged)

**Spherical correction terms:** those proportional to $1/r^{2}$ and $\cot\theta$ listed in §2.2.

### Step 5 — Assemble Each Component Equation

For each coordinate direction:

1. Write LHS = $\rho \dfrac{D v_i}{Dt}$ using §3.1 Step 3.
2. Write pressure gradient: $\hat e_i \cdot \nabla p$.
3. Write gravity: $\rho g_i$.
4. Write viscous term: $\mu\nabla^{2}v_i$ + curvature corrections from §3.1 Step 4.
5. Set LHS = RHS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No derivation is required at the exam** — students must simply **write the final assembled forms** given in §2.1 and §2.2. Memorization is permitted; the *understanding* tested is "which term to drop/keep under which symmetry."

## 3.2 Worked Mini-Example — Continuity in Spherical Coordinates

> **Problem:** Starting from $\nabla \cdot \mathbf v = 0$, expand it in spherical coordinates by replacing the general divergence with the scale-factor form.

**Step 1 — Write the general orthogonal divergence formula.**

$$\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v = \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3}\!\left[\frac{\partial (h_2 h_3 v_1)}{\partial q_1}+\frac{\partial (h_1 h_3 v_2)}{\partial q_2}+\frac{\partial (h_1 h_2 v_3)}{\partial q_3}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Insert spherical scale factors and velocity components.**

$$\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v = \frac{1}{(1)(r)(r\sin\theta)}\!\left[\frac{\partial (r\cdot r\sin\theta \cdot v_r)}{\partial r}+\frac{\partial (1\cdot r\sin\theta \cdot v_\theta)}{\partial \theta}+\frac{\partial (1\cdot r \cdot v_\phi)}{\partial \phi}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constant product $h_2 h_3 = r^{2}\sin\theta$.**

$$\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v = \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\!\left[\frac{\partial (r^{2}\sin\theta\,v_r)}{\partial r}+\frac{\partial (r\sin\theta\,v_\theta)}{\partial \theta}+\frac{\partial (r\,v_\phi)}{\partial \phi}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Carry the $\sin\theta$ outside the $r$-derivative (constant w.r.t. $r$) and rearrange.**

$$\nabla\!\cdot\!\mathbf v = \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial (r^{2} v_r)}{\partial r}+\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial (v_\theta\sin\theta)}{\partial \theta}+\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi}$$

**Step 5 — Set equal to zero for incompressible flow.** ✅ Matches §2.2(A).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Form

| Physical Situation | Coordinate Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Flow in straight pipe | Cylindrical | Geometry matches pipe axis |
| Flow around cylinder | Cylindrical | Curved surface ⇒ $r$-constant |
| Flow around sphere/drop | Spherical | Geometry matches droplet |
| Boundary layer on flat plate | Cartesian | Geometry is planar |
| Sedimentation velocity | Spherical | Particle is spherical |
| Helical flow in coil | Cylindrical with $v_r=v_\theta=0,\ v_z=v_z(r,\theta)$ | Twist handled via $\theta$-dependence |
| Rotating shaft (Couette) | Cylindrical | Rotational symmetry |
| Catalyst pellet (diffusion/reaction) | Spherical | Pellets are spherical |
| Heat fin of circular cross-section | Cylindrical | $T=T(r)$ in 1-D fin analysis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coordinate Geometry Schematic

The following functional architecture flow shows how a generic vector equation is **decomposed** into the curvilinear forms, and the **decision logic** for choosing between cylindrical and spherical systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Universal Vector Transport Equation<br/>Continuity / Navier-Stokes / Energy"] --> B{"Which physical<br/>geometry?"}
    B -- "Cylindrical / pipe / vessel / shaft" --> C["Cylindrical Coordinates<br/>(r, theta, z)<br/>h1=1, h2=r, h3=1"]
    B -- "Spherical / droplet / pellet / bubble" --> D["Spherical Coordinates<br/>(r, theta, phi)<br/>h1=1, h2=r, h3=r sin theta"]
    C --> E["Expand operators using<br/>scale factors h1 h2 h3"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Apply symmetry & BCs<br/>(steady / axisymmetric / fully developed)"]
    F --> G["Reduced scalar PDEs<br/>solve analytically or numerically"]
    G --> H["Physical Quantities<br/>v profile, p profile, T profile"]

    style A fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b1b30,color:#000
    style B fill:#cddafd,stroke:#3a4a8a,color:#000

```

        P2["Azimuthal direction theta<br/>(h2 = r)<br/>Dtheta = r x dtheta"]
        P3["Axial direction z<br/>(h3 = 1)<br/>Dz = 1 x dz"]
        P4["Velocity components<br/>vr, vtheta, vz"]
        P5["Pressure gradient<br/>d p / d r,  1/r x d p / d theta,  d p / d z"]

        Q7["Curvature / cot theta terms<br/>present in NS and energy equations"]
