# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024 Model Paper Style]**

### Q1. **Define orthogonal curvilinear coordinates and state the scale factors for cylindrical and spherical systems.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO2, Remembering]`

**Model Answer:**
Orthogonal curvilinear coordinates are coordinate systems in which the three coordinate axes are mutually perpendicular at every point, but the coordinate surfaces are curved. Each coordinate $q_i$ has an associated **scale (metric) factor** $h_i$ such that the differential arc-length is $ds_i = h_i\, dq_i$.

- **Cylindrical** $(r, \theta, z)$: $\quad h_1=1,\ h_2=r,\ h_3=1$
- **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$: $\quad h_1=1,\ h_2=r,\ h_3=r\sin\theta$

---

### Q2. **State the continuity equation for an incompressible fluid in cylindrical coordinates.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO2, Remembering]`

**Model Answer:**
For an incompressible Newtonian fluid, mass conservation gives $\nabla \cdot \mathbf v = 0$. In cylindrical coordinates this expands to:
$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$
This is the **cylindrical form of the continuity equation** and is the starting point for solving all cylindrical flow problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern (Module Internal Choice): Answer ONE 14-mark question out of two alternatives.**

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]`

> **Q. (a)** Write down the **continuity equation**, the three **Navier–Stokes equations** (r, θ, z components), and the **energy equation** for a Newtonian fluid with constant $\mu$, $\rho$, $C_p$, $k$ in **cylindrical coordinates**. Clearly define all symbols used. **OR — alternative derivation approach:** *Starting from the vector form and using the scale factors, justify the appearance of the $v_\theta^2/r$ centrifugal term in the $r$-momentum equation.* `[7 Marks]` `[CO2, Applying]`
>
> **Q. (b)** A viscous fluid flows in a fully developed, steady, axisymmetric manner in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ under a constant pressure gradient $\partial p / \partial z = -G$. Using the cylindrical Navier–Stokes $z$-component, derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille velocity profile** $v_z(r)$ and compute the **average velocity**. **OR — alternative:** *Briefly explain why the centrifugal term $-v_\theta^2/r$ vanishes in this case.* `[7 Marks]` `[CO3, Analysing]`

#### Model Solution — Q(a):

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation:** (write directly from §2.1(A))
$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Navier–Stokes r-component** (write from §2.1(B)):
$$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}-\frac{v_\theta^{2}}{r}+v_z\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z}\right)=-\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}+\rho g_r+\mu\!\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} v_r}{\partial \theta^{2}}-\frac{2}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta}+\frac{\partial^{2} v_r}{\partial z^{2}}\right]$$

Similarly write θ and z components.

**Step 3 — Energy Equation** (write from §2.1(C)):
$$\rho C_p\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}+v_r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}+\frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta}+v_z\frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right)=k\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)+\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial \theta^{2}}+\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial z^{2}}\right]+\Phi+\dot{q}_{\text{gen}}$$

**Justification of the $v_\theta^2/r$ term:** The convective acceleration $\rho(\mathbf v \cdot \nabla)\mathbf v$ contains, for the $\hat e_r$ direction, the contribution $-\rho v_\theta^2/r$. Physically this represents the **centrifugal force per unit volume** experienced by a fluid element moving along a curved streamline of radius $r$ with tangential velocity $v_\theta$. **OR alternative derivation approach:** Using the chain rule on $\partial \hat e_\theta / \partial \theta = -\hat e_r$, the product $v_\theta \partial(v_\theta \hat e_\theta)/\partial\theta$ produces a $-\hat e_r$ component whose magnitude is $v_\theta^2/r$.

#### Model Solution — Q(b):

**Step 1 — Apply physical assumptions:**

- Steady state: $\partial/\partial t = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\partial/\partial \theta = 0$
- Fully developed: $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$
- Unidirectional: $v_r = v_\theta = 0$, so $v_z = v_z(r)$ only
- Constant $p$ gradient in $r$ and $\theta$: $\partial p/\partial r = \partial p/\partial \theta = 0$
- Horizontal pipe: $g_z = 0$

**Step 2 — Continuity check:** $0 + 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓ (trivially satisfied).

**Step 3 — Simplify the z-momentum equation** (most terms drop):
$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{d v_z}{dr}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate once** (multiply by $r/\mu$):
$$r\frac{d p}{d z} = \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{d v_z}{dr}\right)$$

Integrating with respect to $r$ (note $\partial p/\partial z = -G$ is constant):
$$-\frac{G r^{2}}{2} = r\frac{d v_z}{dr} + C_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{d v_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r}{2\mu} + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary condition at $r=0$** (finite velocity ⇒ $C_1 = 0$):
$$\frac{d v_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r}{2\mu}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate again:**
$$v_z(r) = -\frac{G r^{2}}{4\mu} + C_2$$

**Step 7 — Apply no-slip BC at $r = R$:** $v_z(R) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = GR^{2}/(4\mu)$

$$\boxed{\;v_z(r) = \frac{G}{4\mu}\!\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\;}$$

**Step 8 — Average velocity:**
$$\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{1}{\pi R^{2}}\int_{0}^{R} v_z(r)\cdot 2\pi r\, dr = \frac{2}{R^{2}}\int_{0}^{R}\frac{G}{4\mu}(R^{2}-r^{2})\,r\,dr$$
$$= \frac{G}{2\mu R^{2}}\left[\frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2}-\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{G}{2\mu R^{2}}\cdot\frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{G R^{2}}{8\mu}$$

$$\boxed{\;\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{G R^{2}}{8\mu}\;}$$

**Alternative (centrifugal term explanation):** In this problem $v_\theta = 0$, so the centrifugal term $-v_\theta^2/r = 0$ in the r-momentum equation. Physically, fluid moves only in the $z$ direction in straight, parallel streamlines — no rotation around the pipe axis occurs, hence no centrifugal effect.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]`

> **Q. (a)** Write down the **continuity equation** and the three **Navier–Stokes equations** (r, θ, φ components) for a Newtonian fluid with constant $\mu$ and $\rho$ in **spherical coordinates**. Identify and explain the **curvature / cot θ terms** that appear (which are absent in cylindrical form). **OR — alternative:** *Briefly discuss the physical origin of the $-(v_\theta^2 + v_\phi^2)/r$ term in the r-momentum equation.* `[7 Marks]` `[CO2, Applying]`
>
> **Q. (b)** Consider steady, incompressible, **purely radial** flow of a Newtonian fluid towards a sphere of radius $a$ (e.g., flow into a sink). Use the spherical continuity and Navier–Stokes $r$-component to obtain the **radial velocity profile** $v_r(r)$ and the **pressure distribution** $p(r)$. Assume body forces are negligible. **OR — alternative:** *Show that for a sphere moving in a fluid, the drag force can be derived from such a radial analysis.* `[7 Marks]` `[CO3, Analysing]`

#### Model Solution — Q(a):

**Step 1 — Write continuity** (from §2.2(A)):
$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial (r^{2} v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial (v_\theta \sin\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Write all three Navier–Stokes components** (from §2.2(B)): write $r$, $\theta$, $\phi$ components fully.

**Step 3 — Identify curvature/cot θ terms:**

- In the **$r$-component**: $-\dfrac{2v_\theta \cot\theta}{r^{2}}$ arises from the differentiation of $\hat e_\theta$ components with respect to $\theta$ (since $\partial\hat e_r/\partial\theta = \hat e_\theta$, $\partial\hat e_\theta/\partial\theta = -\hat e_r$). The $\cot\theta$ factor appears because **latitude circles shrink as $\theta \to 0$ (north pole)** and **expand as $\theta \to \pi/2$ (equator)**.

- In the **$\theta$-component**: $\dfrac{2\cos\theta}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\dfrac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi}$ is the curvature correction analogous to the cylindrical $\dfrac{2}{r^{2}}\dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}$ but further modulated by $\sin\theta$ in the denominator.

- In the **$\phi$-component**: $\dfrac{2\cos\theta}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \phi}$ and $\dfrac{v_\theta v_\phi \cot\theta}{r}$ are the **Coriolis-type** terms.

**Justification of $(v_\theta^2 + v_\phi^2)/r$ term:** A fluid element moving tangentially to a sphere of radius $r$ with combined tangential speed $\sqrt{v_\theta^2 + v_\phi^2}$ experiences an inward centrifugal force per unit mass of $v_{\text{tan}}^{2}/r$. This contributes a $-v_{\text{tan}}^{2}/r$ acceleration in the $\hat e_r$ direction, hence the term $-v_\theta^2 - v_\phi^2$ over $r$ in the r-momentum equation.

#### Model Solution — Q(b):

**Step 1 — Apply assumptions:**

- Steady state: $\partial/\partial t = 0$
- Spherical symmetry: $\partial/\partial \theta = \partial/\partial \phi = 0$
- Purely radial: $v_\theta = v_\phi = 0$, so $v_r = v_r(r)$ only
- No body forces: $g_r = 0$

**Step 2 — Continuity equation simplifies:**
$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{d (r^{2} v_r)}{dr} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; r^{2} v_r = \text{constant} = Q$$

where $Q$ is the **volumetric flow rate** (per unit solid angle × scaling).

**Step 3 — Velocity profile:** Let $Q = A$ (constant).

$$\boxed{\;v_r(r) = \frac{A}{r^{2}}\;}$$

For flow **into** a sphere at rate $Q_{\text{tot}}$ over the full $4\pi$ solid angle: $A = -Q_{\text{tot}}/(4\pi)$, so $v_r(r) = -\dfrac{Q_{\text{tot}}}{4\pi r^{2}}$ (negative = inward).

**Step 4 — Simplify r-momentum equation:**

Most terms vanish. The viscous term in spherical coordinates for purely radial flow reduces to:
$$\mu\left[\frac{d^{2} v_r}{dr^{2}}+\frac{2}{r}\frac{d v_r}{dr}-\frac{2 v_r}{r^{2}}\right]$$

Substitute $v_r = A/r^{2}$:
$$\frac{d v_r}{dr} = -\frac{2A}{r^{3}}, \quad \frac{d^{2} v_r}{dr^{2}} = \frac{6A}{r^{4}}$$
$$\mu\left[\frac{6A}{r^{4}}+\frac{2}{r}\!\left(-\frac{2A}{r^{3}}\right)-\frac{2}{r^{2}}\!\left(\frac{A}{r^{2}}\right)\right] = \mu\left[\frac{6A}{r^{4}}-\frac{4A}{r^{4}}-\frac{2A}{r^{4}}\right] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Viscous term vanishes** (Euler / inviscid flow possible) — a Stokes-flow paradox. The r-momentum equation reduces to:
$$0 = -\frac{d p}{dr} \;\Rightarrow\; p = \text{constant} = p_\infty$$

**Step 6 — Pressure distribution:**
$$\boxed{\;p(r) = p_\infty \quad \text{(uniform)}\;}$$

**Alternative (Stoke's drag connection):** The uniform pressure and inviscid nature of purely radial flow implies **zero drag** — this is **d'Alembert's paradox**. Real drag arises only when the full Navier–Stokes equations with **tangential velocity components** are considered, leading to the famous **Stokes' law** $F_D = 6\pi\mu a U$ for a sphere of radius $a$ moving at velocity $U$ in a viscous fluid (derived from the $v_\theta$ component, not $v_r$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $v_\theta^2/r$ centrifugal term** in the cylindrical $r$-momentum equation → **−3 marks**
> 2. **Forgetting the $(v_\theta^2 + v_\phi^2)/r$ term** in the spherical $r$-momentum equation → **−3 marks**
> 3. **Confusing $\phi$ (azimuthal) and $\theta$ (polar)** in spherical notation — KTU uses $(\theta, \phi)$ where some textbooks use $(\phi, \theta)$ → **−2 marks**
> 4. **Missing $\cot\theta$ factors** in spherical viscous terms → **−2 marks**
> 5. **Writing $\nabla^2$ as if it were Cartesian** (no $1/r$ or $1/r^2$ prefactors) → **−4 marks**
> 6. **In the energy equation, forgetting the material derivative convective terms** $v_r\partial T/\partial r + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\partial T/\partial\theta + \cdots$ → **−2 marks**
> 7. **Mixing up scale factors**: writing $h_2 = r\sin\theta$ for cylindrical (should be just $r$) → **−1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Curvilinear Transport Equations):**

- ⭐ **Always write the three equation sets: continuity → Navier–Stokes (all 3 components) → energy.** This is the KTU-mandated structure.
- ⭐ **Cylindrical scale factors:** $h_1 = 1,\ h_2 = r,\ h_3 = 1$. **Spherical:** $h_1 = 1,\ h_2 = r,\ h_3 = r\sin\theta$.
- ⭐ **Continuity** is the **simplest** curvilinear equation — start with it; it often **decouples** velocity components.
- ⭐ **Centrifugal terms** in NS: cylindrical has $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$ (r-comp) and $+\dfrac{v_r v_\theta}{r}$ (θ-comp); spherical has $-\dfrac{v_\theta^2 + v_\phi^2}{r}$ (r-comp), $-\dfrac{v_\phi^2\cot\theta}{r}$ (θ-comp), $+\dfrac{v_\theta v_\phi\cot\theta}{r}$ (φ-comp).
- ⭐ **Laplacian** (scalar): cylindrical uses $\dfrac{1}{r}\partial_r(r\,\partial_r\psi)+\dfrac{1}{r^2}\partial_\theta^2\psi+\partial_z^2\psi$; spherical uses $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\partial_r(r^2\,\partial_r\psi)+\dfrac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\partial_\theta(\sin\theta\,\partial_\theta\psi)+\dfrac{1}{r^2\sin^2\theta}\partial_\phi^2\psi$.
- ⭐ **Material derivative $D/Dt$** in curvilinear form is **NOT** simply $\partial_t + v_x\partial_x + \cdots$ — it has $\dfrac{v_\theta}{r}\partial_\theta$ and $\dfrac{v_\phi}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi$ scaling.
- ⭐ **Assumption shortcuts** (steasy/axisymmetric/fully-developed/spherical-symmetric) **kill** many terms — apply them **after** writing the full equation, not before.
- ⭐ **Symbol conventions:** $(r, \theta, z)$ for cylindrical; $(r, \theta, \phi)$ for spherical (KTU standard). Velocity components: $(v_r, v_\theta, v_z)$ and $(v_r, v_\theta, v_\phi)$ respectively.
- ⭐ **Engineering rule of thumb:** if the problem geometry has a **central axis or straight cylinder** → cylindrical. If it has a **central point and spherical surface** → spherical.
- ⭐ **Bonus: Hagen–Poiseuille** $\langle v_z\rangle = GR^2/(8\mu)$ and **Stokes drag** $F_D = 6\pi\mu a U$ are the **two most-tested end results** derivable from these equations.
- ⭐ **For "no derivation" type questions:** Just **write the final equations** from §2.1 and §2.2 — do not attempt to derive. Marks are awarded for **correctness and completeness** of the expanded forms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of transport equations to steady flow problems - flow through tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application of transport equations to steady flow problems - flow through tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady Flow Through a Tube (Hagen-Poiseuille Flow):** The fully-developed, laminar, axisymmetric, Newtonian flow of an incompressible fluid through a cylindrical tube of constant cross-section under a constant imposed pressure gradient. It represents the canonical application of the **equation of motion (momentum transport equation)** in cylindrical coordinates.

The governing equation is obtained by simplifying the **general equation of motion** (Navier–Stokes equation) under the following constraints:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \mathbf{v} = v_z(r)\hat{\mathbf{e}}_z, \quad v_r = v_\theta = 0
$$

The fluid is assumed **Newtonian** with constant viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho$.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a crowded highway tunnel.** Cars enter from one end and exit from the other. In the center lane, cars move fast (free of wall friction). Cars near the tunnel walls slow down because they brush against the concrete (this is **viscous drag / no-slip condition**). A traffic police officer at the entrance maintains a steady flow by controlling the inflow rate (analogous to the **pressure gradient**). The tunnel has circular cross-section, steady inflow, and no cars entering sideways — exactly the conditions of flow through a tube!

The flow rate you can push through the tunnel depends *quadratically* on the number of lanes... wait, actually for a circular tube it depends on the **fourth power of the radius** $R^4$, which is why even a small increase in pipe diameter dramatically boosts the throughput.

---

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| $\rho$ | Density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $R$ | Tube radius | $\text{m}$ |
| $L$ | Tube length | $\text{m}$ |
| $\Delta P$ | Pressure drop across length $L$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $g_c$ | Newton's-law proportionality factor | $\text{1 (SI)}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Logical Reduction

**Step 1 — Start from the general equation of motion in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$
\rho\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla\mathbf{v}\right) = -\nabla P + \mu\nabla^2\mathbf{v} + \rho\mathbf{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the physical assumptions one-by-one:**

- **Steady state:** $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{v}}{\partial t} = 0$
- **Unidirectional flow:** $\mathbf{v} = (0,\,0,\,v_z(r))$ — only the $z$-component is non-zero, and it depends only on $r$.
- **Constant density:** $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ is automatically satisfied.
- **Neglect gravity** (horizontal pipe or absorbed into $P$).
- **Fully developed flow:** $\dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$.

**Step 3 — The z-component collapses to a single ODE:**

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions (BCs):**

1. **No-slip at the wall:** $\;v_z(r=R) = 0$
2. **Symmetry / finite velocity at center:** $\;\dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg|_{r=0} = 0$

**Step 5 — Integrate twice** to obtain the famous **parabolic velocity profile** (Poiseuille profile).

---

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the boxed equations — these appear in **every KTU exam**.

| # | Quantity | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity profile | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | Parabolic |
| 2 | Maximum velocity (centerline) | $v_{z,\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P\,R^2}{4\mu L}$ | At $r = 0$ |
| 3 | Average velocity | $\langle v_z \rangle = \dfrac{\Delta P\,R^2}{8\mu L} = \dfrac{v_{z,\max}}{2}$ | Half of max |
| 4 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ | **Hagen–Poiseuille Law** |
| 5 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \rho \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ | — |
| 6 | Shear stress distribution | $\tau_{rz}(r) = -\dfrac{r\,\Delta P}{2L}$ | Linear in $r$ |
| 7 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = -\tau_{rz}\vert_{r=R} = \dfrac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}$ | Useful for friction |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \langle v_z \rangle D}{\mu} = \dfrac{2\rho \langle v_z \rangle R}{\mu}$ | $D = 2R$ |
| 9 | Fanning friction factor | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | Laminar only |
| 10 | Kinetic energy correction | $\alpha = 2$ | For parabolic profile |

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Chemical engineering:** Sizing pumps, calculating pressure drop in long pipelines, designing heat-exchanger tube banks.
> - **Biomedical:** Modeling blood flow in capillaries and arteries (with non-Newtonian corrections).
> - **Process industries:** Determining flow rates in polymer processing, lubrication systems, and microfluidics devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Velocity Profile from Equation of Motion

#### Step 3.1: Write the z-component of the equation of motion in cylindrical coordinates

The complete z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$
\rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z^2}\right] + \rho g_z
$$

#### Step 3.2: Apply the simplifying assumptions

- Steady flow: $\partial v_z / \partial t = 0$
- Only $v_z(r)$ is non-zero, hence $v_r = v_\theta = 0$
- Fully developed: $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\partial v_z/\partial \theta = 0$
- Horizontal pipe: $g_z = 0$

Every term on the left side vanishes. The result is:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)
$$

#### Step 3.3: Use the fact that $P = P(z)$ only

For fully-developed flow, the pressure depends only on $z$. We replace $\partial P/\partial z$ by the constant pressure gradient over length $L$:

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \frac{P(L) - P(0)}{L} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

Substituting:

$$
0 = \frac{\Delta P}{L} + \mu\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}
$$

#### Step 3.4: First integration (multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate)

$$
r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r^2 + C_1
$$

Apply **BC-2:** $\dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg|_{r=0}$ is finite $\Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

$$
r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r^2
$$

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r
$$

#### Step 3.5: Second integration

$$
v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\,r^2 + C_2
$$

Apply **BC-1:** $v_z(R) = 0$:

$$
0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\,R^2 + C_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_2 = \frac{\Delta P\,R^2}{4\mu L}
$$

#### Step 3.6: Final velocity profile

$$
\boxed{\,v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)\,}
$$

---

### Derivation 2 — Average Velocity and Volumetric Flow Rate

The average velocity is defined by:

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{1}{A}\int_A v_z \, dA = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^R v_z(r)\,r\,dr\,d\theta
$$

Since $v_z$ does not depend on $\theta$:

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\cdot 2\pi \int_0^R v_z(r)\,r\,dr
$$

Substituting the velocity profile:

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{2}{R^2}\int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) r\,dr
$$

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L\, R^2}\int_0^R \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\int_0^R \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{\Delta P\,R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{1}{2}v_{z,\max}\,}
$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q = \langle v_z \rangle \cdot \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}\,}\quad\text{(Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)}
$$

---

### Derivation 3 — Shear Stress Distribution

The viscous shear stress for Newtonian fluid in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}
$$

From the derivative obtained earlier:

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r
$$

Substituting:

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) = -\mu\left(-\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r\right) = \frac{r\,\Delta P}{2L}
$$

The minus sign convention of momentum flux (negative of stress on +z face) gives:

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) = -\frac{r\,\Delta P}{2L}
$$

Wall shear stress at $r = R$:

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_w = \frac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}\,}
$$

---

### Derivation 4 — Fanning Friction Factor

The Fanning friction factor relates wall shear stress to kinetic energy per unit volume:

$$
f = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho \langle v_z\rangle^2}
$$

Substituting $\tau_w$ and $\langle v_z \rangle$:

$$
f = \frac{R\,\Delta P / (2L)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left[\Delta P\,R^2/(8\mu L)\right]^2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
f = \frac{16\mu}{\rho \langle v_z\rangle D} = \frac{16}{Re}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f = \frac{16}{Re}\,}\quad\text{(valid for laminar flow only)}
$$

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Profile | Centerline velocity | Wall velocity | $\langle v_z \rangle / v_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow through circular tube | $v_{\max}$ | 0 | 0.5 |
| Flow between parallel plates | $v_{\max}$ | 0 | 2/3 |
| Flow in annular region | depends on $k = R_i/R_o$ | 0 | $< 0.5$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Physical Setup of Tube Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reservoir at high pressure P1"] --> B["Inlet cross-section, z = 0"]
    B --> C["Cylindrical tube of radius R and length L"]
    C --> D["Outlet cross-section, z = L, lower pressure P2"]
    D --> E["Reservoir at low pressure P2"]
    F["No-slip condition: v_z = 0 at r = R"] -.-> C
    G["Maximum velocity v_max at r = 0"] -.-> C
    H["Pressure gradient drives flow"] -.-> C
```

### Diagram 2 — Velocity Profile Parabola

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["At r = 0: v_z = v_max (centerline, fastest)"] --> P2["At r = R/2: v_z = 0.75 v_max"]
    P2 --> P3["At r = R: v_z = 0 (no-slip at wall)"]
    P4["Parabolic shape: v_z proportional to R squared minus r squared"] --> P1
    P4 --> P2
    P4 --> P3
```

### Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["General Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates"] --> B["Apply assumptions: steady, incompressible, Newtonian, fully developed, axisymmetric"]
    B --> C["Reduced ODE: mu times d/dr of r dvz/dr equals dP/dz"]
    C --> D["Integrate once: r dvz/dr equals negative dP over 2 mu L times r squared plus C1"]
    D --> E["Apply BC: dvz/dr finite at r = 0, so C1 = 0"]
    E --> F["Integrate second time: vz equals negative dP over 4 mu L times r squared plus C2"]
    F --> G["Apply BC: vz = 0 at r = R, so C2 equals dP R squared over 4 mu L"]
    G --> H["Velocity profile: vz equals dP over 4 mu L times R squared minus r squared"]
    H --> I["Integrate across area: average velocity = dP R squared over 8 mu L"]
    I --> J["Volumetric flow rate: Q = pi R to the 4th dP over 8 mu L"]
    H --> K["Differentiate: tau rz equals negative r dP over 2 L"]
    K --> L["Wall shear stress: tau w = R dP over 2 L"]
    J --> M["Fanning friction factor: f = 16 over Re"]
```

### Diagram 4 — Force Balance on Fluid Element in Tube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pressure P at z"] --> B["Fluid cylindrical element of length dz and radius r"]
    B --> C["Pressure P plus dP at z plus dz"]
    D["Viscous shear tau rz on curved surface"] -.-> B
    E["Net force in z direction"] --> F["dP over dz times pi r squared dz plus tau rz times 2 pi r dz"]
    F --> G["For steady uniform flow: net force = 0"]
    G --> H["Solve: tau rz equals negative r over 2 times dP over dz"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the assumptions made in deriving the velocity profile for steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular tube.*

**Model Answer:**

The key assumptions are:

1. **Steady state:** Flow properties do not change with time, $\partial / \partial t = 0$.
2. **Incompressible flow:** Density $\rho$ is constant.
3. **Newtonian fluid:** Constant viscosity $\mu$, obeys Newton's law of viscosity.
4. **Laminar, fully developed flow:** Velocity profile is unchanging along the length, $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$.
5. **Unidirectional:** Only $v_z$ is non-zero; $v_r = v_\theta = 0$.
6. **Axisymmetric:** No $\theta$-dependence.
7. **Constant pressure gradient:** $\partial P / \partial z$ is constant along the tube.
8. **No-slip at the wall:** $v_z(r = R) = 0$.
9. **Negligible gravity effect** (or absorbed into modified pressure).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Fanning friction factor. Write its expression for laminar flow through a circular tube.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Fanning friction factor** $f$ is a dimensionless number that relates the wall shear stress to the kinetic energy per unit volume of the flowing fluid:

$$
f = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho \langle v_z \rangle^2}
$$

For steady, laminar Newtonian flow through a circular tube:

$$
\boxed{\,f = \frac{16}{Re}\,}
$$

where $Re = \dfrac{\rho \langle v_z \rangle D}{\mu}$ is the Reynolds number based on tube diameter.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Starting from the general equation of motion in cylindrical coordinates, derive the velocity profile for the steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular tube under a constant pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$. Clearly state all assumptions and boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the flow described in part (a), derive expressions for:
- (i) the average velocity,
- (ii) the volumetric flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille equation), and
- (iii) the wall shear stress. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Velocity Profile Derivation**

The z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
\rho\left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z^2}\right] + \rho g_z
$$

**Assumptions applied:**

| Assumption | Mathematical Consequence |
|---|---|
| Steady state | $\partial v_z/\partial t = 0$ |
| Newtonian, incompressible | Constant $\mu$, $\rho$ |
| Unidirectional | $v_r = v_\theta = 0$ |
| Axisymmetric | $\partial v_z/\partial \theta = 0$ |
| Fully developed | $\partial v_z/\partial z = 0$ |
| Horizontal | $g_z = 0$ |

The equation collapses to:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)
$$

For fully developed flow, $\partial P/\partial z = -\Delta P / L$ is constant. Rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}
$$

**First integration** (multiply by $r$ and integrate):

$$
r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r^2 + C_1
$$

**Apply BC-1** (finite velocity at centerline, $dv_z/dr$ finite at $r = 0$):
This forces $C_1 = 0$.

**Second integration:**

$$
v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\,r^2 + C_2
$$

**Apply BC-2** (no-slip at wall, $v_z(R) = 0$):
Solving gives $C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P\,R^2}{4\mu L}$.

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)\,}
$$

The velocity profile is **parabolic**, with the maximum at the centerline $r = 0$ and zero at the wall $r = R$.

---

**Part (b) — Average Velocity, Flow Rate, and Wall Shear**

**(i) Average velocity:**

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R v_z(r)\,(2\pi r)\,dr = \frac{2}{R^2}\int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)\,r\,dr
$$

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L\,R^2}\left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\Delta P\,R^2}{8\mu L}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = \langle v_z\rangle \cdot \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}
$$

**(iii) Wall shear stress:**

From $\tau_{rz} = -\mu\,dv_z/dr$ and $dv_z/dr = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,r$:

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) = \frac{r\,\Delta P}{2L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tau_w = \tau_{rz}(R) = \frac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] — Alternative Choice`

**(a)** For the steady laminar flow of an oil ($\mu = 0.1 \,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\rho = 900 \,\text{kg/m}^3$) through a horizontal circular tube of diameter $D = 0.05\,\text{m}$ and length $L = 10\,\text{m}$, the pressure drop is $\Delta P = 50\,\text{kPa}$.
Calculate:
- (i) the maximum velocity at the centerline,
- (ii) the average velocity,
- (iii) the volumetric flow rate, and
- (iv) the Fanning friction factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the physical significance of the Hagen–Poiseuille equation. Explain why this equation **fails** for non-Newtonian fluids and for turbulent flow. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Numerical Computations**

Given: $\mu = 0.1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\rho = 900\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $D = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $R = 0.025\,\text{m}$, $L = 10\,\text{m}$, $\Delta P = 50{,}000\,\text{Pa}$.

**(i) Maximum velocity at centerline:**

$$
v_{z,\max} = \frac{\Delta P\,R^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{50{,}000 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times 0.1 \times 10} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 6.25\times 10^{-4}}{4}
$$

$$
v_{z,\max} = \frac{31.25}{4} = 7.8125 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Average velocity:**

$$
\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{v_{z,\max}}{2} = \frac{7.8125}{2} = 3.906 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = \langle v_z \rangle \cdot \pi R^2 = 3.906 \times \pi \times (0.025)^2 = 3.906 \times 1.9635\times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
Q = 7.67 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \;\;\approx\; 7.67\,\text{L/s}
$$

**(iv) Fanning friction factor:**

Reynolds number:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \langle v_z\rangle D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 3.906 \times 0.05}{0.1} = 1757.8
$$

Fanning friction factor:

$$
f = \frac{16}{Re} = \frac{16}{1757.8} = 9.10 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Since $Re < 2100$, the flow is **laminar**, validating our use of $f = 16/Re$.

---

**Part (b) — Discussion**

**Physical significance of Hagen–Poiseuille equation:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}
$$

1. It establishes that **flow rate is proportional to the fourth power of the radius** — doubling the diameter increases $Q$ by **16 times**, a fact of enormous practical importance in piping design.
2. It provides a direct method to measure fluid viscosity using a **capillary viscometer**.
3. It gives the **pressure drop** required to maintain a desired flow rate — essential for pump selection.
4. It is a benchmark result in **momentum transport** — the first closed-form solution of the Navier–Stokes equation.

**Why it fails for non-Newtonian fluids:**

- The derivation assumes $\tau = \mu\,dv_z/dr$ (linear relation).
- Non-Newtonian fluids (e.g., polymer melts, blood) have a **non-linear** $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ relationship (power-law, Bingham, etc.).
- The parabolic profile is **replaced** by a plug-like flow profile for shear-thinning fluids.
- For Bingham plastics, there is a **yield stress** that must be exceeded before flow begins.

**Why it fails for turbulent flow:**

- For $Re > 2100$, the laminar assumption breaks down.
- Fluid undergoes chaotic, multi-scale, time-dependent motion.
- The **molecular viscosity $\mu$** is dominated by **eddy viscosity** (much larger).
- The velocity profile becomes much **flatter** in the core and steeper near the wall.
- An empirical correlation like the **Fanning friction factor** must be used: $f = 0.079\,Re^{-0.25}$ (Blasius equation for smooth pipes).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $\partial P/\partial z$.** Students often write $+\Delta P / L$ instead of $-\Delta P / L$. Always remember pressure *decreases* in the flow direction.
> 2. **Missing BC-1 (symmetry at centerline).** Without applying $dv_z/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$, the constant $C_1$ remains and the derivation is incomplete — typically **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Incorrect evaluation of $\int_0^R (R^2 - r^2)\,r\,dr$.** A careless student writes $R^4/2$ instead of $R^4/4$. Double-check every integral.
> 4. **Mixing up Fanning and Darcy friction factors.** Darcy $f_D = 4 \times$ Fanning $f$. KTU 2024 syllabus uses **Fanning**.
> 5. **Forgetting to validate $Re < 2100$** before applying $f = 16/Re$ — examiners test whether you know the **laminar regime assumption**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flow Through a Tube**

- [x] The equation of motion reduces to **one ODE** in $r$ after applying all assumptions.
- [x] The two boundary conditions are **no-slip at wall** and **symmetry at center**.
- [x] The **velocity profile is parabolic**: $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$.
- [x] **Maximum velocity** at center = $\dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$.
- [x] **Average velocity** = half of maximum = $\dfrac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L}$.
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille Equation**: $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ — **flow rate scales with $R^4$**.
- [x] **Wall shear stress**: $\tau_w = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2L}$.
- [x] **Fanning friction factor**: $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ — **valid only for laminar flow** ($Re < 2100$).
- [x] **Kinetic energy correction factor**: $\alpha = 2$ for parabolic profile.
- [x] Derivation requires knowing the $z$-component of $\nabla^2 \mathbf{v}$ in cylindrical coordinates: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r\dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\right)$.
- [x] Hagen–Poiseuille fails for **non-Newtonian fluids** and **turbulent flow**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tangential annular flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tangential annular flow ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) **Tangential Annular Flow** (also called **axial-couette tangential flow** or **flow between concentric rotating cylinders**) is a fully-developed, laminar, incompressible viscous flow that occurs in the annular space bounded by two coaxial cylindrical surfaces — an inner cylinder of radius $\kappa R$ and an outer cylinder of radius $R$ — in which the only non-zero velocity component is the **azimuthal (circumferential) velocity**, $v_\theta = v_\theta(r)$. The flow is generated when one or both cylinders are rotated about the common axis at constant angular velocities ($\Omega_i$ and $\Omega_o$ respectively). By virtue of the no-slip condition and the viscous coupling in the fluid film, the fluid in the annulus is dragged into a purely circular (swirling) motion, with **streamlines that are concentric circles**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Momentum Transport):** > The tangential annular flow problem is a classic boundary-value problem used to determine the **velocity profile $v_\theta(r)$**, the **shear-stress distribution $\tau_{r\theta}(r)$**, and the **torque transmitted between the cylinders**. It is the theoretical foundation of the **concentric-cylinder viscometer**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two nested cylindrical containers, like the drum of a top-loading washing machine sitting inside an outer tub. Now pour a thick syrup (or honey) into the narrow gap between them. If you spin the **inner drum only**, the syrup touching the drum gets dragged along, while the syrup touching the **outer (stationary) tub wall** tries to stay still. Because the syrup is viscous, it carries the rotation outward layer by layer. The result is a smooth, spiral-like flow where every fluid particle moves in a circle around the central axis — faster near the spinning drum, slower near the stationary wall. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** > Even though the inner drum is moving, **no fluid actually travels along the axis or radially inward/outward**. The fluid simply *orbits* the axis. The mathematical symbol for this is $v_r = 0$, $v_z = 0$, $v_\theta = v_\theta(r)$. ### 1.3 Standard Notation and Constants | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $R$ | Radius of the outer cylinder | m | | $\kappa R$ | Radius of the inner cylinder | m | | $\kappa$ | Radius ratio $\kappa = R_i / R$ | dimensionless, $0 < \kappa < 1$ | | $L$ | Length of the cylinders | m | | $\Omega_i$ | Angular velocity of inner cylinder | rad/s | | $\Omega_o$ | Angular velocity of outer cylinder | rad/s | | $\mu$ | Fluid (dynamic) viscosity | Pa·s or kg/(m·s) | | $\rho$ | Fluid density | kg/m³ | | $v_\theta$ | Tangential (azimuthal) velocity | m/s | > [!NOTE] > **Assumption Set (Standard):** > 1. Steady state ($\partial / \partial t = 0$). > 2. Laminar, Newtonian, incompressible flow. > 3. No end effects ($L \gg R$). > 4. Constant fluid properties ($\mu, \rho$). > 5. Negligible gravity influence in the $\theta$-direction. > 6. No imposed pressure gradient in the $\theta$-direction ($\partial p / \partial \theta = 0$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Governing Equations in Cylindrical Coordinates The equation of continuity for incompressible flow in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ is: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r v_r\right) + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$ The $\theta$-component of the Navier–Stokes equation (neglecting gravity in $\theta$ and assuming constant $\mu$) is: $$\rho\!\left(\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + v_z \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z} + \frac{v_r v_\theta}{r}\right) = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta} + \mu\!\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_\theta}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_\theta}{\partial z^2} - \frac{v_\theta}{r^2}\right]$$ ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Simplification Applying the postulates $v_r = 0$, $v_z = 0$, $v_\theta = v_\theta(r)$ only: - **Continuity check:** $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(0) + 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓ (automatically satisfied). - **Inertial terms:** All convective terms vanish. - **Pressure term:** $\partial p / \partial \theta = 0$ for a closed concentric-cylinder geometry. - **Reduced ODE:** $$0 = \mu\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dv_\theta}{dr}\right) - \frac{v_\theta}{r^2}\right]$$ which simplifies to the **Euler (Cauchy) differential equation**: $$\frac{d^2 v_\theta}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dv_\theta}{dr} - \frac{v_\theta}{r^2} = 0$$ ### 2.3 General Solution via Cauchy–Euler Method Substitute the trial solution $v_\theta = r^n$: $$n(n-1)r^{n-2} + \frac{n r^{n-1}}{r} - \frac{r^n}{r^2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n^2 - 1 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n = \pm 1$$ Therefore the general solution is: $$\boxed{\,v_\theta(r) = A\,r + \frac{B}{r}\,}$$ The constants $A$ and $B$ are evaluated from the **no-slip boundary conditions** on the two cylinder walls. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | General Formula | Units | Boundary Condition Applied | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tangential velocity (general) | $v_\theta(r) = A r + B/r$ | m/s | $v_\theta = r \Omega$ at each wall | | 2 | Constant $A$ (both rotating) | $A = (\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o)/(\kappa^2 - 1)$ | 1/s | $v_\theta(\kappa R) = \Omega_i \kappa R$ | | 3 | Constant $B$ (both rotating) | $B = \kappa^2 R^2 (\Omega_o - \Omega_i)/(\kappa^2 - 1)$ | m²/s | $v_\theta(R) = \Omega_o R$ | | 4 | Velocity (inner only, $\Omega_o = 0$) | $v_\theta = \dfrac{\Omega_i \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}\!\left(r - \dfrac{R^2}{r}\right)$ | m/s | $v_\theta(\kappa R) = \Omega_i \kappa R$ | | 5 | Shear stress $\tau_{r\theta}$ | $\tau_{r\theta} = -2\mu B / r^2$ | Pa | Definition of $\tau_{r\theta}$ | | 6 | Shear stress magnitude | $\vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert = 2\mu \vert B \vert / r^2$ | Pa | — | | 7 | Torque on inner cylinder | $T_i = 4\pi \mu L \Omega_i \kappa^2 R^2 / (\kappa^2 - 1)$ (with $\Omega_o = 0$) | N·m | Force $\times$ lever arm | | 8 | Torque on outer cylinder | $T_o = -T_i$ (equal and opposite, $\Omega_o = 0$) | N·m | Newton's 3rd law | | 9 | General torque on inner cylinder | $T_i = 4\pi \mu L R^2 \kappa^2 (\Omega_i - \Omega_o)/(\kappa^2 - 1)$ | N·m | Both rotating | | 10 | Power dissipated | $W = T_i \Omega_i$ | W | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic Trick:** > $v_\theta = Ar + B/r$ → "**A**rchimedes and **B**ob have a **1/r** relationship" — helps recall the form instantly. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Concentric-cylinder rotational viscometer:** measures $\mu$ by relating the measured torque to angular speed. - **Journal bearings and seals:** compute friction losses between rotating shafts and stationary housings. - **Mixing in agitated tanks (Turbine impellers):** Tangential flow component dominates in low-viscosity regimes. - **Torsion viscometers (Mooney–Ewart).** - **Rheological characterisation of non-Newtonian fluids.** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A — Velocity Profile: Only Inner Cylinder Rotates **Statement of the problem:** Outer cylinder (radius $R$) is **stationary**; inner cylinder (radius $\kappa R$) rotates with angular velocity $\Omega_i = \Omega$. Find $v_\theta(r)$. **Boundary conditions (no-slip):** $$v_\theta(\kappa R) = \Omega \kappa R, \qquad v_\theta(R) = 0$$ **Apply to general solution $v_\theta = A r + B/r$:** $$A(\kappa R) + \frac{B}{\kappa R} = \Omega \kappa R \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$A R + \frac{B}{R} = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Solve (ii) for $B$:** $$B = -A R^2$$ **Substitute into (i):** $$A \kappa R - \frac{A R^2}{\kappa R} = \Omega \kappa R$$ $$A R\!\left(\kappa - \frac{1}{\kappa}\right) = \Omega \kappa R$$ $$A \cdot \frac{\kappa^2 - 1}{\kappa} = \Omega \kappa \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Then $B$ becomes:** $$B = -\,\frac{\Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Final velocity profile:** $$\boxed{\,v_\theta(r) = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}\!\left(r - \frac{R^2}{r}\right)\,}$$ **Verification (sandbox check):** - At $r = \kappa R$: $v_\theta = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2-1}\left(\kappa R - \frac{R}{\kappa}\right) = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2-1}\cdot \frac{R(\kappa^2-1)}{\kappa} = \Omega \kappa R$ ✓ - At $r = R$: $v_\theta = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2-1}(R - R) = 0$ ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation B — Shear-Stress Distribution $\tau_{r\theta}(r)$ For tangential annular flow, the relevant component of the viscous stress tensor is: $$\tau_{r\theta} = -\mu\!\left[r\,\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}\right] = -\mu r\,\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right)$$ Compute $v_\theta / r$: $$\frac{v_\theta}{r} = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}\!\left(1 - \frac{R^2}{r^2}\right)$$ Differentiate w.r.t. $r$: $$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}\!\left(\frac{2 R^2}{r^3}\right)$$ Therefore: $$\tau_{r\theta} = -\mu r \cdot \frac{2 \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{(\kappa^2-1)\,r^3} = -\frac{2 \mu \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r^2}$$ **Magnitude (used in torque calculation):** $$\boxed{\,\vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert = \frac{2 \mu \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r^2}\,}$$ Notice that $\tau_{r\theta} \propto 1/r^2$ — the shear stress is **largest at the inner cylinder** ($r = \kappa R$) and **decreases as $1/r^2$** outward. ### 3.3 Derivation C — Torque Transmitted to the Outer Cylinder **Force per unit area** on the inner cylinder wall is the wall shear stress evaluated at $r = \kappa R$: $$\vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert_{r = \kappa R} = \frac{2 \mu \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,(\kappa R)^2} = \frac{2 \mu \Omega}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Total tangential force** on the inner cylinder surface (area $= 2 \pi \kappa R L$): $$F = \vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert_{r=\kappa R} \cdot 2\pi \kappa R L = \frac{2 \mu \Omega}{\kappa^2 - 1} \cdot 2 \pi \kappa R L = \frac{4 \pi \mu \Omega \kappa R L}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Torque** = Force $\times$ Lever arm (= $\kappa R$): $$T = F \cdot \kappa R = \frac{4 \pi \mu \Omega \kappa^2 R^2 L}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ $$\boxed{\,T = \frac{4 \pi \mu L \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}\,}$$ By Newton's third law, the torque experienced by the outer cylinder is **equal in magnitude and opposite in sign**. ### 3.4 Derivation D — General Case: Both Cylinders Rotating Let inner cylinder rotate at $\Omega_i$ and outer cylinder at $\Omega_o$ (with $\Omega_o \neq 0$). Boundary conditions: $$v_\theta(\kappa R) = \Omega_i \kappa R, \qquad v_\theta(R) = \Omega_o R$$ Apply to $v_\theta = A r + B/r$: $$A \kappa R + \frac{B}{\kappa R} = \Omega_i \kappa R \quad \text{...(I)}$$ $$A R + \frac{B}{R} = \Omega_o R \quad \text{...(II)}$$ **From (II):** $B = R(\Omega_o R - A R) = R^2(\Omega_o - A)$ **Substitute into (I):** $$A \kappa R + \frac{R(\Omega_o - A)}{\kappa} = \Omega_i \kappa R$$ Multiply through by $\kappa$: $$A \kappa^2 R + R(\Omega_o - A) = \Omega_i \kappa^2 R$$ $$A R(\kappa^2 - 1) + R \Omega_o = \Omega_i \kappa^2 R$$ $$A = \frac{\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Substitute back for $B$:** $$B = R^2\!\left(\Omega_o - \frac{\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o}{\kappa^2 - 1}\right) = R^2 \cdot \frac{\Omega_o(\kappa^2-1) - \Omega_i \kappa^2 + \Omega_o}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ $$B = \frac{R^2 \kappa^2 (\Omega_o - \Omega_i)}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Final general velocity profile:** $$\boxed{\,v_\theta(r) = \frac{(\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o)\,r}{\kappa^2 - 1} + \frac{\kappa^2 R^2 (\Omega_o - \Omega_i)}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r}\,}$$ **General torque on the inner cylinder:** $$\boxed{\,T_i = \frac{4 \pi \mu L R^2 \kappa^2 (\Omega_i - \Omega_o)}{\kappa^2 - 1}\,}$$ (Sign convention: positive torque when $\Omega_i > \Omega_o$.) ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Behaviour | Limiting Case | $\kappa$ Behaviour | Physical Implication | |---|---|---| | $\kappa \to 1$ (very thin gap) | $v_\theta \to \Omega r$ (linear in $r$) | Reduces to simple **Couette flow** in planar form | | $\kappa \to 0$ (negligible inner rod) | $A \to 0$, $B \to \Omega R^2$ | Approximates **Stokes' flow** past a rotating rod | | $\Omega_i = \Omega_o$ | $v_\theta = \Omega r$ everywhere | **Solid-body rotation** — no relative motion, no shear | | $\Omega_o = -\Omega_i$ | $A = 0$, $v_\theta = B/r$ | Pure **irrotational** free-vortex flow, $\tau_{r\theta} \to 0$ as $r \to \infty$ | | $\Omega_i \cdot \Omega_o > 0$, $\Omega_i = \Omega_o$ | $v_\theta = A r$, $B = 0$ | Linear velocity profile, no $1/r$ term | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Tangential Annular Flow Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Problem Setup:
Inner cylinder radius kappaR
Outer cylinder radius R
Length L, fluid mu"] --> B0["Assumptions:
v_r = 0, v_z = 0
v_theta = v_theta of r only
Steady, laminar, incompressible"] B0 --> C0["Continuity:
Auto satisfied"] B0 --> C1["Navier-Stokes theta-component:
Reduces to Euler ODE"] C1 --> D0["D2 v_theta / Dr2 plus 1/r Dv_theta/Dr
minus v_theta/r squared equals 0"] D0 --> E0["Cauchy-Euler solution:
v_theta = A r + B by r"] E0 --> F0["Boundary conditions:
v_theta at kappaR equals Omega_i kappaR
v_theta at R equals Omega_o R"] F0 --> G0["Solve for A and B
A equals Omega_i kappa squared minus Omega_o over kappa squared minus 1
B equals kappa squared R squared times Omega_o minus Omega_i over kappa squared minus 1"] G0 --> H0["Velocity profile v_theta of r
Tangential only"] G0 --> H1["Shear stress tau r theta of r
equals 2 mu B over r squared"] H1 --> I0["Torque T on inner cylinder
T equals 4 pi mu L R squared kappa squared times Omega_i minus Omega_o over kappa squared minus 1"] H1 --> I1["Power dissipated W equals T times Omega_i"] I0 --> J0["Application:
Rotational viscometer measures mu
T and Omega_i are measured experimentally"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Cylinder Geometry and Coordinate System ```mermaid flowchart LR A["z-axis
vertical line of symmetry"] --> B["Inner cylinder
radius equals kappaR
rotating with Omega_i"] A --> C["Outer cylinder
radius equals R
rotating with Omega_o or stationary"] B --> D["Annular fluid film
v_theta equals function of r only
streamlines are concentric circles"] C --> D D --> E["Wall shear stress tau r theta
maximum at inner wall
decays as one over r squared"] D --> F["No-slip:
v_theta at kappaR equals Omega_i kappaR
v_theta at R equals Omega_o R"] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description (Verbal Replica of the Physical Setup) - **Vertical $z$-axis:** the common axis of both cylinders. - **Inner cylinder** at $r = \kappa R$: solid shaft of length $L$, possibly driven by a motor. - **Outer cylinder** at $r = R$: hollow shell, often stationary in viscometers. - **Annular gap** of width $R(1-\kappa)$ filled with the test fluid. - **Streamlines:** concentric circles lying in horizontal planes ($z$ = constant). - **Velocity vectors** at any radius $r$ are tangent to the circle of radius $r$, pointing in the $+\theta$ direction (if $\Omega_i > 0$). - **No-slip walls:** the fluid layer in immediate contact with each cylinder moves exactly with that cylinder ($v_\theta = \Omega r$ at the wall). > [!NOTE] > The vector plot would show velocity vectors of **length proportional to $r$** at each wall, but **non-linear decay** through the annulus. Maximum speed is at $r = \kappa R$ (the moving inner wall). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** Define *tangential annular flow*. Under what assumptions does the Navier–Stokes equation reduce to the form $\frac{d^2 v_\theta}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dv_\theta}{dr} - \frac{v_\theta}{r^2} = 0$? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Tangential annular flow is the viscous, laminar, incompressible flow in the annular region between two concentric cylinders in which the only non-zero velocity component is the azimuthal velocity $v_\theta = v_\theta(r)$, and the streamlines are concentric circles about the common axis. (1 mark) The reduced form of the Navier–Stokes equation is obtained when: - $v_r = 0$, $v_z = 0$, and $v_\theta$ depends only on $r$ (1 mark) - Flow is steady, $\partial p/\partial \theta = 0$, gravity has no $\theta$-component, and all inertial terms vanish. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the general form of the velocity profile for tangential flow in an annulus, and what are the boundary conditions used to evaluate the integration constants? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The general solution of the reduced equation of motion is: $$v_\theta(r) = A r + \frac{B}{r} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ The no-slip boundary conditions are: - At the inner cylinder wall ($r = \kappa R$): $v_\theta = \Omega_i \kappa R$ (1 mark) - At the outer cylinder wall ($r = R$): $v_\theta = \Omega_o R$ (1 mark) ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern] **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the velocity distribution $v_\theta(r)$ for tangential flow in the annulus between two concentric cylinders when the inner cylinder of radius $\kappa R$ rotates at angular velocity $\Omega$ and the outer cylinder of radius $R$ is stationary. Also, write the expression for the shear stress distribution $\tau_{r\theta}(r)$. **(b)** [7 Marks] An oil with viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s fills the gap of a concentric-cylinder viscometer. The inner cylinder radius is $\kappa R = 0.025$ m, outer radius is $R = 0.028$ m, and the length of the cylinder is $L = 0.10$ m. If the inner cylinder rotates at $N = 50$ rev/min, calculate the **torque** required to overcome the viscous resistance and the **power** consumed. #### Model Solution — Q.A(a) **Step 1 — Equation of motion reduction** (1 mark): Starting from the $\theta$-component of Navier–Stokes, with $v_r = v_z = 0$, $v_\theta = v_\theta(r)$, $\partial p/\partial \theta = 0$ and steady state: $$\frac{d^2 v_\theta}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dv_\theta}{dr} - \frac{v_\theta}{r^2} = 0$$ **Step 2 — General solution** (1 mark): Using the Cauchy–Euler substitution $v_\theta = r^n$ gives $n^2 = 1$, hence $n = \pm 1$. Therefore: $$v_\theta(r) = A r + \frac{B}{r}$$ **Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions** (2 marks): - $v_\theta(\kappa R) = \Omega \kappa R \Rightarrow A \kappa R + B/(\kappa R) = \Omega \kappa R$ … (i) - $v_\theta(R) = 0 \Rightarrow A R + B/R = 0$ … (ii) From (ii): $B = -A R^2$. Substituting in (i) and solving: $$A = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}, \qquad B = -\frac{\Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ **Step 4 — Velocity profile** (2 marks): $$\boxed{\,v_\theta(r) = \frac{\Omega \kappa^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}\!\left(r - \frac{R^2}{r}\right)\,}$$ **Step 5 — Shear stress** (1 mark): $$\tau_{r\theta} = -\mu r \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) = -\frac{2 \mu \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r^2}$$ Hence the magnitude is: $$\vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert = \frac{2 \mu \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r^2}$$ #### Model Solution — Q.A(b) **Given data:** - $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s - $\kappa R = 0.025$ m $\Rightarrow \kappa = 25/28 = 0.8929$ - $R = 0.028$ m - $L = 0.10$ m - $N = 50$ rev/min $\Rightarrow \Omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi(50)/60 = 5.236$ rad/s **Step 1 — Compute $\kappa^2$ and $\kappa^2 - 1$** (1 mark): - $\kappa^2 = (0.8929)^2 = 0.7972$ - $\kappa^2 - 1 = -0.2028$ (negative; magnitude $= 0.2028$) **Step 2 — Apply torque formula** (3 marks): $$T = \frac{4 \pi \mu L \Omega \kappa^2 R^2}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ Using the magnitude (the negative sign only indicates the direction): $$T = \frac{4 \pi (0.1)(0.10)(5.236)(0.7972)(0.028)^2}{0.2028}$$ Compute stepwise: - $4\pi = 12.566$ - $\mu L \Omega = 0.1 \times 0.10 \times 5.236 = 0.05236$ - $\kappa^2 R^2 = 0.7972 \times (0.028)^2 = 0.7972 \times 7.84 \times 10^{-4} = 6.250 \times 10^{-4}$ - Numerator $= 12.566 \times 0.05236 \times 6.250 \times 10^{-4} = 4.117 \times 10^{-4}$ - Denominator $= 0.2028$ $$T = \frac{4.117 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2028} = 2.030 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m} \approx 2.03 \text{ mN·m}$$ **Step 3 — Power** (1 mark): $$W = T \Omega = 2.030 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.236 = 1.063 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} \approx 10.6 \text{ mW}$$ **Step 4 — Units check & result** (2 marks): - Units of $T$: Pa·s × m × (1/s) × m² = (kg/m·s)(m)(1/s)(m²) = kg·m²/s² = N·m ✓ - Units of $W$: N·m × 1/s = W ✓ $$\boxed{\,T \approx 2.03 \text{ mN·m}, \quad W \approx 10.6 \text{ mW}\,}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern] **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **general velocity profile** $v_\theta(r)$ for tangential flow between two concentric cylinders of radii $\kappa R$ and $R$ rotating at angular velocities $\Omega_i$ and $\Omega_o$, respectively. Show the complete step-by-step solution. **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the result obtained in part (a), derive the **expression for the shear stress distribution** $\tau_{r\theta}(r)$ and the **torque** required to rotate the inner cylinder. Discuss the limiting case when $\Omega_i = \Omega_o$. #### Model Solution — Q.B(a) **Step 1 — Equation setup** (1 mark): Same reduced Navier–Stokes equation as before. General solution: $$v_\theta(r) = A r + \frac{B}{r}$$ **Step 2 — Boundary conditions** (1 mark): - $v_\theta(\kappa R) = \Omega_i \kappa R$ - $v_\theta(R) = \Omega_o R$ **Step 3 — Algebraic system** (2 marks): Equations: - $A \kappa R + B/(\kappa R) = \Omega_i \kappa R$ … (I) - $A R + B/R = \Omega_o R$ … (II) From (II): $B = R^2(\Omega_o - A)$. Substitute into (I) and multiply by $\kappa$: $$A \kappa^2 R + R(\Omega_o - A) = \Omega_i \kappa^2 R$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $A$ and $B$** (2 marks): $$A R(\kappa^2 - 1) = R(\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o)$$ $$\boxed{\,A = \frac{\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o}{\kappa^2 - 1}\,}$$ $$\boxed{\,B = \frac{\kappa^2 R^2 (\Omega_o - \Omega_i)}{\kappa^2 - 1}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Final velocity profile** (1 mark): $$v_\theta(r) = \frac{(\Omega_i \kappa^2 - \Omega_o)\,r}{\kappa^2 - 1} + \frac{\kappa^2 R^2 (\Omega_o - \Omega_i)}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r}$$ #### Model Solution — Q.B(b) **Step 1 — Compute $v_\theta / r$** (1 mark): $$\frac{v_\theta}{r} = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate** (1 mark): $$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) = -\frac{2B}{r^3}$$ **Step 3 — Shear stress formula** (1 mark): $$\tau_{r\theta} = -\mu r \cdot \left(-\frac{2B}{r^3}\right) = \frac{2\mu B}{r^2}$$ Substituting $B$: $$\tau_{r\theta} = \frac{2\mu \kappa^2 R^2 (\Omega_o - \Omega_i)}{(\kappa^2 - 1)\,r^2}$$ **Step 4 — Torque on inner cylinder** (2 marks): Force per unit area at $r = \kappa R$: $$\vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert_{r = \kappa R} = \frac{2\mu \kappa^2 R^2 (\Omega_i - \Omega_o)}{(\kappa^2 - 1)(\kappa R)^2} = \frac{2\mu (\Omega_i - \Omega_o)}{\kappa^2 - 1}$$ Total torque: $$T = \vert \tau_{r\theta} \vert_{r = \kappa R} \cdot (2\pi \kappa R L) \cdot (\kappa R)$$ $$T = \frac{2\mu(\Omega_i - \Omega_o)}{\kappa^2 - 1} \cdot 2\pi \kappa^2 R^2 L$$ $$\boxed{\,T = \frac{4\pi \mu L R^2 \kappa^2 (\Omega_i - \Omega_o)}{\kappa^2 - 1}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Limiting case discussion** (2 marks): When $\Omega_i = \Omega_o$, the numerator $(\Omega_i - \Omega_o) = 0$, hence $T = 0$. Physically, both cylinders rotate at the **same angular speed**, so the fluid film undergoes **solid-body rotation** ($v_\theta = \Omega r$ everywhere), with **no relative motion between adjacent fluid layers** and therefore **no viscous shear stress**. The fluid rotates as if it were a rigid body and no power is dissipated. This corresponds to the trivial case where the constant $B$ also becomes zero, leaving $v_\theta = A r = \Omega r$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > 1. **Sign errors in $A$ and $B$** — most students forget the no-slip at the *outer* wall first. Write the BCs explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting $\partial p / \partial \theta = 0$** — it is a crucial assumption, not obvious from the geometry. Always state it. > 3. **Wrong radius ratio definition** — verify whether your $\kappa$ is $R_i / R$ or $R / R_i$. In KTU convention, $\kappa = R_i / R < 1$. > 4. **Confusing $\tau_{r\theta}$ direction** — the *negative* sign on $\tau_{r\theta}$ simply denotes the direction on the inner cylinder; use the magnitude when computing torque. > 5. **Numerical slip-ups** in part (b): missing the $\pi$ factor, mixing $\Omega$ with $N$ (rev/min), and not converting $N \to \Omega = 2\pi N / 60$. > 6. **Not specifying units** in the final numerical answer (always write N·m for torque and W for power). ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Tangential annular flow is governed by the **Euler–Cauchy equation** $\frac{d^2 v_\theta}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dv_\theta}{dr} - \frac{v_\theta}{r^2} = 0$ with the universal solution $v_\theta = A r + B/r$. - The **two integration constants $A$ and $B$** are determined by the **no-slip condition** at $r = \kappa R$ and $r = R$. - The **shear stress decays as $1/r^2$**, so the maximum viscous resistance is always on the **inner cylinder wall**. - The **torque formula** is the heart of every rotational viscometer equation: $T = 4\pi \mu L R^2 \kappa^2 (\Omega_i - \Omega_o)/(\kappa^2 - 1)$. - **Solid-body rotation** (no shear) occurs when $\Omega_i = \Omega_o$. - For a **thin gap** ($\kappa \to 1$), the flow reduces to simple planar Couette flow with a linear $v_\theta$ profile. - For a **rod-like inner cylinder** ($\kappa \to 0$), the velocity profile becomes the **free-vortex form** $v_\theta \propto 1/r$. - **Units hygiene:** $\mu$ in Pa·s, $R$ in m, $L$ in m, $\Omega$ in rad/s → $T$ in N·m, $W$ in W. - Remember to **convert RPM to rad/s**: $\Omega = 2\pi N / 60$. - The tangential annular flow problem is a **direct analogue of the electrical engineering coaxial cable problem** ($V(r) = A \ln r + B$), with $1/r$ replacing $\ln r$.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotating Liquid:** A *rotating liquid system* refers to a Newtonian fluid contained in a cylindrical vessel that is rotated about its vertical axis at a constant angular velocity $\omega$. When the liquid reaches steady-state (solid-body rotation), every fluid element moves in a circular path with the **same angular velocity** as the container. The free surface of the liquid, under the combined action of gravity and centrifugal force, forms a characteristic surface of revolution called a **paraboloid of revolution**.

This phenomenon is governed by the **Navier-Stokes equations** in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ under the assumptions of **steady-state, incompressible, laminar flow with no-slip at the walls**.

## 1.2 Physical Setup

A typical KTU problem setup consists of:

- A **cylindrical vessel** of radius $R$ filled to a height $h_0$ with a Newtonian liquid of density $\rho$.
- The vessel rotates about its **central vertical axis** at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s).
- Gravity acts downward ($g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$).
- The free surface is open to atmosphere (constant pressure $P_0$).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The spinning cup of tea":**
> Imagine stirring a cup of tea with a spoon until the liquid swirls uniformly with the cup. At this point, the liquid rotates as if it were a *solid body* — every drop completes one revolution in exactly the same time. Look at the surface: it dips in the center and rises along the walls, forming a curved shape. That curved shape is the **paraboloid of revolution**. The deeper the dip and the steeper the rise at the wall, the faster the rotation.

The same physics governs:
- **Spin coating** (photoresist deposition on silicon wafers in semiconductor manufacturing).
- **Centrifuges** used in dairy and biomedical labs.
- **Rotating bioreactors** and **angular momentum washers** in chemical process industries.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values and dimensions are critical for KTU problems:

- **Angular velocity:** $\omega$ [rad/s] or $N$ [rpm], with $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- **Centrifugal acceleration at radius $r$:** $a_c = \omega^2 r$ [m/s²]
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of liquid:** $\rho$ [kg/m³]
- **Atmospheric pressure:** $P_0$ [Pa]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations in Cylindrical Coordinates

For a rotating Newtonian fluid in steady, axisymmetric flow, the velocity field has only one non-zero component:

$$v_\theta = \omega r$$

$$v_r = 0, \quad v_z = 0$$

This is the **solid-body rotation** velocity profile — a cornerstone result.

### Why Solid-Body Rotation Occurs (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Continuity equation** (incompressible, axisymmetric): automatically satisfied because $v_r = v_z = 0$ and $v_\theta$ depends only on $r$.
2. **θ-momentum equation** reduces to a balance between the centrifugal body force and the pressure gradient.
3. The viscous term $\nu \nabla^2 v_\theta$ vanishes identically when $v_\theta = \omega r$ because this is the **irrotational-with-respect-to-vorticity** solution for a rotating frame.
4. Hence the Navier-Stokes equations are satisfied without any shear stress — the fluid behaves like a rigid body.

## 2.2 Pressure Distribution in the Rotating Liquid

Integrating the radial and vertical momentum balances yields:

$$P(r, z) = P_0 + \rho g (h_s - z) + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$$

where:
- $P_0$ = atmospheric pressure at the free surface
- $h_s$ = height of the free surface at radius $r$
- $z$ = vertical coordinate (measured upward from the bottom)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Pressure increases *outward* (centrifugal effect) AND *downward* (hydrostatic effect). The total pressure at any point is the sum of these two contributions.

## 2.3 Shape of the Free Surface (Paraboloid of Revolution)

Applying the boundary condition $P = P_0$ on the free surface gives:

$$h_s(r) = h_0 + \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$$

This is the equation of a **paraboloid of revolution**, with vertex at $r = 0$.

### Height Difference Between Wall and Center

$$\Delta h = h_s(R) - h_s(0) = \frac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Angular velocity (from rpm) | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Tangential velocity | $v_\theta = \omega r$ | m/s | Solid-body rotation |
| 3 | Centripetal acceleration | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | m/s² | Points toward axis |
| 4 | Total pressure | $P(r,z) = P_0 + \rho g(h_s - z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$ | Pa | KTU high-yield |
| 5 | Free surface height | $h_s(r) = h_0 + \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$ | m | Paraboloid |
| 6 | Wall-to-center height difference | $\Delta h = \dfrac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g}$ | m | Often asked in exams |
| 7 | Volume conservation check | $V = \pi R^2 h_0 = \displaystyle\int_0^R 2\pi r \, h_s(r) \, dr$ | m³ | Verifies $h_0$ |
| 8 | Force on circular lid (closed vessel) | $F_z = \int_0^R (P - P_0) 2\pi r \, dr$ | N | Vertical force balance |
| 9 | Volume of paraboloid | $V_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \pi R^2 \Delta h$ | m³ | Useful for filling problems |
| 10 | Critical $\omega$ for spillover | $\omega_{cr}^2 = \dfrac{2g(H - h_0)}{R^2}$ | rad²/s² | When $\Delta h = H - h_0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam note:** KTU problems often ask for *both* the free-surface profile and the pressure at a given $(r, z)$. Always draw the paraboloid and label $h_0$, $R$, and $\Delta h$ before substituting numbers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Centrifugal separators:** Based on the radial pressure gradient $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \rho \omega^2 r$.
- **Spin coating:** Uniformity of photoresist films depends on the paraboloid shape.
- **Rotating biological reactors:** Mimic microgravity environments.
- **Volute pumps and turbines:** The rotating-liquid analogy explains the *Euler turbine equation* $g H = u_2 v_{u2} - u_1 v_{u1}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Velocity Profile $v_\theta = \omega r$

We start from the **θ-component of the Navier-Stokes equation** in cylindrical coordinates, for steady, axisymmetric, incompressible flow:

$$\rho \left( v_r \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + v_z \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z} + \frac{v_r v_\theta}{r} \right) = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial P}{\partial \theta} + \mu \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} \right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_\theta}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_\theta}{\partial z^2} \right] + \rho g_\theta$$

**Step 1 — Apply axisymmetry:** All $\theta$-derivatives vanish: $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta} = 0$, so $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \theta} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial^2 v_\theta}{\partial \theta^2} = 0$. Also $g_\theta = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply solid-body assumption:** $v_r = v_z = 0$, leaving only $v_\theta(r)$.

$$\rho \cdot 0 = 0 + \mu \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( \frac{1}{r}\frac{d(r v_\theta)}{dr} \right) + 0 + 0 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Integrate twice:** Let $f(r) = r v_\theta$. Then

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( \frac{1}{r}\frac{df}{dr} \right) = 0 \implies \frac{1}{r}\frac{df}{dr} = 2C_1 \implies f(r) = C_1 r^2 + C_2$$

So $v_\theta(r) = C_1 r + \dfrac{C_2}{r}$.

**Step 4 — Apply no-slip at $r = R$:** $v_\theta(R) = \omega R$.
**Step 5 — Apply finiteness at $r = 0$:** $v_\theta$ must be finite, so $C_2 = 0$.

$$\boxed{v_\theta(r) = \omega r}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Distribution $P(r, z)$

**Radial momentum equation** (steady, axisymmetric, $v_r = v_z = 0$):

$$-\rho \frac{v_\theta^2}{r} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial r}$$

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \rho \omega^2 r$$

**Integrate with respect to $r$:**

$$P(r, z) = \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2 + f(z)$$

**Vertical momentum equation** (with $g_z = -g$):

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} - \rho g \implies \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

**Integrate with respect to $z$:**

$$P(r, z) = -\rho g z + g(r)$$

**Combine both integrals:**

$$P(r, z) = \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2 - \rho g z + C$$

**Apply BC:** At the free surface $z = h_s(r)$, $P = P_0$. Hence

$$C = P_0 + \rho g h_s(r) - \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$$

**Eliminate $C$** and use the free-surface boundary condition $P(r, h_s) = P_0$ again to find $h_s(r)$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2 - \rho g h_s + C = P_0$$

Substituting $C$ gives identically $P_0 = P_0$ — so we use the **alternate route**:

Set $P = P_0$ on the free surface directly:

$$P_0 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2 - \rho g h_s(r) + C \quad \text{and} \quad P_0 = -\rho g h_0 + C \text{ (at center)}$$

Subtracting:

$$\boxed{h_s(r) = h_0 + \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}}$$

**Pressure distribution in final form:**

$$\boxed{P(r, z) = P_0 + \rho g [h_s(r) - z] + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Force on the Bottom of the Vessel

The vertical force on the bottom plate (at $z = 0$) is:

$$F_z = \int_0^R [P(r, 0) - P_0] \, 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 1:** Pressure at $z = 0$:

$$P(r, 0) = P_0 + \rho g h_s(r) + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$$

**Step 2:** Gauge pressure on bottom:

$$P(r, 0) - P_0 = \rho g \left( h_0 + \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g} \right) + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2 = \rho g h_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2$$

**Step 3:** Integrate:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_z &= \int_0^R \left( \rho g h_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2 \right) 2\pi r \, dr \\
&= 2\pi \rho g h_0 \int_0^R r \, dr + 2\pi \rho \omega^2 \int_0^R r^3 \, dr \\
&= 2\pi \rho g h_0 \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} + 2\pi \rho \omega^2 \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} \\
&= \pi \rho g h_0 R^2 + \frac{\pi}{2} \rho \omega^2 R^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Force on side wall (per unit height), for completeness:

$$\frac{dF_r}{dz} = \int_0^{2\pi} P(R, z) \, R \, d\theta = 2\pi R \left[ P_0 + \rho g (h_s(R) - z) + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 R^2 \right]$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Situation | Key Equation | What to Compute |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| Free surface profile | $h_s(r) = h_0 + \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$ | Dip or rise of surface |
| Spillover check | $\omega_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g(H - h_0)}{R^2}}$ | Max $\omega$ before liquid spills |
| Pressure at any point | $P(r, z) = P_0 + \rho g (h_s - z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$ | Hydrostatic + centrifugal |
| Force on bottom | $F_z = \pi \rho g h_0 R^2 + \tfrac{\pi}{2}\rho \omega^2 R^4$ | Bearing load on shaft |
| Volume conservation | $\pi R^2 h_0 = \pi R^2 \bar{h}$ where $\bar{h} = h_0 + \tfrac{\omega^2 R^2}{4g}$ | Verifies $h_0$ given |
