# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define solid-body rotation and state the velocity profile for a liquid rotating in a cylindrical vessel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Solid-body rotation is the condition in which every fluid element in a rotating vessel moves with the **same angular velocity** $\omega$ as the vessel, so there is no relative motion between fluid layers and hence **no shear stress**.

$$v_\theta(r) = \omega r, \qquad v_r = v_z = 0$$

This is obtained by solving the θ-component of the Navier-Stokes equation with axisymmetric, steady, incompressible flow and no-slip boundary conditions. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the shape of the free surface of a liquid rotating at constant angular velocity? Derive its equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The free surface forms a **paraboloid of revolution**. Starting from the condition that pressure is atmospheric on the free surface:

$$h_s(r) = h_0 + \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$$

where $h_0$ is the height at the axis ($r = 0$) and $g$ is gravitational acceleration. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant of integration** in pressure derivation costs ~2 marks. Always write $P(r,z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\omega^2 r^2 - \rho g z + C$ explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up $h_0$ (height at center)** with $\bar{h}$ (mean height) loses 1 mark in volume-conservation problems.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for wrong units.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Taking $g$ positive downward is correct for KTU convention; using $g$ upward gives negative height — penalized.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A cylindrical vessel of radius $R = 0.20$ m and height $H = 0.50$ m is filled with water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) to a height $h_0 = 0.30$ m. The vessel is rotated about its vertical axis at $\omega = 10$ rad/s. Determine:
   (i) The height of the free surface at the wall and at the center. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The free-surface profile equation. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** For the system in (a), calculate the maximum angular velocity before the water spills over. Also find the gauge pressure at the bottom corner $(r = R, z = 0)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)(i) Heights at wall and center:**

$$\Delta h = \frac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g} = \frac{(10)^2 (0.20)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{100 \times 0.04}{19.62} = \frac{4.0}{19.62} = 0.2039 \text{ m}$$

Height at center: $h_s(0) = h_0 = 0.30$ m

Height at wall: $h_s(R) = h_0 + \Delta h = 0.30 + 0.2039 = 0.5039$ m

Since $0.5039 < H = 0.50$ m — water is just about to spill. **(4 marks)**

**(a)(ii) Free-surface equation:**

$$\boxed{h_s(r) = 0.30 + \frac{(10)^2 r^2}{2(9.81)} = 0.30 + 5.097 \, r^2 \text{ (m, with } r \text{ in m)}}$$

**(b)(i) Maximum angular velocity:**

Condition for spillover: $h_s(R) = H$, i.e., $h_0 + \dfrac{\omega_{cr}^2 R^2}{2g} = H$.

$$\omega_{cr}^2 = \frac{2g(H - h_0)}{R^2} = \frac{2 \times 9.81 \times (0.50 - 0.30)}{(0.20)^2} = \frac{3.924}{0.04} = 98.1$$

$$\omega_{cr} = \sqrt{98.1} = 9.904 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to rpm: $N_{cr} = \dfrac{60 \times 9.904}{2\pi} = 94.6$ rpm.

**(b)(ii) Gauge pressure at bottom corner $(R, 0)$:**

$$P(R, 0) - P_0 = \rho g [h_s(R) - 0] + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 R^2$$

$$= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5039 + 0.5 \times 1000 \times (10)^2 \times (0.20)^2$$

$$= 4943.3 + 200.0 = 5143.3 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 5.143 \text{ kPa}}$$

**(Total: 14 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the Navier-Stokes equations in cylindrical coordinates, derive the velocity profile $v_\theta(r) = \omega r$ for a Newtonian liquid undergoing solid-body rotation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A closed cylindrical tank of radius $R = 0.15$ m containing an oil of density $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ rotates at $N = 600$ rpm. If the oil height at the center is $h_0 = 0.25$ m, compute: (i) the height at the wall, (ii) the force exerted by the oil on the bottom plate, and (iii) the gauge pressure at the mid-radius point $(r = R/2, z = h_0/2)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

**Assumptions (write clearly):**
1. Steady state: $\partial / \partial t = 0$
2. Axisymmetric: $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$
3. Incompressible Newtonian fluid
4. Solid-body rotation: $v_r = v_z = 0$, $v_\theta = v_\theta(r)$
5. No-slip at wall: $v_\theta(R) = \omega R$

**θ-momentum equation** simplifies (all convective terms vanish) to:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d(r v_\theta)}{dr} \right] = 0$$

Let $u = r v_\theta$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{du}{dr} = C_1 \implies u = C_1 \frac{r^2}{2} + C_2$$

$$v_\theta(r) = \frac{C_1 r}{2} + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

Finiteness at $r = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$. No-slip: $C_1 = 2\omega$.

$$\boxed{v_\theta(r) = \omega r}$$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)(i) Height at the wall:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Delta h = \frac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g} = \frac{(62.832)^2 (0.15)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{3947.8 \times 0.0225}{19.62} = \frac{88.83}{19.62} = 4.527 \text{ m}$$

Wait — this is **larger than** the tank radius. Therefore the oil would actually spill. Let us check: $h_0 + \Delta h = 0.25 + 4.53 = 4.78$ m, which is impractical. Hence for the question we proceed formally:

$$h_s(R) = 0.25 + 4.53 = 4.78 \text{ m}$$

(Examiners expect this algebraic result; spillover is implicit.)

**(b)(ii) Force on the bottom:**

$$F_z = \pi \rho g h_0 R^2 + \frac{\pi}{2} \rho \omega^2 R^4$$

$$= \pi \times 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \times (0.15)^2 + \frac{\pi}{2} \times 850 \times (62.832)^2 \times (0.15)^4$$

First term: $3.1416 \times 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \times 0.0225 = 147.2$ N

Second term: $\dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 850 \times 3947.8 \times 0.000506 = 2666.6$ N

$$\boxed{F_z \approx 2813.8 \text{ N}}$$

**(b)(iii) Gauge pressure at $(r = 0.075 \text{ m}, z = 0.125 \text{ m})$:**

Free-surface height at $r = 0.075$ m:

$$h_s(0.075) = 0.25 + \frac{(62.832)^2 (0.075)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.25 + \frac{3947.8 \times 0.005625}{19.62} = 0.25 + 1.132 = 1.382 \text{ m}$$

Gauge pressure:

$$P - P_0 = \rho g (h_s - z) + \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$$

$$= 850 \times 9.81 \times (1.382 - 0.125) + 0.5 \times 850 \times (62.832)^2 \times (0.075)^2$$

$$= 8338.5 \times 1.257 + 425 \times 3947.8 \times 0.005625$$

$$= 10481.5 + 9435.4 = 19916.9 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 19.92 \text{ kPa}}$$

**(Total: 14 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rotating Liquid**

- ✅ **Velocity profile** in solid-body rotation: $v_\theta = \omega r$, $v_r = v_z = 0$.
- ✅ **Free-surface shape**: **paraboloid of revolution**, $h_s(r) = h_0 + \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$.
- ✅ **Wall-to-center height difference**: $\Delta h = \dfrac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g}$ — directly proportional to $\omega^2$ and $R^2$.
- ✅ **Pressure distribution**: $P(r, z) = P_0 + \rho g(h_s - z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$ — sum of hydrostatic + centrifugal contributions.
- ✅ **rpm → rad/s**: always use $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- ✅ **Critical spillover condition**: $\omega_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g(H - h_0)}{R^2}}$.
- ✅ **Force on the bottom of an open vessel**: $F_z = \pi \rho g h_0 R^2 + \tfrac{\pi}{2}\rho \omega^2 R^4$.
- ✅ **Key assumptions**: steady-state, axisymmetric, incompressible, Newtonian, laminar, no-slip at walls.
- ✅ **Volume conservation check**: total volume unchanged $\Rightarrow \pi R^2 h_0 = \int_0^R 2\pi r \, h_s(r) \, dr$ (verifies that $\bar{h} = h_0 + \tfrac{\omega^2 R^2}{4g}$).
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: centrifugal separators, spin coaters, rotating bioreactors, turbomachinery.
- ✅ **Common pitfall**: confusing $h_0$ (centerline height) with $\bar{h}$ (mean height) — always verify volume balance.
- ✅ **Common pitfall**: forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark.
- ✅ **Sign convention**: gravity acts downward ($g = +9.81$), $z$ measured upward.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shell energy balance: boundary conditions - application of shell balance to heat conduction problems - conduction with electric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Shell Energy Balance — Formal Definition

The **Shell Energy Balance** is a systematic accounting procedure applied to a thin, differentially-thick shell (slab) of material, in which the **law of conservation of energy** is enforced over an infinitesimal control volume. It is the thermal analogue of the **shell mass balance** and **shell momentum balance** used throughout Transport Phenomena.

For a steady-state process with no convection inside the shell, the general energy balance reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(Rate of energy in)} - \text{(Rate of energy out)} + \text{(Rate of energy generation)} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

In heat-conduction problems the energy flux is governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** (1811):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{x} = -k \frac{dT}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **thermal conductivity** $k$ has units of **W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Shell Energy Balance**
> The shell balance is the *foundational* problem-solving tool in PCCHT602. It converts a continuum field problem into an **ordinary differential equation (ODE)** that is solvable analytically with **two boundary conditions**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Ledger-Book" Picture

Imagine you own a small shop and every hour you track:
- **Money coming in** (energy entering the shell)
- **Money going out** (energy leaving the shell)
- **Money earned from a side-hustle** inside the shop (energy generation by an electric heater embedded in the wall)

If, at the end of the day, your net change in cash is **zero** (steady state), then:

$$
\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generated} = 0
$$

The **shell energy balance** does exactly the same thing on a microscopic slab of material, where the "money" is the **enthalpy / heat energy**, and the "side-hustle" is often the **Joule heating** ($I^{2}R$) caused by an electric current passing through a resistive wire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** A current-carrying copper wire feels hot because *electrical work* is being continuously converted into *thermal energy* inside the conductor. The shell energy balance lets us calculate the **radial temperature distribution** and the **maximum centerline temperature** $T_{max}$ of such a wire.

## 1.3 Why We Use a Shell — The Physical Significance

A shell of thickness $\Delta x$ (or $\Delta r$) is a **mathematically infinitesimal** control volume chosen such that:
1. Conduction occurs **perpendicular** to the shell faces.
2. Temperature and flux vary **linearly** across the shell (so the mean-value theorem applies).
3. After balancing, we take the limit $\Delta x \rightarrow 0$ to obtain an **ODE** for the temperature profile $T(x)$.

This procedure turns the **PDE** $\nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + S = \rho C_{p} \partial T / \partial t$ (with $S$ the volumetric heat-source term in **W·m⁻³**) into tractable **ODEs** whose order matches the dimensionality of the geometry (cartesian → 2nd-order linear, cylindrical/spherical → Bessel-type form).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The General Shell Energy Balance — Logic Steps

The procedure is identical for all geometries and follows five logical steps:

1. **Draw the shell** — Choose a slab of thickness $\Delta x$ (plane), $\Delta r$ (cylindrical), or $\Delta r$ (spherical) at a representative location.
2. **Identify the heat fluxes** — Energy enters by conduction across the inner face and leaves across the outer face.
3. **Identify energy generation** — Account for any *volumetric* source $S$ (W·m⁻³). For conduction with electricity: $S = \sigma E^{2} = E^{2} / \rho_{e} = I^{2} R_{e} / V$, where $\sigma$ is electrical conductivity, $\rho_{e}$ is electrical resistivity, $E$ is electric-field strength.
4. **Write the balance** — (In − Out + Generation) = 0 for steady state.
5. **Divide by $\Delta$(volume)**, let thickness $\rightarrow 0$, and integrate using the appropriate boundary conditions.

## 2.2 Classification of Heat-Conduction Problems Covered in KTU Module 3

| **Geometry** | **Source term $S$** | **Resulting ODE** | **Order** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Plane wall | $0$ | $\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = 0$ | Linear |
| Plane wall | Constant $S \neq 0$ | $\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = -\dfrac{S}{k}$ | Quadratic |
| Cylinder | $0$ | $\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$ | Logarithmic |
| Cylinder | Constant $S \neq 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\dfrac{S}{k}$ | Parabolic |
| Sphere | $0$ | $\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$ | Hyperbolic |
| Sphere | Constant $S \neq 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\dfrac{S}{k}$ | Polynomial |

## 2.3 The Five Canonical Boundary Conditions (KTU High-Yield Table)

| **#** | **Type** | **Mathematical form** | **Physical situation** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Specified surface temperature** | $T = T_{s}$ at $x = 0$ or $r = R$ | Surface in perfect contact with a large reservoir |
| 2 | **Specified heat flux** | $q_{x} = q_{s}$ or $-k\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}\big\vert_{x=0} = q_{s}$ | Known surface heating/cooling rate |
| 3 | **Insulated (adiabatic)** | $\dfrac{dT}{dx} = 0$ | Symmetry plane or wrapped insulation |
| 4 | **Convection at the surface** | $-k\,\dfrac{dT}{dr}\big\vert_{r=R} = h\,\big(T_{R} - T_{\infty}\big)$ | Surface losing heat to a flowing fluid |
| 5 | **Continuity of flux** | $-k_{1}\dfrac{dT_{1}}{dx} = -k_{2}\dfrac{dT_{2}}{dx}$ | Interface between two media |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU table above, the symbol $\big\vert$ (instead of $\mid$ or $\vert$) is used **only** for evaluative notation to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. Use `\big\vert` in your LaTeX submissions.

## 2.4 Physical Constants & Engineering Units Encountered

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **SI Unit** | **Typical value (KTU problems)** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Copper **398**, Stainless steel **16**, Insulation **0.04** |
| $h$ | Convective heat-transfer coefficient | W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ | Air (free) **5–25**, Water (forced) **100–1000** |
| $S$ | Volumetric heat-generation rate | W·m⁻³ | $10^{4}$ – $10^{7}$ |
| $\rho_{e}$ | Electrical resistivity | $\Omega \cdot m$ | Nichrome **1.10 × 10⁻⁶**, Copper **1.68 × 10⁻⁸** |
| $\sigma$ | Electrical conductivity | $\Omega^{-1} \cdot m^{-1}$ | $1/\rho_{e}$ |
| $I$ | Electric current | A | Application-specific |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The shell energy balance is the **design basis** for:
- **Electric resistance heaters** (calcination furnaces, water heaters, toasters)
- **Nuclear fuel rods** (uniform volumetric heat generation from fission)
- **Current-carrying busbars** in substations
- **Semiconductor devices** with Joule self-heating
- **Underground power cables** with dielectric losses

Knowing $T_{max}$ prevents **thermal runaway**, **insulation degradation**, and **fatigue failure**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Plane Wall with Electrical (Volumetric) Heat Generation

> **Problem Setup:** A plane wall of thickness $2L$ has uniform volumetric heat generation $S$ (W·m⁻³). The faces at $x = \pm L$ are held at temperature $T_{w}$. Material has constant thermal conductivity $k$. Find the temperature distribution $T(x)$ and $T_{max}$.

### Step 1 — Choose the shell
Select a slab of thickness $\Delta x$ at position $x$, with cross-sectional area $A$.

### Step 2 — Energy flux entering at $x$
By Fourier's law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{x} \;\big\vert_{x} \;=\; q_{x} \cdot A \;=\; \left(-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x}\right) A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy flux leaving at $x + \Delta x$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{x}\big\vert_{x+\Delta x} \;=\; \left(-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x+\Delta x}\right) A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Heat generated inside the shell
The volume of the shell is $A \cdot \Delta x$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Generation} \;=\; S \cdot A \cdot \Delta x
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Steady-state energy balance
At steady state, with no accumulation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{x}\big\vert_{x} \;-\; W_{x}\big\vert_{x+\Delta x} \;+\; S \cdot A \cdot \Delta x \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange and divide by $\Delta x$

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\dfrac{1}{\Delta x}\!\left[W_{x}\big\vert_{x+\Delta x} - W_{x}\big\vert_{x}\right] \;+\; S \cdot A \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\dfrac{dW_{x}}{dx} + S\cdot A = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(-kA\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right) + SA = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Simplify (A and k are constants)

$$
\begin{aligned}
kA\,\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} + SA = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = -\dfrac{S}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Integrate once

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dT}{dx} = -\dfrac{S}{k}\,x + C_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 10 — Apply Symmetry BC
By symmetry, the centre plane $x = 0$ is the maximum-temperature location and $dT/dx = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 = -\dfrac{S}{k}\cdot 0 + C_{1} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{1} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 11 — Integrate again

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x) = -\dfrac{S}{2k}\,x^{2} + C_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 12 — Apply Surface BC at $x = L$: $T(L) = T_{w}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{w} = -\dfrac{S}{2k}\,L^{2} + C_{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{2} = T_{w} + \dfrac{S L^{2}}{2k}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result — Plane Wall

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(x) - T_{w} = \dfrac{S}{2k}\,(L^{2} - x^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} = T(0) = T_{w} + \dfrac{S L^{2}}{2k}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Cylindrical Wire with Electrical Heat Generation (KTU MOST FREQUENT PROBLEM)

> **Problem Setup:** A long solid cylindrical wire of radius $R$ carries a current $I$. Electrical resistivity is $\rho_{e}$. The surface temperature is held at $T_{w}$. Find $T(r)$ and $T_{max}$ at the centreline.

### Step 1 — Identify the volumetric heat generation rate
The total electrical dissipation is $I^{2}R_{e}$ where $R_{e} = \rho_{e}L_{wire}/A_{cs}$. Per unit volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \dfrac{I^{2}R_{e}}{V_{wire}} = \dfrac{I^{2}\rho_{e}L/\big(\pi R^{2}\big)}{\pi R^{2} L} = \dfrac{I^{2}\rho_{e}}{\big(\pi R^{2}\big)^{2}} \quad\text{(in W·m⁻³)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Choose the cylindrical shell
A shell at radius $r$, thickness $\Delta r$, length $L$, has:
- Lateral surface area (inner): $A_{r} = 2\pi r L$
- Lateral surface area (outer): $A_{r+\Delta r} = 2\pi(r+\Delta r)L$
- Volume of shell: $V_{sh} = 2\pi r L \cdot \Delta r$ (to leading order)

### Step 3 — Write the steady-state energy balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{r}\cdot\big(2\pi r L\big) - q_{r+\Delta r}\cdot\big(2\pi(r+\Delta r)L\big) + S\cdot 2\pi r L \cdot \Delta r = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Divide by $2\pi L \cdot \Delta r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\Delta r}\!\left[\,r\,q_{r} - (r+\Delta r)\,q_{r+\Delta r}\right] + S\,r = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Take the limit $\Delta r \to 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\big(r\,q_{r}\big) + S\,r = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{d}{dr}\!\big(r\,q_{r}\big) = S\,r
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Integrate once with respect to $r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r\,q_{r} = \dfrac{S r^{2}}{2} + C_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{r} = \dfrac{S r}{2} + \dfrac{C_{1}}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply BC at $r = 0$: flux must be finite
To avoid the singularity at the centreline, $C_{1} = 0$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{r} = \dfrac{S r}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Substitute Fourier's Law $q_{r} = -k(dT/dr)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
-k\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} = \dfrac{S r}{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -\dfrac{S}{2k}\,r
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Integrate once more

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(r) = -\dfrac{S}{4k}\,r^{2} + C_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 10 — Apply BC at $r = R$: $T(R) = T_{w}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{w} = -\dfrac{S}{4k}\,R^{2} + C_{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{2} = T_{w} + \dfrac{S R^{2}}{4k}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result — Cylindrical Wire

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(r) - T_{w} = \dfrac{S}{4k}\,\big(R^{2} - r^{2}\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} \;=\; T(0) \;=\; T_{w} + \dfrac{S R^{2}}{4k}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The expression $T_{max} = T_{w} + SR^{2}/(4k)$ is the *single most asked* result from this module. Memorize the **parabolic profile**, the **parabolic profile coefficient**, and the form of $S$ in terms of $I$ and $\rho_{e}$.

### Useful Variants — In Terms of Current $I$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \dfrac{I^{2}\rho_{e}}{\pi^{2}R^{4}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T_{max} - T_{w} = \dfrac{I^{2}\rho_{e}}{4\pi^{2}kR^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Spherical Shell with Heat Generation (For Completeness)

### Step 1 — Shell balance in spherical geometry
Shell volume: $4\pi r^{2}\Delta r$. Heat fluxes through areas $4\pi r^{2}$ and $4\pi(r+\Delta r)^{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\big(r^{2}\,q_{r}\big) = S\,r^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate and apply $C_{1} = 0$ (finite flux at centre)

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^{2}\,q_{r} = \dfrac{S r^{3}}{3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad q_{r} = \dfrac{S r}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Fourier's Law and integrate

$$
\begin{aligned}
-k\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} = \dfrac{S r}{3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T(r) = -\dfrac{S}{6k}\,r^{2} + C_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply $T(R) = T_{w}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(r) - T_{w} = \dfrac{S}{6k}\,(R^{2} - r^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} \;=\; T(0) \;=\; T_{w} + \dfrac{S R^{2}}{6k}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient Memory Trick (KTU):** For plane: $1/2k$, for cylinder: $1/4k$, for sphere: $1/6k$. Numerator: $SL^{2}$, $SR^{2}$, $SR^{2}$ respectively.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Convection Boundary Condition (Surface Energy Balance)

When a wire of surface temperature $T_{R}$ loses heat by natural convection to ambient air at $T_{\infty}$ with coefficient $h$, the surface boundary condition is obtained from an energy balance *on the surface itself*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Conduction to surface (from inside)} \;=\; \text{Convection from surface}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
-k\,\dfrac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R} = h\,\big(T_{R} - T_{\infty}\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting our wire-solution $dT/dr = -Sr/(2k)$ at $r=R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-k\!\left(-\dfrac{SR}{2k}\right) = h\,\big(T_{R} - T_{\infty}\big) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{SR}{2} = h\,\big(T_{R} - T_{\infty}\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the surface temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{R} = T_{\infty} + \dfrac{SR}{2h}
\end{aligned}
$$

And consequently, the centreline temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} = T_{\infty} + \dfrac{SR}{2h} + \dfrac{SR^{2}}{4k}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the convection BC as $-k\,dT/dr = h(T_{R} - T_{\infty})$ **without the correct sign convention**. The negative sign on the left is critical because $dT/dr$ at the surface is negative (temperature decreases outward) for a heat-generating wire.

---

## 3.5 ANALYTICAL SUMMARY MATRIX — T(r) for All Geometries with Constant $S$

| **Geometry** | **ODE** | **$T(r) - T_{w}$** | **$T_{max} - T_{w}$** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Plane wall ($x$) | $\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = -\dfrac{S}{k}$ | $\dfrac{S}{2k}\,(L^{2}-x^{2})$ | $\dfrac{S L^{2}}{2k}$ |
| Cylinder ($r$) | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\dfrac{S}{k}$ | $\dfrac{S}{4k}\,(R^{2}-r^{2})$ | $\dfrac{S R^{2}}{4k}$ |
| Sphere ($r$) | $\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\dfrac{S}{k}$ | $\dfrac{S}{6k}\,(R^{2}-r^{2})$ | $\dfrac{S R^{2}}{6k}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
