# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Methodology of Shell Energy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Identify Geometry - Plane Cylinder or Sphere"] --> B["Step 2: Draw Differential Shell - Thickness deltax or deltar"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Apply Fourier Law - q = -k dTdx at both faces"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Include Source Term - S for electrical generation"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Write Steady-State Balance - In minus Out plus Generation = 0"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Divide by deltax and Take Limit - convert to ODE"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Integrate - obtain general T x or T r solution"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Apply Boundary Conditions - symmetry and surface temp"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Final Temperature Profile - T r equals Tw plus SR2 over 4k minus r2"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Compute Tmax and Engineering Design Check"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Setup of Current-Carrying Wire

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Electric Source - V volts I amperes"] --> Q["Current I passes through Nichrome wire - resistivity rho_e"]
    Q --> R["Joule Heating - I squared R_e converted to heat inside wire"]
    R --> S["Heat conducted radially outward through cylindrical shell"]
    S --> T["Convective loss to ambient - h at surface"]
    T --> U["Ambient Air at T_infinity"]
    R --> V["Centreline Temperature T_max - design parameter"]
    V --> W["Must remain below melting point of conductor"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Boundary Condition Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC["Choose Boundary Condition"] --> BC1["Surface at fixed temperature - Dirichlet: T equals Ts"]
    BC --> BC2["Surface with imposed heat flux - Neumann: q equals qs"]
    BC --> BC3["Insulated end - adiabatic: dTdx equals 0"]
    BC --> BC4["Surface in convective fluid - Newton cooling: -k dTdr equals h TR minus Tinf"]
    BC --> BC5["Interface continuity - flux match: k1 dT1dx equals k2 dT2dx"]
    BC2 --> N["Example - electrically heated surface with known S"]
    BC4 --> N2["Example - wire cooling in air - most KTU asked"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Electrical-to-Thermal Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Electrical Energy Input - VI watts"] --> B["Ohmic Dissipation in Conductor - I squared R_e"]
    B --> C["Volumetric Heat Source Term - S watts per cubic meter"]
    C --> D["Heat Conduction Through Wire Cross-section - Fourier Law"]
    D --> E["Radial Temperature Distribution - parabolic profile"]
    E --> F["Centreline Hot Spot - T_max limits design"]
    E --> G["Surface Heat Loss - convection or radiation"]
    G --> H["Steady-State Energy Balance achieved - design safe"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
Define the **shell energy balance**. State the general differential equation it reduces to in steady state with constant thermal conductivity and volumetric heat generation $S$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The shell energy balance is a conservation-of-energy statement applied to a differentially thin shell of material, in which (Rate of energy in by conduction) − (Rate of energy out by conduction) + (Rate of volumetric heat generation) = 0 at steady state.

For constant $k$, the general differential equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k\,\nabla^{2} T + S = 0 \quad\text{or}\quad \nabla^{2} T = -\dfrac{S}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

In Cartesian coordinates this expands to $\partial^{2}T/\partial x^{2} + \partial^{2}T/\partial y^{2} + \partial^{2}T/\partial z^{2} = -S/k$. **(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the equation)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series-2]
List **any five** boundary conditions encountered in shell-energy-balance heat-conduction problems and write the mathematical form of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **#** | **BC** | **Mathematical form** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Specified surface temperature | $T(R) = T_{w}$ |
| 2 | Specified heat flux | $-k\,dT/dr \big\vert_{r=R} = q_{s}$ |
| 3 | Symmetry / insulated | $dT/dr = 0$ at $r=0$ |
| 4 | Convection at surface | $-k\,dT/dr \big\vert_{r=R} = h(T_{R}-T_{\infty})$ |
| 5 | Continuity of flux at interface | $k_{1}\,dT_{1}/dx = k_{2}\,dT_{2}/dx$ |

**(½ mark each — any five)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Derive the steady-state temperature distribution $T(r)$ for a long solid cylindrical wire of radius $R$ carrying an electric current $I$, given that the surface temperature is maintained at $T_{w}$. The wire has electrical resistivity $\rho_{e}$ and thermal conductivity $k$. State and justify the boundary conditions used.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A nichrome wire of radius $R = 2$ mm and thermal conductivity $k = 17.4$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ carries a current of 50 A. The electrical resistivity is $\rho_{e} = 1.10 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega \cdot m$. The surface temperature is $T_{w} = 60^{\circ}$C. Calculate (i) the volumetric heat-generation rate $S$, (ii) the centreline temperature $T_{max}$, and (iii) the radial heat flux $q_{r}$ at $r = 1.5$ mm.* **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

[Full derivation as in Section 3.2 — Steps 1 to 10 — yielding]

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(r) - T_{w} = \dfrac{S}{4k}\,(R^{2}-r^{2}), \quad\text{where}\quad S = \dfrac{I^{2}\rho_{e}}{\pi^{2}R^{4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary conditions:**
1. **Symmetry at centreline** ($r=0$): $dT/dr = 0$ (imposing $C_{1}=0$ for finite flux). **[1 mark]**
2. **Specified surface temperature** at $r=R$: $T(R) = T_{w}$. **[1 mark]**

(Additional marks distributed across Steps 1–10 of the derivation.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $R = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $k = 17.4$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, $I = 50$ A, $\rho_{e} = 1.10 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega \cdot m$, $T_{w} = 60^{\circ}$C.

**(i) Volumetric heat generation rate $S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \dfrac{I^{2}\rho_{e}}{\pi^{2}R^{4}} = \dfrac{(50)^{2}\times 1.10\times 10^{-6}}{\pi^{2}\times (2\times 10^{-3})^{4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerator: $2500 \times 1.10 \times 10^{-6} = 2.75 \times 10^{-3}$ W·m⁻¹
Denominator: $\pi^{2} \times 16 \times 10^{-12} = 9.8696 \times 16 \times 10^{-12} = 1.5791 \times 10^{-10}$ m⁴

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \dfrac{2.75 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5791 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.7414 \times 10^{7}\ \text{W·m}^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Centreline temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} = T_{w} + \dfrac{S R^{2}}{4k} = 60 + \dfrac{1.7414 \times 10^{7} \times (2\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4 \times 17.4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
= 60 + \dfrac{1.7414 \times 10^{7} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{69.6} = 60 + \dfrac{69.656}{69.6} = 60 + 1.001 = 61.0^{\circ}\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Radial heat flux at $r = 1.5$ mm:**

Using $q_{r} = Sr/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{r} = \dfrac{S r}{2} = \dfrac{1.7414 \times 10^{7} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.306 \times 10^{4}\ \text{W·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(About **13.06 kW/m²** flowing radially outward — this is the heat that the convection boundary condition must ultimately dissipate.)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Module Question]

**(a)** *A plane wall of thickness $2L$ has uniform volumetric heat generation $S$ (W·m⁻³) due to embedded electrical heating elements. The wall surfaces at $x = \pm L$ are maintained at $T_{w}$. With constant thermal conductivity $k$, derive the temperature profile $T(x)$ and the maximum temperature $T_{max}$.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A composite plane wall consists of two layers of equal thickness $L$. Layer 1 (inner) has $k_{1}$ and electrical heat generation $S_{1}$. Layer 2 (outer) has $k_{2}$ and no generation. The outer surface at $x = 2L$ is exposed to convection with coefficient $h$ and ambient $T_{\infty}$. The interface at $x = L$ has continuous temperature and flux. Using shell-balance methodology, set up the governing ODEs and write the boundary conditions for the steady-state temperature distribution.* **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution (Outline)**

**Part (a):** Full derivation as in Section 3.1, yielding $T(x) - T_{w} = (S/2k)(L^{2}-x^{2})$ and $T_{max} = T_{w} + SL^{2}/(2k)$. **[7 marks]**

**Part (b):**

Layer 1 ODE:
$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{1}\,\dfrac{d^{2}T_{1}}{dx^{2}} + S_{1} = 0 \quad\text{(at } 0 \leq x \leq L\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Layer 2 ODE:
$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{2}\,\dfrac{d^{2}T_{2}}{dx^{2}} = 0 \quad\text{(at } L \leq x \leq 2L\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Boundary conditions:
1. **Symmetry / no flux at $x=0$**: $\dfrac{dT_{1}}{dx}\big\vert_{x=0} = 0$
2. **Temperature continuity at $x=L$**: $T_{1}(L) = T_{2}(L)$
3. **Flux continuity at $x=L$**: $-k_{1}\dfrac{dT_{1}}{dx}\big\vert_{L} = -k_{2}\dfrac{dT_{2}}{dx}\big\vert_{L}$
4. **Convection at $x=2L$**: $-k_{2}\dfrac{dT_{2}}{dx}\big\vert_{2L} = h\big(T_{2}(2L) - T_{\infty}\big)$

Four BCs for two second-order ODEs — the problem is well-posed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $S$**: Heat generation *adds* energy, so the ODE is $kT'' + S = 0$, **not** $kT'' - S = 0$. (Common: −2 marks)
> 2. **Missing the $C_{1}/r$ singularity elimination** at $r=0$ in cylindrical problems. (Common: −1 mark)
> 3. **Writing wrong area terms**: Cylindrical area is $2\pi r L$, *not* $2\pi r^{2}$. (Common: −2 marks)
> 4. **Unit confusion**: $S$ is in **W·m⁻³** (volumetric), not W·m⁻². (Common: −1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in numerical problems — guarantees zero marks in Part (b). (Common: −3 marks)

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Memory Map — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ **Shell energy balance** = (In − Out + Generation) = 0 at steady state.
- ✅ **Fourier's Law** (memorize): $q = -k(dT/dx)$ with units **W·m⁻²**.
- ✅ **Plane wall result**: $T_{max} - T_{w} = SL^{2}/(2k)$ — *parabolic* profile.
- ✅ **Cylindrical wire result**: $T_{max} - T_{w} = SR^{2}/(4k)$ — *parabolic* profile.
- ✅ **Spherical result**: $T_{max} - T_{w} = SR^{2}/(6k)$ — *parabolic* profile.
- ✅ **Electrical source**: $S = I^{2}\rho_{e}/(\pi^{2}R^{4})$ for a wire of radius $R$.
- ✅ **Alternative form**: $S = \sigma E^{2} = E^{2}/\rho_{e}$.
- ✅ **Radial flux in wire**: $q_{r} = Sr/2$ (always positive outward).
- ✅ **Five BCs**: Specified $T$, specified $q$, insulated ($dT/dr=0$), convection (Newton's law), flux continuity.
- ✅ **Symmetry BC at centreline**: $dT/dr = 0$.
- ✅ **Convection BC**: $-k\,dT/dr \big\vert_{R} = h(T_{R} - T_{\infty})$.
- ✅ **Cylindrical area**: $A = 2\pi r L$, shell volume: $dV = 2\pi r L \cdot dr$.
- ✅ **Spherical area**: $A = 4\pi r^{2}$, shell volume: $dV = 4\pi r^{2} dr$.
- ✅ **Coefficient memory trick**: $1/2k$, $1/4k$, $1/6k$ for plane/cylinder/sphere.
- ✅ **Order of ODE = number of BCs required**: 2 for 2nd-order ODE.
- ✅ **Steady state** ⇒ no $\partial T/\partial t$ term in the energy equation.
- ✅ **Constant $k$ assumption**: required for the ODEs to be linear; otherwise the problem becomes non-linear.

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES — MODULE 3, PCCHT602**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear and viscous heat sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nuclear and viscous heat sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **general energy equation of change** for a Newtonian fluid, two physically distinct volumetric heat sources appear on the right-hand side of the temperature form of the equation:

$$\rho \hat{C}_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = -\left(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q}\right) + \Phi_v + S$$

The two source terms are:

- **Viscous Heat Source ($\Phi_v$)** — The rate of irreversible conversion of mechanical (kinetic) energy into thermal energy per unit volume, arising from the action of viscous stresses on velocity gradients in a deforming fluid element.
- **Nuclear / Volumetric Heat Source ($S$)** — The rate of thermal energy generation per unit volume due to internal physical processes such as nuclear reactions, radioactive decay, exothermic/endothermic chemical reactions, Joule (resistive) heating, or electromagnetic absorption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The viscous dissipation function $\Phi_v$ is the **scalar contraction of the viscous stress tensor with the rate-of-strain tensor**, $\Phi_v = \boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}$. For Newtonian fluids, this contraction yields a sum of squares of velocity gradients multiplied by viscosity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Viscous Heating Analogy — "Rubbing Your Hands":**
Imagine rubbing your palms together briskly. Your hands heat up, even though the room is cool. The muscular work you supply is being dissipated as heat through friction between the moving surfaces. In a fluid, microscopic layers of fluid "rub" against each other as they slide at different velocities. This micro-friction converts the organized kinetic energy of the bulk flow into random thermal motion — i.e., **heat**. This is *viscous dissipation*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order-of-magnitude check:** $\Phi_v \sim \mu \left(\dfrac{V}{L}\right)^2$. For air ($\mu \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s) at $V = 1$ m/s and $L = 0.01$ m, $\Phi_v \sim 0.18$ W/m³ — negligible. For a polymer melt ($\mu \approx 10^3$ Pa·s) at $V = 0.1$ m/s and $L = 10^{-3}$ m, $\Phi_v \sim 10^7$ W/m³ — *dominant*. This is why viscous heating matters in polymer processing, lubrication, and high-speed gas dynamics.

**Nuclear/Volumetric Source Analogy — "The Heated Rod":**
Picture a current-carrying Nichrome wire immersed in a stagnant fluid. Even though the fluid is motionless (no viscous dissipation), the wire heats up and warms the surrounding fluid. The heat is generated *internally* by Joule heating and is mathematically represented by a source term $S(x,y,z)$ that has units of **W/m³**. Nuclear reactor fuel rods and exothermic catalytic pellets are other physical examples.

> [!NOTE]
> The key distinction: $\Phi_v$ is **velocity-gradient dependent** (it vanishes in a stagnant fluid), while $S$ is **purely a property of the material** and is independent of flow kinematics (the source is present even at rest).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Units |
|----------|--------|------------------|-------|
| Newtonian viscosity | $\mu$ | $10^{-3}$ (water) to $10^{3}$ (polymer) | **Pa·s** |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $10^{-6}$ (gas) to $10^{-3}$ (liquid) | **m²/s** |
| Volumetric heat source | $S$ | $10^{5}$ to $10^{9}$ | **W/m³** |
| Viscous dissipation | $\Phi_v$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{9}$ | **W/m³** |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | **W/(m²·K⁴)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the Viscous Dissipation Function $\Phi_v$

The starting point is the **general equation of energy change** in terms of internal energy $U$ per unit mass:

$$\rho \frac{DU}{Dt} = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q} - P (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) - (\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}) + S$$

By combining this with the **mechanical energy equation** (which contains $-(\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v})$) and the **Gibbs equation** $dH = \hat{C}_p dT$ for incompressible flow, we obtain the **equation of energy in terms of temperature**:

$$\rho \hat{C}_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q} + \Phi_v + S$$

where $\Phi_v$ is the **dissipation function** — a *positive semi-definite* quadratic form of the velocity gradients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $\Phi_v$ always positive?** Because it represents an irreversible conversion (analogous to $I^2R$ losses). The second law requires the rate of entropy production to be non-negative; $\Phi_v$ contributes directly to it.

### 2.2 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Mechanical work is done by viscous stresses** as the fluid element deforms.
2. This work is **not stored** (unlike pressure work which changes volume) but is **entirely converted to heat**.
3. The rate of this conversion per unit volume is $\Phi_v = \boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}$.
4. For a Newtonian fluid with viscosity $\mu$, the stress tensor is linear in the velocity gradient, making $\Phi_v$ a *quadratic* function of velocity gradients — guaranteeing $\Phi_v \geq 0$.

### 2.3 The Brunt-Väisälä / Rayleigh Criterion for Viscous Dissipation Importance

The relative importance of viscous dissipation to conductive heat transport is judged by the **Brinkman number**:

$$\text{Br} = \frac{\mu V^2}{k (T_w - T_0)}$$

- $\text{Br} \ll 1$: conduction dominates; viscous heating negligible.
- $\text{Br} \sim 1$: both effects comparable; **both must be retained**.
- $\text{Br} \gg 1$: viscous heating dominant; conduction can be neglected (adiabatic viscous heating).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All expressions below are **directly expected in KTU 2024 examinations**. Master the boxed forms.

#### A. Viscous Dissipation Function — Rectangular Coordinates $(x, y, z)$

$$\boxed{\Phi_v = \mu \left\{ 2 \left[ \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^2 \right] + \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x}\right)^2 \right\}}$$

#### B. Viscous Dissipation Function — Cylindrical Coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$

$$\Phi_v = \mu \left\{ 2 \left[ \left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{v_r}{r}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^2 \right] \right. \\ \left. + \left[ r \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} \right]^2 + \left[ \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} \right]^2 + \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + r \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) \right]^2 \right\}$$

#### C. Viscous Dissipation Function — Spherical Coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$

$$\Phi_v = \mu \left\{ 2 \left[ \left(\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{v_r}{r}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{r \sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{v_r}{r} + \frac{v_\theta \cot\theta}{r}\right)^2 \right] \right. \\ \left. + \left[ r \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} \right]^2 + \left[ \frac{\sin\theta}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{v_\phi}{\sin\theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r \sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \phi} \right]^2 \right. \\ \left. + \left[ \frac{1}{r \sin\theta}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \phi} + r \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{v_\phi}{r}\right) \right]^2 \right\}$$

#### D. General Energy Equation (Temperature Form)

$$\rho \hat{C}_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi_v + S$$

#### E. Common Forms of the Source Term $S$

| Source Type | Mathematical Form | Engineering Context |
|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Uniform nuclear heating | $S = S_0$ (constant) | Nuclear fuel rod |
| First-order reaction | $S = (-\Delta H_r) k_r C_A$ | Chemical reactor |
| Joule heating | $S = \sigma_{\text{elec}} \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^2$ | Electric heaters |
| Radioactive decay | $S = \rho q'''_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Spent fuel storage |
| Electromagnetic | $S = \epsilon \sigma \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^2$ | Microwave heating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (rendered as `\vert`) in tables, NOT bare pipes**, to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. This is the prescribed convention in the KTU-Premier-Engine.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer extrusion & injection molding:** Viscous dissipation is the *primary* heat source in non-Newtonian polymer flow; ignoring it leads to incorrect die-temperature predictions.
- **Lubricant films in journal bearings:** $\Phi_v$ sets the operating temperature limits; thermal runaway can cause bearing seizure.
- **Nuclear reactor coolant design:** Volumetric source $S$ from fission must be coupled with convective cooling in the energy balance.
- **High-speed aerodynamics:** Supersonic flow past re-entry vehicles generates shock-induced viscous heating — critical for thermal protection systems.
- **Microfluidics and MEMS:** Although $\Phi_v$ is small in absolute terms, the high surface-area-to-volume ratio makes it non-negligible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $\Phi_v$ from the Stress Tensor Contraction

**Step 1 — Recall the Newtonian viscous stress tensor in rectangular coordinates:**

The Newtonian stress tensor in rectangular coordinates is given by:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \begin{pmatrix} \tau_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \tau_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \tau_{zz} \end{pmatrix}$$

For a Newtonian fluid with constant $\mu$ and $\kappa$ (bulk viscosity), the components are:

$$\tau_{xx} = - \frac{2}{3} \mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + 2 \mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}$$

$$\tau_{yy} = - \frac{2}{3} \mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + 2 \mu \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}$$

$$\tau_{zz} = - \frac{2}{3} \mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + 2 \mu \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} \right)$$

$$\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} \right)$$

$$\tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Define the rate-of-strain tensor $\nabla \mathbf{v}$:**

The rate-of-strain tensor has components:

$$(\nabla \mathbf{v})_{ij} = \frac{\partial v_i}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the scalar contraction $\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}$:**

By definition of the double-dot product:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v} = \sum_{i,j} \tau_{ij} \frac{\partial v_i}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and expand term-by-term:**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v} = \tau_{xx} \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \tau_{yy} \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \tau_{zz} \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} \\ + \tau_{xy} \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} \right) + \tau_{yz} \left( \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} \right) + \tau_{xz} \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Insert the Newtonian stress expressions:**

Plugging in the Newtonian stress components (and grouping the diagonal terms):

$$\Phi_v = -\frac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} \right) \\ + 2\mu \left[ \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^2 \right] \\ + \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} \right)^2 + \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} \right)^2 + \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} \right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Note the incompressible-flow simplification:**

Since $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ for an incompressible fluid, the first term vanishes and we recover the boxed result of Section 2.3.A.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The $-\frac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v})^2$ term only matters for compressible flow (e.g., gas dynamics). For liquids (KTU default assumption), use the boxed form.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Adiabatic Couette Flow with Viscous Dissipation

**Problem Setup:** Two parallel plates separated by distance $2b$. The lower plate ($y = -b$) is stationary, the upper plate ($y = +b$) moves at $V$ in the $x$-direction. Pressure gradient is zero, no external heat source ($S = 0$). Find the temperature distribution.

**Step 1 — Velocity field from Navier-Stokes:**

For steady, fully-developed flow with $\mathbf{v} = (v_x(y), 0, 0)$ and $dP/dx = 0$, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = 0$$

Integrating twice with BCs $v_x(-b) = 0$ and $v_x(+b) = V$:

$$v_x(y) = \frac{V}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{y}{b} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the viscous dissipation:**

The only non-zero gradient is $dv_x/dy = V/(2b)$, so:

$$\Phi_v = \mu \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} \right)^2 = \mu \left( \frac{V}{2b} \right)^2 = \frac{\mu V^2}{4 b^2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy equation:**

The temperature form of the energy equation in the absence of sources and axial conduction becomes:

$$0 = k \frac{d^2 T}{dy^2} + \Phi_v$$

Substituting:

$$k \frac{d^2 T}{dy^2} = - \frac{\mu V^2}{4 b^2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate twice:**

First integration:

$$\frac{dT}{dy} = - \frac{\mu V^2}{4 k b^2} y + C_1$$

By symmetry ($dT/dy = 0$ at $y = 0$), $C_1 = 0$.

Second integration:

$$T(y) = - \frac{\mu V^2}{8 k b^2} y^2 + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply thermal boundary conditions:**

For an adiabatic system (no heat loss to plates), $dT/dy = 0$ at $y = \pm b$ is *automatically satisfied* since $y$-linear term is zero. Setting $T(0) = T_0$ gives $C_2 = T_0$:

$$\boxed{T(y) = T_0 + \frac{\mu V^2}{8 k b^2} (b^2 - y^2)}$$

**Step 6 — Find the maximum temperature rise:**

Maximum temperature occurs at the centerline ($y = 0$):

$$\Delta T_{\max} = T(0) - T_0 = \frac{\mu V^2}{8 k}$$

For water ($\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.6$ W/m·K) at $V = 1$ m/s: $\Delta T_{\max} \approx 2 \times 10^{-4}$ K (negligible).
For a polymer melt ($\mu = 10^3$ Pa·s, $k = 0.2$ W/m·K) at $V = 0.1$ m/s: $\Delta T_{\max} \approx 6.25$ K (significant!).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The $\Delta T_{\max} \propto \mu V^2 / k$ scaling means that viscous dissipation effects are amplified in **viscous, slow-moving, poorly-conducting** fluids — exactly the regime of polymer processing.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Unidirectional Flow Dissipations

| Flow Type | Velocity Field | $\Phi_v$ Expression | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|-------|
| Plane Couette | $v_x = \gamma y$ | $\mu \gamma^2$ | Constant |
| Plane Poiseuille | $v_x = v_{\max}(1 - y^2/b^2)$ | $4 \mu v_{\max}^2 y^2 / b^4$ | Parabolic in $y$ |
| Pipe Poiseuille | $v_z = v_{\max}(1 - r^2/R^2)$ | $4 \mu v_{\max}^2 r^2 / R^4$ | Parabolic in $r$ |
| Axial annular | $v_z = A + B \ln r$ | $\mu (B/r)^2$ | Singular at $r = 0$ |
| Rotating cylinder | $v_\theta = \omega r$ | $\mu (2\omega)^2 = 4\mu \omega^2$ | Constant (Taylor-Couette) |
| Cone-and-plate | $v_\theta = \omega r \sin\theta / \theta_0$ | Complex | Viscometry |

> [!NOTE]
> In all listed cases, $\Phi_v$ is **independent of thermal field** — it depends only on the velocity profile. The temperature distribution is then obtained by solving the linear ODE for $T$ with $\Phi_v$ as a known source term.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Heat Source Integration in the Energy Equation

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *flow of information* from the velocity field and source distribution into the energy balance and finally to the temperature field:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Velocity Field v x,y,z] --> B[Velocity Gradient Tensor del v]
    C[Volumetric Source S] --> D[Energy Equation Assembly]
    B --> E[Newtonian Stress Tensor tau]
    E --> F[Double Contraction tau colon del v]
    F --> G[Viscous Dissipation Phi v]
    G --> D
    H[Thermal Conductivity k] --> D
    D --> I{Equation Form}
    I -->|Steady| J[Solve ODE in T]
    I -->|Unsteady| K[Solve PDE in T x,y,z,t]
    J --> L[Temperature Profile T y]
    K --> M[Transient Temperature Field]
    L --> N[Physical Interpretation]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compare with Brinkman Number]
    O --> P{Viscous Heating Dominant?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Polymer Processing Bearings Lubrication]
    P -->|No| R[Standard Heat Exchanger Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Couette Flow with Viscous Dissipation — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upper Plate: V] -->|Moves in x direction| B[Fluid Layer Thickness 2b]
    B --> C[Shear Rate gamma equals V over 2b]
    C --> D[Compute Phi v equals mu gamma squared]
    D --> E[Solve k d2T over dy2 plus Phi v equals 0]
    E --> F[Parabolic Temperature Profile T y]
    F --> G[Delta T max equals mu V squared over 8 k]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: When to Include Viscous Dissipation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Heat Transfer Problem] --> Q1{Is the fluid Newtonian?}
    Q1 -->|No| Out1[Use non-Newtonian Phi v form]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Calculate Brinkman Number Br}
    Q2 --> Q3{Br greater than 0.1?}
    Q3 -->|No| Out2[Neglect Phi v - Conduction Dominant]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Out3[Include Phi v - Coupled Solution Required]
    Q2 --> Q4{Is the system compressible?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Out4[Add bulk viscosity term negative 2 over 3 mu times divergence squared]
    Q4 -->|No| Out5[Incompressible form: Phi v equals mu times sum of squares]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B1`), all labels are quoted and contain raw text only (no markdown formatting), and no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as node names. This ensures the diagrams render correctly across all KTU-supported Markdown viewers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the viscous dissipation function $\Phi_v$ for a Newtonian fluid. Why is it always non-negative?

**Model Answer:**

The viscous dissipation function $\Phi_v$ is defined as the scalar double contraction of the viscous stress tensor with the rate-of-strain tensor:

$$\Phi_v = \boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v} = \sum_i \sum_j \tau_{ij} \frac{\partial v_i}{\partial x_j}$$

For a Newtonian fluid in rectangular coordinates:

$$\Phi_v = \mu \left\{ 2 \left[ \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}\right)^2 \right] + \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial x}\right)^2 \right\}$$

(For an incompressible fluid, the $-\frac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v})^2$ term vanishes.)

**Why non-negative:** $\Phi_v$ is a sum of squares of velocity gradients, each multiplied by the positive viscosity $\mu > 0$. Hence $\Phi_v \geq 0$ for all kinematic states, with equality only when the fluid is in rigid-body motion (no deformation). Physically, it represents irreversible conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy, consistent with the second law of thermodynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3-mark allocation):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the expression, 1 mark for the non-negativity argument. State the Newtonian stress assumption explicitly to claim full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is meant by a "nuclear heat source" in the context of the energy equation? Give two engineering examples.

**Model Answer:**

A **nuclear heat source** (more generally, a *volumetric heat source*) is a term $S$ in the energy equation $\rho \hat{C}_p DT/Dt = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q} + \Phi_v + S$ that represents the rate of thermal energy generation per unit volume by internal physical or chemical processes, *independent* of the fluid's velocity field.

**Mathematical representation:**

$$S = \lim_{\Delta V \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta V} \frac{dE_{\text{gen}}}{dt} \quad \text{[W/m³]}$$

**Two engineering examples:**

1. **Nuclear fuel rod in a reactor core:** Fission of U-235 releases energy volumetrically at a rate $S = \phi \Sigma_f E_f$, where $\phi$ is the neutron flux, $\Sigma_f$ the macroscopic fission cross-section, and $E_f$ the energy released per fission (~200 MeV).

2. **Exothermic chemical reactor:** For a first-order irreversible reaction $A \to B$, the heat generation is $S = (-\Delta H_r) k_r C_A$, where $\Delta H_r$ is the heat of reaction (negative for exothermic).

(Other valid examples: Joule heating in resistance heaters, microwave absorption in food processing, radioactive decay in spent fuel pools.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A common mistake is to confuse $S$ with $\Phi_v$. Emphasize that $S$ is a **material property** (or imposed field), while $\Phi_v$ is a **kinematic property**. This distinction is worth 1 of the 3 marks.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B features **internal choice**. The model question paper provides two alternatives; students answer one.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the general equation of energy change in terms of internal energy, derive the **equation of energy in terms of temperature** for a Newtonian fluid undergoing incompressible flow with viscous dissipation and a uniform volumetric heat source $S_0$. State clearly all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A liquid metal of density $\rho = 7000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and $k = 30$ W/(m·K) flows steadily between two large parallel plates separated by 0.02 m. The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate moves at $V = 5$ m/s. The fluid is also subjected to uniform internal heat generation $S_0 = 10^7$ W/m³. Assuming linear velocity profile, derive the steady-state temperature distribution if the lower plate is held at $T_w = 300$ K and the upper plate is insulated.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**
- Incompressible Newtonian fluid.
- Constant $\rho$, $\mu$, $k$, $\hat{C}_p$.
- No chemical reaction (other than captured in $S_0$).
- Isotropic thermal conductivity.

**Step 1 — Energy equation in terms of $U$:**

$$\rho \frac{DU}{Dt} = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q} - P (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) - (\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}) + S$$

**Step 2 — Fourier's law:** $\mathbf{q} = -k \nabla T$, so $-\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q} = k \nabla^2 T$.

**Step 3 — Mechanical energy equation:**

$$\rho \frac{D}{Dt}\left(\tfrac{1}{2} v^2\right) = -\nabla \cdot (P \mathbf{v}) - (\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v})$$

**Step 4 — Subtract to get thermal energy equation:**

$$\rho \frac{D}{Dt}\left(U + \frac{P}{\rho}\right) = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{q} - P (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + S$$

**Step 5 — Use the chain rule on the left side:**

$$\rho \frac{DU}{Dt} + \rho \frac{D}{Dt}\left(\frac{P}{\rho}\right) = \rho \frac{DU}{Dt} - P (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + \frac{DP}{Dt}$$

Equating and simplifying:

$$\rho \frac{DU}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T - P (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) + S$$

**Step 6 — Gibbs relation for incompressible fluid:** $dU = \hat{C}_v dT \approx \hat{C}_p dT$ (since incompressible).

For incompressible flow, $P (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 0$.

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\rho \hat{C}_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi_v + S_0}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Velocity profile:**

Let $y$ be measured from the lower plate ($0 \le y \le h = 0.02$ m), upper plate at $y = h$.

Linear Couette flow: $v_x(y) = V \cdot y/h = 5y/0.02 = 250y$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute $\Phi_v$:**

The only non-zero gradient is $dv_x/dy = V/h = 250$ s⁻¹.

$$\Phi_v = \mu \left(\frac{V}{h}\right)^2 = (2 \times 10^{-3})(250)^2 = 125 \text{ W/m³}$$

**Step 3 — Energy equation (steady, fully developed, $T = T(y)$):**

With no $x$-dependence and steady-state:

$$0 = k \frac{d^2 T}{dy^2} + \Phi_v + S_0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE:**

$$k \frac{d^2 T}{dy^2} = -(\Phi_v + S_0) = -(125 + 10^7) \approx -10^7 \text{ W/m³}$$

(We can neglect $\Phi_v$ compared to $S_0$ here — good engineering judgment.)

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dy^2} = -\frac{S_0}{k} = -\frac{10^7}{30} = -3.33 \times 10^5 \text{ K/m²}$$

Integrating:

$$T(y) = -\frac{S_0}{2k} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- BC1: $T(0) = T_w = 300$ K $\Rightarrow C_2 = 300$ K.
- BC2: $dT/dy(h) = 0$ (insulated upper plate) $\Rightarrow -\frac{S_0}{k} h + C_1 = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{S_0 h}{k}$.

$$C_1 = \frac{10^7 \times 0.02}{30} = 6666.67 \text{ K/m}$$

**Step 6 — Final temperature distribution:**

$$\boxed{T(y) = 300 + 6666.67 \, y - 1.667 \times 10^5 \, y^2 \quad [\text{K}]}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum temperature (at the insulated plate $y = h$):**

$$T_{\max} = T(0.02) = 300 + 6666.67(0.02) - 1.667 \times 10^5 (0.02)^2$$

$$T_{\max} = 300 + 133.33 - 66.67 = 366.67 \text{ K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Forgetting the $\Phi_v$ term entirely — even though it's small here, the question explicitly asks for its role. Always state its magnitude to claim the "engineering judgment" marks.
> 2. Mixing up the insulated BC: $dT/dy = 0$, not $T(h) = T_w$. Using the wrong BC yields $T_{\max}$ off by a factor of 2.
> 3. Sign error in the energy equation source term: should be $+S_0$ on the right side, but the heat equation is $k d^2T/dy^2 = -S_0$ after rearranging.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **viscous dissipation function in cylindrical coordinates** for a Newtonian fluid undergoing purely axisymmetric flow with $\mathbf{v} = v_\theta(r) \mathbf{e}_\theta$ (i.e., swirling flow between concentric cylinders). Show that the only surviving term is $\Phi_v = \mu [r \frac{d}{dr}(v_\theta/r)]^2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical nuclear fuel rod of radius $R = 0.01$ m and thermal conductivity $k_f = 30$ W/(m·K) generates volumetric heat at a uniform rate $S_0 = 5 \times 10^8$ W/m³. The rod is clad in a stainless-steel sheath (thickness $0.5$ mm, $k_s = 16$ W/(m·K)) and is cooled by a flowing fluid at $T_\infty = 350$ K with convective coefficient $h = 5000$ W/(m²·K). Derive and solve for the **centerline temperature** $T(0)$ and the **outer surface temperature** $T(R + t)$ of the cladding. State any assumptions.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

For purely azimuthal flow $\mathbf{v} = v_\theta(r) \mathbf{e}_\theta$:

**Step 1 — Identify non-zero velocity gradients:**

The only non-zero component of $\nabla \mathbf{v}$ in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} - \frac{v_\theta}{r} = -\frac{v_\theta}{r}$$

and

$$r \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) = r \frac{d}{dr}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cylindrical $\Phi_v$ formula:**

The cylindrical form of $\Phi_v$ (see Section 2.3.B) has six terms. For pure $v_\theta(r)$ flow, all terms vanish except the one involving $r d(v_\theta/r)/dr$:

$$\Phi_v = \mu \left[ r \frac{d}{dr}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) \right]^2$$

**Step 3 — Verify via the strain-rate tensor:**

The shear-strain rate for rigid-body-like rotation plus shear is $r d/dr(v_\theta/r)$. For a fluid rotating as a rigid body ($v_\theta = \omega r$), this derivative is zero, so $\Phi_v = 0$ — consistent with the physical expectation that no dissipation occurs in rigid-body rotation. For a fluid with $v_\theta = A r + B/r$ (Taylor-Couette), the term is non-zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation note:** The most efficient approach is to substitute the velocity field directly into the full cylindrical expression and identify the surviving term, then verify by computing $\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}$ component-by-component.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state, 1-D radial conduction.
- Constant $k_f$, $k_s$, $h$.
- Uniform volumetric source in the fuel only; cladding has $S = 0$.
- Perfect thermal contact between fuel and cladding.
- Constant surface convection coefficient $h$.

**Step 1 — Heat generation rate per unit length:**

The total heat generated per unit length of the rod:

$$\dot{q}' = S_0 \cdot \pi R^2 = (5 \times 10^8)(\pi)(0.01)^2 = 1.571 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature distribution in the fuel ($0 \le r \le R$):**

The energy equation in cylindrical coordinates, with axial and angular symmetries:

$$0 = \frac{k_f}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dT_f}{dr} \right) + S_0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dT_f}{dr} \right) = -\frac{S_0 r}{k_f}$$

Integrating once:

$$r \frac{dT_f}{dr} = -\frac{S_0 r^2}{2 k_f} + C_1$$

Applying the symmetry BC at $r = 0$: $dT_f/dr$ is finite, so $C_1 = 0$ (since $C_1/r$ would blow up):

$$\frac{dT_f}{dr} = -\frac{S_0 r}{2 k_f}$$

Integrating again:

$$T_f(r) = T(0) - \frac{S_0 r^2}{4 k_f}$$

**Step 3 — Heat flux at the fuel-cladding interface ($r = R$):**

$$q''(R) = -k_f \frac{dT_f}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R} = \frac{S_0 R}{2}$$

Numerically: $q''(R) = (5 \times 10^8)(0.01)/2 = 2.5 \times 10^6$ W/m².

This is the same as $\dot{q}'/(2\pi R) = (1.571 \times 10^5)/(2\pi \times 0.01) = 2.5 \times 10^6$ W/m². ✓

**Step 4 — Conduction through the cladding ($R \le r \le R + t$):**

For a thin cylindrical shell with no generation, the temperature drop is:

$$\Delta T_{\text{clad}} = \frac{\dot{q}'}{2\pi k_s} \ln\left(\frac{R + t}{R}\right)$$

With $t = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m: $R + t = 0.0105$ m.

$$\Delta T_{\text{clad}} = \frac{1.571 \times 10^5}{2\pi (16)} \ln(1.05) = \frac{1.571 \times 10^5}{100.53} \times 0.04879 = 76.25 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Convection from outer surface to fluid:**

$$T_s - T_\infty = \frac{q''}{h} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{5000} = 500 \text{ K}$$

$$T_s = 350 + 500 = 850 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T(0)$:**

Adding all temperature drops from center to fluid:

$$T(0) - T_\infty = \frac{S_0 R^2}{4 k_f} + \Delta T_{\text{clad}} + \frac{q''}{h}$$

$$\frac{S_0 R^2}{4 k_f} = \frac{(5 \times 10^8)(0.01)^2}{4 \times 30} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{120} = 416.67 \text{ K}$$

$$T(0) = 350 + 416.67 + 76.25 + 500 = 1342.92 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7 — Outer surface temperature:**

$$T(R + t) = T_s = 850 \text{ K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Using the *plane wall* formula $\Delta T = \dot{q}'' t / k$ for the cladding — this is only valid for $t \ll R$. Here $t/R = 0.05$ (5%), so the **logarithmic cylindrical formula is required**.
> 2. Forgetting the $\ln$ factor in cladding conduction.
> 3. Computing $\dot{q}'$ as $S_0 \pi R^2$ (correct) vs. $S_0 \cdot 4\pi R^2$ (wrong) — the latter is the surface area, not the volume.
> 4. Not verifying that $T(0)$ is below the fuel melting point — for UO₂, $T_{\text{melt}} \approx 3120$ K, so 1343 K is operationally safe, but the engineer must always check.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Viscous dissipation** $\Phi_v$ represents irreversible conversion of mechanical energy to heat; always **non-negative**.
- [x] $\Phi_v = \boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{v}$ — a *scalar* double contraction.
- [x] For Newtonian incompressible flow, the boxed form (Section 2.3.A) is **the** KTU-required expression.
- [x] For Newtonian compressible flow, an **extra term** $-\frac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v})^2$ must be added.
- [x] **Brinkman number** $\text{Br} = \mu V^2 / [k (T_w - T_0)]$ — judge importance of $\Phi_v$ vs. conduction.
- [x] For unidirectional flow with $v_i = v_i(x_j)$ only, $\Phi_v$ reduces to a **single squared-gradient term**.
- [x] **Nuclear/volumetric source** $S$ has units of **W/m³** and is **independent of velocity**.
- [x] Common forms: $S_0$ (uniform), $(-\Delta H_r) k_r C_A$ (reaction), $\sigma_{\text{elec}} \vert \mathbf{E} \vert^2$ (Joule).
- [x] Final temperature form of the energy equation: $\rho \hat{C}_p DT/Dt = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi_v + S$.
- [x] For cylindrical source problems, fuel-cladding-coolant analysis uses: $\Delta T = \frac{\dot{q}' R}{2 k_f} \cdot \frac{R}{2} + \frac{\dot{q}'}{2\pi k_s} \ln(r_o/r_i) + \frac{\dot{q}''}{h}$.
- [x] **Boundary condition discipline:** Symmetry $\Rightarrow dT/dr = 0$ at centerline; insulated $\Rightarrow dT/dy = 0$; fixed temperature $\Rightarrow T = T_w$; convective $\Rightarrow -k \, dT/dn = h(T_s - T_\infty)$.
- [x] **Always compute** the *order of magnitude* of $\Phi_v$ vs. $k \nabla^2 T$ before deciding to drop terms — a KTU-favorite conceptual question.
- [x] **Engineering judgment:** Drop $\Phi_v$ if $\text{Br} < 0.01$; keep both if $0.01 \le \text{Br} \le 10$; drop conduction if $\text{Br} > 10$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Mantra:** *Viscous dissipation = friction → heat. Nuclear source = chemistry/physics → heat. Both are added to conduction in the energy equation. The rest is coordinate-system geometry and boundary conditions.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of shell heat balances in variable thermal conductivity systems to derive temperature and heat flux profiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Use of shell heat balances in variable thermal conductivity systems to derive temperature and heat flux profiles # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition In the **shell heat balance** method (a cornerstone technique introduced by Bird, Stewart, and Lightfoot), a thin cylindrical shell of inner radius $r$, outer radius $r + \Delta r$, and length $L$ is treated as a control volume. The energy balance is written as: $$ \underbrace{\left. W_r \right|_r}_{\text{Rate of energy IN at } r} \;-\; \underbrace{\left. W_r \right|_{r+\Delta r}}_{\text{Rate of energy OUT at } r+\Delta r} \;+\; \underbrace{0}_{\text{No generation}} \;=\; 0 $$ Here, $W_r$ is the radial energy flux (W) and the heat flux is expressed through **Fourier's Law with variable thermal conductivity**: $$ W_r \;=\; 2\pi r L \, q_r \;=\; -\,2\pi r L \, k(T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variable Thermal Conductivity Systems** are those in which $k$ is *not* a constant but a function of temperature, e.g., $k = k_0(1+\beta T)$ or $k = k_0 e^{\alpha T}$. The product $k(T)\cdot\frac{dT}{dr}$ inside Fourier's Law introduces non-linearity that prevents the trivial logarithmic temperature profile (which assumes constant $k$). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) Imagine pouring hot water through a thick rubber pipe (a cylindrical shell). The rubber's ability to conduct heat depends on how hot it already is — the hotter the rubber gets, the better (or worse, depending on $\beta$) it conducts heat. Picture a **sponge that changes its thickness as it absorbs water**: a constant $k$ sponge gives a smooth, predictable temperature fall-off across the radius (logarithmic). A variable-$k$ sponge keeps changing its texture as heat flows, so the temperature drop accelerates or decelerates non-linearly with $r$. The **shell heat balance** is simply the accountant's ledger of energy: *"Whatever heat enters the shell on the inner side must leave on the outer side (since nothing is generated and we are at steady state)"*. This ledger equation, combined with Fourier's Law, is the entire foundation for deriving both the temperature and heat flux profiles. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Universal Physical Constant:** Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is *NOT* used here. We deal with **conduction only** (no radiation). > > **Standard Units Used Throughout This Module:** > - Thermal conductivity $k$ → **W / (m·K)** > - Heat flux $q_r$ → **W / m²** > - Temperature $T$ → **K** (Kelvin, thermodynamic) > - Temperature coefficient $\beta$ → **K⁻¹** > - Radial coordinate $r$ → **m** > - Length $L$ → **m** --- **Section 1 Summary in 60 Seconds:** - **Shell balance** writes the energy ledger for a thin cylindrical shell. - **Variable $k$** means $k = k(T)$, making Fourier's Law non-linear. - Standard units follow SI: W, m, K throughout. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of the Shell Heat Balance Method The method proceeds in a strict sequence. Understanding the **logic** behind each step is essential for KTU's problem-solving questions. ### Step-by-Step Logic: 1. **Define the control volume** — a thin cylindrical shell of radius $r$, thickness $\Delta r$, length $L$. 2. **Identify the transport mechanism** — radial heat conduction only (no convection in solid). 3. **Apply conservation of energy** — at steady state with no internal heat generation: $$ W_r \big|_r - W_r \big|_{r+\Delta r} = 0 $$ 4. **Apply the Mean Value Theorem** (BSL trick) to reduce $\frac{W_r}{\Delta r}$ to $\frac{dW_r}{dr}$: $$ \lim_{\Delta r \to 0} \frac{W_r\big|_r - W_r\big|_{r+\Delta r}}{\Delta r} \;=\; -\dfrac{dW_r}{dr} = 0 $$ 5. **This yields:** $W_r = \text{constant} = W$ (the total heat flow through the cylinder is independent of $r$). 6. **Substitute Fourier's Law** with variable $k$: $$ W = -\,2\pi r L \, k(T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ 7. **Separate variables and integrate** with appropriate boundary conditions (BCs). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Variable Thermal Conductivity | **Physical Cause** | **Mathematical Form** | **Typical Application** | |---|---|---| | Linear dependence on $T$ | $k = k_0 (1 + \beta T)$ | Insulating solids, moderate temperature range | | Exponential dependence | $k = k_0 e^{\alpha T}$ | Metallic conductors, semiconductors | | Reference-based shift | $k = k_0 [1 + \beta(T - T_0)]$ | Industrial practice (uses $T_0$ as reference) | | Power law | $k = k_0 T^n$ | Rarefied gases, plasmas | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite (Highest Yield):** The linear form $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ and its integrated reference version $k = k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]$ are the most commonly tested. Always verify which form your problem uses before integrating. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference Card** for Shell Heat Balance with Variable $k$ | **#** | **Equation / Identity** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_r = -2\pi r L \, k(T) \,\dfrac{dT}{dr}$ | Fourier's Law in cylindrical shell (variable $k$) | W | | 2 | $W_r = \text{constant} = W$ | Steady-state, no generation | W | | 3 | $k = k_0 (1 + \beta T)$ | Linear $k$–$T$ model | W/(m·K) | | 4 | $k = k_0 \bigl[1 + \beta(T - T_0)\bigr]$ | Linear model (reference $T_0$) | W/(m·K) | | 5 | $\displaystyle T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T^2 - T_1^2) = \dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{R}{r}$ | Temperature profile (linear $k$, BC at $r=R$, $T=T_1$) | K | | 6 | $\displaystyle q_r = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi r L}$ | Heat flux profile (always holds, independent of $k$) | W/m² | | 7 | $\displaystyle \int_0^T \dfrac{dT}{k(T)} = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R}$ | General integral form for any $k(T)$ | K·m/W | | 8 | $\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left[r\,k(T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right] = 0$ | ODE form after substituting (5) into (1) | — | | 9 | $r \cdot k(T) \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L} = \text{const}$ | Reduced first-order ODE | — | | 10 | $\beta$ | Temperature coefficient of thermal conductivity | K⁻¹ | > [!WARNING] > **Table Formatting Note:** The $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbol has been replaced with explicit descriptions to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. The logarithm $\ln$ is always **natural log** in Transport Phenomena problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Nuclear Reactor Cladding:** $k$ of Zircaloy varies with temperature; predicting fuel centerline temperature requires variable-$k$ analysis. - **Power Cable Insulation:** XLPE cables have $k$ that increases with $T$ (typically $\beta > 0$); accurate ampacity ratings demand this treatment. - **High-Temperature Furnace Linings:** Refractory bricks exhibit strong $k(T)$ dependence — design of safe shell temperatures. - **Semiconductor Wafers:** Silicon's $k$ drops sharply with $T$; thermal management of integrated circuits. - **Geothermal Pipes:** Steam-transporting pipes operating across wide $T$ ranges where constant-$k$ assumption fails. --- **Section 2 Quick-Recall:** - Shell balance + Fourier's Law with $k(T)$ → ODE → integrate. - Heat flux profile $q_r = -W/(2\pi r L)$ is *always* the same form, regardless of $k(T)$. - Temperature profile requires **explicit integration** of $1/k(T)$. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A: Temperature Profile for $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ ### Setup A cylindrical shell of inner radius $r = R_1$ and outer radius $r = R_2$ carries radial heat flow. Boundary conditions: - At $r = R_1$: $T = T_1$ (inner surface) - At $r = R_2$: $T = T_2$ (outer surface) - Steady state, constant $W$. ### Step 1 — Write the shell heat balance $$ W_r\big|_r - W_r\big|_{r+\Delta r} = 0 $$ In words: *"Energy entering the shell at $r$ equals energy leaving at $r+\Delta r$."* ### Step 2 — Take the limit $\Delta r \to 0$ using the Mean Value Theorem $$ \lim_{\Delta r \to 0}\dfrac{W_r\big|_r - W_r\big|_{r+\Delta r}}{\Delta r} = -\dfrac{dW_r}{dr} = 0 $$ **Conversion Logic:** Since the *difference* in flux vanishes, the *derivative* must vanish, implying $W_r$ is independent of $r$. $$ W_r \;=\; \text{constant} \;=\; W $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Fourier's Law with variable $k$ $$ W \;=\; -\,2\pi r L \, k_0(1 + \beta T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Fourier's Law $q_r = -k(T)\,\frac{dT}{dr}$ is multiplied by the shell surface area $2\pi r L$ to get total radial energy flow $W$ in watts. ### Step 4 — Separate variables Rearranging the equation to isolate $T$ and $r$: $$ (1 + \beta T)\,dT \;=\; -\,\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\,\dfrac{dr}{r} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Cross-multiplying puts $T$-dependent terms on the left and $r$-dependent terms on the right, enabling integration. ### Step 5 — Integrate both sides $$ \int_{T_1}^{T}(1 + \beta T')\,dT' \;=\; -\,\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\int_{R_1}^{r}\dfrac{dr'}{r'} $$ The left side yields: $$ \int(1 + \beta T')\,dT' = T' + \dfrac{\beta T'^{\,2}}{2} $$ The right side yields: $$ -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\bigl[\ln r\bigr]_{R_1}^{r} = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ ### Step 6 — Assemble the temperature profile $$ T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T^2 - T_1^2) \;=\; -\,\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\,\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ Equivalently (flipping the log): $$ \boxed{\;T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl(T^2 - T_1^2\bigr) \;=\; \dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\,\ln\!\dfrac{R_1}{r}\;} $$ **Conversion Logic:** A sign flip occurs because $\ln(r/R_1) = -\ln(R_1/r)$, giving a positive quantity for $R_1 > r$ — the standard physical form. ### Step 7 — Heat flux profile By definition: $$ q_r \;=\; \dfrac{W_r}{2\pi r L} \;=\; \dfrac{W}{2\pi r L} $$ With direction (heat flows from hot to cold, in $-r$ direction): $$ \boxed{\;q_r \;=\; -\,\dfrac{W}{2\pi r L}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The heat flux profile $q_r \propto 1/r$ is universal** — it does NOT depend on whether $k$ is constant or variable. Only the **temperature profile** changes when $k = k(T)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation B: General Integral Form for Arbitrary $k(T)$ When $k(T)$ does not admit a closed-form integral easily, we write the **master implicit form**: $$ \int_{T_1}^{T}\dfrac{dT'}{k(T')} \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ **Derivation Steps:** ### Step 1 — Start from Fourier's Law $$ W = -\,2\pi r L \, k(T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange $$ k(T)\,dT \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L}\,\dfrac{dr}{r} $$ ### Step 3 — Divide both sides by $k(T)$ $$ dT \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L\,k(T)}\,\dfrac{dr}{r} $$ ### Step 4 — Integrate formally $$ \int_{T_1}^{T}\dfrac{dT'}{k(T')} \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L}\int_{R_1}^{r}\dfrac{dr'}{k_0\,r'} \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ **Conversion Logic:** This is the *most general result*. For constant $k$ ($\beta = 0$), the integral becomes $\frac{T - T_1}{k_0}$, recovering the classical logarithmic profile $T - T_1 = -\frac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln(r/R_1)$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation C: Special Case — Constant Heat Flux at Inner Boundary Sometimes the BC is given as a specified heat flux $q_{R_1}$ at $r = R_1$ (e.g., from a known current through an electrical heater). ### Step 1 — Heat flux is independent of $r$ $$ q_r \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi r L} $$ At $r = R_1$: $$ q_{R_1} \;=\; -\dfrac{W}{2\pi R_1 L} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; W \;=\; -\,2\pi R_1 L\,q_{R_1} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute into Fourier's Law $$ -2\pi R_1 L\,q_{R_1} \;=\; -\,2\pi r L\,k_0(1+\beta T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ ### Step 3 — Cancel $-2\pi L$ $$ R_1 q_{R_1} \;=\; r\,k_0(1+\beta T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ ### Step 4 — Separate and integrate $$ k_0(1 + \beta T)\,dT \;=\; \dfrac{R_1 q_{R_1}\,dr}{r} $$ Integrating from $r = R_1$ (where $T = T_1$) to general $r$: $$ k_0\!\left[T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T^2 - T_1^2)\right] \;=\; R_1 q_{R_1}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\;T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T^2 - T_1^2) \;=\; \dfrac{R_1 q_{R_1}}{k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Check:** If $q_{R_1} > 0$ (heat flows outward, $-r$ direction from inner surface), then for $r > R_1$, $\ln(r/R_1) > 0$, so $T > T_1$ — consistent with heating from inside outward. ✓ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Across $k(T)$ Models | **Property** | **Constant $k$** | **Linear $k = k_0(1+\beta T)$** | **Exponential $k = k_0 e^{\alpha T}$** | |---|---|---|---| | ODE form | $\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\bigl(r\,\dfrac{dT}{dr}\bigr) = 0$ | $\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\bigl[r(1+\beta T)\dfrac{dT}{dr}\bigr] = 0$ | $\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\bigl[r\,e^{\alpha T}\dfrac{dT}{dr}\bigr] = 0$ | | Temperature profile | $T - T_1 = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1}$ | $T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T^2 - T_1^2) = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1}$ | $-\dfrac{1}{\alpha}\bigl(e^{-\alpha T} - e^{-\alpha T_1}\bigr) = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1}$ | | Heat flux profile | $q_r = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi r L}$ | $q_r = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi r L}$ (unchanged) | $q_r = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi r L}$ (unchanged) | | Solution type | Closed-form (log) | Implicit quadratic in $T$ | Implicit transcendental | | Typical use | Quick estimates | Insulation, moderate $T$ range | Metals, semiconductors | **Conversion Logic:** The right column's implicit forms must be solved numerically (Newton–Raphson) for $T(r)$ unless $\beta$ is small and we accept the linear approximation. --- **Section 3 Closing Anchor:** The complete mathematical derivation chain is now established. Recall: **shell balance → ODE → integrate with BCs → temperature profile**. The heat flux profile follows immediately from $W = 2\pi r L \, q_r$. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Cylindrical Shell Control Volume ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Steady-State Cylindrical Shell

The **green path** is the most common in KTU problems. The **red path** is rare and requires additional data (e.g., overall heat balance). --- **Section 4 Takeaway:** The diagrams emphasise the **sequential** nature of the method. Whether $k$ is constant or variable, the *structure* of the analysis is identical — only the **integration step** changes. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q:** Define the term "variable thermal conductivity" and state why the shell heat balance approach becomes non-linear when $k$ depends on temperature. **Model Answer:** **Variable thermal conductivity** refers to a material property in which $k$ is a function of temperature $k = k(T)$ rather than a constant. The shell heat balance becomes non-linear because Fourier's Law $W = -2\pi r L k(T)\frac{dT}{dr}$ contains the product of $k(T)$ and $\frac{dT}{dr}$, both of which depend on $T$. When this ODE is integrated, the resulting equation for $T(r)$ involves non-linear terms (e.g., $T^2$ for linear $k$). **(3 marks)** **Mark Distribution:** - Definition of variable $k$: **1 mark** - Identification of non-linear term $k(T) \cdot dT/dr$: **1 mark** - Correct statement of resulting non-linearity: **1 mark** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Q:** Write the general integral form of the temperature profile obtained from a shell heat balance for an arbitrary $k(T)$. **Model Answer:** Starting from the shell heat balance and Fourier's Law, the general integral form is: $$ \int_{T_1}^{T}\dfrac{dT'}{k(T')} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\,\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ For constant $k$, this reduces to $T - T_1 = -\frac{W}{2\pi L k}\ln(r/R_1)$. **(3 marks)** **Mark Distribution:** - Correct integral bounds $T_1$ to $T$: **1 mark** - Correct RHS containing $W$, $L$, $k_0$, $\ln(r/R_1)$: **1 mark** - Mention of constant-$k$ limit case: **1 mark** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **[14 Marks]** **Q:** A thick-walled cylindrical pipe of inner radius $R_1 = 25$ mm and outer radius $R_2 = 75$ mm carries a hot fluid. The inner wall temperature is $T_1 = 500$ K and the outer wall is at $T_2 = 400$ K. The thermal conductivity varies linearly with temperature as $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ where $k_0 = 0.15$ W/(m·K) and $\beta = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹. The pipe length is $L = 10$ m. **(a)** Using the shell heat balance method, derive the temperature profile $T(r)$ in implicit form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the steady-state heat loss rate $W$ (in watts) and the heat flux $q_r$ at $r = R_1$ and $r = R_2$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Shell heat balance** (1 mark): $$ W_r\big|_r - W_r\big|_{r+\Delta r} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{dW_r}{dr} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; W_r = W $$ **Step 2 — Fourier's Law with variable $k$** (1 mark): $$ W = -2\pi r L \, k_0(1 + \beta T)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ **Step 3 — Separate variables** (1 mark): $$ (1 + \beta T)\,dT = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\,\dfrac{dr}{r} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate from inner wall** ($r = R_1$, $T = T_1$) to general $r$ (2 marks): $$ \int_{T_1}^{T}(1 + \beta T')\,dT' = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ Left-side evaluation: $$ T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl(T^2 - T_1^2\bigr) = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ **Step 5 — Final implicit form** (2 marks): $$ \boxed{\;T - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl(T^2 - T_1^2\bigr) = \dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{R_1}{r}\;} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The implicit form means $T$ appears both linearly and quadratically. For the numerical solution in part (b), we substitute the outer-wall BC. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Apply BC at $r = R_2$, $T = T_2$** (1 mark): $$ T_2 - T_1 + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl(T_2^2 - T_1^2\bigr) = \dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} $$ **Step 2 — Numerical evaluation of LHS** (2 marks): $$ T_2 - T_1 = 400 - 500 = -100 \text{ K} $$ $$ T_2^2 - T_1^2 = (400)^2 - (500)^2 = 160000 - 250000 = -90000 \text{ K}^2 $$ $$ \text{LHS} = -100 + \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2}(-90000) = -100 + (-22.5) = -122.5 \text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $W$** (2 marks): $$ -122.5 = \dfrac{W}{2\pi (10)(0.15)}\ln\!\dfrac{0.025}{0.075} $$ $$ \ln\!\dfrac{0.025}{0.075} = \ln(0.3333) = -1.0986 $$ $$ -122.5 = \dfrac{W}{9.4248}(-1.0986) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; W = \dfrac{-122.5 \times 9.4248}{-1.0986} $$ $$ \boxed{\;W \approx 1050.7 \text{ W}\;} $$ **Step 4 — Heat flux at $r = R_1$** (1 mark): $$ q_r\big|_{R_1} = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi R_1 L} = -\dfrac{1050.7}{2\pi (0.025)(10)} = -669.0 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Heat flux at $r = R_2$** (1 mark): $$ q_r\big|_{R_2} = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi R_2 L} = -\dfrac{1050.7}{2\pi (0.075)(10)} = -223.0 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Verification:** $|q_r|$ decreases with $r$ as expected for a cylindrical geometry (heat spreads over larger area). ✓ --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **[14 Marks]** **Q:** A cylindrical nuclear fuel cladding has inner radius $R_1 = 5$ mm and outer radius $R_2 = 8$ mm. The inner surface receives a uniform heat flux of magnitude $q_{R_1} = 1.5 \times 10^6$ W/m² directed outward (radially). The cladding's thermal conductivity is $k = k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]$ with $k_0 = 21$ W/(m·K), $\beta = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹, and $T_0 = 300$ K. The inner wall temperature is $T_1 = 600$ K. **(a)** Set up the shell heat balance and derive the temperature profile. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the temperature at the outer surface $r = R_2$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Heat flow rate from given flux** (1 mark): $$ W = -2\pi R_1 L\,q_{R_1} $$ Note: $q_{R_1} > 0$ is heat flux *magnitude* in the outward (+r) direction. However, since the wall is hotter inside, heat actually flows *outward* from the cladding into the reactor coolant, which means the **net radial heat flow** is in $+r$ direction. So $W > 0$ if we take the sign convention consistent with our derivation. For this problem, we write: $$ W = +2\pi R_1 L\,q_{R_1} \quad (\text{outward radial flow}) $$ **Step 2 — Apply shell balance and Fourier's Law** (1 mark): $$ W = -2\pi r L\,k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]\,\dfrac{dT}{dr} $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange and separate** (1 mark): $$ k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]\,dT = -W\dfrac{dr}{2\pi r L} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate from $r = R_1$ to general $r$** (2 marks): $$ k_0\int_{T_1}^{T}[1 + \beta(T' - T_0)]\,dT' = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ Left side: $$ k_0\!\left[(T - T_1) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl((T - T_0)^2 - (T_1 - T_0)^2\bigr)\right] $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $W = 2\pi R_1 L\,q_{R_1}$ and finalise** (2 marks): $$ k_0\!\left[(T - T_1) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl((T - T_0)^2 - (T_1 - T_0)^2\bigr)\right] = -R_1 q_{R_1}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1} $$ Or, equivalently: $$ \boxed{\;(T - T_1) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl((T - T_0)^2 - (T_1 - T_0)^2\bigr) = -\dfrac{R_1 q_{R_1}}{k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1}\;} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Because the cladding is being heated from inside and the heat flows outward to the coolant, the *mathematical* gradient $dT/dr < 0$ (temperature decreases outward). The minus sign on the RHS with $\ln(r/R_1) > 0$ gives the correct sign. ✓ --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Plug in $r = R_2$** (1 mark): $$ (T_2 - T_1) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}\bigl((T_2 - T_0)^2 - (T_1 - T_0)^2\bigr) = -\dfrac{R_1 q_{R_1}}{k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate known constants** (1 mark): $$ T_1 - T_0 = 600 - 300 = 300 \text{ K} $$ $$ \ln\!\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \ln\!\dfrac{0.008}{0.005} = \ln(1.6) = 0.4700 $$ $$ -\dfrac{R_1 q_{R_1}}{k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = -\dfrac{(0.005)(1.5 \times 10^6)}{21}(0.4700) = -357.14 \times 0.4700 = -167.86 \text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Set up equation in $T_2$** (2 marks): Let $\Delta = T_2 - T_0 = T_2 - 300$. Then $T_2 - T_1 = \Delta - 300$, and $(T_2 - T_0)^2 = \Delta^2$. $$ (\Delta - 300) + \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{2}\bigl(\Delta^2 - 90000\bigr) = -167.86 $$ $$ (\Delta - 300) + 10^{-4}(\Delta^2 - 90000) = -167.86 $$ **Step 4 — Solve (neglect small $\beta$ term first as estimate)** (2 marks): First approximation (assume $\beta = 0$): $$ \Delta \approx -167.86 + 300 = 132.14 \text{ K} $$ So $T_2 \approx 432.14$ K. Iterative refinement with $\beta$ term: $$ \Delta = 132.14,\quad \Delta^2 = 17461 $$ $$ 10^{-4}(17461 - 90000) = -7.254 $$ New equation: $$ \Delta - 300 - 7.254 = -167.86 \;\Longrightarrow\; \Delta = 132.14 + 7.254 = 139.39 $$ Refining once more (converged to ~3 sig. fig.): $$ \boxed{\;T_2 \approx 439 \text{ K}\;} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Notice how $\beta > 0$ causes the outer surface to be **slightly hotter** than the constant-$k$ prediction because higher $T$ means higher $k$, which means heat "leaks out" more easily — but here the temperature drop is less because of the increased conductivity near the inner (hot) side. The numerical result is consistent with this physical intuition. **Step 5 — Verification of physical sanity** (1 mark): - $T_2 < T_1$ ✓ (heat flows outward) - $T_2 > T_0$ ✓ (cladding is hotter than reference) - Effect of $\beta$ is small (correction ~7 K out of ~432 K) ✓ (consistent with $\beta \Delta T \approx 0.06$, "weakly variable") --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** — Read Before Writing Your Exam! > > 1. **Forgetting the area factor $2\pi r L$** when writing Fourier's Law in cylindrical coordinates. Many students write $q_r = -k\,dT/dr$ directly, losing the geometry transformation. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.** > > 2. **Sign errors on the logarithm.** When the inner surface is hot and heat flows outward, $\ln(r/R_1) > 0$ for $r > R_1$, and the LHS $T - T_1 < 0$ for $r > R_1$. A missing minus sign flips the entire temperature profile. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.** > > 3. **Not carrying units through the calculation.** Sloppy unit conversion between mm and m is a top reason for arithmetic errors. **Penalty: 1 mark even if final answer is "close".** > > 4. **Confusing $T_0$ (reference temperature) with $T_1$ (boundary temperature)** in the shifted form $k = k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]$. They are different quantities. **Penalty: 2 marks.** > > 5. **Claiming "heat flux profile changes with $k(T)$".** It does NOT. $q_r = -W/(2\pi r L)$ is purely a geometric consequence of conservation. Examiners *will* deduct for stating otherwise. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.** > > 6. **Skipping the implicit-vs-explicit classification.** Many students fail to state that the linear-$k$ profile is **implicit** in $T$ (a quadratic equation) rather than closed-form. Always state this explicitly. **Penalty: 1 mark.** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shell Heat Balance with Variable $k$** - [x] **Core method:** Shell heat balance + Fourier's Law with $k(T)$ → first-order ODE → integrate. - [x] **Steady-state result:** $W_r = \text{constant} = W$ for no internal generation. - [x] **General integral form:** $\displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T}\dfrac{dT'}{k(T')} = -\dfrac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln\!\dfrac{r}{R_1}$ - [x] **Linear $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$:** Profile becomes **implicit quadratic** in $T$: $\;T - T_1 + \frac{\beta}{2}(T^2 - T_1^2) = \frac{W}{2\pi L k_0}\ln(R_1/r)$ - [x] **Shifted form $k = k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]$:** Profile uses $(T - T_0)$ instead of $T$. - [x] **Exponential $k = k_0 e^{\alpha T}$:** Profile is **implicit transcendental** in $T$. - [x] **Heat flux profile** $q_r = -W/(2\pi r L)$ is **universal** — independent of $k(T)$ form. - [x] **Boundary conditions:** Always two needed (e.g., $T_1$ at $R_1$ and $T_2$ at $R_2$, or $T_1$ and $q_{R_1}$). - [x] **Sign convention:** Heat flows from hot to cold; $q_r < 0$ if temperature decreases outward. - [x] **Units:** $k$ in W/(m·K), $T$ in K, $r$ in m, $W$ in W, $q_r$ in W/m². - [x] **Real-world use:** Reactor cladding, high-temp insulation, power cables, semiconductor wafers. - [x] **Common exam trap:** Confusing $T_0$ (reference) with $T_1$ (boundary). - [x] **Mark safety:** Always state whether the temperature profile is implicit or explicit. --- **End of Module 3 — Shell Heat Balances with Variable Thermal Conductivity** *For Module 4 (heat transfer coefficients / convection correlations) and Module 5 (mass transfer analogies), follow the same rigorous BSL methodology — the shell balance is your universal starting point.*

Shell mass balance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shell mass balance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shell Mass Balance** is a macroscopic (control-volume) formulation of the law of conservation of mass applied to an *infinitesimally thin shell* (a slice of the system) bounded by two parallel surfaces. It expresses the principle that the **rate of mass accumulation inside the shell = (rate of mass entering the shell) − (rate of mass leaving the shell) + (rate of mass generated by chemical reaction within the shell)**.

Mathematically, for a species **A** in a thin shell of thickness `Δr` (or `Δz`, `Δx`):

$$
\frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t}\,dV \;=\; \bigl(\text{flux in at inner face}\bigr) - \bigl(\text{flux out at outer face}\bigr) + R_A\,dV
$$

where `dV` is the differential shell volume and `R_A` is the **rate of generation of species A per unit volume** `[kmol/(m³·s)]`.

The shell mass balance is the **bridge between a microscopic molecular picture (Fick's law) and the macroscopic differential equation of change**. It is the most direct path by which the general differential mass-balance equation can be derived in Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical coordinates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam Wall Leak" Picture

Imagine a **cylindrical water tank** with a slow leak. Pick any horizontal slice of water (a thin cylindrical *shell*) inside the tank:
- **Water entering the slice from above** = mass flux `in`.
- **Water leaving the slice from below** = mass flux `out`.
- **If water vanishes inside the slice (e.g., leaks out through the side wall)** = generation term (negative).

A shell balance is **literally counting how much mass crosses two faces of a thin slice per unit time** and comparing it with how much accumulates or is created inside. Shrink the slice to zero thickness and you obtain a *differential equation* — the famous **general differential mass-balance equation** of Bird, Stewart & Lightfoot (BSL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Shell"?** Because in cylindrical and spherical coordinates the natural control volume is a *thin curved layer* (a shell of thickness `Δr`), unlike the rectangular slab used in Cartesian coordinates.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants / Metrics (Bolded)

- **Molar flux** `N_A` `[kmol/(m²·s)]` — moles of A crossing per unit area per unit time.
- **Mass concentration** `ρ_A` `[kg/m³]` or molar concentration `c_A` `[kmol/m³]`.
- **Diffusivity** `D_AB` `[m²/s]` — proportionality constant in Fick's first law.
- **Stefan diffusion tube** `[m]` — canonical 1-D apparatus for shell-balance derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Procedure for Applying a Shell Mass Balance

The shell-balance methodology has **five canonical steps** that examiners love to test:

1. **Sketch the system** and define the coordinate system (Cartesian / cylindrical / spherical).
2. **Identify the appropriate thin shell** of dimensions `Δx`, `Δy`, `Δz` (or `Δr`, `Δz`) where transport is occurring.
3. **Write the flux expression** at the inner and outer faces using the constitutive law (Newton's, Fourier's, or **Fick's**).
4. **Apply the conservation law** (Rate in − Rate out + Generation = Accumulation).
5. **Divide by the shell volume** and take the limit as the shell thickness → `0`. The result is the **differential equation** in that coordinate system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Every derivation of the differential equation of change (momentum, heat, mass) ultimately originates from this exact 5-step shell balance. Master this and Module 4, 5, 6 of BSL become *trivial*.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Shell Mass Balance Equations

| # | Geometry | Steady-State Shell-Balance Equation | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cartesian (1-D, z-direction)** | $-\dfrac{d N_{Az}}{dz} + R_A = 0$ | Simplest form; slab geometry |
| 2 | **Cylindrical (radial, r-direction)** | $-\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,N_{Ar}\right) + R_A = 0$ | Tube / pipe walls |
| 3 | **Spherical (radial, r-direction)** | $-\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\,N_{Ar}\right) + R_A = 0$ | Catalyst pellets, droplets |
| 4 | **Transient Cartesian** | $\dfrac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial N_{Ax}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial N_{Ay}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial N_{Az}}{\partial z} = R_A$ | Full continuity form |
| 5 | **Fick's First Law (constitutive)** | $N_{A,z} = -D_{AB}\dfrac{d c_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A+N_B)$ | Total flux = molecular + convective |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shell mass balance underpins the design of:
- **Catalytic converters** in automobiles — diffusion + reaction inside porous spherical pellets.
- **Gas absorbers** (e.g., CO₂ capture in amine towers) — diffusion across a stagnant gas film (Stefan flow).
- **Pharmaceutical drying** — moisture diffusion from a spherical droplet.
- **Membrane separators** — mass transport through a thin polymer film.
- **Polymerization reactors** — monomer diffusion towards growing chain ends.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cartesian Shell Mass Balance (Steady State)

Consider species **A** diffusing in the `z`-direction through a thin rectangular shell of cross-sectional area `S` and thickness `Δz`. No reaction (`R_A = 0`). Steady state.

**Step 1 — Identify the faces.**
- Inner face at `z`: molar flow rate in = `N_A(z) · S`  
- Outer face at `z+Δz`: molar flow rate out = `N_A(z+Δz) · S`  

**Step 2 — Conservation of mass (steady state, no reaction):**

$$
N_A(z)\,S \;-\; N_A(z+\Delta z)\,S \;+\; 0 \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by `S·Δz` and rearrange:**

$$
\frac{N_A(z) - N_A(z+\Delta z)}{\Delta z} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit `Δz → 0`** to obtain the differential form:

$$
-\frac{d N_A}{d z} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Fick's First Law** for a binary mixture with stagnant B (`N_B = 0`, `N_A = -D_{AB}\,dc_A/dz + x_A\,N_A`):

$$
N_A(1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{d c_A}{d z}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
N_A \;=\; -\frac{c\,D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\,\frac{d x_A}{d z}
$$

> *Derivation detail:* `c_A = c\,x_A` (ideal gas, dilute); dividing by `dc_A/dz = c\,dx_A/dz` gives the boxed result.

**Step 6 — Integrate** between `z = z_1` (`x_A = x_{A1}`) and `z = z_2` (`x_A = x_{A2}`):

$$
N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz \;=\; -c\,D_{AB} \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} \frac{dx_A}{1-x_A}
$$

$$
N_A\,(z_2 - z_1) \;=\; c\,D_{AB}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)
$$

**Final boxed result — Stefan's Law (one of the most-tested equations in Transport Phenomena):**

$$
\boxed{\;N_A \;=\; \frac{c\,D_{AB}}{z_2-z_1}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logarithmic-mean correction:** For low concentrations (`x_A ≪ 1`), `ln(1-x_A) ≈ -x_A` and the equation collapses to `N_A ≈ -c D_{AB}(x_{A2}-x_{A1})/(z_2-z_1)` (linear profile).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylindrical Shell Mass Balance (with Reaction)

Consider diffusion in the **radial direction** through a cylindrical shell of inner radius `r`, outer radius `r+Δr`, and length `L`. Homogeneous first-order reaction `A → products` with rate `-R_A = k\,c_A`.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the cylindrical shell.**
- Lateral surface area at `r`: `A_r = 2π r L`
- Lateral surface area at `r+Δr`: `A_{r+Δr} = 2π (r+Δr) L`
- Volume of shell: `ΔV = π[(r+Δr)² - r²]L ≈ 2π r L Δr` (for `Δr ≪ r`)

**Step 2 — Mass fluxes at the two faces.**
- Molar flow in at `r`: `Ψ_A(r) = N_{A,r}(r) · 2π r L`
