- Molar flow out at `r+Δr`: `Ψ_A(r+Δr) = N_{A,r}(r+Δr) · 2π (r+Δr) L`

**Step 3 — Apply the conservation law** (rate in − rate out + generation = 0 at steady state):

$$
2\pi r L\,N_{A,r}(r) \;-\; 2\pi(r+\Delta r)L\,N_{A,r}(r+\Delta r) \;+\; R_A\,(2\pi r L\,\Delta r) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by `2π L Δr` and rearrange:**

$$
\frac{r\,N_{A,r}(r) - (r+\Delta r)\,N_{A,r}(r+\Delta r)}{\Delta r} \;+\; r\,R_A \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit `Δr → 0`** (numerator becomes a derivative by definition):

$$
-\frac{d}{dr}\!\bigl(r\,N_{A,r}\bigr) \;+\; r\,R_A \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 6 — Cylindrical form of the mass-balance equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r}\,\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,N_{A,r}\right) \;-\; R_A \;=\; 0\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute Fick's Law** `N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\,dc_A/dr` (no convection) and `R_A = -k\,c_A` (consumption):

$$
-\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,D_{AB}\,\frac{dc_A}{dr}\right) \;+\; k\,c_A \;=\; 0
$$

Assuming constant `D_{AB}`:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dc_A}{dr}\right) - \frac{k}{D_{AB}}\,c_A \;=\; 0
$$

This is the **modified Bessel equation of order zero** for `c_A(r)`.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spherical Shell Mass Balance (Catalyst Pellet)

Consider diffusion + first-order reaction inside a spherical catalyst pellet of radius `R`.

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
- Area at `r`: `4π r²`
- Area at `r+Δr`: `4π (r+Δr)²`
- Volume of shell: `4π r² Δr`

**Step 2 — Conservation law:**

$$
4\pi r^{2}\,N_{A,r}(r) \;-\; 4\pi(r+\Delta r)^{2}\,N_{A,r}(r+\Delta r) \;-\; k\,c_A\,(4\pi r^{2}\Delta r) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by `4π Δr` and take limit `Δr → 0`:**

$$
-\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\,N_{A,r}\right) \;-\; k\,c_A\,r^{2} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Spherical form:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r^{2}}\,\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\,N_{A,r}\right) \;+\; k\,c_A \;=\; 0\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Insert Fick's Law** `N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\,dc_A/dr`:

$$
\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\,D_{AB}\,\frac{dc_A}{dr}\right) \;=\; k\,c_A
$$

Expanding the derivative:

$$
\frac{d^{2}c_A}{dr^{2}} \;+\; \frac{2}{r}\,\frac{dc_A}{dr} \;-\; \frac{k}{D_{AB}}\,c_A \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 6 — Boundary conditions.**
- `At r = 0`: `c_A` is finite (no singularity).
- `At r = R`: `c_A = c_{A,s}` (known surface concentration).

**Step 7 — Solution** (let `φ = R\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}` be the **Thiele modulus**):

$$
\frac{c_A}{c_{A,s}} \;=\; \frac{R}{r}\cdot\frac{\sinh(r\phi/R)}{\sinh(\phi)}
$$

**Step 8 — Effectiveness factor `η`** (ratio of actual reaction rate to rate at surface concentration):

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{3}{\phi^{2}}\left[\phi\coth(\phi) - 1\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering relevance:** `η ≈ 1` when `φ < 0.5` (reaction-limited regime, no diffusion limitation).  
> `η ≈ 3/φ` when `φ > 5` (diffusion-limited regime — common in industrial porous catalysts).

---

### 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Geometry | Governing ODE | Generic Solution Form | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (1-D) | `d²c_A/dz² = (k/D) c_A` | Hyperbolic sine/cosine | Membrane, stagnant film |
| Cylindrical | `(1/r)d(r·dc_A/dr)/dr = (k/D) c_A` | Modified Bessel `I₀, K₀` | Tubular reactor wall |
| Spherical | `d²c_A/dr² + (2/r)dc_A/dr = (k/D) c_A` | `(1/r)sinh(r√(k/D))` | Catalyst pellet |
| **Common origin** | **Shell mass balance** | — | **All above** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Shell-Balance Methodology (5-Step Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STEP1: SKETCH SYSTEM AND CHOOSE COORDINATE SYSTEM] --> B[STEP2: IDENTIFY THE THIN SHELL GEOMETRY]
    B --> C[STEP3: WRITE FLUX EXPRESSIONS AT INNER AND OUTER FACES USING FICK LAW]
    C --> D[STEP4: APPLY MASS CONSERVATION RATE IN MINUS RATE OUT PLUS GENERATION EQUALS ACCUMULATION]
    D --> E[STEP5: DIVIDE BY SHELL VOLUME AND TAKE LIMIT THICKNESS TO ZERO]
    E --> F[DIFFERENTIAL MASS BALANCE EQUATION]
    F --> G[INSERT CONSTITUTIVE LAW FICK OR STEFAN]
    G --> H[APPLY BOUNDARY CONDITIONS]
    H --> I[SOLVE FOR CONCENTRATION PROFILE]
    I --> J[COMPUTE ENGINEERING QUANTITY FLUX OR EFFECTIVENESS FACTOR]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Three Coordinate Geometries Used in Shell Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CART["CARTESIAN COORDINATES\nRECTANGULAR SLAB\nTHICKNESS DELTAZ"]
    CYL["CYLINDRICAL COORDINATES\nHOLLOW CYLINDRICAL SHELL\nINNER R OUTER R PLUS DELTAR"]
    SPH["SPHERICAL COORDINATES\nHOLLOW SPHERICAL SHELL\nINNER R OUTER R PLUS DELTAR"]
    CART --> EQ1["ODE FORM: D2CA BY DZ2 EQUALS K OVER D TIMES CA"]
    CYL --> EQ2["ODE FORM: ONE BY R D BY DR R DCA BY DR EQUALS K OVER D TIMES CA"]
    SPH --> EQ3["ODE FORM: D2CA BY DR2 PLUS TWO BY R DCA BY DR EQUALS K OVER D TIMES CA"]
    EQ1 --> APP1["APPLICATIONS: STAGNANT FILM, MEMBRANE, STEFAN DIFFUSION"]
    EQ2 --> APP2["APPLICATIONS: TUBULAR CATALYST, PIPE WALL REACTION"]
    EQ3 --> APP3["APPLICATIONS: POROUS SPHERICAL CATALYST PELLET"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Classical Application: Diffusion + Reaction in a Porous Spherical Pellet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PELL["SPHERICAL CATALYST PELLET OF RADIUS R"] --> BULK["EXTERNAL BULK CONCENTRATION CA BULK"]
    BULK --> SURF["SURFACE CONCENTRATION CA AT R EQUALS R"]
    SURF --> SHELL["SHELL AT RADIUS R TO R PLUS DELTAR"]
    SHELL --> PROC1["DIFFUSION INWARD VIA FICK LAW"]
    SHELL --> PROC2["HOMOGENEOUS REACTION FIRST ORDER RATE K CA"]
    PROC2 --> PROD["DEPLETED CONCENTRATION AT CENTER R EQUALS ZERO"]
    PROC1 --> THIELE["DEFINE THIELE MODULUS PHI EQUALS R SQRT K BY D"]
    THIELE --> EFF["EFFECTIVENESS FACTOR ETA EQUALS 3 BY PHI SQUARED OPEN BRACKET PHI COTH PHI MINUS 1 CLOSE BRACKET"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Boundary-Condition Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC["APPLY SHELL MASS BALANCE"] --> Q1{"IS SPECIES CONSUMED OR PRODUCED AT WALLS"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"SPECIFY SURFACE CONCENTRATION OR FLUX"}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{"GAS LIQUID OR SOLID INTERFACE"}
    Q2 -- CA KNOWN --> BC1["DIRICHLET BC: CA AT R EQUALS R IS FIXED"]
    Q2 -- NA KNOWN --> BC2["NEUMANN BC: DCRADR AT R EQUALS R IS FIXED"]
    Q3 -- INTERFACE --> BC3["EQUILIBRIUM OR HENRY LAW CA GAS EQUALS H CA LIQUID"]
    BC1 --> SOL["SOLVE ODE"]
    BC2 --> SOL
    BC3 --> SOL
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define *Shell Mass Balance*. List the four key terms that appear in its general statement.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The shell mass balance is the statement of **conservation of mass** applied to an infinitesimally thin *shell* (control volume) within a fluid or solid medium. The general form is:

> **(Rate of mass entering the shell) − (Rate of mass leaving the shell) + (Rate of mass generated inside the shell) = (Rate of mass accumulation inside the shell)**

The four key terms are: (i) **convective/diffusive flux**, (ii) **net outflow**, (iii) **generation/consumption by chemical reaction** (`R_A`), and (iv) **transient accumulation** (`∂ρ_A/∂t`). For steady state with no reaction, the equation reduces to `Rate in = Rate out`, the simplest form of Stefan's diffusion problem.

*(Award: 1 mark – definition, 1 mark – equation, 1 mark – naming the four terms.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Write the steady-state shell mass balance equation for species **A** diffusing radially through a **spherical shell** when a first-order homogeneous reaction `R_A = -k c_A` is occurring.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{r^{2}}\,\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^{2}\,N_{A,r}\right) + k\,c_A \;=\; 0\;}
$$

After substituting **Fick's first law** `N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\,(dc_A/dr)`, this expands to:

$$
\frac{d^{2}c_A}{dr^{2}} + \frac{2}{r}\,\frac{dc_A}{dr} - \frac{k}{D_{AB}}\,c_A \;=\; 0
$$

The boundary conditions for a catalyst pellet of radius `R` are: `c_A` is finite at `r = 0`, and `c_A = c_{A,s}` at `r = R`.

*(Award: 1 mark – spherical shell-balance eqn, 1 mark – Fick substitution, 1 mark – boundary conditions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the steady-state **cylindrical shell mass balance equation** for species **A** diffusing radially in a tube of inner radius `R_1` and outer radius `R_2`, with a homogeneous first-order reaction `-R_A = k c_A`. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a gas absorption experiment, **A** diffuses through a stagnant gas film **B** of thickness `δ = 0.002 m`. The total molar concentration is `c = 0.04 kmol/m³`. The mole fraction of **A** at the gas–liquid interface (`z = 0`) is `x_{A,1} = 0.30`, and at the bulk gas (`z = δ`) is `x_{A,2} = 0.05`. If `D_{AB} = 1.8 × 10^{-5} m²/s`, calculate the **molar flux `N_A`** and the **mass-transfer rate** if the area is `1 m²`. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Assumptions:** (i) Steady state, (ii) radial diffusion only (no `z`, `θ` components), (iii) constant `D_{AB}`, (iv) ideal-gas behaviour, (v) no net convection.

2. **Geometry of shell** at radius `r`, thickness `Δr`, length `L`:  
   Inner lateral area `= 2π r L`, outer `= 2π(r+Δr)L`, volume `= 2π r L Δr`.

3. **Mass balance** (rate in − rate out + generation = 0):

$$
2\pi r L\,N_{A,r}(r) - 2\pi(r+\Delta r)L\,N_{A,r}(r+\Delta r) + R_A\,(2\pi r L\,\Delta r) = 0
$$

4. **Divide by `2π L Δr`** and take limit `Δr → 0`:

$$
-\frac{d}{dr}(r N_{A,r}) + r R_A = 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}(r N_{A,r}) = R_A
$$

5. **Substitute Fick + first-order kinetics** (`R_A = -k c_A`):

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r D_{AB}\frac{d c_A}{dr}\right) - k c_A = 0
$$

6. **Final ODE:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{d^{2}c_A}{dr^{2}} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dc_A}{dr} - \frac{k}{D_{AB}} c_A = 0\;}
$$

*(Mark split: 1 – sketch/assumptions, 2 – mass-balance equation, 2 – limit & derivative step, 2 – Fick + kinetic substitution.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

1. **Identify geometry:** Cartesian (planar stagnant film), so Stefan's law applies directly.

$$
N_A = \frac{c\,D_{AB}}{z_2 - z_1}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}}\right)
$$

2. **Plug in numerical values:**

$$
N_A = \frac{(0.04)\,(1.8 \times 10^{-5})}{0.002 - 0}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - 0.05}{1 - 0.30}\right)
$$

3. **Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\frac{1 - 0.05}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.95}{0.70} = 1.3571
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\ln(1.3571) = 0.3054
$$

4. **Compute numerator:**

$$
c\,D_{AB} = 0.04 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 7.2 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{kmol/(m·s)}
$$

5. **Compute flux:**

$$
N_A = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-7}}{0.002}\,(0.3054) = (3.6 \times 10^{-4})\,(0.3054)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_A = 1.10 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kmol/(m²·s)}\;}
$$

6. **Mass-transfer rate** for `A = 1 m²`:

$$
\dot{n}_A = N_A \cdot A = (1.10 \times 10^{-4})(1) = \boxed{1.10 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kmol/s}}
$$

*(Mark split: 2 – selecting correct formula, 2 – log evaluation, 2 – flux & rate calculation, 1 – units.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the **spherical-shell mass balance** for species **A** with diffusion and first-order reaction, and obtain the expression for the **effectiveness factor `η`** in terms of the **Thiele modulus `φ`**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Methanol vapour **A** diffuses through a stagnant air film **B** of thickness `L = 0.005 m` at `T = 298 K`, `P = 1 atm`. The mole fraction at `z = 0` is `x_{A,1} = 0.10` and at `z = L` is `x_{A,2} = 0.02`. Given `D_{AB} = 1.5 × 10^{-5} m²/s`, compute the molar flux `N_A` and verify whether the assumption of **stagnant B** is reasonable at the gas–liquid interface. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Geometry:** Spherical shell between `r` and `r + Δr`, areas `4πr²` and `4π(r+Δr)²`, volume `4πr²Δr`.

2. **Mass balance:**

$$
4\pi r^{2}\,N_{A,r}(r) - 4\pi(r+\Delta r)^{2}\,N_{A,r}(r+\Delta r) - k c_A (4\pi r^{2}\Delta r) = 0
$$

3. **Limit `Δr → 0`:**

$$
-\frac{d}{dr}(r^{2} N_{A,r}) - k c_A\,r^{2} = 0
$$

4. **Insert Fick's law** `N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}(dc_A/dr)`:

$$
\frac{d^{2}c_A}{dr^{2}} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{dc_A}{dr} - \frac{k}{D_{AB}} c_A = 0
$$

5. **Solution** (Thiele modulus `φ = R\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}`):

$$
\frac{c_A}{c_{A,s}} = \frac{R}{r}\cdot\frac{\sinh(r\phi/R)}{\sinh(\phi)}
$$

6. **Effectiveness factor:**

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{actual rate}}{\text{rate at } c_{A,s}} = \frac{3}{\phi^{2}}\bigl[\phi\coth(\phi) - 1\bigr]
$$

*(Mark split: 2 – shell balance setup, 2 – ODE form, 2 – solution form, 1 – effectiveness factor.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

1. **Ideal gas concentration:**

$$
c = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{(8.314)(298)} = 40.88\;\text{mol/m}^{3} = 0.04088\;\text{kmol/m}^{3}
$$

2. **Apply Stefan's law:**

$$
N_A = \frac{c D_{AB}}{L}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A,2}}{1 - x_{A,1}}\right)
$$

3. **Substitute values:**

$$
N_A = \frac{(0.04088)(1.5 \times 10^{-5})}{0.005}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - 0.02}{1 - 0.10}\right)
$$

4. **Compute:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{0.98}{0.90}\right) = \ln(1.0889) = 0.0852
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{6.132 \times 10^{-7}}{0.005}\,(0.0852) = (1.226 \times 10^{-4})(0.0852)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_A = 1.044 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kmol/(m²·s)}\;}
$$

5. **Verify stagnant B assumption:** Since `N_B = 0` is imposed by the Stefan model, the consistency check requires that the convective contribution `x_A (N_A + N_B)` remains small compared to the diffusive term. Here, the maximum `x_A = 0.10`, so the convective correction `(x_A N_A)` is small (~10%), validating the assumption as a reasonable engineering approximation.

*(Mark split: 2 – c calculation, 2 – Stefan equation, 2 – numerical evaluation, 1 – comment on assumption.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error on the generation term.** The shell-balance equation must read `Rate in − Rate out + Generation = 0`. Many students flip the sign of `R_A` and lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the `r` and `r²` terms in cylindrical and spherical geometries.** The shell *area changes with radius* — this is the whole reason for the `(1/r)d(r·)/dr` and `(1/r²)d(r²·)/dr` forms. Marks deducted: up to 4.
> 3. **Confusing the Thiele modulus `φ`** with the dimensionless radial coordinate `u = r/R`. `φ = R√(k/D)` is a *constant*; `u` is the *variable*.
> 4. **Using `ln[(1-x_{A,1})/(1-x_{A,2})]` instead of `ln[(1-x_{A,2})/(1-x_{A,1})]`** in Stefan's law — reversed log argument is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the boundary conditions** in Part (a) derivations. Even in derivations, KTU examiners award 1 mark for explicitly writing the BCs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Shell mass balance** = microscopic mass conservation applied to a thin *control-volume shell*.
- [x] **5-step procedure:** Sketch → Identify shell → Flux → Conservation → Limit to zero thickness.
- [x] **Cartesian (z):** `−d N_A/dz + R_A = 0`.
- [x] **Cylindrical (r):** `−(1/r) d(r N_{A,r})/dr + R_A = 0`.
- [x] **Spherical (r):** `−(1/r²) d(r² N_{A,r})/dr + R_A = 0`.
- [x] **Constitutive law:** Fick's first law `N_{A,z} = -D_{AB} dc_A/dz + x_A (N_A + N_B)`.
- [x] **Stagnant B (`N_B = 0`)** ⇒ Stefan's law: `N_A = (c D_{AB}/δ) ln[(1 − x_{A,2})/(1 − x_{A,1})]`.
- [x] **Linearised form** for `x_A ≪ 1`: `N_A ≈ c D_{AB} (x_{A,1} − x_{A,2}) / δ`.
- [x] **Thiele modulus** `φ = R √(k/D_{AB})` quantifies diffusion–reaction competition.
- [x] **Effectiveness factor** `η = (3/φ²)[φ coth φ − 1]`; `η → 1` (reaction-limited) for small `φ`, `η → 3/φ` (diffusion-limited) for large `φ`.
- [x] **Engineering apps:** Catalyst pellets, gas absorbers, membranes, polymer-film permeation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five lines and you master Module 4.** Every equation in Module 4 (BSL Chapter 18–19) originates from the same shell-balance template above. Practise deriving the Cartesian version first, then generalise to cylindrical and spherical — that is exactly the order KTU examiners expect students to answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boundary conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Transport Phenomena**, **Boundary Conditions** (BCs) are the prescribed mathematical constraints (in the form of equations or values) imposed on the dependent variables — velocity $\mathbf{v}$, temperature $T$, or concentration $c_A$ — at the physical edges of the domain of interest. They are the essential "closing statements" required to obtain a unique, physically meaningful solution to the governing differential equations (continuity, Navier–Stokes, energy, and species continuity equations).

Mathematically, boundary conditions transform a *general solution* (containing arbitrary integration constants) into a *particular solution* that satisfies the physics of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The student must clearly distinguish between the **three classical types** of boundary conditions:
> 1. **Dirichlet (First Kind):** Specifies the value of the dependent variable at the boundary (e.g., $T = T_s$ at a heated wall).
> 2. **Neumann (Second Kind):** Specifies the value of the derivative (flux) at the boundary (e.g., $\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\Big|_w = 0$ at an insulated wall).
> 3. **Robin / Mixed (Third Kind):** Specifies a linear combination of the variable and its derivative (e.g., $-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\Big|_w = h(T_w - T_\infty)$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a rubber sheet stretched on a frame. The governing equation (Laplace/Poisson type) tells you *how the sheet wants to curve*, but without specifying what happens at the edges, the sheet can flop into infinite shapes. The boundary conditions are like **the clamps holding the frame** — they lock the edges at fixed positions (Dirichlet), at fixed tilts (Neumann), or with elastic springs (Robin). Only when the edges are clamped does the sheet form a unique, predictable shape.

In transport phenomena:
- The **"frame"** is the physical wall, free surface, or interface.
- The **"clamp"** is the physics at that interface — no-slip, no-penetration, no-temperature-jump, no-mass-flux, etc.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of Newtonian fluid | $10^{-3}$ (water at 20°C) | **Pa·s** |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | $1000$ (water) | **kg/m³** |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | $0.6$ (water) | **W/(m·K)** |
| $D_{AB}$ | Binary diffusivity | $10^{-9}$ (gas–gas) | **m²/s** |
| $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | $10 - 10000$ | **W/(m²·K)** |
| $k_c$ | Mass transfer coefficient | $10^{-3} - 10^{-1}$ | **m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions are the bridge between mathematics and physics.** A perfectly derived governing equation with the wrong BC will give a *physically meaningless* result. Always state your BCs explicitly before solving.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation
First, identify whether the transport problem is in **momentum**, **heat**, or **mass** transfer. Each has its own differential equation:

$$\text{Momentum: } \rho \frac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

$$\text{Heat: } \rho C_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \dot{q}_{\text{gen}}$$

$$\text{Mass: } \frac{D c_A}{Dt} = D_{AB} \nabla^2 c_A + R_A$$

### Step 2: Identify the Boundary Types
The domain may contain any combination of the following physical boundaries:
- **Solid wall** (stationary or moving)
- **Free surface** (gas–liquid interface)
- **Interface** between two fluids or two solids
- **Inlet / outlet** planes
- **Axis of symmetry**

### Step 3: Apply the Appropriate Physics at Each Boundary
For each boundary, ask:
- Is mass flux zero? → Then *no-penetration* and *no-slip* may apply.
- Is heat flux specified? → Then a Neumann-type condition applies.
- Is temperature specified? → Then a Dirichlet-type condition applies.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Physical Reasoning

- **Why no-slip?** Molecules adjacent to a solid wall adhere to it due to strong intermolecular forces. This is an empirical fact (verified for all Newtonian fluids except extreme rarified gases).
- **Why no-penetration?** A solid wall is impermeable to mass; therefore, the normal component of velocity at the wall must vanish.
- **Why no-temperature-jump?** Energy is conserved across an interface; hence, the temperature field must be continuous.
- **Why continuity of flux?** Conservation of energy/mass requires the flux leaving one side equals flux entering the other.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Transport Mechanism | Boundary Type | Mathematical Condition | Physical Name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Momentum | Solid wall (stationary) | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ | **No-slip condition** |
| Momentum | Solid wall (moving with $\mathbf{v}_s$) | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_s$ | **No-slip with surface motion** |
| Momentum | Impermeable wall | $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$ | **No-penetration** |
| Momentum | Interface fluid 1/fluid 2 | $v_{n,1} = v_{n,2}$, $\tau_{t,1} = \tau_{t,2}$ | **Continuity of velocity & shear** |
| Momentum | Symmetry line | $\frac{\partial v_t}{\partial n} = 0$ | **Symmetry BC** |
| Heat | Surface at fixed $T_s$ | $T = T_s$ | **Dirichlet (1st kind)** |
| Heat | Insulated surface | $\frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = 0$ | **Neumann (2nd kind)** |
| Heat | Convection at surface | $-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\Big\vert_w = h(T_w - T_\infty)$ | **Robin / Convective BC** |
| Heat | Two-solid interface | $T_1 = T_2$, $-k_1\frac{\partial T_1}{\partial n} = -k_2\frac{\partial T_2}{\partial n}$ | **Continuity of $T$ & flux** |
| Mass | Impermeable wall | $\mathbf{N}_A \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$ | **No mass flux** |
| Mass | Surface at fixed $c_{A,s}$ | $c_A = c_{A,s}$ | **Dirichlet** |
| Mass | Convective mass transfer | $-D_{AB}\frac{\partial c_A}{\partial n}\Big\vert_w = k_c(c_{A,w} - c_{A,\infty})$ | **Robin** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Boundary conditions are the *operational heart* of CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics), finite-element analysis, and process equipment design. Wrong BCs in a reactor simulation can predict temperatures off by hundreds of degrees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Continuity of Shear Stress at a Fluid–Fluid Interface

**Problem Setup:** Consider two Newtonian fluids (1 and 2) in contact along a planar interface located at $y = 0$. Fluid 1 occupies $y > 0$ and Fluid 2 occupies $y < 0$. Both fluids are in laminar, parallel flow along the $x$-direction.

### Step 1: Write the velocity field assumptions

For steady, fully developed, pressure-driven parallel flow with no body forces, the only non-zero velocity component is $v_x = v_x(y)$, and it depends solely on $y$.

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = (v_x(y), 0, 0) \quad \text{in Fluid 1}$$

$$\mathbf{v}_2 = (v_x(y), 0, 0) \quad \text{in Fluid 2}$$

### Step 2: Apply the continuity equation

The continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ reduces to $\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} = 0$, which is automatically satisfied because $v_x$ depends only on $y$.

### Step 3: Apply the Navier–Stokes equation (x-component)

For fully developed flow with $\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \text{const}$, the Navier–Stokes equation in each fluid becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu_1 \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} \quad \text{(Fluid 1)} \\
0 &= -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu_2 \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} \quad \text{(Fluid 2)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Integrate the Navier–Stokes equation

Integrating twice with respect to $y$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_x^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{2\mu_1}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2 \quad \text{for } y \ge 0 \\
v_x^{(2)} &= \frac{1}{2\mu_2}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y^2 + C_3 y + C_4 \quad \text{for } y \le 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Apply boundary conditions to determine constants

**Condition 1: Velocity continuity at the interface ($y=0$)**
The velocity must be continuous across the interface to prevent a physical discontinuity:

$$v_x^{(1)}\Big\vert_{y=0} = v_x^{(2)}\Big\vert_{y=0}$$

This yields: $C_2 = C_4$.

**Condition 2: Continuity of shear stress at the interface**

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is $\tau_{yx} = \mu \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}$. At the interface, this shear stress must be continuous (Newton's third law, force balance on a vanishingly thin interface element):

$$\mu_1 \frac{\partial v_x^{(1)}}{\partial y}\Big\vert_{y=0} = \mu_2 \frac{\partial v_x^{(2)}}{\partial y}\Big\vert_{y=0}$$

Differentiating:

$$\mu_1 C_1 = \mu_2 C_3$$

Therefore:

$$C_3 = \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} C_1$$

### Step 6: Physical interpretation

The four boundary conditions required are typically:
- Velocity at far boundaries (e.g., $v_x^{(1)}\vert_{y \to \infty}$ or wall velocity).
- Velocity continuity at interface (gives one equation).
- Shear stress continuity at interface (gives one equation).
- Either a wall or another far-field condition for the second fluid.

This gives exactly the **four equations** required to solve for the four unknowns $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** The continuity of shear stress at the interface is the *key* boundary condition that distinguishes two-fluid flow from single-fluid flow. Forgetting this leads to an underdetermined system.

## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Boundary Condition with Convection (Robin)

Consider a semi-infinite solid occupying $x > 0$, initially at temperature $T_i$. At time $t = 0$, the surface $x = 0$ is suddenly exposed to a fluid at temperature $T_\infty$ with convective heat transfer coefficient $h$. Determine the boundary condition at $x = 0$.

### Step 1: Write the energy balance at the surface

The energy flux conducted into the solid at the surface must equal the energy convected away from the surface (assuming no radiation or generation):

$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = h \left[ T(0,t) - T_\infty \right]$$

### Step 2: Rearrange into standard Robin form

$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = h \left[ T_s - T_\infty \right]$$

This is the classical **Newton's law of cooling** written as a boundary condition. It is of the third kind (Robin), since it relates both the value of $T$ and its gradient at the boundary.

### Step 3: Limiting cases (very important for KTU)

**Case A: $h \to \infty$ (perfect convection)**
The convective resistance $1/h \to 0$, so the surface instantly equilibrates with the fluid:

$$\lim_{h \to \infty} T_s = T_\infty$$

This reduces to a **Dirichlet condition**.

**Case B: $h \to 0$ (perfectly insulated)**
The convective flux vanishes:

$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = 0$$

This reduces to a **Neumann (insulated) condition**.

**Case C: $k \to \infty$ (infinite conductivity)**
The solid temperature becomes spatially uniform:

$$T(x,t) = T_s(t)$$

The gradient inside the solid vanishes and the entire solid obeys a lumped capacitance equation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Mistake Patterns

| Mistake | Correct Statement |
| :--- | :--- |
| ❌ Setting $v_x = 0$ at a free surface | ✅ At a free surface: $\tau_{yx}\vert_s = 0$ (i.e., $\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\vert_s = 0$), but $v_x$ is not zero. |
| ❌ Setting $-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial n}$ continuous only if $k$ is the same | ✅ Flux continuity: $-k_1\frac{\partial T_1}{\partial n} = -k_2\frac{\partial T_2}{\partial n}$ always, regardless of $k$ values. |
| ❌ Forgetting the gradient sign in Neumann BC | ✅ Heat flows from hot to cold: gradient points into the cooler region. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Boundary Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boundary Conditions in Transport Phenomena] --> B[Type 1 Dirichlet Value Specified]
    A --> C[Type 2 Neumann Flux Specified]
    A --> D[Type 3 Robin Mixed Value Plus Flux]

    B --> B1[Fixed Wall Temperature Ts]
    B --> B2[Fixed Wall Velocity v wall]
    B --> B3[Fixed Surface Concentration cAs]

    C --> C1[Insulated Surface dT by dn equals zero]
    C --> C2[Impermeable Wall N A dot n equals zero]
    C --> C3[Specified Heat Flux q dot]

    D --> D1[Convective Heat Transfer h Ts minus T infinity]
    D --> D2[Convective Mass Transfer kc cAs minus cA infinity]
    D --> D3[Interface Continuity of Flux]

    A --> E[Physical Boundaries]
    E --> E1[Solid Wall]
    E --> E2[Free Surface]
    E --> E3[Fluid Fluid Interface]
    E --> E4[Inlet Outlet Plane]
    E --> E5[Axis of Symmetry]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Boundary Condition Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Identify Boundary Type] --> Q1{Is surface solid impermeable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Apply No Slip AND No Penetration]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is surface free liquid gas?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Apply Zero Shear AND Zero Normal Stress]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is there heat transfer at surface?}
    Q3 -- Fixed T --> BC1[Dirichlet T equals Ts]
    Q3 -- Insulated --> BC2[Neumann dT by dn equals zero]
    Q3 -- Convection --> BC3[Robin h Ts minus T infinity]
    Q3 -- No heat transfer --> Q4{Is there mass transfer at surface?}
    Q4 -- Fixed cA --> BC4[Dirichlet cA equals cAs]
    Q4 -- Impermeable --> BC5[Neumann NA dot n equals zero]
    Q4 -- Convection --> BC6[Robin kc cAs minus cA infinity]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Continuity Conditions at Fluid Fluid Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fluid 1 Region y greater than 0] -- Velocity vx continuous --> Interface[Interface at y equals 0]
    Interface -- Shear stress tau yx continuous --> F2[Fluid 2 Region y less than 0]
    F1 -- Same normal stress P1 --> Interface
    Interface -- Same P2 --> F2
    Note1[Note Four BCs required to close Navier Stokes problem]
    Interface -.- Note1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The three Mermaid diagrams above are designed as **functional architectural maps** that visually trace the logical decision-making process a KTU student must follow when assigning boundary conditions. Each "block" represents a physical situation; the arrows indicate the conditional transitions. This mirrors the analytical reasoning expected by KTU examiners.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Application to Classic Hagen-Poiseuille Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Plane z equals 0 Uniform Velocity v avg] --> B[Pipe Wall r equals R]
    B -- No Slip vr equals 0 AND vz equals 0 --> C[Outlet Plane z equals L Fully Developed]
    C -- d vz by dz equals 0 --> D[Axis r equals 0 Symmetry]
    D -- dvz by dr equals 0 --> B
```

For Hagen–Poiseuille flow, the boundary conditions are:
1. **At wall** ($r = R$): $v_z(R) = 0$ (no-slip).
2. **At axis** ($r = 0$): $\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\Big\vert_{r=0} = 0$ (symmetry — finite velocity).
3. **At inlet/outlet:** $v_z = v_{\text{avg}}$ at inlet; fully developed at outlet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the no-slip and no-penetration boundary conditions. Why are they physically necessary at a solid-fluid interface?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No-slip condition:** The fluid velocity at a solid wall equals the velocity of the wall itself. For a stationary wall, $\mathbf{v}\vert_w = \mathbf{0}$.

**No-penetration condition:** The normal component of the fluid velocity at an impermeable solid wall must vanish: $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}\vert_w = 0$.

**Physical necessity:** These arise from the strong adhesive forces between fluid molecules and the solid surface (molecular-scale momentum transfer). For a Newtonian continuum fluid, the no-slip condition is an empirically verified constitutive assumption. The no-penetration condition is a direct consequence of mass conservation — a fluid cannot pass through an impermeable wall.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any three types of boundary conditions used in solving transport problems. Give one example of each from heat transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Mathematical Form | Heat Transfer Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dirichlet (1st kind)** | $T = T_s$ at boundary | Surface of a heat exchanger held at $T_s = 100°C$ |
| **Neumann (2nd kind)** | $-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = q_s$ at boundary | Surface receiving constant heat flux $q_s$ (e.g., electrical heater) |
| **Robin (3rd kind)** | $-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Surface losing heat by convection to surrounding fluid |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Derive the boundary conditions for steady, fully developed, laminar flow of two immiscible Newtonian fluids (1 and 2) flowing parallel between two parallel plates separated by a distance $2h$. Fluid 1 occupies the region $-h \le y \le 0$ and Fluid 2 occupies $0 \le y \le h$. The plates at $y = \pm h$ are stationary.**

#### (a) State and justify the four required boundary conditions. (7 marks)

**Step 1: No-slip at the stationary plates.**

At $y = +h$, fluid 2 touches the stationary upper plate:
$$v_x^{(2)}(h) = 0$$

At $y = -h$, fluid 1 touches the stationary lower plate:
$$v_x^{(1)}(-h) = 0$$

These arise from molecular adhesion and the no-slip empirical principle.

**Step 2: Continuity of velocity at the interface.**

At $y = 0$, both fluids must have the same tangential velocity (otherwise a velocity discontinuity would imply infinite shear):
$$v_x^{(1)}(0) = v_x^{(2)}(0)$$

**Step 3: Continuity of shear stress at the interface.**

At a fluid-fluid interface, Newton's third law requires force balance across the interface. For a thin interface slab in the limit of vanishing thickness:
$$\tau_{yx}^{(1)}\Big\vert_{y=0} = \tau_{yx}^{(2)}\Big\vert_{y=0}$$

For Newtonian fluids:
$$\mu_1 \frac{dv_x^{(1)}}{dy}\Big\vert_{y=0} = \mu_2 \frac{dv_x^{(2)}}{dy}\Big\vert_{y=0}$$

These four conditions provide the mathematical closure to solve the Navier–Stokes equation in each fluid.

#### (b) Write the integrated form of the Navier–Stokes equation in each fluid and determine the integration constants. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Navier–Stokes (x-component) for fully developed flow.**

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu_1 \frac{d^2 v_x^{(1)}}{dy^2} \quad \text{(in Fluid 1)} \\
0 &= -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu_2 \frac{d^2 v_x^{(2)}}{dy^2} \quad \text{(in Fluid 2)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Integrate twice.**

For constant pressure gradient $\frac{dP}{dx}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_x^{(1)}(y) &= \frac{1}{2\mu_1}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + A_1 y + A_2 \quad \text{for } -h \le y \le 0 \\
v_x^{(2)}(y) &= \frac{1}{2\mu_2}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + B_1 y + B_2 \quad \text{for } \phantom{-}0 \le y \le h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply the four BCs.**

From $v_x^{(1)}(-h) = 0$:
$$\frac{1}{2\mu_1}\frac{dP}{dx} h^2 - A_1 h + A_2 = 0$$

From $v_x^{(2)}(+h) = 0$:
$$\frac{1}{2\mu_2}\frac{dP}{dx} h^2 + B_1 h + B_2 = 0$$

From $v_x^{(1)}(0) = v_x^{(2)}(0)$:
$$A_2 = B_2$$

From shear stress continuity at $y = 0$:
$$\mu_1 A_1 = \mu_2 B_1 \implies B_1 = \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} A_1$$

**Step 4: Solve the linear system.**

Subtracting the two plate equations and using $A_2 = B_2$:

$$\frac{h^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mu_1} - \frac{1}{\mu_2}\right)\frac{dP}{dx} - h(A_1 + B_1) = 0$$

Substituting $B_1 = (\mu_1/\mu_2) A_1$:

$$A_1 = \frac{h}{2(\mu_1 + \mu_2)}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\mu_2 - \mu_1\right)$$

And:

$$A_2 = B_2 = -\frac{h^2}{2(\mu_1 + \mu_2)}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\mu_1 + \mu_2\right) = -\frac{h^2}{2}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

The final expressions for velocity are then obtained by substitution back.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**A semi-infinite solid ($x > 0$) initially at uniform temperature $T_i$ is suddenly immersed at time $t = 0$ in a fluid at temperature $T_\infty$ with convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ at its surface $x = 0$.**

#### (a) Formulate the governing differential equation and state all initial and boundary conditions. (7 marks)

**Governing PDE (1D transient conduction):**

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

where $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ is the thermal diffusivity.

**Initial condition (IC):**
$$T(x, 0) = T_i \quad \text{for all } x > 0$$

**Boundary conditions:**

**BC 1 — Semi-infinite far-field:** The temperature far from the surface must remain at the initial value (no disturbance reaches infinity in finite time):
$$\lim_{x \to \infty} T(x, t) = T_i$$

**BC 2 — Convection at the surface:** Newton's law of cooling applied as an energy balance at $x = 0$:

$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = h \left[ T(0, t) - T_\infty \right]$$

This is a **Robin-type (third kind)** boundary condition.

#### (b) Define a dimensionless variable $\theta = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}$ and rewrite the problem. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply the substitution.**

$$T = T_\infty + \theta (T_i - T_\infty)$$

$$T - T_\infty = \theta (T_i - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2: Transform the PDE.**

Differentiate with respect to $t$ (note: $T_i, T_\infty$ are constants):
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = (T_i - T_\infty)\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t}$$

Differentiate with respect to $x$:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = (T_i - T_\infty)\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} = (T_i - T_\infty)\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}$$

Substituting back:
$$(T_i - T_\infty)\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \alpha (T_i - T_\infty)\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}$$

Dividing by $(T_i - T_\infty)$:

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 3: Transform the IC.**

$$T(x, 0) = T_i \implies \theta(x, 0) = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = 1$$

**Step 4: Transform the BCs.**

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \theta(x, t) = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = 1$$

At $x = 0$:
$$-k (T_i - T_\infty)\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = h (T_i - T_\infty)\theta(0, t)$$

Dividing by $(T_i - T_\infty)$:
$$-k\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = h \theta(0,t)$$

Or in canonical form:
$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = -\frac{h}{k}\theta(0,t)$$

This dimensionless form is the standard textbook formulation used to derive the complementary error function solution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Read Before Exam!)**
> 1. **Sign convention in Neumann BCs:** Always check whether the gradient points *into* or *out of* the domain. A sign error in $-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial n}$ alone can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting flux continuity at interfaces:** When two materials meet, *both* $T$ and flux must be continuous. Stating only $T$ continuity is incomplete (lose 2 marks).
> 3. **Treating free surface as no-slip:** A free surface has *zero shear stress*, not zero velocity. $\tau = 0$ does NOT mean $v_x = 0$.
> 4. **Incorrect identification of problem type:** A student may confuse "fixed flux" (Neumann) with "fixed temperature" (Dirichlet). Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Initial condition vs Boundary condition:** ICs are at $t = 0$; BCs are at spatial boundaries. Mixing them up loses marks.
> 6. **Units in convective BC:** The coefficient $h$ must be in **W/(m²·K)** for SI; ensure both sides of the equation have units of W/m².

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Night Revision!)**

- **Three types of BCs:** Dirichlet (value), Neumann (flux), Robin (mixed).
- **No-slip:** $\mathbf{v}\vert_w = \mathbf{v}_s$ at solid wall.
- **No-penetration:** $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}\vert_w = 0$ at impermeable wall.
- **Symmetry:** $\frac{\partial v}{\partial n}\Big\vert_{\text{axis}} = 0$.
- **Free surface:** $\tau_{yx} = 0$ (zero shear), but $v_x$ is generally non-zero.
- **Interface continuity:** Both $T$ (or $c_A$) and its flux are continuous across an interface.
- **Convective BC (Robin):** $-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial n}\vert_w = h(T_w - T_\infty)$.
- **Mass transfer Robin:** $-D_{AB}\frac{\partial c_A}{\partial n}\vert_w = k_c(c_{A,w} - c_{A,\infty})$.
- **Initial condition:** Always for transient problems; prescribed at $t=0$.
- **Number of BCs:** Must equal the order of the spatial derivative for unique solution.
- **Limiting cases:** $h \to \infty$ → Dirichlet; $h \to 0$ → Neumann (insulated).
- **Importance in CFD:** Correct BCs are non-negotiable for valid numerical simulations.
- **Two-fluid problems:** Always include shear-stress continuity at the interface.
- **Symmetry exploitation:** Reduces computational domain by half (or more), but requires $\frac{\partial}{\partial n} = 0$ BC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion through stagnant gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT602 - TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diffusion through stagnant gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of binary mass transfer, **diffusion of A through stagnant B** (classically known as **Stefan Diffusion** or **equimolar counter-diffusion with zero molar flux of B**, $N_B = 0$) is the steady-state molecular transport process in which a chemical species **A** migrates through a stationary (non-moving) medium of species **B**, driven solely by a **concentration gradient** and accompanied by a *bulk convective flow* directed opposite to the diffusive flux of A.

The system is governed by the **Stefan equation** (also called **Stefan’s Law of Diffusion**):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{p_{BM}}$$

where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{(P_{B2} - P_{B1})}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{B1}}{P_{B2}}\right)}$$

This situation arises in **closed systems** or systems where species B is not consumed/produced at the interface (e.g., a liquid surface evaporating into a stagnant air gap).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** When one component of a binary gas mixture is diffusing through the other which is *stationary* (e.g., water vapor diffusing upward through still air), the resulting mass-transfer equation is known as **Stefan's equation**. The "stagnancy" of B is a *constraint*, not an assumption of physical immobility — it results from the **no net molar flux condition** ($N_B = 0$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Closed Room with Perfume:**
> Imagine you spray perfume (species **A**) at one end of a sealed, narrow tube whose other end is filled with perfectly still air (species **B**). The perfume molecules want to spread down the tube, but as they do, they *push* the air molecules ahead of them. Since the tube is closed, the air cannot escape, so the air molecules experience a small *back-flow* (counter-current) that exactly cancels any net movement of air. The result: **only perfume appears to move**; air stays "stagnant" in bulk even though its individual molecules jiggle thermally.
>
> **Engineering analogy:** A pond of water evaporating into still, dry air. The water vapor (A) diffuses upward through air (B). The air, being heavy and undisturbed, has *zero net molar flux* — yet a *bulk convective flow* exists because the water vapor "drags" the local gas mixture along with it.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $D_{AB}$ | Binary diffusivity | **m²/s** (typical gas: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$) |
| $P$ | Total system pressure | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K (Kelvin)** |
| $N_A$ | Molar flux of A | **kg·mol/(m²·s)** |
| $z_2 - z_1$ | Diffusion path length | **m (metres)** |
| $p_{BM}$ | Log-mean partial pressure of B | **Pa** |

---

## 1.4 Physical Picture of the Phenomenon

Consider a vertical **Stefan tube** partially filled with a volatile liquid (say, pure benzene at the bottom) in an open tube exposed to still air at the top:

- At the **liquid interface** ($z = z_1$): partial pressure of benzene vapour equals its saturation pressure $P_{A1} = P_A^{sat}$.
- At the **top of the tube** ($z = z_2$): benzene is carried away by gentle convection, so $P_{A2} \approx 0$.
- The "B" (air) partial pressure changes from $P - P_{A1}$ at the bottom to $P$ at the top.
- A **bulk (Stefan) flow** arises, directed upward, which enhances the transport of A beyond pure molecular diffusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bulk flow exists because more moles of A are entering any horizontal cross-section than are leaving — the imbalance must be compensated by a small bulk velocity of the gas mixture. This bulk velocity is **always in the same direction as the diffusive flux of A** for the stagnant-B case.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Equations of Stefan Diffusion

### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law (Molar Form) — Starting Point

For binary diffusion with bulk flow:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

where $x_A$ is the mole fraction of A. Rearranging:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{x_A \, N_B}{1 - x_A}$$

### 2.1.2 The Stagnancy Constraint

For species B to be stagnant (zero net molar flux):

$$N_B = 0$$

Substituting into the previous equation:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### 2.1.3 Conversion from Concentration to Partial Pressure

Using the **ideal gas law**:

$$C_A = \frac{P_A}{R T}, \quad C = \frac{P}{R T}$$

So:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dz} = \frac{1}{R T} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

### 2.1.4 Expressing the Mole Fraction in Terms of Partial Pressures

Since $P$ (total pressure) is constant in a stagnant system:

$$P_B = P - P_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - x_A = 1 - \frac{P_A}{P} = \frac{P - P_A}{P} = \frac{P_B}{P}$$

### 2.1.5 Final Integration

The full derivation (shown in detail in Section 3) yields **Stefan's Equation**:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}$$

---

## 2.2 Logical Flow of Reasoning (Why each step exists)

1. **Why is there a bulk flow even though B is stagnant?** Because A is being continuously added/removed at the interface, requiring a convective transport component to maintain mass balance. *Bulk flow compensates for unequal molar fluxes at boundaries.*
2. **Why use partial pressures instead of concentrations?** Because for gases at constant $T$ and $P$, $P_A$ is linearly proportional to $C_A$, simplifying the differential equation to a form with constant total concentration.
3. **Why does the log-mean $p_{BM}$ appear?** Because the flux-driving term $(1 - x_A)$ varies with position; integrating $dP_A / (P - P_A)$ produces a logarithm — and the *log-mean* is the standard engineering shorthand for that integral.
4. **What limits Stefan's equation?** It assumes: (a) ideal gas behaviour, (b) constant $T$ and $P$, (c) steady state, (d) one-dimensional diffusion along $z$, and (e) $N_B = 0$ exactly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **KTU Board Exam Cheat Sheet** for diffusion through stagnant gas. Memorize this entirely — Stefan-diffusion problems appear in **Module-4 long-answer (14-mark) and Part-A (3-mark)** slots almost every semester.

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ | General molar flux with bulk flow |
| 2 | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Setting $N_B = 0$ (stagnant B) |
| 3 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\, P}{R T\, \Delta z}\dfrac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$ | **Stefan Equation** (final integrated form) |
| 4 | $p_{BM} = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B1}/P_{B2})}$ | Log-mean partial pressure of B |
| 5 | $N_A^{o} = \dfrac{D_{AB}\, (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{\Delta z}$ | Pure molecular diffusion (no bulk flow) reference |
| 6 | $\dfrac{N_A}{N_A^{o}} = \dfrac{P}{p_{BM}}$ | Ratio of actual flux to pure molecular diffusive flux |
| 7 | $P_A + P_B = P$ | Dalton's law (total pressure = sum of partials) |
| 8 | $C_A = P_A / (R T)$ | Ideal-gas partial pressure to molar concentration |
| 9 | $P_{BM} \approx (P_{B1} + P_{B2})/2$ | Approximation when $P_{B1}/P_{B2} < 2$ (arithmetic mean) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | A diffusing | B stagnant |
|---|---|---|
| Drying of porous solids in still air | Water vapour | Air |
| Humidification towers | Water vapour | Air |
| Burning candle / coal combustion | Oxygen | Combustion gases ($CO_2$, $N_2$) |
| Evaporation of solvent in a closed vessel | Solvent vapour | Air |
| Sublimation of naphthalene balls | Naphthalene vapour | Air |
| Absorption of $SO_2$ into alkaline solution | $SO_2$ | Air |
| Packed-column distillation (one side) | More volatile component | Less volatile component |
| Bubble dissolution in a liquid | Dissolved gas | Liquid solvent (diffusion-limited) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Whenever one phase is "static" relative to another across an interface (e.g., liquid evaporating into a gas gap, gas absorbing into a liquid), Stefan-type diffusion controls the rate. Equipment designers use the Stefan equation to size **dryers**, **humidifiers**, **evaporators**, and **gas-absorption columns**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stefan's Equation

### Step 1 — Start from Fick's First Law for a Binary Mixture with Bulk Flow

For two species A and B diffusing in the z-direction, the molar flux of A is given by:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

*Textual logic:* The first term is the **molecular diffusion** (Fickian) contribution. The second term is the **bulk convective flux**, where $(N_A + N_B)$ is the total molar flux of the mixture and $x_A$ is the mole fraction of A.

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Stagnancy Condition $N_B = 0$

Setting $N_B = 0$ means species B has **zero net molar flux** through any cross-section. Substituting:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A \cdot N_A$$

Collecting $N_A$ terms on the left:

$$N_A - x_A N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

*Textual logic:* The denominator $(1 - x_A)$ accounts for the fact that A must diffuse against the bulk drift induced by its own transfer. As $x_A \to 1$, the bulk-flow correction becomes very large (high concentrations of A dominate transport).

---

### Step 3 — Convert Concentration Gradient to Partial-Pressure Gradient

For an ideal gas:

$$C_A = \frac{P_A}{R T}$$

Since total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ are assumed constant (closed stagnant system):

$$\frac{dC_A}{dz} = \frac{1}{R T} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

Substituting:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \cdot \frac{1}{R T} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

---

### Step 4 — Express $(1 - x_A)$ in Terms of Partial Pressures

By Dalton's law of partial pressures:

$$P_A + P_B = P \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_B = P - P_A$$

And the mole fraction is:

$$x_A = \frac{P_A}{P}$$

Therefore:

$$1 - x_A = 1 - \frac{P_A}{P} = \frac{P - P_A}{P} = \frac{P_B}{P}$$

Substituting:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{(P_B/P)} \cdot \frac{1}{R T} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \cdot \frac{1}{P_B} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

---

### Step 5 — Separate Variables

Since $P_A + P_B = P$ and $P$ is constant, differentiating gives:

$$dP_A = -dP_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad dP_A = -dP_B$$

So:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \cdot \frac{1}{P_B} \cdot \frac{dP_B}{dz}$$

Separating variables:

$$N_A \, d z = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \cdot \frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

---

### Step 6 — Integrate Between the Two Boundaries

Integrate from $z_1$ (point 1) to $z_2$ (point 2), with corresponding partial pressures $P_{B1}$ and $P_{B2}$:

$$\int_{z_1}^{z_2} N_A \, dz = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \int_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}} \frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

Because $N_A$ is constant at steady state:

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \Big[\ln P_B\Big]_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}}$$

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

---

### Step 7 — Solve for $N_A$

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)}$$

---

### Step 8 — Convert Logarithm to Log-Mean Form

Using the algebraic identity:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right) = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$$

where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is defined as:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on sign:** The logarithmic term is naturally *positive* in direction (since $P_{B2} > P_{B1}$ when A diffuses from 1 to 2). Some textbook conventions write $\ln(P_{B1}/P_{B2})$ and negate; both are equivalent.

Substituting:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$$

Now since $P_{B2} - P_{B1} = (P - P_{A2}) - (P - P_{A1}) = P_{A1} - P_{A2}$:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}$$

This is the **canonical Stefan equation** in its standard textbook form.

---

### Step 9 — Special Case: $P_{A2} = 0$ (Pure A evaporating into pure B)

If A is completely removed at the upper boundary (e.g., swept away by an infinite atmosphere), then $P_{A2} = 0$, $P_{B2} = P$, and $P_{B1} = P - P_{A1}^{sat}$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{A1}^{sat}}{p_{BM}}$$

with:

$$p_{BM} = P - \frac{P_{A1}^{sat}}{2}$$

when the arithmetic-mean approximation is used (valid for $P_{A1}/P < 0.5$).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Exam Pattern)

### Problem Statement

A tube of length **0.20 m** contains a small pool of liquid benzene at the bottom and is exposed at the top to a large volume of perfectly still air at **1 atm** and **25 °C (298 K)**. The vapour pressure of benzene at 25 °C is **0.125 atm**. The binary gas diffusivity of benzene–air is **$D_{AB} = 0.93 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s**. Calculate:

**(a)** the steady-state molar flux of benzene, and  
**(b)** the rate of evaporation of benzene in kg/(m²·s).

### Solution

**Step 1 — List the data and convert to SI units.**

| Variable | Value |
|---|---|
| $P$ | $1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa |
| $T$ | 298 K |
| $R$ | 8.314 J/(mol·K) |
| $\Delta z = z_2 - z_1$ | 0.20 m |
| $D_{AB}$ | $0.93 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s |
| $P_{A1}$ | $0.125 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 1.2666 \times 10^4$ Pa |
| $P_{A2}$ | $0$ (large air volume, swept clean) |

**Step 2 — Compute partial pressures of B at the two ends.**

$$P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 - 1.2666 \times 10^4 = 8.8659 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the log-mean partial pressure of B.**

$$p_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

$$p_{BM} = \frac{(1.01325 \times 10^5) - (8.8659 \times 10^4)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.01325 \times 10^5}{8.8659 \times 10^4}\right)}$$

$$p_{BM} = \frac{1.2666 \times 10^4}{\ln(1.1429)} = \frac{1.2666 \times 10^4}{0.13353} = 9.4859 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Stefan's equation.**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, \Delta z} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

Numerator (first factor):

$$\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, \Delta z} = \frac{(0.93 \times 10^{-5})(1.01325 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(298)(0.20)}$$

$$= \frac{0.9423}{495.51} = 1.9016 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s·Pa)}$$

Second factor:

$$\frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}} = \frac{1.2666 \times 10^4}{9.4859 \times 10^4} = 0.1335$$

Multiply:

$$N_A = (1.9016 \times 10^{-3})(0.1335) = 2.539 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass flux using $M_{benzene} = 78.11$ kg/kmol.**

$$\dot{m}_A = N_A \cdot M_A = (2.539 \times 10^{-4})(78.11 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.984 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{kg/(m²·s)}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{N_A = 2.54 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{kg·mol/(m²·s)} \quad ; \quad \dot{m}_A = 1.98 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{kg/(m²·s)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Comparison Table — Stefan Diffusion vs Equimolar Counter-Diffusion

| Feature | Stefan Diffusion ($N_B = 0$) | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion ($N_A = -N_B$) |
|---|---|---|
| Driving gradient | Concentration gradient **and** bulk flow | Concentration gradient only |
| Bulk flow direction | Same as $N_A$ | None (zero bulk velocity) |
| Flux equation | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}P}{RT\Delta z}\dfrac{P_{A1}-P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT\Delta z}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$ |
| Log-mean correction | Yes ($p_{BM}$) | No |
| Examples | Evaporation, sublimation | Distillation at constant molar overflow |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Stefan Diffusion Physical Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Liquid A at bottom z1"] -->|Evaporation| B["Vapour-Liquid Interface z = z1"]
    B -->|Mole fraction xA1 saturated| C["Bulk stagnant gas region z1 to z2"]
    C -->|"Bulk Stefan flow upward"| D["Open top z = z2 in still air"]
    D -->|PA2 approx 0| E["Infinite Air Reservoir xB approx 1"]

    subgraph Boundary1["Boundary at z = z1 (Liquid Surface)"]
        F["PA1 = PA(sat) high"]
        G["PB1 = P - PA1 low"]
    end

    subgraph Boundary2["Boundary at z = z2 (Top of tube)"]
        H["PA2 approx 0"]
        I["PB2 approx P (pure air)"]
    end

    Boundary1 -->|Diffusion zone| Boundary2
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the physical geometry: a volatile liquid **A** sits at the bottom of a stagnant gas column, evaporates at $z_1$ (where $P_{A1}$ is high), and the vapour diffuses upward through stagnant air **B**, reaching $z_2$ at the top where the partial pressure of A drops to nearly zero. The "bulk Stefan flow" is the small upward convective velocity that compensates for the continuous addition of A at the interface.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mass Flux Components in Stefan Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Total NA"] --> M2["Molecular Diffusion: -DAB dCA/dz"]
    M1 --> M3["Bulk Convective Flux: xA NA due to NA only"]
    M2 --> M4["Acts along concentration gradient"]
    M3 --> M5["Acts in same direction as NA"]
    M4 --> M6["Effective NA = -DAB/(1-xA) dCA/dz"]
    M5 --> M6
    M6 --> M7["Stefans Final Form"]
```

**Interpretation:** The total molar flux $N_A$ is the sum of two contributions — a Fickian diffusive term and a bulk-flow convective term. The "1 − x_A" denominator in Stefan's equation is the mathematical fingerprint of this convective enhancement.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Calculation Procedure for Stefan Diffusion Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Given DAB, P, T, dz, PA1, PA2"] --> S2["Compute PB1 = P - PA1"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute PB2 = P - PA2"]
    S3 --> S4["Compute pBM = PB2-PB1 / lnPB2/PB1"]
    S4 --> S5["Apply Stefan Eq: NA = DAB P / RT dz * PA1-PA2 / pBM"]
    S5 --> S6["Compute NA in mol/(m2 s)"]
    S6 --> S7["If mass flux needed, multiply by molecular weight MA"]
    S7 --> S8["Report final answer with units"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the step-by-step algorithm a KTU student should follow on the exam paper. Note the order: always compute partial pressures of **B** first, then $p_{BM}$, then apply the Stefan equation.

---

## 4.4 Concept Map — How Stefan's Equation Fits in Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Molecular Diffusion in Gases"] --> B["Binary Systems"]
    B --> C["Equimolar Counter-Diffusion NA = -NB"]
    B --> D["Diffusion of A through Stagnant B - NB = 0"]
    B --> E["Multicomponent Diffusion"]
    D --> F["Stefan Equation"]
    F --> G["Log-Mean PB correction"]
    F --> H["Bulk convective enhancement"]
    F --> I["Application: Drying, Humidification, Evaporation"]
```

**Interpretation:** Stefan diffusion is a *constrained* special case of binary molecular diffusion where the no-net-flux condition on B forces a bulk flow term into the equation. This is conceptually distinct from equimolar counter-diffusion (e.g., distillation) and multicomponent systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** Define Stefan's law of diffusion. State the assumptions involved.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Stefan's law of diffusion describes the steady-state molar transfer of a species **A** through a *stagnant* (non-moving) gas **B**. The governing equation is:
>
> $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$
>
> where $p_{BM} = (P_{B2} - P_{B1}) / \ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})$ is the log-mean partial pressure of B.
>
> **Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion in the z-direction.
> 2. Ideal gas behaviour; constant $T$ and $P$ throughout.
> 3. No net molar flux of B: $N_B = 0$.
> 4. Constant binary diffusivity $D_{AB}$.
> 5. No chemical reaction in the diffusion path.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** What is the physical significance of the log-mean partial pressure $p_{BM}$ in Stefan's equation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The factor $p_{BM}$ in Stefan's equation represents the **effective driving partial-pressure difference** experienced by species A across the diffusion path, *corrected for the non-linear variation* of partial pressure of B with position.
>
> **Physical meaning (1.5 marks):** Since the bulk flow term depends on $(1 - x_A) = P_B / P$, and $P_B$ varies from $P_{B1}$ at one boundary to $P_{B2}$ at the other, the *average* value of $P_B$ along the diffusion path is not a simple arithmetic mean but a **logarithmic (geometric) mean**. This log-mean appears naturally when integrating $dP_A / (P - P_A)$ during the derivation.
>
> **Limiting cases (1.5 marks):**
> - When $P_{B1} \approx P_{B2}$ (i.e., $P_{A1}, P_{A2} \ll P$), $p_{BM} \approx (P_{B1} + P_{B2})/2 \approx P$, and the equation reduces to pure Fickian diffusion.
> - When $P_{A1}$ is significant (concentrated A), $p_{BM}$ is appreciably smaller than $P$, causing the flux to be *larger* than pure molecular diffusion (bulk-flow enhancement).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE regulation: each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice: students answer **either Question A or Question B** in full. Both options are provided below.

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Derive Stefan's equation for diffusion of species A through stagnant gas B starting from Fick's first law. A vertical tube of length 0.5 m has liquid acetone at the bottom. Acetone vapour pressure at 30 °C is 0.32 atm. The tube is open at the top to still air at 1 atm and 30 °C. The binary diffusivity of acetone vapour in air is $1.04 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Fick's first law with bulk flow:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Stagnancy condition $N_B = 0$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Ideal-gas conversion $C_A = P_A / (R T)$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{(1 - x_A) R T} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Use $1 - x_A = P_B / P$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \cdot \frac{1}{P_B} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Since $dP_A = -dP_B$:

$$N_A \, dz = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \cdot \frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Integrate from $z_1$ to $z_2$:

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final Stefan equation with $p_{BM}$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given data:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $T$ | 303 K |
| $P$ | $1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa |
| $\Delta z$ | 0.5 m |
| $P_{A1}$ | $0.32 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 3.242 \times 10^4$ Pa |
| $P_{A2}$ | 0 (open to atmosphere) |
| $D_{AB}$ | $1.04 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s |

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute $P_{B1}$ and $P_{B2}$.

$$P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 - 3.242 \times 10^4 = 6.890 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $p_{BM}$.

$$p_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{3.242 \times 10^4}{\ln(1.4706)} = \frac{3.242 \times 10^4}{0.3857} = 8.405 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $N_A$.

$$N_A = \frac{(1.04 \times 10^{-5})(1.01325 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(303)(0.5)} \cdot \frac{3.242 \times 10^4}{8.405 \times 10^4}$$

$$N_A = \frac{1.0538}{1259.59} \cdot 0.3857 = 8.367 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.3857 = 3.227 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mass flux of acetone ($M_A = 58.08$ kg/kmol).

$$\dot{m}_A = (3.227 \times 10^{-4})(58.08 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.874 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{kg/(m²·s)}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$\boxed{N_A = 3.23 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)} \quad ; \quad \dot{m}_A = 1.87 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{kg/(m²·s)}}$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Ammonia gas (A) is absorbed from a stagnant mixture with air (B) into water in a vertical tube of length 0.4 m. The partial pressure of $NH_3$ at the gas-liquid interface is 0.10 atm, and at the top of the tube it is 0.02 atm. The total pressure is 1 atm and temperature is 25 °C. The diffusivity of $NH_3$ in air at this condition is $2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Determine: (a) Derive the steady-state molar flux equation when gas B is stagnant. (b) Calculate the molar flux of $NH_3$ and the total molar flux of the gas mixture.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

(See identical derivation as Question A; full marks awarded for the same 7-step derivation of Stefan's equation.)

**Final Result:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given data:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $T$ | 298 K |
| $P$ | $1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa |
| $\Delta z$ | 0.4 m |
| $P_{A1}$ | $0.10 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 1.0133 \times 10^4$ Pa |
| $P_{A2}$ | $0.02 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 2.027 \times 10^3$ Pa |
| $D_{AB}$ | $2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s |

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute partial pressures of B.

$$P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 9.1193 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 9.9293 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $p_{BM}$.

$$p_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{9.9293 \times 10^4 - 9.1193 \times 10^4}{\ln(9.9293/9.1193)}$$

$$= \frac{8100}{\ln(1.0888)} = \frac{8100}{0.08509} = 9.5188 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $N_A$.

$$N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.01325 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(298)(0.4)} \cdot \frac{1.0133 \times 10^4 - 2.027 \times 10^3}{9.5188 \times 10^4}$$

$$= \frac{2.3305}{990.92} \cdot \frac{8.106 \times 10^3}{9.5188 \times 10^4}$$

$$= 2.352 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.08516 = 2.003 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Bulk (total) flux of the gas mixture.

Since $N_B = 0$, the total molar flux is:

$$N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B = N_A = 2.003 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mass flux of $NH_3$ ($M = 17.03$ kg/kmol).

$$\dot{m}_{NH_3} = (2.003 \times 10^{-4})(17.03 \times 10^{-3}) = 3.411 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{kg/(m²·s)}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{N_A = 2.00 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)} \quad ; \quad \dot{m}_{NH_3} = 3.41 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{kg/(m²·s)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $p_{BM}$ factor (lose 4-5 marks):** Many students mistakenly use $(P - P_{A1})/2$ or the arithmetic mean of partial pressures of B. The **correct average is the logarithmic mean**, which emerges from the integration of $dP_B / P_B$. Using the arithmetic mean gives a flux that is only correct when $P_{A1}/P < 0.1$.
>
> 2. **Unit-mismatch trap (lose 1-2 marks):** Pressure must be in **Pa (not atm)** when used with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Length must be in **metres**, temperature in **K**, diffusivity in **m²/s**. Always check dimensions before writing the final numerical answer.
>
> 3. **Sign confusion in $dP_A = -dP_B$ (lose 1 mark):** When integrating, remember that $P_B$ *increases* in the direction of diffusion (A leaves, so B's share grows). The resulting $\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})$ is positive — sign errors here propagate throughout.
>
> 4. **Confusing "stagnant B" with "pure A" (lose 3 marks):** Stefan's equation is for **diffusion of A through B where $N_B = 0$**. It is **not** the same as pure Fickian diffusion (which would imply zero bulk flow). The whole point of Stefan diffusion is the *bulk-flow enhancement* term $(1 - x_A)$ in the denominator.
>
> 5. **In Part-A, writing Stefan's equation without naming the assumptions** (lose 1.5 marks). The KTU examiner will not award full marks unless at least 3-4 assumptions are stated explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — Stefan Diffusion**
>
> - **Definition:** Diffusion of species A through a *stationary* gas B where $N_B = 0$.
> - **Driving force:** Partial-pressure gradient of A combined with a *bulk convective flow* in the same direction as A.
> - **Governing equation (Stefan Equation):**
>   $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, \Delta z} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$
> - **Log-mean partial pressure of B:**
>   $$p_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$
> - **Stagnancy condition:** $N_B = 0$ exactly; this *forces* a bulk-flow contribution that *enhances* $N_A$ beyond pure Fickian diffusion.
> - **Enhancement ratio:**
>   $$\frac{N_A}{N_A^{o}} = \frac{P}{p_{BM}} \geq 1$$
>   where $N_A^{o}$ is the flux that would occur by pure molecular diffusion alone.
> - **Limiting cases:**
>   - If $P_{A1} \ll P$: $p_{BM} \approx P$ → bulk flow negligible → equation reduces to pure Fickian diffusion.
>   - If $P_{A1}$ comparable to $P$: $p_{BM} \ll P$ → strong bulk-flow enhancement.
> - **Assumptions to declare on KTU exam paper:**
>   1. Steady-state
>   2. One-dimensional diffusion along $z$
>   3. Ideal gas behaviour
>   4. Constant $T$ and $P$
>   5. Constant $D_{AB}$
>   6. $N_B = 0$ (no net molar flux of B)
> - **Typical applications:** Evaporation of liquids into still air, drying of porous solids, humidification, gas absorption with stagnant gas film, sublimation, candle/coal combustion.
> - **Working unit-system:** $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $\Delta z$ in m, $D_{AB}$ in m²/s, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), giving $N_A$ in mol/(m²·s).
> - **Conversion to mass flux:** Multiply $N_A$ by $M_A$ (molecular weight in kg/kmol).
> - **Approximation:** When $P_{A1}/P < 0.1$, the arithmetic mean $p_{BM} \approx (P_{B1} + P_{B2})/2$ is acceptable (within ~1% error).
> - **Distinguishing feature:** Stefan's equation contains the **bulk-flow correction factor** $P/p_{BM} > 1$, which is *absent* in equimolar counter-diffusion.
> - **Numerical answer format on KTU paper:** Always report the final flux with units in **scientific notation** (e.g., $2.54 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m²·s)).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
